                                                  xii
                                                  Official Use Only
                                  EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
    1. Project Overview and Objectives
    Project Overview
     The Hai Phong City Resilience Development Project (HPRDP) is a World Bank–financed
investment project implemented by the Hai Phong People’s Committee through the Hai Phong
Transport and Agricultural Works Construction Investment Project Management Unit (PMU).
The Project is designed to improve urban resilience, environmental quality, and climate
adaptation capacity of Hai Phong City through integrated investments in flood risk
management, wastewater and drainage infrastructure, river rehabilitation, and climate-resilient
transport systems.
    Hai Phong is a centrally governed Grade-I city, the third largest city in Viet Nam and a
major economic, industrial, logistics and seaport hub of the Northern Key Economic Region.
The city plays a strategic role as the main maritime gateway for Hanoi and northern provinces,
and is identified as a key growth pole of the Northern Coastal Region. Rapid urbanization,
industrial development, and population growth have placed increasing pressure on the city’s
drainage systems, urban rivers, and environmental quality.
     Hai Phong faces significant climate-related risks, including sea-level rise, tidal flooding,
heavy rainfall events, saline intrusion, and increasing frequency of extreme weather. The
northern area of the Cam River is identified as a strategic corridor for future urban expansion
but lacks a regional-scale resilient transport axis. The historic urban core in the southern area
of the Cam River experiences recurrent flooding and environmental degradation. The Re River,
which is a major freshwater source for the city, has shown increasing levels of pollution due to
untreated wastewater discharges.
    The HPRDP is designed as an integrated urban resilience project to address these
challenges by improving flood protection, enhancing wastewater management, rehabilitating
urban rivers, and strengthening institutional capacity for climate adaptation.
    Project Objectives
     The Project Development Objective (PDO) is to enhance climate resilience, environmental
sustainability, and urban livability in Hai Phong City through integrated flood-risk
management, wastewater collection and treatment, and resilient transport infrastructure.
    Specific objectives are to:
   •   Reduce urban flooding and tidal inundation through rehabilitation of Re and Tam Bac
       Rivers, construction of sluice gates and pumping stations, and improvement of the city’s
       drainage network;
   •   Improve water-environment quality by expanding wastewater collection and treatment,
       thereby reducing pollutant loads discharged to surface waters;
   •   Strengthen urban connectivity and transport resilience by upgrading key corridors such
       as Ring Road No. 3 and associated bridges;




                                                                                             xiii
                                                                                             Official Use Only
   •   Promote institutional capacity building for integrated flood-risk and environmental
       management consistent with the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework
       (ESF).
    2. Project Description and Components
    a) Project Information
     The Project will support the development of Hai Phong’s key coastal economic corridor
in the medium and long term, strengthening regional connectivity to the Northeastern provinces
and enhancing the utilization of existing seaport and airport infrastructure. The Project will
create conditions for industrial growth and land development along the new transport corridor
and improve intra-urban transport connectivity. It will strengthen the city’s drainage and tidal-
flood control capacity and enable the revitalization and multifunctional use of the Tam Bac
River waterfront. The investment in wastewater treatment systems and the rehabilitation of the
Re River will safeguard the city’s freshwater security, improve environmental sanitation, and
create green corridors and landscapes that reinforce urban infrastructure connectivity. Overall,
the Project aims to develop an integrated system for flood protection, drainage, wastewater
treatment and transport resilience, thereby enhancing Hai Phong’s adaptive capacity and
improving environmental quality for approximately 1.5 million residents.
     The Project is implemented across 13 wards of Hai Phong City, including Dong Hai, Nam
Trieu, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Thuy Nguyen, Luu Kiem, Hong Bang, An Duong, An Hai, Ngo
Quyen, Gia Vien, Le Chan and An Bien, covering a total land area of approximately 197.73
ha, including about 49.88 ha of rice land and 0.76 ha of protection forest plantation.
    b) Project Components
    The HPRDP consists of four inter-related components combining physical investments
and institutional support.
     Component 1 (Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure
Development). This component involves the development of an approximately 18.68-km road
alignment with associated bridges and drainage structures, traversing Dong Hai, Nam Trieu,
Thuy Nguyen, Hoa Binh, Bach Dang and Luu Kiem wards. The works include Ring Road No.
3 and associated bridge crossings over the Cam River and Ruot Lon River, together with an
additional connector from Bui Vien Road to the Hanoi–Hai Phong Expressway to enhance
connectivity and traffic efficiency.
     The total permanent land acquisition area for Component 1 is approximately 1,233,828
m², affecting 2,007 households and 12 organizations, of which 181 households are required to
be relocated. Affected land types include urban residential land, agricultural land, aquaculture
land, cemetery land, rivers and canals, irrigation and transport land, and protection forest land.
     Component 2 (Re River Environmental Improvement). Component 2 focuses on
rehabilitation and environmental improvement of the Re River corridor. The main works
include:
   •   Construction of riverbank embankments and slope protection works (with a total length
       of 9,67 km).



                                                                                              xiv
                                                                                              Official Use Only
   •   Construction of management road along Re River (with a total length of 13.74 km).
   •   Construction of interceptor sewers along both banks of Re River with 12 pumping
       stations, pressure pipelines, and the construction of approximately 8.42 km of level-3
       sewers to collect household wastewater along both banks of the Re River and convey it
       to the An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP).
   •   Pumping stations: three stations (PS-01 to PS-03) with individual capacities of 5,000–
       15,000 m³/day each, transmitting wastewater to the WWTP.
   •   Construction of An Duong 1 WWTP: located in An Hai Ward and occupying
       approximately 2.99 ha. The WWTP is designed with an average treatment capacity of
       8,900 m³/day and a maximum capacity of 10,000 m³/day, discharging treated effluent
       that meets QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT – Column A standards into the Lach Tray River.
     Component 3 (City Center Flood Reduction). This component includes construction of
two flow-regulating culverts on the Tam Bac River, together with a wastewater collection
system conveying flows to the Vinh Niem WWTP and new stormwater drainage pipelines
across the wards of Ngo Quyen, Gia Vien and Le Chan. The component aims to reduce urban
flooding and improve drainage conditions in the city center.
    Component 4 (Systems and Capacity Development to Improve Urban Resilience).
Component 4 is a non-construction component focusing on technical assistance and capacity
building to improve urban resilience, including institutional strengthening, system
development, and support for integrated flood-risk and environmental management.
    c) Scope of ESIA
     The scope of the ESIA focuses on Components 1, 2 and 3, which involve construction
activities. Component 4 does not include physical works and is limited to technical assistance
and capacity building.
    3. Policy, Legal and Institutional Framework
     The Hai Phong City Resilience Development Project (HPRDP) is financed by the World
Bank and therefore must comply with both the legal and regulatory framework of Viet Nam
and the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) and associated
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs). The ESIA has been prepared based on applicable
Vietnamese laws, decrees, circulars and national technical regulations (QCVNs) on
environmental protection, water resources, land, construction, labor, occupational safety and
health, biodiversity conservation and cultural heritage, together with the World Bank ESF,
relevant Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines (EHSGs), and relevant international
conventions to which Viet Nam is a party.
    Under the Vietnamese legal framework, the key applicable laws include the Law on
Environmental Protection (2020), Land Law (2024), Law on Water Resources (2023),
Construction Law (2014, amended 2020), Law on Urban and Rural Planning (2024), Law on
Natural Disaster Prevention and Control (2013), Law on Irrigation (2017), Labor Code (2019),
Law on Occupational Safety and Hygiene (2015), and Law on Cultural Heritage (2001,
amended). The ESIA is prepared in accordance with Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP, as amended
by Decree No. 05/2025/ND-CP, and relevant national technical regulations and standards



                                                                                           xv
                                                                                          Official Use Only
(QCVNs/TCVNs). Where the World Bank EHSGs provide more stringent requirements, the
higher standards are applied in accordance with Good International Industry Practice (GIIP).
    Under the World Bank ESF, the Project is classified as a Substantial risk project and is
subject to ESS1, ESS2, ESS3, ESS4, ESS5, ESS6, ESS8 and ESS10. ESS7 (Indigenous
Peoples) and ESS9 (Financial Intermediaries) are not applicable. The Project will comply with
the World Bank Group EHSGs, including the General EHSGs, EHSGs for Water and
Sanitation, and EHSGs for Toll Roads/Roads.
     Viet Nam is also a signatory to several international conventions relevant to environmental
and social management, including the Convention on Biological Diversity, Ramsar
Convention, UNFCCC and Paris Agreement, Kyoto Protocol, and core ILO Fundamental
Conventions. The principles of these conventions are embedded in the Project’s ESIA and
related ESF instruments.
     Differences between the Vietnamese regulatory system and the World Bank ESF remain
in terms of scope, risk classification, assessment instruments, consultation, disclosure and
supervision. To address these differences, the HPRDP applies both systems in parallel,
preparing ESIA and ESF instruments (ESCP, SEP, LMP, RPF, RPs) while also preparing the
national EIA and environmental permit dossiers in accordance with Vietnamese regulations.
    The World Bank reviews and clears the ESIA and ESF instruments prior to project
appraisal, while Vietnamese authorities (MAE/DAE) appraise and approve the national EIA
and environmental permits prior to project implementation. Monitoring and reporting
requirements are defined in the ESCP and ESMP, with supervision by both the World Bank
and relevant Vietnamese authorities.
    4. Baseline Environmental and Social Conditions
     Hai Phong City is located in a low-lying coastal area of northern Viet Nam, characterized
by a dense network of rivers, canals and estuaries, with flat topography and strong tidal
influence. The city lies within the tropical monsoon climate zone, with high annual rainfall, a
rainy season from May to October, and frequent exposure to extreme weather events such as
heavy rainfall, storms and typhoons. Flooding and waterlogging are recurrent problems in low-
lying urban areas, particularly in the central districts and along major river corridors.
    Baseline environmental conditions in the project area indicate that air quality is generally
within national standards but localized exceedances of dust and noise occur along major roads
and densely populated areas. Surface water quality in urban rivers and canals, particularly the
Re River and Tam Bac River, shows signs of organic pollution, elevated nutrient concentrations
and microbiological contamination, mainly due to untreated domestic wastewater discharges.
Groundwater and soil quality are generally acceptable but vulnerable to contamination from
urban activities, industrial discharges and solid waste disposal.
    The project area does not include any officially designated protected areas or critical
natural habitats. Aquatic ecosystems along rivers and canals are mainly modified urban water
bodies with limited biodiversity value, while terrestrial ecosystems consist primarily of urban
land, agricultural land, aquaculture ponds and scattered green spaces.



                                                                                            xvi
                                                                                            Official Use Only
     Socio-economic conditions in the project area are characterized by high population
density, mixed urban and peri-urban livelihoods, and increasing conversion of agricultural land
to residential, commercial and industrial uses. Livelihoods are mainly based on small-scale
trade, services, industrial employment, aquaculture and agriculture. Vulnerable groups include
low-income households, elderly people, female-headed households and households dependent
on agricultural and aquaculture activities.
     Sensitive receptors in the project area include residential communities, schools, health
facilities, religious structures, public buildings and family grave groups located along project
alignments and river corridors. These receptors are potentially exposed to construction-related
disturbances such as noise, dust, traffic disruption and access restrictions.
    5. Project Benefits and Climate Co-benefits
     The Project is expected to generate a range of positive environmental and social impacts
during both the construction and operation phases. These benefits are associated with
improvements in flood control, drainage and wastewater management systems, urban transport
infrastructure, river rehabilitation and institutional capacity for environmental management.
     From an environmental perspective, the Project will contribute to improved flood
protection and drainage capacity, particularly in low-lying and flood-prone areas of Hai Phong
City. The construction and upgrading of embankments, drainage canals, pumping stations and
regulating structures will enhance the capacity of the urban drainage system and reduce the
frequency and severity of urban flooding and waterlogging. The rehabilitation of the Re River
and associated wastewater collection systems will improve surface water quality by reducing
the discharge of untreated domestic wastewater into urban rivers and canals.
     From a social perspective, the Project will contribute to improved living conditions and
public health through better sanitation services, reduced exposure to polluted water, and
improved urban environmental quality. The development of urban roads and bridges under
Component 1 will improve traffic connectivity and accessibility, facilitating mobility, reducing
travel time, and supporting socio-economic development in Thuy Nguyen district and
surrounding areas. The rehabilitation of river corridors will also improve urban landscape and
public spaces, contributing to improved urban aesthetics and recreational opportunities.
     The Project will also generate institutional and capacity-building benefits, including
strengthened planning and management systems for flood risk management, drainage
operation, wastewater treatment and environmental monitoring. These improvements will
enhance the long-term capacity of local authorities to manage urban environmental challenges.
     With regard to climate change adaptation, the Project contributes to enhancing Hai
Phong’s resilience to climate-related hazards, including sea-level rise, tidal flooding and
extreme rainfall events. The climate-resilient design of embankments, drainage systems and
pumping stations increases the robustness of urban infrastructure under future climate scenarios
and reduces the vulnerability of communities and assets to climate-induced risks.
    In terms of climate change mitigation and greenhouse gas (GHG) aspects, the Project
contributes to reducing organic pollution loads and associated GHG emissions through
centralized wastewater collection and treatment. The operation of the An Duong 1 WWTP and


                                                                                            xvii
                                                                                            Official Use Only
improved wastewater management systems will reduce uncontrolled anaerobic decomposition
of organic matter in urban water bodies. Improved operational efficiency of pumping stations
and drainage systems also contributes to energy efficiency and GHG reduction compared to
baseline conditions.
    6. Key Environmental and Social Impacts and Risks
    The environmental and social impacts and risks of the Project have been assessed for the
preparation phase, construction phase and operation phase, as well as induced, cumulative and
climate change–related impacts.
    a) Impacts and Risks during Preparation Phase
    During the preparation phase, the main environmental and social impacts are related to
land acquisition and resettlement, and unexploded ordnance (UXO).
    The Project will require permanent land acquisition of approximately 197.73 ha, affecting
about 2,433 households and 27 organizations, of which approximately 181 households will be
physically relocated. Affected land types include residential land, agricultural land (paddy land,
annual and perennial crop land), aquaculture land, forest land, river and canal surface, and
various types of non-agricultural land.
     In addition to physical displacement, the Project will cause economic displacement due to
loss of productive land, aquaculture ponds and business activities. Affected households may
experience temporary or permanent impacts on income and livelihoods.
     UXO-related risks are identified in some project areas due to historical conflict activities.
UXO poses safety risks to workers and communities during site clearance and earthworks and
therefore requires survey and clearance prior to construction.
    b) Impacts and Risks during Construction Phase
    General Impacts and Risks:
    General environmental and social impacts during construction include:
   •   Air pollution and dust emissions from excavation, material transportation and
       construction machinery.
   •   Noise and vibration affecting nearby residential areas, schools, health facilities and
       other sensitive receptors.
   •   Surface water pollution caused by construction wastewater, runoff, sediment
       disturbance and accidental spills.
   •   Solid and hazardous waste generation, including excavated materials, domestic waste
       from workers and hazardous substances such as oils and chemicals.
   •   Traffic disruption and safety risks due to increased construction traffic, temporary road
       closures and interaction between construction vehicles and local traffic.
   •   Occupational health and safety risks, including accidents, exposure to hazardous
       materials and unsafe working conditions.
   •   Community health and safety risks, including exposure to construction sites, increased
       accident risks and social risks related to labor influx, including risks of GBV/SEA/SH.


                                                                                             xviii
                                                                                              Official Use Only
    Site-Specific Impacts:
     Component 1: Main impacts include land acquisition along road corridors, traffic
disruption, dust and noise affecting nearby communities, and safety risks associated with
bridge and road construction.
     Component 2: Key impacts include disturbance to riverbanks and aquatic habitats,
sediment resuspension during dredging, potential water pollution, and impacts on communities
living along the river corridor.
     Component 3: Impacts include construction disturbance in dense urban areas, temporary
disruption of drainage and wastewater services, noise and vibration affecting sensitive
receptors, and traffic congestion in central districts.
    c) Impacts and Risks during Operation Phase
    During the operation phase, the main impacts and risks include:
   •   Noise and air emissions from increased traffic volumes along upgraded road sections.
   •   Odor and sludge management risks associated with the operation of wastewater
       treatment plants and pumping stations.
   •   Public safety risks around drainage structures, pumping stations, sluice gates and open
       channels.
   •   Operational risks related to wastewater collection and treatment systems, including
       potential system failures affecting receiving water bodies.
    d) Induced Impacts
    Induced impacts may arise from land-use change, urban expansion, increased population
density, increased wastewater generation and rising land values in areas influenced by the
Project. These impacts may place additional pressure on urban infrastructure, public services
and environmental resources.
    e) Cumulative Impacts
     Cumulative impacts may occur as a result of interactions between the Project and other
ongoing or planned development projects in Hai Phong City. These include cumulative
pressures on water quality, air quality, traffic conditions, noise levels and community
disturbance, particularly in urban and industrial areas.
    f) Climate Change–Related Impacts and Risks
     Climate change–related risks include sea-level rise, increased tidal flooding, extreme
rainfall events and saline intrusion, which may affect the long-term performance of drainage
systems, embankments and flood-control infrastructure. These risks may increase the
vulnerability of urban areas and place additional stress on project assets if not adequately
addressed.
    7. Mitigation Measures and Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP)
     Mitigation measures for the Project have been developed in accordance with the project
lifecycle and are defined for the design and preparation phase, construction phase and operation


                                                                                            xix
                                                                                            Official Use Only
phase, as well as for induced, cumulative and climate change–related impacts. These measures
are consolidated in the Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP), including
Environmental and Social Codes of Practice (ESCOPs), monitoring programs, institutional
responsibilities and cost estimates.
    a) Mitigation Measures during Design and Preparation Phase
    Key mitigation measures during the design and preparation phase include preparation and
implementation of the Resettlement Plan (RP) in accordance with ESS5 and Vietnamese
regulations, ensuring compensation at replacement cost, livelihood restoration and support for
vulnerable households. UXO survey and clearance are required prior to commencement of
construction activities in areas identified as having potential UXO risks.
    Climate-resilient design standards are applied for embankments, drainage systems,
pumping stations and other flood-control infrastructure, taking into account future climate
change scenarios and flood risks.
    b) Mitigation Measures during Construction Phase
    General Mitigation Measures
    General mitigation measures during construction include:
   •   Dust control through watering, covering of materials and speed control of vehicles.
   •   Noise and vibration control through restricted working hours, use of appropriate
       equipment and installation of noise barriers where necessary.
   •   Management of construction wastewater and runoff to prevent pollution of surface
       water bodies.
   •   Proper collection, storage and disposal of solid and hazardous waste in accordance with
       regulations.
   •   Implementation of traffic management plans to minimize disruption and safety risks.
   •   Occupational health and safety measures, including worker training, provision of
       personal protective equipment and emergency response procedures.
   •   Community health and safety measures, including fencing of construction sites, signage
       and communication with local communities.
    Site-Specific Mitigation Measures
    Component 1: Traffic management, safety measures at bridge and road construction sites,
protection of nearby residential areas from dust and noise.
    Component 2: Riverbank protection and erosion control, sediment management during
dredging, prevention of water pollution and protection of aquatic habitats.
     Component 3: Management of construction activities in dense urban areas, minimization
of service disruption, protection of sensitive receptors and traffic control in central districts.
    c) Mitigation Measures during Operation Phase
     Mitigation measures during the operation phase include regular monitoring of wastewater
treatment performance, sludge management and odor control at WWTPs and pumping stations,


                                                                                               xx
                                                                                              Official Use Only
routine maintenance of drainage and flood-control infrastructure, and implementation of public
safety measures around operational facilities.
    d) Mitigation for Induced, Cumulative and Climate Change–Related Impacts
     Mitigation measures for induced and cumulative impacts include integrated urban
planning, coordination with other development projects and continued stakeholder
engagement. Climate-related mitigation measures include application of climate-resilient
design standards, adaptive operation of drainage systems and continuous monitoring of climate
risks.
    8. Institutional Arrangements and Responsibilities
     The Hai Phong City People’s Committee (HPCPC) is the Project Owner and is responsible
for overall implementation of the Project. The Hai Phong Transport and Agricultural Works
Construction Investment Project Management Unit (PMU) acts as the implementing agency,
responsible for day-to-day project management, coordination with relevant agencies, and
overall supervision of environmental and social management activities.
    The PMU is responsible for implementation of the Environmental and Social Management
Plan (ESMP), including coordination of land acquisition and resettlement activities, integration
of ESMP requirements into bidding documents and construction contracts, and reporting on
environmental and social performance to the World Bank and relevant Vietnamese authorities.
    Construction contractors are responsible for preparation and implementation of Contractor
Environmental and Social Management Plans (C-ESMPs) in accordance with the approved
ESMP and contractual requirements. Contractors are also responsible for compliance with
occupational health and safety requirements, community safety measures, and applicable
environmental regulations during construction.
    The Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) is responsible for supervision of
construction activities, monitoring contractors’ compliance with environmental and social
requirements, and reporting non-compliance to the PMU. An Independent Environmental and
Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) is engaged to provide independent monitoring and
evaluation of ESMP implementation and to support the PMU in addressing environmental and
social issues.
    Relevant city and district departments, including the Department of Agriculture and
Environment (DAE), Department of Construction (DOC), Department of Home Affairs
(DOHA), and local People’s Committees, are responsible for regulatory oversight, appraisal
and approval of environmental permits, land acquisition, labor management and community
engagement in accordance with their respective mandates.
    The World Bank is responsible for reviewing and supervising the implementation of the
Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) requirements, including clearance of ESIA and
ESF instruments, supervision missions, and review of environmental and social performance
reports submitted by the PMU.
    9. Public Consultation and Grievance Redress Mechanism



                                                                                            xxi
                                                                                            Official Use Only
    Public consultation for the Project has been conducted in accordance with the requirements
of Vietnamese regulations and the Project’s consultation plan. Stakeholder identification and
analysis were undertaken to identify project-affected people, interested parties and vulnerable
groups, including local communities, households affected by land acquisition, businesses, mass
organizations, and relevant government agencies.
     Public consultation activities were carried out during preparation of the ESIA through
community meetings, focus group discussions and written consultations in the project areas.
Information on project scope, potential environmental and social impacts, proposed mitigation
measures and grievance mechanisms was disclosed to stakeholders. Feedback and concerns
raised by stakeholders were recorded and incorporated into the design of mitigation measures
and management plans where appropriate.
    The Project establishes a multi-level Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) to receive and
address complaints and concerns from project-affected people and other stakeholders. The
GRM allows grievances to be submitted verbally or in writing at commune, district and city
levels, and includes procedures for grievance registration, screening, investigation, response
and resolution within specified timeframes.
    The GRM does not limit the right of complainants to access judicial or administrative
remedies under Vietnamese law. A separate grievance mechanism is also established for
project workers in accordance with labor management requirements.




                                                                                           xxii
                                                                                           Official Use Only
                                      OVERVIEW
0.1.   PROJECT BACKGROUND
0.1.1. Project overview
      Hai Phong City is a centrally governed Grade-I urban area, the third largest city in Viet
Nam and the second most significant economic, political, and cultural centre in the Red River
Delta region. Located 106 km east-southeast of Hanoi along National Highway 5, Hai Phong
expanded to a natural area of 3,194.72 km² and a population of 4,664,124 following the merger
with Hai Duong Province. The city currently comprises 114 commune-level administrative
units, including 45 wards, 67 communes, and two special zones. As the largest seaport in
Northern Viet Nam and a major multimodal transport hub, covering inland waterways, road,
rail, air and maritime networks. Hai Phong serves as the principal gateway to the sea for Hanoi
and the northern provinces, and plays a pivotal role within the Northern Key Economic Region
and the “Two Corridors – One Belt” cooperation framework between Viet Nam and China. In
the regional socio-economic development strategy, Hai Phong is identified as a key growth
pole of the dynamic Northern region (Hanoi – Hai Phong – Quang Ninh) and a major economic,
scientific-technical centre of the Northern Coastal Region.
    According to the Adjusted Master Plan for Hai Phong City to 2040, vision to 2050,
approved under Decision No. 323/QĐ-TTg dated 30 March 2023, Hai Phong is oriented toward
becoming a leading industrialized and modernized city, a development engine for both the
Northern Region and the country, with modern, smart and sustainable industries, synchronized
multimodal transport infrastructure connected domestically and internationally, major logistics
and tourism functions, and an international center for education, training, research, science-
technology and marine-based economy. The city is further characterized as a centrally
governed Grade-I city, a national coastal economic hub, a center of education, healthcare and
science-technology in the Northern Coastal Region, a green, civilized and modern port city, a
domestic and international transport hub, and a strategically important area for national defense
and security.
     Hai Phong faces significant climate-related risks, including sea level rise, flooding and
extreme weather events. According to the OECD’s 2016 Green Growth Study for Hai Phong,
the city is among the world’s 13 major port cities expected to sustain the most rapid increase
in asset losses by 2070 due to climate change. Integrating climate-change adaptation into socio-
economic development and infrastructure investment is therefore essential. The northern area
of the Cam River is one of the city’s three strategic development corridors for urban expansion
and socio-economic growth toward the coast; however, it currently lacks a regional-scale
transport corridor and a coastal economic axis aligned with the northwest–southeast direction
of Ring Road No. 3. Meanwhile, the southern area of the Cam River—the historic urban core—
experiences recurrent flooding due to rainfall and tidal influence from the Cam and Tam Bac
Rivers, and its riverfront spaces remain underutilized. The Re River, a critical freshwater
source for the entire city, has also begun to show signs of pollution. Addressing these
constraints and challenges will create favorable conditions and generate strong drivers for
coherent socio-economic and urban development in Hai Phong.


                                                                                               1
                                                                                             Official Use Only
0.1.2. Consistency with Other Plans and Development Projects
     The HPRDP is consistent with the Hai Phong City Master Plan to 2040 and Vision to 2050,
approved by the Prime Minister in Decision No. 323/QĐ-TTg (2023). The Hai Phong City
Master Plan to 2040, with a vision to 2050, approved under Decision No. 323/QĐ-TTg dated
15 March 2023, serves as the overarching spatial and development framework guiding all urban
and infrastructure investments across the city. The plan positions Hai Phong as a modern,
sustainable, and climate-resilient coastal metropolis, one of Vietnam’s key growth poles within
the Northern Economic Corridor and the Red River Delta Region.
    The Master Plan outlines a development vision anchored on four pillars:
   1. Modern industrial and logistics hub integrating deep-sea ports, industrial parks, and
      high-tech manufacturing;
   2. Green and resilient urban infrastructure, emphasizing adaptive drainage, wastewater
      management, and low-carbon transportation;
   3. Cultural and tourism development, promoting heritage preservation and eco-tourism in
      river and coastal zones; and
   4. Smart and livable city, enhancing quality of life through digital transformation, green
      spaces, and sustainable utilities.
    A key strategic orientation within the plan is the establishment of comprehensive water
environment management in line with the national targets for pollution control, flood
mitigation, and climate change adaptation. Specifically, the plan prioritizes:
   •   Rehabilitation and protection of urban rivers and canals (e.g., the Re River, Lach Tray,
       and Ruot Lon canals);
   •   Expansion and modernization of wastewater treatment systems in existing urban areas;
   •   Construction of new combined drainage–wastewater systems in peri-urban districts
       such as An Duong, Kien An, and Thuy Nguyen;
   •   Integration of green-blue corridors to restore ecosystem functions and manage
       stormwater through nature-based solutions.
     The proposed Hai Phong City Resilient Development Project (HPRDP) directly supports
these strategic directions. Its components, particularly the construction of wastewater treatment
plants, rehabilitation of river embankments, and installation of stormwater drainage systems,
are designed as integral sub-projects under the city’s Master Plan.
     Moreover, the project advances the Vision to 2050, which envisions Hai Phong as a coastal
“eco-industrial city” and a model of climate resilience in the Red River Delta. The long-term
vision underscores the importance of balancing rapid urbanization with sustainable resource
management, protecting biodiversity in estuarine and coastal zones, and ensuring inclusive
urban services.
    In this context, the HPRDP’s integrated approach to wastewater collection, flood risk
reduction, and green infrastructure development contributes not only to immediate
environmental improvements but also to the fulfillment of Hai Phong’s long-term development
objectives toward 2050.



                                                                                               2
                                                                                             Official Use Only
    The proposed project is also consistent with key national strategic frameworks that guide
Vietnam’s response to climate change and sustainable growth, notably the following:
    (i) National Strategy on Climate Change (Decision No. 896/QĐ-TTg, 2022):
This strategy sets the vision for a climate-resilient and low-carbon development pathway
toward 2050. It emphasizes the importance of resilient urban infrastructure, modernization of
drainage systems, and comprehensive flood control measures for coastal cities such as Hai
Phong.
The strategy outlines specific objectives to:
   •   Strengthen the adaptive capacity of urban areas against climate-induced flooding and
       sea-level rise.
   •   Modernize water drainage and wastewater collection systems using integrated and
       nature-based solutions.
   •   Promote resilient land-use planning that harmonizes urban development with
       ecosystem conservation.
     The HPRDP directly contributes to these objectives through investments in flood risk
management, river rehabilitation, and wastewater infrastructure, which are designed to enhance
the city’s resilience to both pluvial and tidal flooding. The project’s structural and non-
structural measures - including hydrological monitoring systems, green buffer zones, and
institutional capacity building - align with the national targets for adaptive urban management
and disaster risk reduction under Decision 896/QĐ-TTg.
     (ii) National Green Growth Strategy (Decision No. 1658/QĐ-TTg, 2021): The National
Green Growth Strategy promotes sustainable urban water management, reduction of
environmental pollution, and efficient use of natural resources. It calls for the application of
circular economy principles in wastewater treatment, renewable energy use in urban systems,
and control of pollution in major river basins. Key objectives of the strategy relevant to HPRDP
include:
   •   Improving wastewater and solid waste treatment to meet green urban standards.
   •   Enhancing eco-efficiency of public infrastructure and promoting green investment
       models.
   •   Reducing greenhouse gas emissions through improved energy efficiency and climate-
       smart technologies.
     By investing in advanced wastewater treatment (e.g., MBR technology), integrated
drainage infrastructure, and energy-efficient pumping systems, the HPRDP acts as a pilot
model for implementing the Green Growth Strategy in northern Vietnam’s coastal cities.
Furthermore, its institutional capacity-building component supports the mainstreaming of
green and climate-resilient approaches into Hai Phong’s urban planning and management
systems.

0.1.3. Other Projects in Proposed Project Area
     Other ongoing and planned developments within the vicinity of the HPRDP include urban
residential areas, resettlement sites, commercial–service projects, logistics facilities, and



                                                                                              3
                                                                                            Official Use Only
transport-related infrastructure located along the HP1, HP2, and HP3 corridors. Several of these
projects are currently under construction or are scheduled to commence during the same period
as the HPRDP, and are situated at varying distances from the Project components. Collectively,
these developments may contribute to incremental pressures on shared environmental and
social receptors, including surface water systems, drainage and wastewater networks, traffic
conditions, and nearby communities.
    Purpose and relevance
    This subsection inventories ongoing and reasonably foreseeable developments within and
around the HPRDP’s Area of Influence (AoI) that may contribute to cumulative environmental
and social impacts when combined with the Project’s activities. Per ESF/ESS1 practice,
cumulative assessment will consider concurrent timing, shared environmental media (surface
water, groundwater, air/noise), shared receptors (communities, biodiversity, cultural assets),
and shared infrastructure (drainage/wastewater networks, roads/bridges). The TOR also
requests identification of ongoing or planned activities in the same geographical/watershed
area for integrated ESIA scoping and cumulative assessment, and to evaluate cumulative
impacts from present and reasonably foreseeable projects with appropriate management
measures and reference guidance (e.g., IFC GPN on cumulative impact assessment).

       receiving yard runoff.

         negligible but public communication will be coordinated.
   •     Solid Waste Transfer Point Improvements near haul routes.
   •     Potential interaction: additive truck flows and odour risks if works overlap; need
         routing/scheduling protocols in the HPRDP CEMP.
       Preliminary Interaction Pathways (for cumulative assessment)
   •     Hydrology & Water Quality: simultaneous canal works → turbidity, DO depression,
         and nutrient pulses; combined start-up of sewer networks → first-flush loads until stable
         operation at WWTP An Dương.
   •     Air Quality & Noise: concurrent road/canal construction → additive PM and Leq at
         receptors within 50–200 m; focus on schools/clinics/markets.
   •     Traffic & Community Safety: overlapping lane closures, material haulage, and bridge
         works → compounded collision risk; solution is joint TMP and staggered shifts.
   •     Land & Social: multiple small works → extended access disruption; risk of grievance
         accumulation unless integrated GRM channels are synchronized across projects (TOR
         calls for coordinated stakeholder engagement/SEP throughout project cycle) .
       Data Gaps and Next Steps
   •     Verification of project list with DOC,             DAE,    district   PCs   to   confirm
         status/timing/footprints (living inventory).
   •     GIS overlay of footprints against HPRDP work fronts, haul routes, and sensitive
         receptors (Map to be inserted).
   •     Phasing plan to avoid peak concurrency of in-channel works during monsoon or school
         hours.
   •     Joint monitoring arrangements (water quality, air/noise, traffic safety) and shared
         communication via ward PCs/GRM—TOR indicates continuous stakeholder
         engagement and integrated ES instruments across the project cycle.
   •     Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) hook: this inventory will directly feed Chapter
         4’s Cumulative Impacts section, consistent with TOR’s requirement to analyze
         cumulative/induced impacts and propose ESMP measures.

0.2.     LEGAL BACKGROUND
    The Hai Phong City Resilience Development Project (HPRDP) is financed by the World
Bank and therefore must comply with both (i) the legal and regulatory framework of Viet Nam,
and (ii) the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) and associated



                                                                                                6
                                                                                              Official Use Only
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) and guidelines. This ESIA for the HPRDP has
been prepared based on:
   •   The applicable Vietnamese laws, decrees, circulars and national technical regulations
       (QCVNs) on environmental protection, water resources, land, construction, labor,
       occupational safety and health, biodiversity conservation and cultural heritage;
   •   The World Bank ESF and ESSs and relevant Environmental, Health and Safety (EHS)
       Guidelines; and
   •   Relevant international conventions and agreements to which Viet Nam is a party.
    The ESIA also draws on the Feasibility Study (FS), basic design, detailed planning, and
other technical documents of the HPRDP (including hydraulic modeling, drainage and
wastewater master planning and climate change assessments) to ensure technical robustness
and consistency.

0.2.1. Legal documents and technical basis of the Government of Vietnam
    This ESIA has been prepared in compliance with the applicable legal documents and
technical regulations of Viet Nam, including but not limited to the following:
a) Laws
   •   Law on Environmental Protection, No. 72/2020/QH14 (adopted on 17 November 2020;
       effective from 01 January 2022). This is the principal legal framework governing
       environmental protection in Viet Nam, including EIA/SEA procedures, environmental
       permitting, waste and wastewater management, biodiversity protection, climate change
       adaptation, and environmental monitoring. It forms the legal basis for assessing
       environmental impacts associated with embankments, roads, pumping stations,
       wastewater collection systems, WWTPs, and treated wastewater discharge under the
       Project.
   •   Land Law, No. 31/2024/QH15 (adopted on 18 January 2024; effective from 01 January
       2025), replacing Land Law No. 45/2013/QH13 regulates land allocation, land recovery
       (expropriation), land valuation, compensation, support, and resettlement. This law is
       directly applicable to permanent and temporary land acquisition required for
       embankments, roads, management roads, pumping stations, WWTPs, and other project
       components, and provides the legal basis for the RAP/RPF.
   •   Law on Water Resources, No. 28/2023/QH15 (adopted on 27 November 2023; effective
       from 01 July 2024) governs the management, protection, exploitation, and use of
       surface water and groundwater, including river basin management and classification of
       water bodies. This law is essential for assessing impacts on the Re, Tam Bac, and Lach
       Tray river systems, water abstraction and discharge permits, drainage outfalls, and
       cumulative hydrological impacts.
   •   Construction Law, No. 50/2014/QH13, as amended by Law No. 62/2020/QH14
       Provides the legal framework for construction investment activities, including project
       preparation, design, construction permits, quality management, occupational safety,
       and construction supervision. It applies to all civil works under the Project, such as
       roads, bridges, embankments, pumping stations, and WWTPs. The consolidated
       (amended) version of the law is applicable.




                                                                                           7
                                                                                         Official Use Only
•   Law on Urban and Rural Planning, No. 47/2024/QH15 (dated 26 November 2024)
    regulates urban and rural planning systems, zoning, and integration of technical
    infrastructure such as drainage, wastewater treatment, river corridors, and transport
    networks. This law is relevant for consistency of the Project with approved urban
    master plans and sectoral infrastructure planning of Hai Phong City.
•   Law on Biodiversity, No. 32/VBHN-VPQH (consolidated version issued on 10
    December 2018) governs conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity, including
    protection of natural habitats and ecosystems. This law supports assessment of potential
    impacts on aquatic ecosystems and riparian habitats associated with river
    embankments, drainage discharge points, and construction activities.
•   Law on Inland Waterway Navigation, No. 23/2004/QH11, as amended by Law No.
    48/2014/QH13 regulates management and safety of inland waterways and navigation
    corridors. It is applicable to construction activities near rivers and canals, particularly
    where embankments, culverts, or discharge structures may affect navigation safety or
    river use.
•   Law on Natural Disaster Prevention and Control, No. 33/2013/QH13 provides the legal
    framework for disaster risk management, flood control, and climate resilience. This law
    is relevant for assessing flood risks, climate change adaptation measures, and resilience
    of embankments, drainage systems, pumping stations, and other flood-control
    infrastructure.
•   Law on Irrigation, No. 08/2017/QH14 regulates management, protection, and operation
    of irrigation and drainage works. This law applies to stormwater drainage systems,
    canals, sluices, and flood-control structures that interact with existing irrigation and
    water management infrastructure.
•   Labor Code, No. 45/2019/QH14 governs labor relations, working conditions,
    protection of workers’ rights, and prohibition of child labor. It is applicable to all project
    workers during construction and operation phases, forming the legal basis for labor
    management procedures (LMP).
•   Law on Occupational Safety and Hygiene, No. 84/2015/QH13 regulates occupational
    health and safety measures, prevention of occupational accidents and diseases, and
    responsibilities of employers and workers. This law applies to construction sites,
    pumping stations, WWTPs, and operational facilities under the Project.
•   Law on Fire Prevention and Fighting, No. 40/2013/QH13 (amended) governs fire
    prevention, fire safety design, and firefighting requirements. It is applicable to pumping
    stations, WWTPs, electrical rooms, operation buildings, and other facilities with fire
    and explosion risks.
•   Law on Road Traffic, No. 23/2008/QH12 (as amended and currently effective)
    regulates road traffic safety, traffic organization, and protection of road infrastructure.
    This law is relevant for construction and operation of roads, intersections, and traffic
    management measures, including temporary traffic arrangements during construction.
•   Law on Cultural Heritage, No. 28/2001/QH10, as amended by Law No. 32/2009/QH12
    governs protection and management of cultural, historical, and archaeological heritage.
    It provides the legal basis for chance-find procedures during earthworks and
    construction activities.

   •   Law on Children, No. 102/2016/QH13 regulates child protection and prevention of
       child labor. This law is relevant to labor management, community health and safety,
       and protection of vulnerable groups affected by project activities.

   •   Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP, detailing a number of articles of the Law on

0.2.2. World Bank policies and guidelines on environmental and social safety
     The World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF), effective since 1 October
2018, sets out the Bank’s commitment to sustainable development through a Bank Policy and
a set of Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) designed to support Borrowers’ projects
in achieving environmentally and socially sustainable outcomes. The ESF aims to end extreme
poverty and promote shared prosperity by strengthening the Borrower’s capacity to assess and
manage environmental and social (E&S) risks and impacts.
     To this end, the Bank has defined ten ESSs, which are designed to avoid, minimize, reduce
or mitigate adverse environmental and social risks and impacts. The following ESSs are
relevant to the Hai Phong Resilience Development Project (HPRDP).
     ESS1 – Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and
Impacts. ESS1 requires the Borrower to assess, manage, and monitor E&S risks and impacts
throughout the project lifecycle to achieve outcomes consistent with the ESSs. This ESS is
relevant to the HPRDP because the Project involves multiple civil works under Components 1
– 4, including: rehabilitation of the Re and Tam Bac Rivers; construction of embankments and
pumping stations; development of Ring Road No. 3; and the An Duong 1 WWTP and sewer
system.
    Typical construction impacts include disturbance to aquatic and riverbank ecosystems;
dust, noise and vibration; waste and wastewater pollution; traffic and public safety risks;
embankment subsidence and erosion; temporary service disruptions; community nuisance;
occupational and public health and safety risks; and economic impacts related to land
acquisition.
    During operation, the main impacts are: noise and air emissions from traffic on Ring Road
No. 3, odor and sludge from the sewer and WWTP, and community safety issues around
drainage and flood control structures.
    An Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) has been prepared in accordance
with ESS1, together with an Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) and an
Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) setting out implementation
responsibilities and monitoring requirements.




                                                                                             13
                                                                                            Official Use Only
     ESS2 – Labor and Working Conditions. ESS2 recognizes the importance of
employment creation and income generation in reducing poverty and promoting inclusive
growth. It requires fair treatment of workers and safe, secure and healthy working conditions.
The HPRDP workforce includes: direct workers (PMU staff), contracted workers (through
contractors and consultants), and primary supply workers (providing materials such as
aggregates and cement). Community workers are not expected. Key risks include OHS
incidents, exposure to risks of GBV/SEA linked to labor influx. A Labor Management
Procedures (LMP) has been prepared to manage these risks in line with ESS2 and Vietnamese
law.
    ESS3 – Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management. ESS3
addresses resource efficiency and pollution prevention throughout the project lifecycle.
The HPRDP requires significant construction materials and energy for roads, bridges, drainage
and WWTP facilities. Risks include air emissions, solid waste, hazardous materials handling,
and construction spoils.
     The ESIA assesses these impacts and includes mitigation measures in line with the World
Bank Group Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines (EHSGs) and Good International
Industry Practice (GIIP), covering energy use, dust control, waste management and chemical
storage for the An Duong 1 WWTP and pumping stations.
     ESS4 – Community Health and Safety. ESS4 addresses health, safety, and security risks
to project-affected communities. Risks in Hai Phong include UXO remnants from past
conflicts, traffic safety along transport routes, public exposure to construction sites, GBV/SEA
risks from worker influx, and epidemic risks.
    Standard EHSG measures have been integrated into the ESIA and will be implemented
through contractor CEMPs and the Project ESMP to protect local communities during
construction and operation.
     ESS5 – Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement.
The Project will require significant permanent and temporary land acquisition for river
embankments, Ring Road No. 3, pumping stations, transmission mains and resettlement sites.
According to the latest Resettlement Plan (RP) and Inventory of Losses (IOL), the Project is
expected to permanently acquire approximately 197.73 ha of land, including (1) 5.68 ha of
urban residential land, (2) 1.25 ha of perennial crop land, (3) 49.88 ha of paddy land, (4) 13.46
ha of annual crop land, (5) 22.76 ha of aquaculture land, (6) 0.52 ha of other agricultural land,
(7) 0.76 ha of protection forest land, (8) 26.11 ha of urban transport land, (9) 3.45 ha of
irrigation land, (10) 70.50 ha of river and canal surface, (11) 5.63 ha of commercial and service
land, (12) 0.15 ha of non-agricultural production land, (13) 0.16 ha of education and public
facility land, (14) 0.26 ha of recreation, cultural, and sports land, (15) 0.55 ha of cemetery land,
(16) 0.18 ha of belief establishment land, (17) 0.002 ha of agency office land, (18) 0.002 ha of
security land, and (19) 0.65 ha of unused land. The land acquisition will affect around 2,433
households and 27 organizations, of which approximately 181 households will be physically
relocated.




                                                                                                 14
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     To meet the resettlement needs of the Project, Hai Phong City plans to develop two main
resettlement areas: (i) Bach Dang resettlement area (former Ngu Lao commune), with an area
of about 19.98 ha providing plots for approximately 958 households; and (ii) Nam Trieu
resettlement area (former Lap Le commune), with an area of about 13.66 ha serving around
663 households. These resettlement areas will be provided with complete technical and social
infrastructure (roads, electricity, water supply and drainage, schools, markets and health
stations), facilitating livelihood restoration and social integration of the relocated households.
    In addition to physical relocation, the Project will cause temporary and permanent
economic displacement for households losing productive land (rice land, aquaculture ponds)
and small businesses located along the construction corridors. A Resettlement Plan (RP) will
be prepared in accordance with ESS5 and Vietnamese regulations, specifying entitlements,
compensation at replacement cost, livelihood restoration measures and special assistance for
vulnerable households.
    ESS6 – Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural
Resources. ESS6 aims to protect and conserve biodiversity and maintain ecosystem services.
The project areas comprise modified habitats along urban rivers (Cam, Re, Tam Bac, Lach
Tray) with no critical habitats identified. The ESIA assesses impacts on aquatic terrestrial
ecosystems and includes measures to prevent pollution, control dredging activities and manage
sludge from the WWTP, ensuring protection of riverine flora and fauna.
    ESS7 – Indigenous Peoples / Ethnic Minorities. The Project area is predominantly
populated by the Kinh majority, with only a small number of ethnic minority households
dispersed within mixed urban and peri-urban communities. These ethnic minority households
do not have collective attachment to the specific project sites in terms of territory, natural
resources or distinct institutions. Socio-economic surveys undertaken during project
preparation indicate that their living conditions, access to services and livelihood patterns are
broadly similar to those of neighboring Kinh households.
    Therefore, ESS7 on Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved
Traditional Local Communities is not considered relevant to the Project. Potentially affected
ethnic minority households, if any, will be covered under the general provisions of ESS1, ESS5
and ESS10, and will receive full compensation and assistance consistent with the Resettlement
Plan and Stakeholder Engagement Plan.
     ESS8 – Cultural Heritage. The ESIA screening for the Hai Phong Project did not identify
any temples, pagodas or monuments of national or provincial significance located within the
direct construction footprints of the proposed works. However, there are a number of pagodas,
churches and local shrines located at varying distances (from about 30 m to 300 m) from the
construction alignments of drainage pipelines and river embankments, such as Thanh Luong
Pagoda, Dong Gioi Pagoda, Bao Quang Pagoda, local communal houses and churches in the
central urban area.
    Along certain river sections and road corridors, there are also scattered family grave groups
and small cemeteries located within or close to the proposed rights-of-way, particularly along
the Re River embankments and sections of Ring Road No. 3 as shown on the detailed



                                                                                               15
                                                                                              Official Use Only
topographic and planning maps. The exact number of graves that may need to be relocated will
be confirmed during the detailed design and the preparation of the Resettlement Plan, based on
full inventory and consultation with affected households and local authorities.
     The ESIA requires that any relocation of graves shall follow Vietnamese cultural practices
and legal procedures, including prior notification, consultation with affected families,
compensation for relocation costs, and rituals as requested by the families. A Chance Find
Procedure (CFP) is included in the ESMP to address any unexpected discovery of cultural
heritage during construction, including archaeological remains and unrecorded graves.
    ESS9 – Financial Intermediaries. ESS9 is not relevant, as the HPRDP does not involve
financial intermediaries.
    ESS10 – Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure. ESS10 emphasizes
open, transparent and inclusive engagement with stakeholders throughout the project cycle.
    Key stakeholders include the Hai Phong PMU (under the DOC), relevant departments
(DAE, DOF) and ward authorities, contractors, consultants, affected communities and mass
organizations (Women’s Union, Farmers’ Union, Fatherland Front). A Stakeholder
Engagement Plan (SEP) has been prepared to ensure meaningful consultation and grievance
redress through dedicated community and worker GRMs. The SEP will be continuously
updated throughout project implementation to reflect stakeholder feedback.
     World Bank Group Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines (EHSGs). All WB-
financed projects must comply with the EHSGs, which serve as technical references for Good
International Industry Practice. They include general and industry-specific guidelines for
environmental management, occupational health and safety, community safety and
construction practices.
    The HPRDP will apply the following EHSGs:
        •   General EHSGs (2007);
        •   EHSGs for Water and Sanitation (WWTP and drainage systems);
        •   EHSGs for Toll Roads/Roads (Ring Road No. 3); and
        •   relevant EHS provisions for construction materials and hazardous waste handling.
    Conformance with these Guidelines will be ensured through the ESMP and construction
contracts for all HPRDP packages.
     International waterways and transboundary considerations. The HPRDP interventions are
located entirely within Hai Phong City and do not involve any international waterways as
defined in the World Bank’s Operational Policy on Projects on International Waterways (OP
7.50). The Re River, Tam Bac River, Lach Tray River and the associated canal and drainage
systems are wholly within Viet Nam’s territory and form part of the domestic river network
discharging to the Hai Phong coastal zone. There is no direct abstraction from or discharge to
water bodies forming a boundary between Viet Nam and another riparian state, and no
upstream or downstream transboundary impacts are anticipated. Accordingly, the Project is not
expected to trigger requirements related to transboundary environmental and social impact
assessment.


                                                                                            16
                                                                                           Official Use Only
0.2.3. Relevant International Treaties and Agreements
    Viet Nam is signatory to a number of international agreements and conventions relating to
environmental management, social protection, gender equality, labor, and cultural heritage.
These conventions, though not always fully translated into domestic legislation, set important
guiding principles for environmental and social management of projects such as the Hai Phong
Resilience Development Project (HPRDP).

     The above international conventions provide the global framework for environmental

Management Procedures (LMP) and Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP).

0.2.4. Gap Analysis between the GoV’s regulations and WB’s ESF
     The application of environmental assessment policies in Viet Nam, together with ongoing
efforts towards harmonization between the Government of Viet Nam (GoV) and development
partners, has significantly narrowed the gap between the national system and the World Bank’s
ESF. However, important differences remain in terms of scope, classification, instruments,
public consultation, disclosure, and supervision. Understanding these differences is crucial to
ensure that the HPRDP complies simultaneously with both systems.
     A summary of the main similarities, differences and gap-filling measures between the
World Bank ESF requirements and Viet Nam’s legal framework – as applicable to the HPRDP
– is presented in the table below.
     Table 0.4. Summary of the World Bank ESA and Viet Nam EIA Processes and

     This combined legal and technical basis ensures that the ESIA for the HPRDP is fully
aligned with Viet Nam’s current legal framework and the World Bank’s ESF requirements,
providing a comprehensive and robust foundation for environmental and social impact
assessment, mitigation and monitoring throughout the Project life cycle.

0.3.   ESIA PREPARATION METHOD
    The Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) for the Hai Phong Resilience
Development Project (HPRDP) has been conducted in accordance with the World Bank’s
Environmental and Social Framework (ESF, 2018), the Environmental Protection Law of Viet
Nam (2020), and relevant national decrees and technical standards. A combination of
qualitative and quantitative methods was applied to identify, predict, and assess the
environmental and social impacts of the Project during pre-construction, construction, and
operation stages. The main methods applied are summarized below.
(a) Literature Review and Data Compilation
Existing data and secondary information were reviewed and analyzed, including:
   •   Feasibility Study Report (FS ver 2c, 2025);
   •   National EIA report (2026);
   •   Urban Master Plan and related planning documents of Hai Phong City;
   •   Statistical Yearbooks (2018–2024) of Hai Phong;
   •   Reports from the Department of Agriculture and Environment (DAE), Department of
       Health (DOH), and Department of Urban Infrastructure and Environment (DUIE);
   •   National monitoring data on water, air, and soil quality; and
   •   Relevant previous environmental assessments and monitoring reports.
     This information was used to define baseline conditions, identify data gaps, and design the
field survey program.
(b) Field Investigation and Sampling
     Field investigations were conducted at representative locations of the Project area,
including along the Re River, Tam Bac River, Lach Tray River, Ring Road No.3 corridor, An
Duong 1 WWTP site, and associated pumping stations and drainage networks.
Surveys and sampling were carried out during both dry and rainy seasons (2024–2025),
covering:
   •   Surface water: physical–chemical indicators, nutrients, and heavy metals;
   •   Groundwater: pH, TDS, hardness, Fe, Mn, NH₄⁺, NO₂⁻, and coliforms;
   •   Ambient air: TSP, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, CO, and noise;
   •   Soil: pH, organic matter, and heavy metal concentrations (Pb, Cd, As, Cu, Zn, Cr);
   •   Sediment (rivers and canals): texture and heavy metals;


                                                                                             24
                                                                                            Official Use Only
   •   Biodiversity: flora and fauna composition in aquatic and riparian habitats.
     All samples were collected and preserved in accordance with TCVN 6663-1:2011 (ISO
5667-1:2006) and analyzed by an accredited laboratory (VILAS 1699) following Vietnamese
national standards (QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT, QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT, etc.).
(c) Checklist and Screening
    A screening checklist, adapted from the World Bank’s Environmental and Social
Screening Form (ESS1 Annex 1) and the Vietnamese environmental impact assessment
guideline (Circular 07/2025/TT-BTNMT), was applied to:
   •   Define environmental and social sensitivity of project sites;
   •   Identify key environmental receptors (air, water, land, biodiversity, community,
       heritage);
   •   Identify critical environmental and social issues that require mitigation or management;
   •   Categorize project risk level and determine the scope of further analysis.
(d) Rapid Environmental and Social Assessment
    Empirical and model-based techniques were applied for preliminary quantification of key
impacts:
   •   Air Quality: Emission estimations used empirical coefficients (US EPA, 1996; WHO,
       1993) for dust and exhaust gases, combined with the Sutton dispersion model to
       estimate particulate dispersion near residential areas.
   •   Noise and Vibration: Assessed using prediction formulae from ISO 9613 and WHO
       guidelines for community noise.
   •   Waste and Wastewater Generation: Calculated based on construction volume, material
       quantities, and operation load of the WWTP.
   •   Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emission: Estimated using IPCC 2019 emission factors for
       fuel use and electricity consumption.
   •   Social Impacts: Evaluated through rapid socio-economic assessment of affected
       households, particularly regarding land acquisition, resettlement, and livelihood
       changes.
(e) Expert Judgment and Adjustment
    Expert knowledge and multidisciplinary experience were applied throughout the ESIA
process, particularly in:
   •   Predicting indirect, cumulative, and transboundary impacts;
   •   Assessing feasibility of mitigation measures;
   •   Reviewing engineering designs for environmental and social compliance;
   •   Interpreting analytical data and correlating field findings with socio-economic
       conditions.
    This approach ensured scientific validity and context-specific adaptation to Hai Phong’s
environmental, social, and geographical conditions.
(f) Impact Interaction Matrix
     A Leopold-style impact matrix was developed to systematically identify cause–effect
relationships between project activities (e.g., dredging, construction, operation of WWTP) and




                                                                                            25
                                                                                           Official Use Only
environmental and social receptors (e.g., air, water, soil, flora, fauna, communities).
Each interaction was evaluated for:
   •   Type of impact (positive or negative);
   •   Magnitude and spatial extent;
   •   Duration (short-, medium-, or long-term);
   •   Reversibility;
   •   Probability and significance.
    This matrix formed the core analytical tool for subsequent mitigation planning.
(g) Geographic Information System (GIS) Mapping
    GIS tools (ArcGIS 10.8, MapInfo, and AutoCAD Civil 3D) were used to integrate spatial
data (from KMZ maps and FS design drawings) and produce:
   •   Base maps of project components and sensitive receptors;
   •   Locations of sampling sites for air, water, and soil;
   •   Land use and land cover maps;
   •   Floodplain and topography maps;
   •   Maps of potential impact zones (construction and operation).
    GIS analysis supported spatial correlation between project footprints and environmental
sensitivity, facilitating zoning of impacts and mitigation planning.
(h) Comparative Analysis
    Monitoring results and survey data were compared with:
   •   National technical regulations (QCVNs) for air, water, soil, noise, and vibration;
   •   WHO Environmental Health Criteria and IFC EHS Guidelines;
   •   Baseline conditions from previous EIAs and monitoring reports.
    Comparisons allowed identification of trends, baseline degradation, and key
environmental constraints relevant to project design.
(i) Stakeholder Consultation and Information Disclosure
    Meaningful stakeholder engagement was carried out in accordance with ESS10 –
Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure, including:
   •   Consultation meetings with local authorities, communities, and mass organizations
       (Women’s Union, Fatherland Front);
   •   Focus group discussions with potentially affected households and vulnerable groups;
   •   Disclosure of ESIA-related information in accessible formats and locations (commune
       offices, public boards, and the HPC website);
   •   Integration of feedback into the ESIA and engineering design;
   •   Maintenance of a grievance redress log and continuous community liaison.
   All consultation records and feedback matrices are presented in Annex 9 – Public
Consultation Summary.
(j) Statistical and Data Processing
     All field data, laboratory results, and socio-economic statistics were systematically
processed using MS Excel, SPSS, and GIS-based data models. Data were presented through
tables, graphs, and spatial overlays to:


                                                                                            26
                                                                                            Official Use Only
      •   Characterize baseline conditions and environmental trends;
      •   Quantify changes relative to thresholds or QCVNs;
      •   Support impact prediction and mitigation planning;
      •   Provide a transparent evidence base for monitoring and management.
(k) Quality Assurance and Validation
     The ESIA process incorporated internal peer review, external expert validation, and
iterative consultation with the Project Owner, design consultants, and World Bank safeguard
specialists to ensure methodological rigor, traceability, and consistency with both national and
World Bank requirements.
