                                                                                             27
                                                                                            Official Use Only
                                             PROJECT DESCRIPTION
1.1.      PROJECT INFORMATION
     The Project will support the development of Hai Phong’s key coastal economic corridor
in the medium and long term, strengthening regional connectivity to the Northeastern provinces
and enhancing the utilization of existing seaport and airport infrastructure. It will also open
opportunities for high-value, climate-resilient urban development and create conditions for
industrial growth and land development along the new transport corridor. The completion of
Ring Road No. 3 will form a major external transport axis linking National Highways 10 and
18, reducing travel time between industrial centers of the Northeastern Region and Hai Phong’s
seaports, thereby facilitating investment attraction and regional industrial development. The
Project will simultaneously create the foundation for improved intra-urban transport
connectivity, strengthen the city’s drainage and tidal-flood control capacity, and enable the
revitalization and multifunctional use of the Tam Bac River waterfront. This will support urban
regeneration, expand public spaces, and enhance cultural, historical, and economic values in
the city center. Furthermore, the investment in wastewater treatment systems and the
rehabilitation of the Re River will safeguard the city’s freshwater security, improve
environmental sanitation, and create green corridors and landscapes that reinforce urban
infrastructure connectivity. Overall, the Project aims to develop an integrated system for flood
protection, drainage, wastewater treatment and transport resilience, thereby enhancing Hai
Phong’s adaptive capacity and improving environmental quality for approximately 1.5 million
residents.

1.2.      PROJECT OBJECTIVES AND COMPONENTS
          Project Objectives
     The Project Development Objective (PDO) is to enhance climate resilience, environmental
sustainability, and urban livability in Hai Phong City through integrated flood-risk
management, wastewater collection and treatment, and resilient transport infrastructure.
       Specific objectives are to:
   •      Reduce urban flooding and tidal inundation through rehabilitation of Re and Tam Bac
          Rivers, construction of sluice gates and pumping stations, and improvement of the city’s
          drainage network;
   •      Improve water-environment quality by expanding wastewater collection and treatment,
          thereby reducing pollutant loads discharged to surface waters;
   •      Strengthen urban connectivity and transport resilience by upgrading key corridors such
          as Ring Road No. 3 and associated bridges;
   •      Promote institutional capacity building for integrated flood-risk and environmental
          management consistent with the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework
          (ESF).




                                                                                               28
                                                                                              Official Use Only
       Project Components
    The HPRDP consists of four inter-related components combining physical investments
and institutional support:
Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure
Development:
    This component implements a key regional corridor connecting the northern urban area of
Hai Phong (former Thuy Nguyen District) with the Hai Phong city center and the Hanoi – Hai
Phong Expressway. It will promote socio-economic integration and support safe urban
expansion toward the north bank of Cam River. Main works include:
   •   Construction of Ring Road No. 3: total length ≈ 18.68 km (from Km0 at the intersection
       with Hanoi – Hai Phong Expressway to Km18 + 680 at National Highway 10). The
       cross-section width is 68 m (planned), implemented in Phase 1 with 27 m carriageway.
       Design speed 80 km/h.
   •   Bridges and interchanges: Construction of Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge
       crossing Cam and Ruot Lon Rivers (total length ≈ 2.67 km), category I and special-
       grade bridges with reinforced concrete structure.
   •   Drainage and lighting system, green belts and safety corridors synchronized with the
       road.
    This component will significantly improve regional connectivity and facilitate resilient
urban development in Thuy Nguyen.
Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement
    This component aims to restore and protect the Re River as a strategic urban waterway by
improving its environmental condition and enhancing wastewater collection and treatment
capacity for the western urban area. Key works include:
   •   Re River Embankment: New Construction and Rehabilitation (Approx. 9.67 km)
       Section 1: From National Highway 10 to Re Bridge. Construction of embankments
       using Neoweb retaining walls in combination with Neoweb grassed slopes, with a total
       length of approximately 6.06 km across both riverbanks. Transition embankments will
       be constructed between the designed and existing embankments, as well as between
       adjoining newly designed embankment sections.
       Section 2: From Re Bridge to Cai Tat Bridge. Most existing embankments along this
       section will be retained, with rehabilitation undertaken on selected deteriorated
       portions. Approximately 1.32 km of existing damaged embankments will be
       rehabilitated. In addition, about 0.06 km of new stone revetment will be constructed as
       short connecting sections to link existing embankments where no embankment
       currently exists. Furthermore, new construction and rehabilitation of embankments
       using reinforced concrete frames combined with Neoweb grassed slopes will be
       implemented along approximately 2.23 km on both riverbanks, within which a 690 m
       vertical embankment section will be constructed along the south bank from Re Bridge
       (Phan Dang Luu Road) to address dense riverside housing and facilitate wastewater
       collection while minimizing river channel narrowing. The total embankment length for
       this section is therefore approximately 3.61 km, comprising 1.32 km of rehabilitated



                                                                                           29
                                                                                          Official Use Only
       existing embankments, 0.06 km of newly constructed stone revetment connections, and
       2.23 km of new and rehabilitated Neoweb embankments.
   •   Management Road Along Re River: 13.74 km
       Section 1: From National Highway 10 to Re Bridge. Construction of management roads
       along both riverbanks with a 11.0 m right-of-way (ROW), except where existing
       residential houses are located immediately adjacent to the riverbank; in such locations,
       the ROW will be reduced to 3.5 m. The standard 11.0 m cross-section consists of a 3.5
       m carriageway, a 2.0 m riverside sidewalk, a 2.0 m landside sidewalk, and an earth
       talus, the width of which depends on the fill height relative to surrounding agricultural
       land. The segment from National Highway 10 to Re 2 Bridge (Nguyen Truong To
       Street) contains no residential areas and does not generate wastewater; therefore,
       management roads are not proposed for this section. The total management road length
       in Section 1 is 5.65 km.
       Section 2: From Re Bridge to Cai Tat Bridge. Construction of management roads along
       both riverbanks with a 11.0 m ROW, reduced to 3.5 m in areas where existing
       residential houses are situated adjacent to the riverbank. The standard 11.0 m cross-
       section includes a 3.5 m roadway, a 2.0 m riverside sidewalk, a 2.0 m landside sidewalk,
       and an earth talus. Stone retaining walls will be built to transition from the lower
       elevation of the existing embankments to the higher designed road elevation, including
       locations where the ROW intersects existing ponds. The total management road length
       in Section 2 is 8.09 km.
   •   Drainage and wastewater collection network: Construction of interceptor sewers along
       both banks of Re River with 12 pumping stations, pressure pipelines crossing the Re
       River and An Kim Hai Canal, and the construction of approximately 8.42 km of level-
       3 sewers to collect household wastewater along both banks of the Re River and convey
       it to the An Duong 1 WWTP.
   •   Pumping stations: three stations (PS-01 to PS-03) with individual capacities of 5,000–
       15,000 m³/day each, transmitting wastewater to the central treatment plant.
   •   Construction of An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP): average treatment
       capacity of 8,900 m³/day and a maximum capacity of 10,000 m³/day. The plant is
       located in An Hai ward, occupying 2.99 ha; treatment technology of ASBR meeting
       QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT, Column A before discharge to Lach Tray River (discharge
       coordinates in the VN-2000 coordinate system: X = 2,304,913.8560; Y =
       593,604.8093).
    The component will directly benefit about 215,000 residents in An Duong and adjacent
urban wards by reducing pollution and improving the urban water environment.
Component 3: City Center Flood Reduction
     This component addresses flood risks in the central urban core (former Hong Bang, Ngo
Quyen, Le Chan, Hai An districts) through structural measures on the Tam Bac and Lach Tray
river systems. Main works include:
   •   Construction of one regulating sluice at Tam Bac Bridge connecting Tam Bac River
       and Thuong Ly River, and one regulating sluice at Mui Thuy Doi at the outlet of Tam
       Bac River to Cam River, combined with a 12 m³/s stormwater pumping station at this
       location.



                                                                                             30
                                                                                            Official Use Only
   •   Construction of a wastewater collection system including wastewater collection
       pipelines, interception chambers, and pumping stations along both sides of the Tam Bac
       River, conveying flows to Vinh Niem WWTP:
           o   2,721 m of HDPE wastewater collection pipelines D300–D500;
           o   5 new-construction wastewater pumping stations and the rehabilitation of Lan
               Be Pumping Station;
           o   16 wastewater interception chambers;
           o   590 m of tertiary network (HDPE D160).
   •   Construction of a reinforced-concrete box culvert (B×H = 2.5 × 2.0 m; length = 220 m)
       connecting the existing Tam Bac Lake to the Tam Bac River, converting the lake into
       a regulating reservoir with a surface area of approximately 10.2 ha.
   •   Construction of stormwater drainage culverts (RC box culverts), B×H = 1.0×1.0 m to
       3.0×2.0 m, with a total length of 4.252 km.
    These measures will reduce flooding duration and extent in the city core by 40–60 %,
protect approximately 3,500 ha of urban area, and improve water quality and amenities along
Tam Bac River.
Component 4: Systems and Capacity Development to Improve Urban Resilience
     This component focuses on enhancing institutional capacity, data management and smart
urban governance to ensure effective implementation and operation of resilient infrastructure.
It includes:
   •   Development of an Integrated Flood and Drainage Management Information System
       (GIS & SCADA platform for hydraulic operations and early warning).
   •   Establishment of a Climate Resilience and Urban Management Center under Hai Phong
       City People’s Committee.
   •   Capacity-building programs for urban planning, environmental management and
       disaster risk reduction.




                                                                                           31
                                                                                          Official Use Only
                    Figure 1.1. General layout of project components.

       Associated Projects
     In addition to the main investment components of the Hai Phong Resilience Development
Project (HPRDP), the Hai Phong City People’s Committee is implementing several associated
and supporting public investment projects to facilitate resettlement and improve living
conditions for affected households. Among these, the two resettlement areas are independently
financed public investment projects of the City, funded by the local budget to serve multiple
investment projects across Hai Phong, including HPRDP, and do not use World Bank financing
nor form part of the HPRDP investment scope. According to the Resettlement Plan, HPRDP
requires resettlement for 181 households out of a total of 1,337 resettlement plots provided by
the two resettlement projects; therefore, these resettlement areas are not primarily developed
for HPRDP, but are largely intended to meet resettlement needs for other investment projects
within the city.
(a) Bach Dang Resettlement Area
Location and General Information:
    The Bach Dang Resettlement Area (RA) is located in Bach Dang Ward, approximately 2.8
km northwest of the Tam Bac River subproject area and within the central administrative
boundary of Hai Phong City. The resettlement site covers a total area of about 19.98 ha. It is
designated as a key resettlement site for households affected by the project construction of the
Re River embankment and Ring Road No.3 construction.


                                                                                             32
                                                                                            Official Use Only
Purpose and Scope:
    The project aims to provide fully serviced residential plots and multi-storey apartment
buildings for households who are required to relocate permanently due to HPRDP construction.
The development includes:
   •   Site grading: finished platform level ~+2.65 m.
   •   Roads: 28 routes, total ~5.88 km; ROW 13–20 m; A1 asphalt pavement.
   •   Utilities (water, firefighting, power, lighting, telecom): fully connected to city
       networks.
   •   Stormwater drainage: RC pipes D600–D2000, box culverts 2×(2×3) m, gravity
       discharge to Phan Dat canal.
   •   Wastewater system: D200–D300 sewers, 3 lifting stations; centralized AO–MBBR
       WWTP 950 m³/day.
Current Status (as of December 2025)
   •   The two resettlement projects are expected to be implemented during the 2025–2028
       period. Accordingly, the preparation and approval of the investment policy are
       scheduled for Q1–Q3/2025, followed by the preparation of construction
       implementation documents in Q4/2025. According to the approved documents, the
       construction is planned to commence in Q1/2026, with an estimated construction period
       of approximately 24 months (completed by the end of 2027). However, according to
       the information confirmed at the meeting with the Project Owner in November 2025,
       the project is expected to be completed and put into operation before 30 December
       2026, readily to be used for relocation of the first group of affected households from
       the Re River and Ring Road No. 3 corridors.
Environmental and Social Linkage to HPRDP
    The Bach Dang RA serves as the primary resettlement site for 280 households affected by
land acquisition under Components 1 and 2 of the HPRDP. Although implemented under a
separate city budget, the site’s environmental and social performance (e.g., drainage,
wastewater, and solid waste management) directly influences the sustainability of the overall
project. The resettlement infrastructure complies with the requirements of the Resettlement
Plan (RP) prepared in accordance with ESS5 – Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use
and Involuntary Resettlement.
(b) Nam Trieu Resettlement Area
Location and General Information
    The Nam Trieu Resettlement Area (RA) is situated in Nam Trieu Ward, approximately 5
km southwest of the An Duong 1 WWTP site and adjacent to the planned southern section of
Ring Road No. 3. It is designated as a key resettlement site for households affected by the
construction of the Re River embankment and Ring Road No.3 construction.
Purpose and Scope
   •   The planned resettlement area in Nam Trieu Ward has a total area of 13.66 ha and is
       expected to accommodate approximately 663 households, including households from




                                                                                          33
                                                                                         Official Use Only
       the Hai Phong Climate-Resilient Urban Development Project and households from
       other city investment projects.
Key construction items include:
   •   Site grading: elevation +2.30 m (platform ≥ +2.50 m).
   •   Roads: 25 routes, total ~4.55 km, width 7–9 m, A1 pavement.
   •   Utilities (water, firefighting, power, lighting, telecom): fully connected to city
       networks.
   •   Stormwater drainage: RC pipes D600–D1500, gravity flow.
   •   Wastewater system: D200–D300 sewers, AO–MBBR WWTP 300 & 450 m³/day.
Current Status (as of December 2025)
   •   The two resettlement projects are expected to be implemented during the 2025–2028
       period. Accordingly, the preparation and approval of the investment policy are
       scheduled for Q1–Q3/2025, followed by the preparation of construction
       implementation documents in Q4/2025. According to the approved documents, the
       construction is planned to commence in Q1/2026, with an estimated construction period
       of approximately 24 months (completed by the end of 2027). However, according to
       the information confirmed at the meeting with the Project Owner in November 2025,
       the project is expected to be completed and put into operation before 30 December
       2026, readily to be used for relocation of the first group of affected households from
       the Re River and Ring Road No. 3 corridors.
Environmental and Social Linkage to HPRDP
     In conclusion, the two resettlement areas are independently financed public investment
projects of Hai Phong City, developed to serve the resettlement needs of multiple infrastructure
and urban development projects rather than HPRDP alone. While they are not part of the
HPRDP investment scope and do not use World Bank financing, they play an important
environmental and social linkage role by providing adequate, planned resettlement land for 207
households affected by HPRDP, thereby enabling timely land acquisition and project
implementation. From an environmental and social perspective, the readiness, location, and
capacity of these resettlement areas are critical to ensuring compliance with ESS5 on Land
Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement, supporting livelihood
restoration, improving living conditions of displaced households, and minimizing cumulative
social risks associated with displacement. As such, although implemented separately, the two
resettlement projects constitute an essential enabling factor for the successful and socially
sustainable implementation of HPRDP.

1.3.   PROJECT LOCATION
    Hai Phong City is a centrally governed Class-I urban area, the third-largest city in Viet
Nam and the second-largest economic, political, and cultural center in the Northern Delta
region. It is located approximately 106 km east–southeast of Ha Noi along National Highway
5. After the administrative merger with Hai Duong Province, Hai Phong has a natural area of
3,194.72 km² and a population of 4,664,124. The city currently comprises 114 commune-level
administrative units, including 45 wards, 67 communes and 2 special zones.


                                                                                             34
                                                                                            Official Use Only
     Hai Phong has long been known as the largest seaport in Northern Viet Nam and an
important multimodal transport hub with extensive maritime, road, rail, and domestic and
international air transport networks. It serves as the primary gateway to the sea for Ha Noi and
other northern provinces, and is a key transportation node within the Northern Key Economic
Region, located along the “Two Corridors, One Belt” Viet Nam–China economic cooperation
framework.
    Accordingly, in the socio-economic development strategy for the Red River Delta, Hai
Phong is defined as a major growth pole of the northern economic corridor (Ha Noi – Hai
Phong – Quang Ninh), a comprehensive economic–scientific–technological center of the
Northern Coastal Region, and one of the core development centers of both the Northern Key
Economic Region and the country as a whole.
Project Area and Boundaries:
    The Hai Phong Climate-Resilient Urban Development Project is implemented across the
wards of Dong Hai, Nam Trieu, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Thuy Nguyen, Luu Kiem, Hong Bang,
An Duong, An Hai, Ngo Quyen, Gia Vien, Le Chan and An Bien. The project covers a total
land area of 197.73 ha, including approximately 49.88 ha of rice land and 0.76 ha of protection
forest plantation (according to the approved forest land conversion dossier).
       Main Components and Spatial Distribution:
   1. Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure Development:
      Development of a 18.68-km road alignment with associated bridges and drainage
      structures, traversing Dong Hai, Nam Trieu, Thuy Nguyen, Hoa Binh, Bach Dang and
      Luu Kiem wards, contributing to enhanced regional connectivity and urban mobility.
   2. Re River Environmental Improvement:
   - Rehabilitation of embankments and installation of a wastewater collection system along
      the Re River through the wards of An Duong, An Bien and Hong Bang.
   - An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP): Located in An Hai Ward,
      occupying approximately 2.99 ha, the WWTP is designed with an average treatment
      capacity of 8,900 m³/day and a maximum capacity of 10,000 m³/day, discharging
      treated effluent that meets QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT – Column A standards into the
      Lach Tray River.
   3. City Center Flood Reduction: Construction of two flow-regulating culverts on the Tam
      Bac River. Together with a wastewater collection system conveying flows to the Vinh
      Niem WWTP and new stormwater drainage pipelines across the wards of Ngo Quyen,
      Gia Vien and Le Chan.

1.4.     SCOPE OF WORKS UNDER THE PROJECT
     The scope of the ESIA is focused on components 1, 2 and 3 with sub-components
involving with construction activities (component 4 is non-construction component that only
for technical assistance and capacity building). The detailed descriptions of sub-components
with construction activities is presented in the following table.

                              treatment consists of septage

                              environmental service providers.

1.5.    LIST OF CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT AND MACHINERY
     A range of construction machinery and equipment will be deployed for implementation of
the HPRDP, depending on the nature of each sub-component (river works, roads, bridges,
pumping stations, WWTP, sewers and regulation lakes), contractor capacity and weather
conditions. The table below provides a list of key equipment expected to be used for major
work items. The actual quantities may vary and will be finalized during detailed design and
construction planning; however, the average mobilization presented below represents the
anticipated peak period of construction activities for each major subcomponent.
             Table 1.2. List of construction machinery used for the Project

     The wastewater treatment plant is designed with a comprehensive system of process,
mechanical, electrical, automation, and laboratory equipment. The detailed configuration,
types, units, and quantities of the main equipment are summarized in the following table,
covering all treatment stages from influent pumping and preliminary treatment to biological
treatment, disinfection, sludge handling, odor control, and monitoring systems, ensuring stable
operation and compliance with applicable environmental standards.

   9     Analytical balance                        Unit                               1
  10     Vacuum filtration unit                    Unit                               1
  11     Laboratory glassware and tools            Lot                                1
  12     Laboratory chemicals                      Lot                                1

    The Project is expected to generate 269,324 m³ of stripped topsoil, including (i) 124,782 m³ of paddy field topsoil, (ii) 95,582 m³ of non-saline
organic topsoil from other agriculture areas, and (iii) 48,960 m³ of saline organic topsoil originating from aquaculture areas. Of the topsoil from



                                                                                                                                                    63
                                                                                                                                                   Official Use Only
other agriculture areas volume, 81,456 m³ will be reused for embankment formation and landscaping works within the Project area. In addition,
construction activities will generate approximately 131,560 m³ of excavated soil and 10,661 m³ of demolition waste, while the total embankment
fill demand is estimated at 363,217 m³. As a result of the imbalance between excavation and fill requirements, the Project will produce an estimated
194,646 m³ of surplus soil requiring off-site management.
   − Demolition waste: Approximately 10,661 m³, generated from dismantling works and site clearance. The waste will be temporarily
     stockpiled within the project area or within the 68-m right-of-way (ROW) of the road, arranged in cells of approximately 100 m² each, with
     an estimated temporary storage duration of 1–2 weeks, and then transported and disposed of at the Construction Waste Treatment Plant –
     Plant No. 2, managed by Minh Tan Environmental Recycling and Treatment Joint Stock Company.
   − Topsoil stripped from paddy fields: Approximately 124,782 m³, generated from agricultural land. A specific management and utilization
     plan for this type of soil will be prepared by the Project Owner and submitted to the competent authority for approval in a subsequent
     project stage.
   − Saline organic soil: Approximately 48,960 m³, generated from shrimp pond areas in Nam Trieu Ward along the section from Km 6+500
     to Km 8+900. The material will be temporarily stockpiled at the construction site for 1–2 weeks to allow moisture reduction, and then
     transported for disposal at the disposal site managed by Toan Thang Trading and Services Co., Ltd., Thuy Nguyen Ward, Hai Phong City.
   − Surplus non-saline organic soil: Approximately 14,086 m³, representing the remaining non-saline organic soil after reuse for embankment
     slopes and landscaping works. This material will be temporarily stockpiled within the project area, and subsequently transported and
     disposed of at the Construction Waste Treatment Plant – Plant No. 2, managed by Minh Tan Environmental Recycling and Treatment Joint
     Stock Company, located in fomer An Hong Commune, An Duong District (now Hong An Ward), Hai Phong City, in accordance with
     approvals from the competent authorities.
   − Other excavated soil (common excavated soil): Approximately 131,160 m³, generated from earthworks, foundation excavation, and
     construction of infrastructure components. The soil will be temporarily stockpiled at undeveloped areas within the project boundary or
     within the ROW, and then transported and disposed of at the Construction Waste Treatment Plant – Plant No. 2, managed by Minh Tan
     Environmental Recycling and Treatment Joint Stock Company.

   1.6.1.1.    Supply sources
     Raw materials are procured and delivered directly to the construction site; therefore,
material transportation activities are outside the scope of the ESIA. Fill materials are sourced
from multiple approved materials (sand, soil, gravel, coal ash, and other suitable materials
permitted for filling) and purchased from suppliers within the city and neighboring provinces
for delivery to the site. Concrete is primarily ready-mixed concrete delivered by contractors;
however, for simple works such as sidewalk paving, manholes, covers, and curbs, on-site
mixed concrete may be used. Other construction materials are supplied by local dealers within
the city. Typical potential suppliers are summarized in the following table.

     Contractors will, after bidding, select specific suppliers for construction materials. The
Project Management Unit (PMU) and Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) will verify
the legal status, environmental and social compliance, and capacity of selected suppliers
(including exploitation license, environmental permits, labor contracts, OHS management)
before approval.
    All materials will be tested for quality in accordance with Vietnamese standards. If
materials do not meet the specified technical requirements, contractors will be required to
change suppliers. Material supply will be arranged in line with construction progress and will
take into account losses during transportation and unloading.
   1.6.1.2.    Disposal site
     Non-hazardous solid waste generated during project construction, after being segregated
and temporarily stockpiled within the construction sites, will be transported to approved waste
treatment and disposal facilities within Hai Phong City, in accordance with the characteristics
of each waste type and the receiving capacity of each facility.
    The primary disposal site is the Construction Waste Treatment Plant – Plant No. 2 managed
by Minh Tan Environmental Recycling and Treatment Joint Stock Company, located in Hong
An Ward, Hai Phong City. This area is currently the company’s plant construction site and will
be used for backfilling purposes, with a total area of approximately 62,188 m². The facility is
designated to receive construction and demolition waste, common excavated soil, and surplus
non-saline organic soil generated from project construction activities.
    In terms of spatial location, the Minh Tan disposal site is approximately 5 km from
Component 2 of the Project, accessed via National Highway 5 → National Highway 10, and
approximately 10 km from Component 1, accessed via Provincial Road 359 → National
Highway 10. The acceptance and treatment of construction waste generated by the Hai Phong
Climate-Resilient Urban Development Project at this facility have been approved under Official
Letter No. 1610/25/CV-MT dated 16 October 2025 issued by Minh Tan Environmental
Recycling and Treatment Joint Stock Company, and are consistent with Official Document No.
121/UBND-MT dated 21 January 2025 of the Hai Phong City People’s Committee on solid
waste management within the city.
     In addition, the disposal/treatment site managed by Toan Thang Trading and Services Co.,
Ltd., located in Thuy Nguyen Ward, Hai Phong City, is designated to receive saline organic
soil generated from aquaculture pond areas within the project boundary. The Toan Thang
disposal site is located approximately 7.1 km from the saline aquaculture soil excavation area
in Nam Trieu Ward, accessed mainly via local access roads → Provincial Road 359, providing
convenient transport conditions. The acceptance of this waste type has been agreed by Toan
Thang Trading and Services Co., Ltd. under Official Letter No. 88/CV-TT dated 15 October
2025 regarding disposal arrangements for the Hai Phong Climate-Resilient Urban Development
Project.
     The selection of the above disposal sites, with reasonable transport distances and well-
established road connections, ensures that construction waste generated by the Project is
collected, transported, and treated in compliance with applicable regulations, while minimizing
potential environmental impacts during the construction phase.

                                                                                            68

                                                                                        Official Use Only
                            Figure 1.2. Map of disposal sites location
      1.6.1.3.     Fuel supply
     Fuel demand for operating construction machinery and trucks will be met by existing fuel

consumption during the construction phase is shown in the following table.

     Fuel, oil and lubricants will be supplied by licensed fuel companies whose station networks

      1.6.1.4.      Power supply
    Electricity supply for the Project will be sourced from the national power grid, with supply
conditions varying by component and location.




1
    1 L diesel ≈ 0.85 kg; emissions rounded, based on WHO emission factors

                                                                                             69

                                                                                         Official Use Only
     For Component 1, covering Thuy Nguyen, Dong Hai, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Luu Kiem,
and part of Nam Trieu wards, the electricity network is generally well established in existing
residential areas, along major transport corridors, and within the Vinhomes Royal Island Urban
Area, with high but not complete coverage through existing medium- and low-voltage lines.
Power for construction will be provided via new transformer stations installed for project needs,
supplemented by temporary power solutions where the existing grid does not reach construction
sites; upon completion, the newly built electrical infrastructure will be used for road lighting
and project facilities during operation.
     For Component 2, mainly along Provincial Road 208, electricity supply has been developed
in parallel with the road corridor and adjacent residential areas, with existing medium- and low-
voltage lines and transformer stations providing high coverage along most sections.
    For Component 3, construction takes place along inner-city roads in the central urban area,
where the electricity supply system is fully developed, with dense and continuous distribution
networks and transformer stations enabling direct access to grid electricity for both construction
and temporary power demands.
   1.6.1.5.     Water supply
    Water for construction will mainly be abstracted from Re River, Tam Bac River, Lach Tray
River and existing urban water supply networks. Where construction areas are far from these
sources, water tank trucks will be used to transport water to the sites.
    For domestic water use at worker camps and site offices, depending on the specific
location, water will be supplied either from the city’s piped water system (Hai Phong Water
Supply Company) or from permitted groundwater wells. Where public water networks are not
available, groundwater will be treated through simple on-site filtration and disinfection systems
and/or clean water tanks to ensure that domestic water meets hygiene standards.

       Demand on workers
     The maximum demand for construction workers for the entire Project is estimated at about
605 workers on site at peak periods. To reduce costs, create local employment and limit
potential social impacts associated with labor influx, the Project will prioritize recruitment of
local laborers. It is expected that local workers will account for approximately 80% of the
workforce, and only about 20% of workers will reside continuously in worker camps. The
estimated number of workers by main construction items is presented in the following Table.
              Table 1.8. Expected number of workers at the construction sites

     The proportion of unskilled workers is expected to be about 60–70% of the total workforce
at each site. No worker under 18 years old will be employed for the Project. Most workers will
be men aged 18–35, with a small number of skilled workers and supervisors aged 35–50. At
each construction site, it is anticipated that about 75% of workers will be male and 25% female,
depending on the specific type of work. Worker camps (where required) will be located and
managed by contractors in accordance with national regulations and the Project’s Labor
Management Procedures and ESMP.

1.7.   CONSTRUCTION METHODS
    The Hai Phong Resilience Development Project (HPRDP) is a multi-sector project with
diverse work items, including river rehabilitation, drainage and sewer systems, urban roads and
bridges, pumping stations, wastewater treatment facilities and regulation lakes. Therefore, the
construction plan and technologies will be selected for each type of work item, based on
technical specifications, construction conditions at site and construction requirements. Design
and construction activities must comply with Vietnamese construction standards, regulations
and practices and take into account applicable international standards. The Project’s
construction methods, adapted from the Project Feasibility Study and technical design
documents, are summarized below.

       Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure
       Development
a. Site Clearance and Preparation
   •   Land acquisition and site clearance will be implemented along the 18.68 km alignment.
   •   Existing vegetation, structures, and utilities will be removed after boundary
       demarcation.
   •   Topsoil (20–30 cm) will be stripped and stored for future landscaping.
   •   Clearing performed by excavators and bulldozers; stripped soil is temporarily stored
       within the ROW and later transported to disposal sites via service roads.
b. Construction Site Layout
   •   Construction yards are arranged at major access points: Dong Hai side of Ngo Quyen
       Bridge, Vu Yen Island, and the Ruot Lon Bridge area.
   •   Each yard includes site offices, workers’ camps, power supply, batching plants, precast
       yards, material storage, and internal access roads.
   •   Cross-access: using existing provincial, district, and commune roads.
   •   Longitudinal access: grading and constructing service roads within the ROW for vehicle
       movement along the alignment.
c. Major Construction Fronts
   •   Due to the project’s scale and linear characteristics, multiple work fronts will be
       deployed:
                                                                                        71

                                                                                         Official Use Only
   -   Ngo Quyen cable-stayed main bridge
   -   Ngo Quyen approach bridges (Dong Hai and Vu Yen sides)
   -   Ruot Lon Bridge
   -   Overpass of the Hanoi – Hai Phong Expressway
   -   Vu Yen Island road section
   -   Road section from Ruot Lon Bridge to DT359
   -   Road section from DT359 to the end of the alignment
   •   Construction methods prioritize modern equipment, BIM applications, and maximum
       use of precast components (steel arch segments, Super-T girders, bearings, retaining
       structures).
d. Access to Construction Areas
   •   Expressway overpass: via Bui Vien Road and Dang Kinh Road.
   •   Ngo Quyen Bridge:
   -   Dong Hai side: access directly from National Highway 5 within the 68 m ROW.
   -   Vu Yen side: access from May Chai Bridge, Vu Yen 1 Bridge, and the internal road
       network of Vu Yen Island.
   •   Ruot Lon Bridge:
   -   Vu Yen side: via May Chai Bridge, Vu Yen 1 Bridge, and internal island roads.
   -   Thuy Nguyen side: via longitudinal service roads graded within the ROW (e.g., entering
       from Tran Hung Dao Road to Km8, then following the service road to work sites).
e. Construction of Ngo Quyen Cable-Stayed Main Bridge
   •   Work platforms on both banks with full support facilities.
   •   Anchor piers on land: excavation, steel sheet piles, tremie concrete, bored piles, footing
       and pier construction.
   •   Main piers in water: guide frame installation, bored piling, cofferdam installation,
       dewatering, footing and pier casting.
   •   Superstructure:
   -   Install fixed falsework for segment K0
   -   Construct pylons and crossbeams
   -   Erect segment K0
   -   Perform balanced cantilever erection with cable installation
   -   Close mid-span, complete deck slab, parapets, lighting, joints, and asphalt surfacing.




                                                                                              72

                                                                                          Official Use Only
          Figure 1.3. Illustration of the construction steps for bridge piers in water
f. Construction of Ruot Lon Steel Arch Bridge
   •   Complete piers T3 and T4; install and load-test arch falsework; erect arch segments;
       install deck girders and hangers; install deck slab; final works including parapets,
       lighting, expansion joints, and wearing course.
g. Construction of Approach Bridges
   •   Construction within the cleared ROW.
   •   Install bored piles and sheet piles, cast pile caps, pier columns, and crossbeams.
   •   Super-T girders cast on-site or in precast yards, lifted by cranes or gantries; followed by
       deck slab, parapets, lighting, and joints.
h. Road Works
   •   Equipment: rollers, bulldozers, excavators, cranes, mixers, compactors, total station,
       level, and laboratory testing devices.
   •   Ordinary embankment: vegetation removal, unsuitable soil excavation, layer-by-layer
       filling (K ≥ 0.90), K95 and K98 layers (30 cm each).
   •   Ground improvement: geotextile placement, cement–soil piles, sand drains, sand
       blankets, settlement monitoring, surcharge removal if necessary.
   •   Service road construction: graded along the ROW to ensure continuous access for
       construction machinery.
i. Drainage Works (Stormwater and Culverts)
   •   Set out alignment; excavate trenches; stabilize trench walls using sheet piles near
       residential zones.
   •   Install bedding, pipes, manholes; backfill compacted to K ≥ 0.90.
   •   Cast-in-place RC box culverts include bedding preparation, rebar and formwork
       installation, concrete casting, inlet/outlet structures, and backfilling.
j. Pavement Works
   •   New pavement: crushed stone base layers (lower and upper), prime coat (1.0 kg/m²),
       asphalt concrete base, tack coat (0.5 kg/m²), and asphalt wearing course.
   •   Strengthening of existing pavement: cleaning, leveling, crushed stone base, asphalt
       layers.
k. Finishing Works
   •   Install reflective traffic signs with reinforced foundations.
   •   Paint road markings and medians.
   •   Complete lighting poles, parapets, expansion joints, and ancillary works.
l. Safety, Labor Protection, and Environmental Management

                                                                                               73

                                                                                           Official Use Only
   •   Full compliance with safety regulations for workers, equipment, and traffic.
   •   Install warning signs and barriers; implement storm and flood preparedness plans.
   •   Maintain on-site medical services and submit construction methods for safety approval.
   •   Control dust, wastewater, and solid waste; ensure fire prevention and public safety
       throughout construction.

       Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement
a. Site Clearance and Preparation
   •   Land acquisition and clearance will be implemented along approximately 9.5 km of both
       banks of the Re River.
   •   After boundary demarcation, existing structures, utilities, and vegetation will be
       removed; topsoil (20–30 cm) will be stripped and stored for reuse.
   •   Clearing will be carried out using excavators and bulldozers. Stripped soil will be
       temporarily stockpiled within the ROW and transported to disposal sites through
       designated service roads.
b. Construction Site Layout
   •   Construction yards will be established at suitable locations along the embankment and
       management road alignment, providing space for site offices, worker camps, precast
       yards, batching plants, transformer stations, material storage, and internal access routes.
   •   Temporary internal roads will be created to facilitate access to embankment, drainage,
       and road management works.
c. Access to Construction Sites
   •   Road 208 (TL208) will serve as the primary transport corridor for construction
       materials, precast components, and equipment throughout most of Component 2.
   •   In areas not directly adjacent to TL208, access will rely on existing local roads,
       including inter-village and community roads.
   •   For remote sections, temporary longitudinal service roads will be graded and
       constructed within the ROW to ensure equipment and vehicles can reach embankment
       and management road locations.
   •   Transportation flows will follow the general route:
   •   TL208 → local roads → longitudinal service road → construction site
   •   All transport operations will minimize disturbance to local traffic and limit noise and
       dust emissions.
d. Embankment Construction (Riverbank Protection)
   •   Fiber-Reinforced Concrete Frame + Neoweb Grass System
   -   Excavate foundation; compact subgrade; install geotextile.
   -   Install reinforced concrete frames (precast or cast-in-place).
   -   Install Neoweb cells, anchor per design, fill with granular material, and plant grass.
   •   Stone Masonry Retaining Walls and Rubble Stone Revetments
   -   Excavate foundation; place blinding concrete; construct stone masonry retaining wall.
   -   In densely populated areas, vertical retaining walls will be built to protect houses and
       allow wastewater collection.
   •   Rehabilitation of Existing Revetments
   -   Damaged sections will be dismantled and rebuilt using stone or concrete revetments
       depending on ground conditions and high-water marks.
e. Construction of River Management Roads

                                                                                               74

                                                                                           Official Use Only
   •   Set out road centerline; remove topsoil; construct embankment layers to K ≥ 0.90.
   •   Type 1 road (11 m formation): 3.5 m carriageway, sidewalks on both sides, and earth
       embankment (slope) based on design elevations.
   •   Type 2 road (4 m formation): 3.5 m carriageway without sidewalks.
   •   Construct sub-base and base layers (crushed stone), and surface according to
       management road standards.
f. Stormwater and Wastewater Drainage Works
   •   Excavate trenches; stabilize trench walls with steel sheet piles near residential areas.
   •   Install RC pipes, manholes, and backfill to K ≥ 0.90.
   •   The wastewater interceptor will be installed under the management road, including
       separation chambers and connections to the main collection system.
g. Water Supply, Power, and Street Lighting Installation
   •   Install underground HDPE/uPVC pipelines beneath sidewalks.
   •   Construct foundations for lighting poles; install cables and control cabinets.
   •   Utility manholes will be installed at road bends and at locations with elevation changes.
h. Auxiliary Structures
   •   Construct small box culverts across channels, cross-drainage structures, and upgrades
       to existing irrigation/Drainage structures.
   •   Build access steps, guardrails, and interface works connecting the management road to
       adjacent residential areas.
i. Finishing Works
   •   Apply road markings, install reflective traffic signs, safety barriers, and complete
       landscape works along slopes and embankments.
   •   Flush and clean the entire drainage system before commissioning.
j. An Duong 1 WWTP
   •   Site preparation:
   -   Land clearance, demolition of existing structures, stripping 20–30 cm of topsoil, and
       leveling the site to the design elevation.
   -   Establish temporary access roads, site office, worker camp, power and water supply.
   •   Ground improvement and foundations:
   -   Improve weak soil using replacement fill, geotextiles, sand blankets, or PVDs (as
       required).
   -   Construct foundations for SBR tanks, buildings, and auxiliary structures using RC raft
       foundations or bored piles depending on soil conditions.
   •   Construction of treatment structures:
   -   SBR tanks: excavation and shoring, blinding concrete, waterproofing, reinforcement,
       formwork, and concrete casting for walls and base slabs.
   -   Inlet works, screening chambers, aeration/blower building, sludge thickening and
       dewatering units, disinfection tank, and chemical storage facilities constructed
       according to design.
   •   Outfall channel: RC channel and erosion protection at discharge point.
   •   Mechanical and electrical installation:
   -   Installation of pumps, pipes, valves, electrical cables, MCC panels, control systems, and
       SCADA automation.
   -   Testing of mechanical systems, electrical connections, and instrumentation.
   •   Internal infrastructure:
                                                                                              75

                                                                                         Official Use Only
   -   Internal roads constructed with compacted base and asphalt or concrete surface.
   -   Stormwater drainage, fencing, gates, lighting, and landscaping implemented throughout
       the site.
   •   Testing, commissioning, and trial operation:
   -   Hydrostatic tests of tanks and pipelines; mechanical and electrical commissioning.
   -   30–60 days of trial operation with wastewater loading to verify treatment performance
       and compliance with QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT – Column A.
k. Occupational Safety and Environmental Protection
   •   Install warning signs, barriers, and traffic regulation measures at intersections with
       TL208 and local roads.
   •   Conduct routine dust suppression by water spraying along TL208 and service roads.
   •   Ensure compliance with occupational safety requirements, provide PPE for workers,
       assign safety officers, and maintain onsite first-aid services.
   •   All waste, excavated materials, and construction debris will be collected and transported
       to approved disposal sites; no discharge into the river is allowed.

       Component 3 – City Center Flood Reduction
a. Site Preparation
   •   Conduct surveys, alignment marking, removal of conflicting structures and utilities
       along the wastewater collection routes.
   •   Establish construction offices, storage yards, temporary power supply, water supply,
       and worker camps before starting excavation.
b. Access to Construction Sites
   •   Construction activities will take place along existing urban streets in the city centre,
       including Hoang Dieu – Nguyen Tri Phuong, Dien Bien Phu, Cau Dat, Nguyen Duc
       Canh, Lan Ong, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Hai Ba Trung (from Dang Kim No alley), Tran
       Nguyen Han, Tam Bac, Tam Bac – Hung Vuong, Phan Dinh Phung, The Lu, and Ha
       Ly.
   •   Access for equipment and materials will use these existing streets, with temporary traffic
       control, barricading, lane narrowing, restricted working hours, and road-occupation
       permits coordinated with local authorities to maintain construction progress while
       minimising disruption.
   •   Temporary working zones will be established segment-by-segment to ensure safe access
       within narrow urban corridors.
c. Construction of Wastewater Collection Pipelines
   •   Set out pipeline centreline and excavate using excavators combined with manual work
       in narrow sections.
   •   Stabilise trench walls with steel sheet piles or temporary shoring near buildings and deep
       excavations.
   •   Install bedding layers and place HDPE/RCC pipes (D300–D500); install wastewater
       separation chambers (GT12–GT25) and pumping stations (TB12, TB13, TB14, TB15,
       TB16).
   •   Connect to the existing D500 and D1000 trunk sewers while ensuring continuous
       drainage during construction.
   •   Backfill and compact trenches to the specified density; reinstate pavement surfaces with
       asphalt or concrete in accordance with the original road structure.
d. Construction of Level-3 Household Connections
                                                                                              76

                                                                                          Official Use Only
   •   Install HDPE pipes (D160–D200) connecting household wastewater outlets to the local
       Level-3 network and separation chambers.
   •   Construct household inspection chambers either inside or outside properties depending
       on available space.
   •   Total Level-3 network length is approximately 590 m, ensuring synchronized operation
       with the main interceptor network.
e. Construction of Wastewater Pumping Stations
   •   Excavate and shore pumping station pits; cast blinding concrete and construct reinforced
       concrete chambers (machine room, valve chamber).
   •   Install submersible pumps, control panels, valves, air vents, and electrical systems.
   •   Connect pumping stations to pressure pipelines and the main wastewater trunk sewer.
f. Drainage Works and Surface Restoration
   •   Replace or install new stormwater pipes where conflicts occur; construct new manholes
       and catchpits along the upgraded streets.
   •   Restore sidewalks, kerbs, paving blocks, manhole covers, lighting poles, and other
       affected urban infrastructure.
g. Regulating Sluice Gates (Tam Bac River)
   •   Cofferdam and Dewatering
   -   Install steel sheet pile cofferdams (Larsen IV) to create dry working zones.
   -   Construct tremie concrete bottom seals and pump out water to ensure stable conditions
       for foundation works.
   -   Monitor groundwater levels, settlement, and vibration to protect surrounding structures.
   •   Foundation and Substructure Construction
   -   Excavate foundation pits within the cofferdam and prepare the subgrade.
   -   Install blinding concrete, reinforcement, and formwork for the foundation slab.
   -   Cast reinforced concrete for the gate chamber, pier walls, culvert frames, and hydraulic
       equipment rooms.
   •   Superstructure and Sluice Gate Installation
   -   Install embedded steel plates, guide rails, anchor systems, and lifting beams.
   -   Hoist and install the vertical-lift flat gates (20 m width per gate).
   -   Install sealing systems, rollers, lifting components, and structural steel elements.
   •   Hydraulic, Electrical, and Control Systems
   -   Install hydraulic cylinders, hydraulic power units (HPUs), valves, and pressure lines.
   -   Install electrical panels, control cabinets, wiring, sensors, and safety devices.
   -   Test hydraulic systems, gate movement, emergency stop, and manual override.
   •   Pumping Station (Mui Thuy Doi – 12 m³/s)
   -   Construct the wet well, foundation, and structural components.
   -   Install pumps (2 × 6 m³/s), suction structures, discharge pipelines, and control
       equipment.
   -   Perform mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic testing before commissioning.
   •   Backfilling, Bank Protection, and Restoration
   -   Backfill compacted in layers around the sluice and pump station.
   -   Install scour protection using riprap, concrete blocks, or revetment mats.
   -   Restore pavements, sidewalks, utilities, and landscaping disturbed during construction.
   •   Testing, Commissioning, and Trial Operation
   -   Conduct hydrostatic testing of chambers, pipelines, and gate seals.
   -   Test full opening–closing cycles under different hydraulic conditions.
                                                                                            77

                                                                                        Official Use Only
   -   Commission pumping operations and integrate gate–pump joint operation.
   -   Final handover after verifying full compliance with design and operational safety
       requirements.
h. Safety, Environmental Protection, and Community Access
   •   Install barricades, warning signs, night-time lighting, and provide safe pedestrian access
       around construction zones.
   •   Apply regular dust suppression, manage stockpiles, and collect and transport excavated
       soil to approved disposal sites.
   •   Use compact, low-noise machinery in narrow urban streets to minimise disturbance.
   •   Ensure continuous access for residents, shops, and emergency vehicles throughout
       construction.

       Component 4 – Systems and Capacity Development to Improve Urban
       Resilience
     Activities under this component are mainly non-structural and will be implemented through
consultancy, procurement, and training contracts. They include software installation, hardware
setup for GIS/SCADA systems, data integration, and capacity-building programs for staff of
the PMU, DAE, DOC, and district authorities.

       Operational process of the An Duong 1 WWTP
     The An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) is designed as a centralized facility
with a Phase 1 treatment capacity of 10,000 m³/day, applying an improved Sequencing Batch
Reactor (SBR) activated sludge technology. The plant integrates preliminary treatment,
biological nutrient removal, tertiary disinfection, sludge management, odour control, and online
monitoring into a closed and well-controlled treatment system. The operational process is
structured to ensure stable performance under variable hydraulic and pollutant loading
conditions, compliance with applicable discharge standards, and effective environmental
protection of the receiving water body. The following section outlines the step-by-step
operational process of the WWTP under normal operating conditions.
    Step 1. Wastewater Reception and Flow Control
    Wastewater collected from the gravity sewer network, together with filtrate from sludge
dewatering units and supernatant from sludge thickening tanks, is conveyed to the inlet
reception chamber. At this stage, wastewater flow is evenly distributed to downstream units
through flow-control gates and monitored using online instruments (flow rate, pH, temperature,
conductivity). The chamber is fully enclosed and connected to the odour collection system to
prevent fugitive emissions.
    Step 2. Screening (Coarse and Fine Screens)
     Wastewater passes through automatic coarse and fine screens to remove solid debris larger
than 5 mm. Screened materials are mechanically raked, compacted, and conveyed to containers
for off-site disposal. A standby manual screen is provided for maintenance or emergency
operation. The screening area is enclosed and ventilated, with extracted air routed to the odour
treatment system.
    Step 3. Grit Removal and Oil Separation

                                                                                              78

                                                                                          Official Use Only
    After screening, wastewater enters the grit removal and oil separation tank, where heavier
inorganic particles (sand, grit) settle under gravity and are mechanically collected for
dewatering and disposal. Floating oil and grease are skimmed from the surface. This process
protects downstream biological units from abrasion and clogging.
    Step 4. Biological Treatment – Improved SBR System
    Biological treatment is carried out using an improved SBR activated sludge process,
consisting of Selector tanks and SBR reactors, arranged in two parallel modules operating in
staggered cycles.
    Selector tanks receive influent wastewater and return activated sludge, promoting the
growth of phosphorus-accumulating organisms and improving biological phosphorus removal
without chemical addition.
     SBR reactors perform combined removal of organic matter (BOD, COD), nitrogen
(nitrification–denitrification), and phosphorus within a controlled reaction cycle.
    Each SBR cycle includes: filling and aeration, reaction, settling, decanting, and idle phases,
with an overall cycle time of approximately 4 hours. Oxygen supply and process control are
regulated through online dissolved oxygen monitoring, ensuring stable treatment performance
without the need for separate equalization tanks.
    Step 5. Tertiary Treatment – UV Disinfection
    Treated effluent from the SBR reactors is conveyed to the UV disinfection system, where
pathogenic microorganisms are inactivated using low-pressure UV lamps (wavelength ~253.7
nm). The system ensures effective disinfection without altering the physicochemical
characteristics of the treated water.
    Step 6. Effluent Monitoring and Discharge
Following disinfection, effluent flow is measured using an open-channel flow meter and
continuously monitored by an online monitoring station for key parameters (flow rate, pH,
temperature, TSS, COD, ammonium). Treated wastewater that meets QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT,
Column A is discharged to the Lach Tray River. Monitoring data are transmitted automatically
to the competent environmental authority in accordance with regulatory requirements.

   2025)
       General Information
  •    Line Agency: Hai Phong City People’s Committee (HPC)
  •    Project Management Unit (PMU): Hai Phong Project Management Unit for Transport
       and Agricultural Construction
  •    Director: Mr. Dao Sy Ngoc
  •    Address: No. 14 Minh Khai Street, Hong Bang Ward, Hai Phong City

                                                                                      81

                                                                                  Official Use Only
    Project management and implementation:
    The Project Owner, through the DPF and the HPRDP PMU, directly manages and
coordinates the overall implementation, including procurement, disbursement, environmental
and social safeguard compliance, technical supervision, and reporting to the World Bank and
the Hai Phong People’s Committee.

       Implementation Arrangements
Construction phase:
    The Project will be organized and implemented in accordance with the regulations of the
Government of Viet Nam and the World Bank (WB), as well as in compliance with the Loan
Agreement to be signed.
Project governing authority:
     The Hai Phong City People’s Committee (HPPC) is the investment decision-making
authority and bears overall responsibility for the Project before the Government of Viet Nam.
HPPC is the competent Vietnamese authority responsible for approving the Project and related
documents. HPPC has established a Project Steering Committee, which is responsible for
directing Project implementation from the investment preparation stage through execution in
accordance with the signed Loan Agreement, and for coordinating with relevant ministries,
sectors, and local authorities to ensure Project preparation and implementation meet required
schedules and quality standards.
Investor:
     The Hai Phong Project Management Unit for Transport and Agricultural Construction is
responsible for Project implementation and is assigned to manage and use the construction
investment capital under the direction of HPPC and the Project Steering Committee. The
Investor directly organizes and manages Project implementation until completion and
commissioning. In its state management role, HPPC is also responsible for organizing effective
and sustainable operation of the Project. The Investor’s responsibilities include: (i) organizing
appraisal and approval of detailed designs and cost estimates; (ii) deciding on the establishment
of bidding consultancy teams and contractor appointment; (iii) approving bidding documents
and bid results or contractor appointment; and (iv) other Project-related tasks within the
Investor’s authority.
Project Management Unit (PMU):
     The PMU is responsible for: (i) organizing compensation, land acquisition, clearance, and
resettlement activities under the Investor’s responsibility; (ii) appraising detailed designs and
cost estimates; (iii) organizing contractor selection for Project construction activities; (iv)
negotiating and signing contracts with contractors and monitoring and supervising contract
implementation; (v) managing construction quality, quantities, schedules, and investment costs;
(vi) organizing acceptance, payment, and final settlement of contracts and construction
investment capital; (vii) organizing acceptance and handover of completed works; and (viii)
other Project-related tasks within the PMU’s responsibility.
Operation phase:


                                                                                              82

                                                                                          Official Use Only
     During the operation phase, the Investor will hand over the works to the designated
operation and management entity in accordance with applicable regulations. Prior to handover,
the Investor is responsible for operating the environmental protection facilities.
    Hai Phong City will assume ownership of the Project’s infrastructure assets after
investment completion. Accordingly, the City will establish adequate institutional arrangements
and operation and maintenance (O&M) plans for transport and drainage facilities. Relevant
departments and agencies, under the direction of the city, will participate in developing O&M
arrangements, managing finances, and approving O&M procedures for the facilities.
     On 8 December 2023, the Hai Phong City People’s Committee issued Decision No.
48/2023/QD-UBND regulating the management of urban drainage and wastewater treatment
systems, centralized rural residential areas and industrial zones; as well as the management,
classification, collection, transportation, and treatment of sludge from latrines, septic tanks, and
drainage systems within Hai Phong City. Under this Decision, the Hai Phong City People’s
Committee is designated as the owner of drainage works invested with state budget funds and
assigns the Department of Construction as the owner of the drainage system within its
management area.
     Based on these provisions, upon completion, An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant and
related drainage facilities funded by the city budget will be handed over by the Hai Phong City
People’s Committee (as the asset owner) to the Department of Construction for management
and operation.
    The Department of Construction will organize the selection and contracting of an eligible
drainage service provider to manage, operate, maintain, and protect the drainage system in
accordance with current laws and regulations on public utility service provision.




                                                                                                 83

                                                                                             Official Use Only
                    NATURAL, ENVIRONMENTAL AND
        SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS OF PROJECT AREA
2.1.   NATURAL CONDITIONS OF HAI PHONG
       Geographical Location
      Hai Phong is a coastal city located in the Red River Delta region and is one of the six
centrally governed municipalities of Viet Nam. It is the third-largest city nationwide and serves
as an important seaport city, an industrial hub, and a center of economy, culture, healthcare,
education, science, commerce, and technology in the Northern Coastal Region of Viet Nam.
Hai Phong is classified as a Grade-I urban area and functions as a regional and national central
city.
     Geographically, it borders Quang Ninh Province to the north and Hung Yen Province to
the south, and faces the Gulf of Tonkin of the East Sea to the east. Hai Phong is approximately
70 km from Bach Long Vi Island (Hai Phong’s offshore district) and about 106 km east–
southeast of Ha Noi via National Highway No. 5.
   The Hai Phong Climate-Resilient City Development Project is located within the
administrative boundaries of Dong Hai Ward, Nam Trieu Ward, Bach Dang Ward, Hoa Binh
Ward, Thuy Nguyen Ward, Luu Kiem Ward, Hong Bang Ward, An Duong Ward, An Hai Ward,
Ngo Quyen Ward, Gia Vien Ward, Le Chan Ward, and An Bien Ward.




                                                                                              84

                                                                                          Official Use Only
Figure 2.1. Project location with administrative boundaries




                                                              85
                                                              Official Use Only
       Topographical Characteristics
     Hai Phong features highly diverse topography and geomorphology shaped by long-term
geological evolution and the alluvial accretion of the Red River and Thai Binh River systems. The
northern area consists of plains interspersed with low hills (50–100 m), while the southern area is
dominated by flat, low-lying alluvial plains. The city also includes offshore island groups such as
Cat Ba and Bach Long Vi, along with extensive tidal flats and mangrove areas formed by large
annual sediment deposits. These characteristics strongly influence the hydrological regime: water
concentrates rapidly in limestone and low-hill zones, whereas floodplains and tidal flats are
frequently inundated by tides. The natural slope from northwest to southeast facilitates
downstream drainage toward the sea. The project area features relatively flat terrain, largely
traversing existing agricultural land, intersected by two major rivers: the Cam River and the Ruot
Lon River.
    (1) Ring Road No. 3 alignment:
     The alignment passes mainly through plains and rice-growing areas, with elevations ranging
from 0.01 m to 4.5 m. The route begins at the existing Bui Vien Road, connects to the Ha Noi–Hai
Phong Expressway, and continues to the bank of the Cam River (location of the proposed Ngo
Quyen Bridge). It then crosses to Vu Yen Island—an area currently under urban development with
predominantly flat terrain. The alignment continues across the Ruot Lon River and largely
traverses rice fields, aquaculture ponds, and existing residential areas within Bach Dang, Nam
Trieu, Hoa Binh, and Luu Kiem Wards.
    (2) Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) site:
    The terrain consists mainly of rice fields and natural ponds, with elevations ranging from +0.8
m to +1.1 m. Access roads to the WWTP area have elevations of approximately +2.50 m.
    (3) Along the Re River:
     The terrain is flat, consisting mainly of agricultural land on both sides of the river and nearby
residential clusters. The riverbed elevation is approximately -1.8 m.
    (4) Areas for construction of tidal gates, stormwater drains, and wastewater outlets:
    These are located in the central urban area, characterized by flat and low-lying terrain that is
frequently inundated during heavy rainfall or high tides.

       Geological Conditions
    a) Geological structure
     Hai Phong lies within the Viet–China folded belt and is divided between two major structural
units: the Quang Ninh anticlinorium in the northeast and the Hanoi graben in the southwest. These
units are separated by the deep Kien An Fault, which generally follows the Van Uc River and


                                                                                                   86
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
extends northwest–southeast toward the sea. The northern part of the city is associated with the
crescent-shaped Quang Ninh anticlinorium that stretches from Mong Cai through Hon Gai to Do
Son–Kien An and then curves inland. Meanwhile, the southwestern area belongs to the Tertiary-
aged Hanoi graben, which is further divided into four sub-structures separated by step-like faults.
During geological evolution, several modern geodynamic phenomena in the Hai Phong region
have governed the formation and transformation of the Hai Phong area, including:
•   Karst phenomena: Mainly concentrated on Cat Ba Island, where karstification is intense.
    Typical open-surface karst landforms include “cat-ear” rocky cliffs, steep slopes, trough-
    shaped valleys, and sinkholes. Closed depressions, blocked gullies, and underground caves
    connected by narrow subterranean channels are also developed, accompanied by underground
    mineral springs in Cat Ba and in deep limestone layers at about 700 m below the Tien Lang
    area.
•   Erosion: This is a typical process in newly formed coastal zones under the interaction of
    marine and continental processes. Strong coastal erosion is taking place along the shores of
    Cat Hai, Tien Lang, and the southeastern coast of Dinh Vu Island.
•   Sediment accumulation: Each year, rivers in the area such as the Da Bach, Cam, Lach Tray,
    and Van Uc transport nearly 4 million tons of material to the Hai Phong coastal waters in the
    form of suspended silt and sand and dissolved substances. This sediment supply forms the
    main source material for accumulation in the Hai Phong basin.
    b) Engineering geology:
    (1) Ring Road No. 3 corridor:
      Based on the engineering geological investigation, including 28 boreholes along weak
subgrade sections, five boreholes at large culvert locations, and two boreholes at the 5B overpass—
together with SPT, VST, and laboratory soil tests—the soil profile along the Ring Road No. 3
corridor from the ground surface to a depth of 58 m consists of multiple distinct layers. Layer 1 is
a heterogeneous surface/topsoil–fill layer with a thickness of 0.5–3.2 m. Layers 2 and 4 are high-
plasticity silty clay layers in soft to very soft condition, with significant thickness (average 6.7 m
and 8.3 m) and very low SPT values (1–8 and 2–9), representing the primary weak soil zones.
Interbedded between these weak layers are stiffer strata, including Layer 3 (low-plasticity clay,
stiff, SPT 9–18) and sandy layers such as Layers 4A and 6 (silty sand, loose to medium dense,
SPT 7–32 and 11–41), which provide better bearing capacity. Deeper layers consist of stiff to very
stiff clays (Layers 5 and 7, SPT 7–32 and >100), underlain by weathered siltstone–sandstone
(Layers 8 and 9) with high strength and SPT values exceeding 100 blows.
    Remarks:
      Weak soil layers are identified as Layers 2, 4, and 5. These soft layers lie beneath Layer 1
(fill) or immediately below the natural ground surface and extend along the entire alignment, with
total thicknesses ranging from 6.5 m to 21.9 m, and potentially greater at locations where the
boreholes terminated within these layers. Calculations indicate that, without treatment, the



                                                                                                   87
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
embankment subgrade would undergo large settlements (from 10 cm up to 200 cm), requiring
ground improvement to ensure embankment stability. Based on comparative analysis of economic,
technical, and construction schedule criteria, the following main ground improvement measures
are proposed in weak soil zones.
     Based on the engineering geological findings described above, the design consultant, during
the Basic Design stage, carried out detailed calculations in the technical appendices of the
Feasibility Study (FS). These calculations assessed subgrade stability, total and residual
settlement, bearing capacity, and the required ground-improvement solutions. The purpose was to
ensure long-term stability of the road embankments and bridge foundations, control differential
settlement, comply with applicable technical standards, and establish an appropriate and
technically justified ground-treatment approach for subsequent design stages.
    (2) Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) area
     Based on three boreholes (NM1, NM2, NM3), together with SPT and laboratory test results,
the soil profile at the WWTP site down to 56.6 m consists of a thin surface mud layer (0.3–0.5 m),
underlain by a very soft organic clay layer (Layer 2) of considerable thickness (5.3–15.3 m) with
extremely low strength (NSPT 1–6). Beneath this lies a stiff low-plasticity clay layer (Layer 3)
with moderate strength (NSPT 5–7). This is followed by another thick soft high-plasticity clay
layer (Layer 4), 16.0–24.9 m thick, with low NSPT values (2–9), indicating weak bearing capacity.
Below the soft clays is a medium-dense silty sand layer (Layer 4A), 2.6–10.4 m thick, with
significantly higher strength (NSPT 16–44). The deeper strata include stiff low-plasticity clay
(Layer 5, 13.4–13.6 m thick, NSPT 6–21) and a dense medium-to-coarse sand with gravel (Layer
6) showing very high SPT resistance (NSPT 30–82), indicating a strong granular bearing layer.
    Remarks:
     Weak soil layers at the WWTP site are identified primarily as Layer 2 and Layer 4, consisting
of very soft to soft high-plasticity clay with organic matter. These layers occur immediately below
the surface cover and extend to significant depths (combined thickness 21.3–40.2 m, averaging
~29 m), with low NSPT values (1–9 blows) indicating very low bearing capacity and high
compressibility. Without treatment, large long-term settlements are expected under structural
loads, especially for heavy WWTP units such as aeration tanks, sedimentation tanks, and blower
buildings. The presence of strong sandy layers (Layers 4A and 6) at deeper depths provides suitable
bearing strata for pile foundations. Recommended ground improvement measures include pile
foundation systems, preloading with vertical drains for non-structural embankments, and
excavation–replacement for local structures where weak layers are thin.
    Based on the above geotechnical investigation, the design consultant has, during the Basic
Design stage, incorporated the soil profile characteristics into the structural foundation calculations
presented in the FS technical appendices. For major WWTP structures—particularly reinforced
concrete treatment tanks—the very soft to soft clay layers (Layer 2 and Layer 4) with low bearing
capacity and high compressibility require careful assessment of foundation stability and long-term


                                                                                                    88
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
settlement. Accordingly, the designer evaluated pile foundation solutions by determining
appropriate pile types, allowable bearing capacity, pile lengths, and required pile quantities to
transfer loads to the deeper, competent sandy strata (Layers 4A and 6). These calculations ensure
