that the tanks achieve adequate bearing capacity, avoid excessive total or differential settlement,
and comply with technical standards for wastewater treatment plant foundations.
     (3) Re River area
     Based on eight boreholes (SR01, SR02, SR03, SR16, SR18, SR19, MC01, MC02), supported
by SPT and laboratory testing, the soil profile in the Re River area to a depth of 29 m consists of
a thin surface fill layer (1.3–2.0 m), underlain by a thick very soft organic clay layer (Layer 2)
ranging from 10.6 to 14.5 m, with low strength (NSPT 2–7). Below this is another soft high-
plasticity clay layer (Layer 4), 3.5–12.0 m thick, with slightly higher but still low NSPT values (5–
9). The deepest identified layer is a medium-dense silty sand stratum (Layer 4a), into which
boreholes penetrated 5.0–5.3 m without reaching the base; NSPT values (20–39) indicate a
significantly stronger and more competent bearing layer.
    Remarks:
     Weak soils in the Re River area are dominated by Layer 2 and Layer 4, comprising very soft
to soft high-plasticity clay with organic material, with total weak-layer thicknesses of 14.1–26.5
m (average ~19.3 m) and NSPT values of 2–9 blows, demonstrating low shear strength and high
compressibility. These soft deposits pose risks of excessive settlement and low stability for
embankments, riverbank structures, and road approaches along the Re River corridor. Similar to
the WWTP site, an underlying silty sand layer (Layer 4A) provides a competent bearing stratum
suitable for pile foundations. Recommended ground improvement solutions include prefabricated
vertical drains with surcharge, pile foundations for bridge/culvert structures, and excavation–
replacement for areas with shallower weak zones.
    c) Hydrogeological characteristics
    (1) Ring Road No. 3 and associated bridges
     Groundwater levels measured in boreholes during drilling range from 0.2 m (LK24) to 3.0 m
(LK22) below the ground surface, with an average depth of about 0.8 m. In general, groundwater
is shallow across the area; two boreholes (LK14 and LK15) were flooded with approximately 20
cm of water at the collar.
     Groundwater dynamics in the area are not complex and are not expected to significantly affect
the long-term performance of the works.
    (2) Wastewater Treatment Plant area
     At the WWTP site, all boreholes were flooded, with water depths ranging from 0.1 m (NM2)
to 0.5 m (NM3) from the top of the boreholes.
    (3) Re River area



                                                                                                  89
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    In the river section, submerged boreholes were inundated by 1.0 m (SR02, SR16, SR19) to
1.2 m (SR03) of water. On land, groundwater levels in boreholes range from 0.6 m (MC06) to 0.8
m (MC01) below the ground surface.

       Climate and Meteorological Conditions
    The Project area lies within a humid tropical monsoon climate zone, characterized by cold and
dry winters and hot, humid summers with high rainfall from April to October. The dry and cold
season extends from November of the previous year to March of the following year. The
dispersion, transport, and transformation of pollutants in the atmospheric environment depend on
meteorological factors, including air temperature, humidity, rainfall, and atmospheric stability.
   a) Rainfall
    Hai Phong receives annual rainfall ranging from 713.4 mm to 1,263.6 mm, concentrated
mainly in the rainy season from May to October, which accounts for 80–90% of total annual
precipitation. Rainfall peaks in August under the influence of monsoons, tropical depressions, and
typhoons, while the driest months are November and December. According to QCVN
02:2022/BXD, the city’s long-term annual average rainfall is approximately 1,546.6 mm.
   b) Humidity:
     The average monthly relative humidity ranges from 87.2% to 88.7%. The highest humidity is
typically recorded in April, with average values of 93%–98%. The lowest humidity occurs in
December, ranging from 75% to 82%.
   c) Temperature:
    Hai Phong has a typical humid tropical monsoon climate with clear seasonal contrasts.
Average temperatures range from 24.2–24.7°C, with monthly values varying between 17.2°C in
winter (January–February) and 30°C in peak summer (June–July). The difference between the
highest and lowest monthly temperatures reaches about 12.5°C, reflecting strong seasonal
temperature variation.
   d) Sunshine Duration:
     The average annual sunshine duration ranges from 1,525 to 1,560 hours. Solar radiation
intensity is highest from May to November and lowest during January and February. The average
annual solar radiation is approximately 110–115 kcal/cm². Solar radiation is an important factor
that directly influences the thermal regime of the region, thereby affecting the dispersion and
transformation of atmospheric pollutants.
   e) Wind:
    Wind regimes in Hai Phong show strong seasonal patterns. During summer (May–October),
the prevailing wind direction is southwest, with speeds of 0.9–1.2 m/s. In winter (November–
April), winds shift to the northeast, with speeds of 1.1–1.5 m/s. Overall, wind speeds range from



                                                                                               90
                                                                                              Official Use Only
0.1 to 8.9 m/s, with east winds being most dominant (29.55%), followed by north winds (14.71%)
and calm conditions (4.97%). The annual average wind speed ranges from 3.5–4.2 m/s.

       Natural disaster
     Typhoons: Hai Phong is one of the regions in Viet Nam most heavily affected by typhoons
and tropical depressions. Typhoons typically occur from May to October, with peak frequency in
July, August, and September, often bringing strong winds and heavy rainfall, particularly in island
and coastal areas. Typhoon winds commonly reach 20–40 m/s, accompanied by intense gusts and
widespread disruption to daily life, agriculture, aquaculture, coastal protection structures, power
transmission lines, telecommunications, and transportation networks.
    The frequency of typhoons varies seasonally. Typhoons rarely occur in December; the period
from January to May accounts for only 2.5%, while the highest frequency of 35–36% occurs during
July–September. Hai Phong is located in the zone with the highest typhoon landfall frequency in
Viet Nam, with 28% of all typhoons directly affecting the area each year. On average, Hai Phong
experiences 1–2 direct typhoons and 3–4 indirect storm events annually. Typhoon wind speeds
commonly reach levels 9–10, and in some cases level 12 or above, with rainfall during typhoons
contributing 25–30% of total wet-season precipitation.
    Typhoon Yagi (Typhoon No. 3 in 2024) made landfall in Hai Phong on 7 September 2024,
causing severe human and economic losses. Wind speeds reached level 13, gusting to level 15 on
Bach Long Vi Island; level 11, gusting to level 15 at Phu Lien; level 11, gusting to level 14 at Cat
Hai; level 10, gusting to level 12 at Dong Xuyen (Tien Lang); and level 9, gusting to level 12 at
Hon Dau. Rainfall from 19:00 on 6 September to 19:00 on 7 September ranged from 120–180 mm,
with some areas exceeding 200 mm. Offshore wave heights reached 6.0–8.0 m, and coastal wave
heights at Cat Ba, Do Son, and Hon Dau ranged from 2.0–3.5 m.
    Typhoon Yagi caused widespread damage, including over 100,000 homes, 94 defense and
security structures, 575 schools, 467 healthcare facilities, 895 cultural and historical sites, and
82,000 urban trees damaged or destroyed. More than 25,000 ha of rice, 3,000 ha of crops, 3,300
ha of forest, 4,600 ha of aquaculture, 3,200 power poles, 70 substations, 30,000 industrial facilities,
and 1,200 administrative offices were affected. The total estimated economic loss in Hai Phong
was nearly 12 trillion VND.
     Thunderstorms and Whirlwinds: Hai Phong experiences approximately 40 thunderstorm
days per year, mostly during the rainy season (April–June). Thunderstorms typically occur in the
late afternoon or early morning. Rainfall during thunderstorms can reach 180–200 mm within 1–
2 hours. Severe thunderstorms may produce whirlwinds (tornado-like phenomena) with wind
speeds of 10–20 m/s lasting 5–10 minutes. Strong updrafts during convective storms may also lead
to hail events in some locations.




                                                                                                    91
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Flooding: The old inner-city core cannot drain stormwater by gravity to the Tam Bac River
during rainfall events or even during dry weather when tidal levels rise due to climate change.
Flooding has occurred frequently, without comprehensive and long-term solutions.
     According to Hai Phong Department of Construction, flooding hotspots vary with rainfall
levels and tidal conditions. When rainfall is below 100 mm and tides are low (+1.5 m to +1.8 m),
only two locations experience flooding. However, during high tides (>+1.8 m), flooding points
increase significantly, and inundation may persist for several hours.
     Rainfall <50 mm: Low tide: 1 flooded point (Hung Vuong Street – from Binh Bridge T-
junction to Thuong Ly Bus Station).; High tide (>+1.8 m): 2 flooded points (Hung Vuong Street
and Binh Bridge).
     Rainfall 50–100 mm with high tide: 10 flooded points including Binh Bridge, Hung Vuong
Street, Minh Khai–Tran Hung Dao intersection, Ly Tu Trong Street near the Department of
Construction, Ho Xuan Huong–Ly Tu Trong T-junction, People’s Committee Office Gate, Ton
Duc Thang Martyrs’ Monument, Nguyen Tri Phuong Street, under Lac Long Bridge – Tam Bac
Street, etc.
     Rainfall >100 mm combined with high tide: Additional flooding on Quang Trung Street near
the end of Tam Bac Lake.
     The main causes of flooding in the area include high-intensity rainfall, with inundation
beginning at approximately 45.7 mm of rain and water depths reaching 10–25 cm. Flooding is
further exacerbated by tidal influences and low ground elevations: the central urban area lies
between +1.8 m and +2.6 m, while peak tidal levels can rise to +2.54 m, significantly restricting
drainage. The absence of retention lakes in the old urban core means there is no dedicated storage
capacity for stormwater, leading to rapid surface runoff. In addition, climate change and sea-level
rise—recorded at more than 20 cm over the past decade at Hon Dau station—have intensified tidal
flooding risks. As a result, with the current ground elevation of the project area, flooding is
expected to occur at several locations whenever Tam Bac River water levels rise during high tides
in combination with rainfall exceeding 50 mm. Although the Tam Bac River rehabilitation project
installed two D500 trunk sewers along The Lu and Tam Bac streets, connected to D160–D200
household sewer branches, these main sewers are not deep enough to fully intercept flows from
the existing combined sewer outlets across the entire urban core, thereby limiting their
effectiveness.

       Hydrological and Marine Conditions
    a) Hydrology:
     Hai Phong’s hydrological regime is strongly seasonal, with the flood season from June to
October contributing 75–85% of the annual river discharge (50–70% in July–September), while
the dry season from November to May accounts for only 15–20%, relying mainly on groundwater
and tidal influences. Long-term changes in flow distribution are evident, with decreasing discharge
in the Luoc–Thai Binh River system and a corresponding increase in the Moi River. During the
flood season, northern rivers such as Bach Dang, Van Uc, and Lach Tray are heavily influenced


                                                                                                92
                                                                                               Official Use Only
by the Thai Binh system, whereas southern rivers (Luoc, Hoa, Thai Binh, Moi) are more affected
by the Red River hydrology. Six major rivers flow across the city in a northwest–southeast
direction and divide the territory into five major irrigation systems: Thuy Nguyen, An Hai, Da Do,
Tien Lang, and Vinh Bao.
     Within this broader hydrological context, the Project encompasses the following river
sections: the Cam River and Ruot Lon River (Component 1), the Re River and Lach Tray River
(Component 2), and the Tam Bac River (Component 3), which are described in detail below.




  Figure 2.2. Locations of Major Irrigation Sluices in the An Kim Hai System and the Re
                                           River
    Cam river - component 1
    The Cam River is an important tributary of the Kinh Mon River system. It originates at the
Hop Thanh junction—the confluence of the Kinh Thay and Kinh Mon Rivers—and flows eastward
before merging with the Bach Dang River and discharging to the sea through the Nam Trieu
estuary. The river is approximately 23 km long, with a width ranging from 200 m to 700 m, and a
maximum depth of about –12 m, forming a major inland waterway serving the central area of Hai
Phong.
    In terms of hydraulic and irrigation connectivity, the Cam River forms part of the Thuy
Nguyen – An Hai irrigation system and is directly linked to the larger Thai Binh River basin
through the Kinh Thay – Kinh Mon – Bach Dang river corridor. The river also connects with the
urban drainage network of Hai Phong City, receiving flows from major stormwater canals in the
inner areas before channeling water toward the Nam Trieu estuary.


                                                                                               93
                                                                                              Official Use Only
     Hydrologically, the Cam River is strongly influenced by seasonal monsoon flows. During the
flood season (June–October), runoff is dominated by inflows from the Thai Binh system, while in
the dry season (November–May), river discharge decreases significantly and is governed mostly
by tidal dynamics and groundwater contributions.
     Functionally, the Cam River plays multiple essential roles: conveying stormwater and
domestic wastewater from urban areas, supplying raw water to water treatment and industrial
systems, supporting inland waterway transport, and maintaining hydrological balance for the
central urban core of Hai Phong. Owing to its strategic position, it is a key artery for water
regulation and flood drainage in the inner city.
    Ruot Lon River - component 1
     The Ruot Lon River is one of several medium-sized rivers, running parallel to other local
rivers such as the Gia and Hon Ngoc Rivers. It serves as an important secondary watercourse for
residential and agricultural areas.
     Regarding irrigation connectivity, the Ruot Lon River belongs to the Thuy Nguyen irrigation
system, linking directly with inter-commune drainage and irrigation canals, agricultural
depressions, and several regulating channels. It forms part of a dense water drainage network that
connects with intra-regional rivers and indirectly with the Thai Binh basin.
     Hydrologically, the Ruot Lon River reflects typical monsoon dynamics: flow increases
sharply during the flood season when rainfall is concentrated, and decreases significantly during
the dry season, when water levels depend on tidal influence and limited local inflows. Water level
fluctuations follow the tidal cycle, particularly in areas close to larger downstream rivers.
    The river's key functions include stormwater drainage, agricultural wastewater conveyance,
maintaining water for local production, and helping regulate hydrological conditions. It plays an
important role in reducing localized flooding during the rainy season and supporting irrigation
needs during the dry season.
    Re river - component 2
     The Re River forms the downstream section of the inter-provincial An Kim Hai irrigation
system, receiving water from the An Kim Hai Canal in Hai Duong Province and extending more
than 10 km to the Cai Tat tidal-control sluice in Hong Bang ward. It is a primary hydraulic artery
regulating water for both agricultural and urban areas in western Hai Phong.
     Regarding system connectivity, the Re River is the central channel of the An Kim Hai system,
directly linked to the upstream An Kim Hai Canal and discharging toward the Cam River through
the Cai Tat sluice. It provides irrigation water for 5,169 ha of agricultural land, drainage for 17,811
ha of agricultural and urbanized areas, and raw water for four major water treatment plants (with
a total of 155 million m³/year) as well as 63 million m³/year for rural supply. Under the Hai Phong
Water Supply Master Plan 2025–2050, the Re River is the main raw water source for the An Duong
and Vat Cach Water Treatment Plants, with a combined capacity of 340,000 m³/day.


                                                                                                    94
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Hydrologically, the river section within the project area is 50–60 m wide (up to 100 m at its
widest) and 3–4 m deep. Water levels are maintained at +0.80 m (national datum) via the Cai Tat
sluice, effectively controlling salinity intrusion and stabilizing flows throughout the year.
Historically, this area experienced minimal flooding; however, rapid urbanization in recent years
has led to localized inundation when the drainage system becomes overloaded.
     Functionally, the Re River is a strategically important water source supplying more than 50%
of the city’s treated water demand. It is also the main drainage axis for the An Kim Hai system,
protecting agricultural production, reducing urban flooding, and ensuring stable raw water supply
for domestic and industrial uses.
    Lach Tray river - component 2
    The Lach Tray River is a major tributary of the Van Uc River, extending 43 km before
discharging to the sea at Trang Cat. It is typically 500–600 m wide, 6–8 m deep, and reaches a
maximum depth of 24 m, serving as one of the most important waterways in central Hai Phong.
     In terms of hydraulic connectivity, the Lach Tray River functions as the primary drainage axis
of the An Hai – Da Do irrigation system. It receives inflows from the Van Uc River and from
urban drainage channels serving the wards of Le Chan, Ngo Quyen, and Hong Bang. The river
also receives water from smaller rivers such as Tam Bac, Ha Ly, and Mia, contributing to salinity–
freshwater regulation in the estuarine zone.
     Hydrologically, the river exhibits strong seasonal variability. Peak discharge reaches 1,860
m³/s, with a minimum of 178 m³/s. The tidal amplitude ranges from 2.5 to 3.5 m, with water levels
varying from 0.2–0.3 m at low tide to 3–4 m at high tide. Each year, the river transports 10–15
million m³ of water and about 2 million tons of sediment to the sea. Salinity intrusion can extend
up to 30 km inland, with an average salinity of about 8‰.
     In terms of function, the Lach Tray River is the primary stormwater and wastewater drainage
route for urban Hai Phong, an important inland waterway transport corridor, and the main conduit
for sediment movement toward the coastal zone. It plays a central role in urban flood control,
hydrological balancing, and salinity management across the basin.
    Tam Bac river - component 3
    The Tam Bac River is a key urban watercourse located in the central area of Hai Phong. It
forms part of the inner-city river system comprising the Tam Bac, Ha Ly, Mia, and Moi Rivers,
flowing through densely populated areas of Hong Bang ward and historically serving local
commerce and urban development.
    In terms of system connectivity, the Tam Bac River is the main drainage channel for the
central urban area, directly linked to the municipal canal network and ultimately discharging into
the Lach Tray River. It receives water from secondary and tertiary drainage canals, residential
stormwater systems, and the combined sewer network.




                                                                                                95
                                                                                               Official Use Only
     Hydrologically, the river is strongly influenced by tidal dynamics, especially during the dry
season when upstream inflows are minimal. Tidal fluctuations cause slow-flowing or reverse-flow
conditions at times, affecting the drainage efficiency of the inner city. As a predominantly urban
river, its inflow consists largely of stormwater and domestic wastewater.
     Functionally, the Tam Bac River is the principal stormwater drainage route for the central
city, while also receiving treated and untreated domestic wastewater. It contributes to hydrological
balance within the urban core, supports flood mitigation, and enhances urban landscape and
riverfront commercial–tourism activities.
    b) Marine (Oceanographic) Conditions:
     Tidal currents in the Hai Phong coastal zone are strongest in June, July, December, and
January, and weaker during March, April, August, and September. In the Do Son – Lach Tray
coastal area, tidal flows move along the shoreline and river mouths: ebb currents flow east–
southeast for 12–14 hours with a maximum velocity of 35 cm/s, while flood currents flow north–
northwest for 10–12 hours with a maximum velocity of 42 cm/s. At the Nam Trieu estuary,
influenced by river discharge, ebb currents can reach up to 90 cm/s and flood currents up to 60
cm/s.
     The project area is influenced by a typical diurnal tidal regime, with one high tide and one low
tide each day. The maximum tidal range reaches 3.5–3.9 m, and the highest tidal level ever
recorded at Hon Dau Island was +3.94 m on 23 June 1968. Maximum astronomical tide levels and
design tide levels at a frequency of P = 5% (including storm-surge sea-level rise at P = 20%) at the
estuaries range from approximately 2.06–2.18 m and 3.34–3.64 m, respectively.
     All rivers within the project area—including the Cam River, Ruot Lon River, Re River, Lach
Tray River, and Tam Bac River—are strongly influenced by this diurnal tidal regime. For major
rivers such as Cam and Lach Tray, the 3.5–3.9 m tidal range causes significant fluctuations in
water levels and generates bidirectional flow (ebb and flood currents), especially near estuarine
zones like Nam Trieu. These dynamics directly affect stormwater drainage capacity, sediment
transport, and saline intrusion during the dry season. For the Re River, a key component of the An
Kim Hai irrigation system, tidal action combined with controlled water levels at the Cai Tat sluice
strongly regulates daily water levels and flow direction, influencing both drainage and raw water
supply. Smaller urban rivers such as Tam Bac and Ruot Lon are even more sensitive to tidal
impacts due to limited upstream inflow, resulting in backwater effects, reduced flow velocities,
and decreased drainage efficiency during heavy rainfall when tides are high. Overall, tides play a
decisive role in shaping the hydrological regime of all rivers within the project area and must be
carefully considered in drainage design, flood control, and the operation of hydraulic structures.

       Natural Resources
   a) Water Resources (Surface Water and Groundwater)




                                                                                                  96
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    Located downstream of the Red River and Thai Binh River systems, Hai Phong City possesses
abundant surface water resources, comprising five major river systems: Thai Binh, Van Uc, Kinh
Thay, Bach Dang, and Lach Tray, with a total annual flow of approximately 77.2 billion m³/year.
Due to this hydrological position, the quality of surface water is significantly influenced by
upstream inflows, continental waters, and the diurnal tidal regime of the East Sea.
    Surface water sources in Hai An ward are mainly from the Cam River and Lach Tray River,
both having relatively high flow volumes but elevated salinity levels. The ward also contains
extensive coastal and marine water bodies. This presents advantages for the development of the
marine economy but also challenges arising from extreme and unpredictable climate conditions.
    Groundwater Resources: Hai Phong’s groundwater reserves have the potential to supply
domestic and industrial water, with an estimated exploitable volume of 244,131 m³/day. However,
groundwater is unevenly distributed, concentrated mainly in Cat Ba – Cat Hai, Thuy Nguyen, Kien
An, Do Son, Tien Lang, and Vinh Bao. Due to the city’s coastal location, groundwater is
susceptible to saline intrusion during extraction.
   b) Forest Resources:
    In the project scope:
     After the administrative merger, the new Hai Phong City now manages more than 33,096 ha
of forests and forestry land across 35 wards and special zones, including approximately 11,000 ha
of special-use forests, 16,740 ha of protection forests and nearly 5,000 ha of production forests.
The city has around 10,165 ha of natural forests and nearly 3,000 ha of mangrove forests, which
play an important role in coastal protection and biodiversity conservation. The most significant
forested area is Cat Ba National Park—part of the Ha Long–Cat Ba World Natural Heritage site
(about 20-25km from the project sites)—home to over 4,000 terrestrial and marine species,
including 154 rare and endangered species.
     While the expanded forest area creates opportunities for tourism, research and ecosystem
services, it also poses major management challenges such as illegal hunting, forest fires and
biodiversity loss. To improve protection efficiency, the Hai Phong Forest Protection Department
has reorganised its structure into four management zones and strengthened coordination with local
authorities and communities to enhance patrols, law enforcement, wildfire prevention and public
awareness in forest conservation.
    In the project scope:
     Forest resources within the project area are planted protection forest mainly distributed in
Nam Trieu Ward and Dong Hai Ward, characterised by coastal protective forests—wave-breaking,
mangrove-influenced stands originating from planted forests, predominantly consisting of the
species Sonneratia caseolaris (bần chua). In Nam Trieu Ward, the recorded forest area is 1.24 ha;
although Typhoon Yagi (September 2024) caused 50–70% tree breakage and damage, field
inspections confirm that the area still meets all regulatory criteria to be classified as forest. In



                                                                                                 97
                                                                                                Official Use Only
contrast, the forested area in Dong Hai Ward is only 0.29 ha, and suffered more severe damage
(over 90% tree loss), leaving it no longer eligible to be classified as forest.
     Within the project footprint, 1.53 ha of land falls under the planned protective forest category.
Of this, 1.24 ha is forest that still meets classification criteria and therefore requires procedures for
conversion of forest use; the remaining 0.29 ha has already lost forest attributes due to storm
damage. These areas are all protective coastal forests on mangrove-type sites, with an estimated
standing volume of approximately 43.16 m³/ha. The remaining forest areas in Nam Trieu Ward,
outside the 1.24 ha proposed for conversion, will continue to function as protective forests, playing
an important role in shoreline protection, wave attenuation, and maintaining ecological stability in
the coastal–estuarine environment.




                  Figure 2.3. Forest Area within the Project Boundary
    c) Cultural Resources:
     Hai Phong is a region rich in historical and cultural traditions, home to a diverse range of
tangible and intangible cultural heritage. The city has hundreds of historical sites, temples,
pagodas, and communal houses, many of which are recognized as national or municipal heritage
sites. Notable cultural landmarks include Nghe Temple, Du Hang Pagoda, Hang Kenh Communal




                                                                                                      98
                                                                                                     Official Use Only
House, Ve Pagoda, Tam Ky Temple, and Nguyen Binh Khiem Temple, associated with national
heroes and scholars such as Ngo Quyen, Tran Hung Dao, and Nguyen Binh Khiem.
     These structures not only possess significant architectural and artistic value but also serve as
important places for cultural and spiritual activities. Hai Phong also preserves numerous traditional
festivals such as the Do Son Buffalo Fighting Festival, Nguyen Binh Khiem Temple Festival, and
Tu Luong Xam Festival, attracting large numbers of residents and visitors.
     These cultural assets contribute to shaping Hai Phong as a modern port city that still retains
its distinctive traditional identity.
    d) Biodiversity resource:
    Current Status of Aquatic, Amphibian, and Terrestrial Riparian Ecosystems along the Cam,
Lach Tray, Re, and Tam Bac Rivers
     The Cam, Lach Tray, Re, and Tam Bac rivers form part of the tidal–freshwater wetland system
of the Hai Phong coastal plain. Although urbanization, port development, and hard embankments
have significantly altered the natural landscape, these rivers continue to maintain residual aquatic
habitats, riparian vegetation patches, and ecological functions supported by tidal–freshwater
exchanges. Ecological diversity is highest in semi-natural river sections, most notably in the
estuarine zone of the lower Lach Tray River, while highly urbanized corridors such as the Cam
and Tam Bac Rivers exhibit the lowest levels of ecological diversity.
     Recent studies and monitoring data indicate that water quality in the Cam River, the Tam Bac
River, and parts of the Lach Tray River has shown a declining trend in recent years. At several
locations, concentrations of key pollution parameters such as BOD, COD, ammonium, and nitrite
exceed the permissible limits for Class A surface water under applicable QCVN standards,
reflecting cumulative impacts from domestic wastewater, industrial effluents, and urban runoff.
The Cam River, as a major inland waterway and port corridor of Hai Phong, is subject to additional
pressures from navigation and port-related activities, associated with the Hai Phong Port system,
Dinh Vu Port, and adjacent industrial and logistics zones such as Dinh Vu, Nam Dinh Vu, and
Deep C Industrial Parks. The Tam Bac River, despite recent landscape improvements, continues
to receive domestic wastewater from the urban core and is periodically affected by small-scale
environmental incidents. These pressures have led to a marked decline in aquatic biodiversity, an
increased dominance of pollution-tolerant species, and a reduced function of urban river sections
as natural spawning and nursery habitats for fish and other aquatic organisms.
     In contrast, the lower reaches of the Lach Tray River, which are strongly influenced by tidal
dynamics and the mixing of freshwater and seawater, continue to maintain relatively good
ecological functions. Owing to strong tidal flushing, seasonally variable salinity, and the presence
of wetland ecosystems, these areas support a higher diversity and abundance of economically
important fish species, benthic organisms, and aquatic vegetation compared with the urban river
sections.



                                                                                                  99
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    Aquatic and Wetland Ecosystems
     Aquatic ecosystems show strong spatial differentiation. Urban river sections—particularly the
Cam River, central Tam Bac, and midstream Lach Tray—are dominated by species tolerant of
fluctuating water quality. In contrast, the lower Lach Tray River retains more natural wetland
features such as scattered mangrove patches, tidal mudflats, brackish-water vegetation, and richer
plankton and benthic communities. These habitats provide feeding and nursery grounds for
economically important aquatic species.
     Of particular ecological and livelihood importance in Hai Phong are the polychaete
Tylorrhynchus heterochaetus (locally known as “ruoi”) and the spotted shad (Tenualosa reevesii).
While these species are no longer present in highly urbanized river sections such as the Cam and
Tam Bac rivers, they remain closely associated with the lower Lach Tray River and its estuarine
mudflat system. Notably, polychaete farming areas are primarily distributed along the lower Lach
Tray River near the estuary, where salinity regimes, tidal dynamics, and sediment characteristics
are favorable, within the administrative boundaries of Duong Kinh and Hai An wards.
     The main polychaete farming zones are located approximately 20 km downstream, along the
river course, from the proposed discharge point of the An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant
(Coordinates in the VN-2000 coordinate system: X = 2,304,913.8560; Y = 593,604.8093). Given
this substantial downstream separation, these farming areas are considered to be beyond the zone
of direct influence of the project discharge. In combination with tidal dilution and estuarine mixing
processes, and assuming compliance of treated effluent with applicable discharge standards,
wastewater discharges from the An Duong 1 WWTP are not expected to cause measurable changes
in water quality conditions at the polychaete farming locations. Accordingly, no material impacts
on traditional polychaete-based livelihoods of local communities in Duong Kinh and Hai An wards
are anticipated.




                                                                                                 100
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
Figure 2.4. Location map of the discharge point of An Duong 1 WWTP and the polychaete
         aquaculture areas in Hai An and Duong Kinh wards in Lach Tray river
    Fish Diversity and Fisheries Resources
    The linked river–estuary system supports dozens of freshwater, brackish, and coastal fish
species. The Lach Tray estuary is recognised as a migration and spawning corridor for shad, while
general freshwater species (carp, catfish, tilapia) dominate the upper Lach Tray and Re River.
Urban sections of the Cam and Tam Bac Rivers show substantially reduced diversity, functioning
primarily as water conveyance channels rather than reproductive or nursery habitats.
    Amphibians, Reptiles, and Terrestrial Riparian Habitats
    Riparian habitats differ significantly by location. Along the Cam and Tam Bac Rivers—where
both banks are almost fully hardened—terrestrial biodiversity is limited to urban-adapted birds
(pigeons, sparrows), small herons, lizards, and a few amphibians. More diverse riparian habitats
occur along the Re River and upper Lach Tray, where agricultural fields, canals, and patches of
vegetation support frogs, waterbirds, small reptiles, and rodents typical of the Red River Delta
agro-ecosystem.
    River-Specific Ecological Characteristics
    (i) Cam River




                                                                                             101
                                                                                             Official Use Only
     The Cam River is a heavily urbanized waterway lined with ports, industrial areas, and hard
embankments. Its ecological function is now primarily hydrological—navigation, drainage, and
flood conveyance. Aquatic life is dominated by pollution-tolerant species, and the river does not
support habitats for polychaetes or shad due to the absence of tidal mudflats and the high degree
of channel modification.
    (ii) Lach Tray River
    The Lach Tray River exhibits the highest ecological value among the project rivers, primarily
within its lower reaches and estuarine zone. These downstream sections are characterized by
brackish-water conditions, remnant mangrove patches, tidal mudflats, and productive aquatic
habitats, which provide important feeding and spawning grounds for spotted shad and support the
main distribution of polychaetes in Hai Phong. In contrast, the mid–upper reaches of the Lach Tray
River are more influenced by existing urban and agricultural land use and continue to support
common freshwater fish, plankton, and benthic organisms, although with reduced habitat quality
compared to the estuarine zone.
     Taking into account the location of sensitive estuarine habitats relative to the proposed
discharge point, together with the natural tidal flushing and mixing characteristics of the river
system, the potential interaction between treated effluent and the lower Lach Tray River has been
considered in the impact assessment. Environmental management and response measures relevant
to water quality protection are outlined in Chapter 5 to ensure appropriate management under
different operating conditions.
    (iii) Re River
     The Re River is a controlled freshwater channel within the An Kim Hai irrigation system,
serving as a major raw-water supply for Hai Phong. Its ecology is shaped by slow-flowing
freshwater and significant inputs of domestic and agricultural wastewater. Species present are
mainly pollution-tolerant freshwater fish and small aquatic organisms. The river does not support
polychaetes or shad due to its freshwater character and absence of tidal flats, but its environmental
quality remains critical for water supply security.
    (iv) Tam Bac River
     The Tam Bac River, entirely within the urban core, has been fully embanked and transformed
into a landscape and stormwater conveyance channel. Natural habitats have largely disappeared,
leaving only resilient plankton, benthic taxa, and common urban fish. It does not serve as habitat
for polychaetes or shad. Its ecological role is limited to hydraulic connectivity between the Lach
Tray and Cam Rivers.
    Overall Assessment
    Based on baseline surveys and secondary data, no plant or animal species recorded in the
project area are classified as rare, endangered, or listed in the Vietnam Red Data Book or the IUCN
Red List. Biodiversity along the Cam, Lach Tray, Re, and Tam Bac rivers is generally


                                                                                                 102
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
characterized by common, disturbance-tolerant species, with urban sections showing notably
degraded ecological conditions.
    In contrast, the lower Lach Tray River retains ecologically valuable estuarine and brackish-
water habitats that support locally important species such as Tylorrhynchus heterochaetus and
Tenualosa reevesii (spotted shad). These habitats are located approximately 20 km downstream
(along the river course) from the proposed discharge point of the An Duong 1 WWTP. Although
not legally protected, their ecological sensitivity is primarily associated with their estuarine
characteristics, and they are expected to be buffered from direct project influences by the
substantial downstream separation and natural tidal mixing processes, with protection further
supported through appropriate project design and environmental management measures.

         Climate Change and Sea-Level Rise
       Temperature Trends
     According to the 2020 Climate Change and Sea-Level Rise Scenario (MAE), Hai Phong lies
in Region I (Mong Cai – Hon Dau) and is recommended to apply the RCP4.5 scenario for planning
purposes. Average annual temperatures are projected to increase by approximately 1.3–1.5°C by
2050 and 1.7–2.2°C by the end of the century. The number of hot days above 35°C is expected to
rise significantly, especially during May–July, intensifying evaporation, urban heat stress, and the
risk of declining surface water quality.
       Rainfall Trends
     Annual rainfall in the region is projected to increase by about 5–10% by mid-century, mainly
during the rainy season (June–October). Extreme rainfall events are expected to become more
frequent and intense, increasing the risk of urban flooding, slope instability, and overloading
existing drainage systems.
       Sea-Level Rise
     Under the RCP4.5 scenario, sea level in Hai Phong is expected to rise by about 12 cm by 2030,
22 cm by 2050, and around 52 cm by 2100 (range 33–75 cm). Rising sea levels will heighten the
risk of coastal inundation and drive saline intrusion deeper into estuaries such as Van Uc, Lach
Tray, and Thai Binh, while elevating baseline tidal water levels, thereby worsening the severity of
urban flooding when combined with extreme rainfall or storm surges.

2.2.     EXISTING ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY IN PROJECT AREAS
    To comprehensively assess the potential environmental impacts during the construction phase
within the project’s area of influence, the Project conducted baseline environmental quality
monitoring prior to commencement. The assessment covered ambient air quality, surface water,
coastal seawater, groundwater, soil, and sediment quality.




                                                                                                103
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     Sampling locations were selected in proximity to the project sites. The monitoring was carried
out by Asia Green Electrical and Environmental Engineering Joint Stock Company, which is fully
certified to perform environmental sampling and analysis in accordance with VIMCERTS 174,
issued by the Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment.
    All field measurement, sampling, preservation, transportation, treatment, and laboratory
analysis methods were implemented in accordance with the applicable Vietnamese Standards
(TCVN).

       Air quality; Noise & vibration
     Air quality monitoring was conducted at twenty locations within the project area from 20 to
24 September 2025. A total of 16 air and noise samples were collected to assess ambient air quality,
noise, and vibration conditions in the project area. Monitoring parameters included meteorological
factors (temperature, humidity, wind direction and speed, atmospheric pressure), air pollutants
(TSP, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, CO, NO₂, SO₂), noise levels (LAeq, LAmax, LA50), and vibration. The
results were compared with QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT for ambient air quality, QCVN
26:2025/BNNMT for noise, and QCVN 27:2025/BTNMT for vibration.
     Results show that ambient air conditions generally meet national standards. At sites such as
the wastewater treatment plant area, Pumping Station No. 6, Thai Lai Pagoda gate, and residential
clusters along Thanh Binh Street and Pham Ngu Lao Street, concentrations of SO₂, NO₂, and CO
remained low and were all within the allowable limits of QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT.
     Particulate matter levels (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, and TSP) were also largely compliant across most
monitoring points. Slightly higher values were recorded at locations with dense traffic and urban
activities—such as the area near Tam Bac Bridge, the Tam Bac–Pham Hong Thai intersection, the
QL5–QL5B junction, and the intersection with National Highway 10—but these remained within
permissible thresholds. These localized elevations are consistent with typical traffic-related
emissions in congested zones.
     With regard to the noise monitoring results, comparison against the threshold of 68 dBA
stipulated in QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT (Maximum permissible values for noise levels generated
by road traffic, applicable to Area A and B, as specified in Table 5) indicates that the majority of
the 20 monitoring points (KK-1 to KK-20) comply with the applicable standard. Only two
locations exceeded the permissible limit, namely KK-3 at Pumping Station No. 9 – Cai Tat Bridge,
which recorded 68.6 dBA (exceeding the limit by 0.6 dBA), and KK-7 at the intersection of DT208
and DT351, with a measured level of 68.4 dBA (exceeding the limit by 0.4 dBA); these
exceedances are minor and negligible relative to the regulatory threshold.
     With regard to the vibration monitoring results, comparison against the threshold of 65
stipulated in QCVN 27:2025/BNNMT (Maximum permissible values for vibration levels
generated from other sources, applicable to Area A, as specified in Table 3) shows that all 20
monitoring points (KK-1 to KK-20) recorded vibration levels within the permissible limit.



                                                                                                104
                                                                                                Official Use Only
Measured values ranged from 36.2 to 54.9, which are well below the regulatory threshold,
indicating full compliance with the applicable vibration standard.
     Overall, the air and acoustic environment within the project corridor is stable, with only minor
and localized fluctuations associated with transportation activities. Detailed analytical tables and
full environmental assessment results are provided in the Appendix 2.

       Surface water
     Surface water quality varies noticeably between the Tam Bac River area and the Re River
discharge points, based on samples collected from 20–24 September 2025. Totally, twelve surface
water samples were collected to evaluate surface water quality in the project area. Analyzed
parameters included temperature, pH, DO, TSS, BOD₅, COD, NH₄⁺, NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, Cl⁻, Zn, Cr,
coliforms, and total oil and grease. The results were assessed against QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT.
    At the upstream discharge gate of Lac Long Bridge and the discharge gate near the Tam Bac–
Lan Ong intersection, pH, temperature, and dissolved oxygen were within normal ranges, with
moderate organic pollution (BOD₅, COD) typical of mixed urban wastewater.
     In contrast, samples collected at the Hospital Canal, Ba Go culvert (DT208), Bay Mau Sewer
(DT208), Vuon Vai Canal, Ba Nhi Sewer (DT208), and Cai Tat Market Sewer (DT208) showed
significantly higher pollution levels, with BOD₅ and COD exceeding allowable limits at multiple
locations. Nutrient concentrations (ammonium, phosphate) were elevated, and suspended solids
increased sharply at the Bay Mau and Ba Nhi discharge points.
     Microbial contamination (coliform) was highest at the Re River discharge points, reflecting
direct discharges of untreated domestic wastewater from nearby residential areas. In comparison,
the Tam Bac River samples exhibited lower, though still typical, urban wastewater characteristics.
     Overall, the Re River system shows more severe pollution than the Tam Bac area, mainly due
to concentrated household wastewater inputs.

       Groundwater
     One groundwater sample was taken from 20–24 September 2025 to assess groundwater
quality. The analyzed parameters included temperature, pH, DO, TS, BOD, KMnO₄, NH₄⁺, PO₄³⁻,
Cl⁻, Fe, Mn, and coliforms. The results were compared with QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT.
     Groundwater quality at the sampling point shows generally acceptable conditions for most
basic parameters when compared with QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT. pH (6.92), permanganate index
(0.81 mg/L), ammonium (0.42 mg/L), and chloride (128.9 mg/L) were all within allowable limits,
indicating no signs of chemical contamination. Total suspended solids were low, and phosphate
levels remained minor, reflecting stable physicochemical characteristics.
    However, dissolved oxygen was low (2.8 mg/L), suggesting limited natural aeration or slow
groundwater movement. The most notable concern is microbial contamination, with coliform



                                                                                                 105
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
concentrations reaching 2.1 × 10³ MPN/100 mL, significantly exceeding the limit of 3 MPN/100
mL, indicating potential infiltration of domestic wastewater or surface runoff.
    Iron and manganese were below detection limits, within the permissible thresholds.
    Detailed groundwater analysis results are provided in the Appendix 2.

       Soil & sediment
    Soil samples collected at 12 locations from 20–24 September 2025 with the analyzed
parameters included As, Cd, Pb, Zn, Cr, and Cu, and the results were compared with QCVN
03:2023/BTNMT. The results show that heavy metal concentrations are generally low and well
within the allowable limits of QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT – Type 3. Arsenic concentrations range
from 1.63 to 11.38 mg/kg, far below the limit of 200 mg/kg. Cadmium was detected at several
locations (0.09–0.20 mg/kg) but remains significantly lower than the allowable limit of 60 mg/kg.
Lead concentrations (17.19–48.68 mg/kg) also fall comfortably below the standard threshold of
700 mg/kg.
     Zinc concentrations vary from 49.67 to 197 mg/kg, staying well within the permissible limit
of 2,000 mg/kg. Total chromium was either not detected (KPH) or found at very low levels (up to
10.64 mg/kg), far below the limit of 250 mg/kg. Copper concentrations (11.29–51.68 mg/kg) also
remain low compared to the allowable limit of 2,000 mg/kg.
    Overall, all soil samples meet environmental quality standards, indicating no signs of heavy
metal contamination at the surveyed locations. Detailed analytical data are presented in the
Appendix 2.
    Two sediment samples were collected to assess heavy metal accumulation in sediments. The
analyzed parameters included As, Cd, Pb, Zn, Cr, and Cu, and the results were compared with
QCVN 43:2017/BTNMT.
    Sediment samples collected at two locations show that all analyzed heavy metal
concentrations comply with the limits set by QCVN 43:2017/BTNMT. Arsenic levels (6.36–10.73
mg/kg) remain far below the allowable limit of 41.6 mg/kg. Cadmium concentrations (0.29–0.31
mg/kg) are also very low compared to the limit of 4.2 mg/kg. Lead (42.01–45.82 mg/kg) and Zinc
(108–114 mg/kg) fall well within the respective thresholds of 112 mg/kg and 271 mg/kg.
    Total chromium was not detected in either sample (below MDL = 8.5 mg/kg), and copper
concentrations (22.95–34.78 mg/kg) are significantly lower than the allowable limit of 108 mg/kg.
     Overall, sediment quality at both sampling locations meets national environmental standards,
indicating no signs of heavy metal contamination. Detailed analytical results are presented in the
Appendix 2.




                                                                                              106
                                                                                              Official Use Only
         Wastewater
    A total of 8 wastewater samples were collected from 20–24 September 2025 to evaluate
wastewater quality. Parameters analyzed included temperature, pH, DO, TSS, BOD₅, COD, NH₄⁺,
NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, Cl⁻, total nitrogen, total phosphorus, Zn, Cr, coliforms, and total oil and grease. The
results were compared with QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT.
     Wastewater samples from the Tam Bac River area—including the sample at the upstream
discharge gate of Lac Long Bridge on Tam Bac Street (NT-1) and at the discharge gate near the
intersection of Tam Bac Street and Lan Ong Street (NT-2)—show the highest pollution levels.
Both locations recorded very high BOD₅, COD, and ammonium well above QCVN
14:2025/BTNMT limits, indicating direct discharge of untreated domestic wastewater.
     Samples from the Re River area—including those at the Hospital Canal discharge point (NT-
3), Ba Go culvert on DT208 (NT-4), Bay Mau Sewer on DT208 (NT-5), Vuon Vai Canal (NT-6),
Ba Nhi Sewer on DT208 (NT-7), and Cai Tat Market Sewer on DT208 (NT-8)—generally show
lower and mostly compliant pollutant levels, although a few locations show elevated ammonium
or organic matter. Coliform and oil–grease values remain within allowable limits.
    Overall, wastewater pollution is most severe at Tam Bac River discharge points, while the Re
River discharge points show moderate pollution with occasional exceedances. Detailed analytical
results are provided in the Appendix 2.

2.3.     SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS
         Population and Land Resources
    According to the Resolution of the National Assembly on the establishment of the new Hai
Phong City, following its merger with Hai Duong Province, the newly formed Hai Phong City has
a revised administrative structure under the name “Hai Phong City,” comprising a total of 114
commune-level administrative units (67 communes, 45 wards, and 2 special zones).
   The project area spans 13 wards and communes of Hai Phong City, including Hong Bang,
Ngo Quyen, Gia Vien, Le Chan, An Bien, Dong Hai, An Duong, An Hai, Thuy Nguyen, Hoa Binh,
Nam Trieu, Bach Dang, and Luu Kiem. The demographic characteristics across these
administrative units show significant variation in population size and density.

         Economic Conditions
       (1) Hai Phong City
     Hai Phong City’s economy is growing strongly, with GRDP in the first six months of 2025
estimated at over VND 209.6 trillion, up 11.2% year-on-year—about 1.5 times the national
average. The city ranks second nationwide and first among centrally governed cities in GRDP
growth, with industrial production increasing by 14.6% and total investment capital reaching VND
139.8 trillion. Budget revenue exceeded VND 100 trillion, tourism welcomed over 7.5 million



                                                                                                107
                                                                                                Official Use Only
visitors, and FDI reached about USD 1.3 billion. The city has not reported a unified official poverty
rate in the documents reviewed, but social security and poverty reduction are reported as being
implemented adequately and in a timely manner. No city-wide average per-capita income is given
in the available data.
    (2) An Hai Ward
     In An Hai Ward, the local economy shows a balanced structure between industry and services,
with industrial production reaching VND 137.5 billion, strong retail activity of nearly VND 2,000
billion, and close to VND 470 billion from accommodation and food services in the first nine
months. Agriculture and aquaculture (rice, vegetables, flowers, ornamental plants and 67 ha of
ponds) still play a complementary role. No official per-capita income data are available at ward
level, as detailed socio-economic statistics are still being consolidated after the administrative
merger effective 1 July 2025. The near-poor household rate stands at 0.47%, and although no
explicit poor-household rate is given, it can be inferred that poverty is relatively low.
     (3) Thuy Nguyen Ward
     Thuy Nguyen Ward has a diversified economic base in which handicrafts, construction and
services are key strengths, supported by 1,616 business households and 612 enterprises.
Agriculture remains important, with nearly 200 ha of rice, extensive vegetable cultivation and 79
ha of aquaculture, alongside a small registered fishing fleet. No official per-capita income figures
have yet been published following the recent administrative reorganisation. The ward no longer
has poor households, while 155 households are classified as near-poor, indicating relatively good
but still uneven living standards in some segments.
     (4) An Duong Ward
     In An Duong Ward, the economy is characterised by strong budget revenue performance, a
large number of enterprises and household businesses, and an extensive agricultural base with
more than 1,000 ha of rice and several hundred hectares of vegetables and flowers. Commerce and
services are expanding, supported by over 400 enterprises and more than 2,500 business
households. However, no official data on average per-capita income or poverty rate are available
in the reviewed documents, as the ward is affected by the 1 July 2025 administrative merger and
related ongoing statistical consolidation.
    (5) Ngo Quyen Ward
    Ngo Quyen Ward has a predominantly urban economic structure, with state budget revenue,
industrial production, retail sales, and accommodation and food services all achieving around 75–
80% of their annual targets in the first nine months, reflecting a strong service- and trade-led
economy. While the documents do not provide a specific per-capita income figure, socio-economic
conditions are relatively favourable: the ward has no poor households and a near-poor rate of 0%,
suggesting high living standards and broad access to economic opportunities.
    (6) Bach Dang Ward


                                                                                                 108
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
     In Bach Dang Ward, the local economy combines agriculture with commerce and services.
Around 650 ha of Summer–Autumn rice and rotational vegetable cultivation (about 20 ha) form
the agricultural base, supported by stable livestock activities. Commercial and service activities
meet local consumption needs and are supported by an increasing number of business registrations.
No ward-level per-capita income statistics are available yet due to the ongoing completion of post-
merger data. The ward has 15 identified poor households, and near-poor data are not specified,
indicating that pockets of vulnerability remain in an otherwise stable rural–peri-urban economy.
     (7) Gia Vien Ward
     Gia Vien Ward shows a dynamic service-oriented structure, with substantial retail sales (over
VND 24 trillion in nine months) and significant revenues from accommodation and food services,
alongside moderate industrial output. The high number of business registrations demonstrates
active private-sector development. Average per-capita income and detailed poverty indicators for
the ward are not yet officially published, as statistical work following the administrative boundary
changes is still being completed.
    (8) An Bien Ward
      In An Bien Ward, the economic profile is clearly service- and trade-dominated, reflected in
very strong budget revenues—156% of the assigned target in the first nine months of 2025—with
particularly high registration fee and fee/charge collections. This suggests high transaction activity,
vibrant real estate and business development. However, no ward-level per-capita income or formal
poverty statistics are provided in the available documents, likely because socio-economic data are
still being harmonised after the administrative merger.
    (9) Le Chan Ward
     Le Chan Ward also exhibits a strong urban, service-led economy. State budget revenue
reached 131% of the annual target in the first nine months, with especially high personal income
tax and fee/charge collections, indicating robust household incomes and active business activity.
Public investment disbursement remains modest compared to the plan, suggesting ongoing project
implementation. While no official per-capita income figure is given, the ward maintains support
for 46 near-poor households; the number of poor households is not specified, implying that poverty
is relatively limited but not entirely absent.
    (10) Dong Hai Ward
     In Dong Hai Ward, socio-economic conditions remain stable with impressive budget
performance—state budget revenue reached 155.6% of the estimate, and key revenue sources such
as non-state tax and personal income tax significantly exceeded their targets. The economic
structure is driven by industrial production, especially processing and manufacturing, supported
by steady commerce and services. Agriculture and aquaculture are declining due to land
conversion for projects. No ward-level average income or poverty data are available at this stage
due to ongoing post-merger statistical consolidation.



                                                                                                   109
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
    (11) Luu Kiem Ward
     Luu Kiem Ward has a mixed economic structure combining commerce and services,
handicrafts, construction, transportation, and a substantial agricultural base. Of 2,270 business
households, most are involved in commerce and services, while agriculture includes more than
1,000 ha of rice and other crops. This mix supports stable employment for over 1,100 workers. No
official per-capita income data or formal poverty rate are recorded in the reviewed documents;
however, social support and gift-giving to disadvantaged households indicate that while the
general living standard is stable, certain vulnerable groups still require assistance.
    (12) Hong Bang Ward
     In Hong Bang Ward, economic conditions are characterised by solid budget performance, with
state budget revenue reaching 100% of the assigned target within the first nine months of 2025.
As a central urban area, its structure is dominated by services, trade, administration and non-
agricultural activities, although specific sectoral shares are not provided. No average per-capita
income figure is available in the documents reviewed, reflecting the broader challenge of
consolidating ward-level statistics after 1 July 2025. The ward has five near-poor households; the
number of poor households is not indicated, implying very low levels of poverty.
    (13) Nam Trieu Ward
     Nam Trieu Ward presents a composite economy of commerce–services, industry,
construction, transportation and a strong agricultural and fisheries base. With 526 business
households and 131 enterprises, the ward has a broad non-agricultural economic foundation, while
agriculture and aquaculture remain significant with 342.57 ha of cultivated land and 366 ha of
aquaculture, as well as a sizeable fishing fleet. No official per-capita income statistic or ward-level
poverty rate is available in the provided data, partly due to the recent administrative merger and
ongoing statistical work, although the scale of production suggests relatively diverse income
sources with remaining pockets of vulnerability.
    (14) Hoa Binh Ward
    Hoa Binh Ward has a diversified economic structure led by industry, construction, commerce,
services and transport. With 159 enterprises, over 3,400 business households and a strong transport
union employing more than 2,000 workers, non-agricultural sectors play the leading role.
Agriculture and aquaculture remain important, with over 1,000 ha of rice and vegetables and 214
ha of aquaculture contributing to livelihoods. Unlike many other wards, Hoa Binh Ward has a
reported per-capita income of VND 104 million per person per year, meeting its planned target
and reflecting relatively high living standards. The ward maintains a 0% poverty rate and a near-
poor rate of about 0.58% (82 households), confirming that while the vast majority of households
have stable incomes, a small vulnerable group still requires targeted support.

       Social Conditions
    (1) Hai Phong City


                                                                                                   110
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Hai Phong City maintains a strong social foundation, underpinned by a stable labour force,
high education performance and a well-developed healthcare network. In 2024, the city had an
estimated population of 2.124 million people, with a balanced gender ratio and a large working-
age population, reflecting a continuing “golden population structure.” Education is one of Hai
Phong’s key strengths: the city ranks third nationwide in the education index, supported by a
system of four universities, 16 colleges and 26 vocational secondary schools. The literacy rate
reaches 97.6%, the highest in Viet Nam, and students continue to achieve strong academic results,
including 216 district-level and 97 city-level awards in the 2023–2024 school year. Enrolment for
all entry levels in the 2024–2025 school year was completed successfully, with average Grade-10
entrance scores above 8.1. At the same time, Hai Phong possesses a comprehensive healthcare
system consisting of nine city-level hospitals, 16 inpatient medical facilities, 12 preventive health
centres and functional health stations in all wards and communes, ensuring broad access to
essential healthcare, disease prevention and public health services.
     (2) An Hai Ward
    Within the project area, An Hai Ward demonstrates strong education and healthcare
performance. All children are enrolled at the correct age in preschool, primary and lower secondary
levels, and the ward continues investing in upgrading schools to national standards. Healthcare
coverage is also high, with 97.5% of residents participating in health insurance and all commune-
level health stations meeting national healthcare criteria. These indicators reflect a well-equipped
ward capable of supporting social development.
    (3) Thuy Nguyen Ward
    Thuy Nguyen Ward maintains solid progress in education and local healthcare provision.
Schools in the ward have achieved notable results, with 08 out of 17 institutions recognised as
Excellent Labour Collectives and all schools certified as Advanced Labour Collectives. Healthcare
services remain stable, with no medical incidents reported and adequate supplies for preventive
health activities. The ward currently manages health records for 11,420 children in the official
management system, indicating efficient population health monitoring.
    (4) An Duong Ward
    An Duong Ward continues to invest heavily in its education system. Children achieve a 100%
enrolment rate across all levels, supported by major school infrastructure improvements such as
new facilities for An Duong Primary School, An Duong Lower Secondary School and several
ongoing school upgrading projects. Healthcare services are equally robust, with 95.1% health
insurance coverage and active surveillance of communicable diseases including influenza, dengue
fever and seasonal respiratory illnesses. The ward enforces strict food safety inspections and
oversees 31 private medical establishments, all of which meet Good Pharmacy Practice
requirements.
    (5) Bach Dang Ward




                                                                                                 111
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
     Bach Dang Ward’s education system includes 14 public schools and one private preschool,
which are now managed directly by the Ward People’s Committee following recent administrative
adjustments. Schools have implemented enhanced fire safety and emergency response measures.
Healthcare services are delivered through the Ward Health Station, which handled 602 patient
visits in the reporting period and maintains regular immunisation and disease prevention for
dengue fever, hand-foot-mouth disease and influenza.
     (6) Ngo Quyen Ward
     Ngo Quyen Ward demonstrates strong social performance with complete enrolment at entry
levels—100% for 5-year-olds, Grade 1 and Grade 6. Healthcare access is universal, with 95.8%
health insurance participation and 100% of the population using clean water in accordance with
local standards. Solid waste collection and treatment also achieve complete coverage. These
indicators show a ward with comprehensive and well-managed social services.
     (7) Gia Vien Ward
    Gia Vien Ward remains active in improving education quality and managing population
health. The ward has enrolled 462 classes for the new school year and maintains oversight of both
public and private preschool establishments. Healthcare services are delivered through the Ward
Health Station, which provides primary healthcare to 1,471 residents while conducting regular
monitoring of dengue vectors and household sanitation.
    (8) An Bien Ward
     In An Bien Ward, the education network is extensive, including 92 public preschool classes,
over 1,500 students in non-public preschools, 163 primary classes and 127 lower secondary
classes. Healthcare services remain stable, supported by regular disease prevention campaigns,
expanded immunisation and inspection of private medical facilities. These efforts highlight a well-
organised social system serving a densely populated urban area.
    (9) Le Chan Ward
     Le Chan Ward serves as a major education hub, having recently integrated 23 public schools
formerly managed at district level. Additional preschool classes have also been transferred under
ward management. The healthcare system includes 136 private medical practice establishments
and 80 pharmaceutical outlets, supported by strong dengue prevention measures and multiple
steering committees responsible for public health and disease control.
    (10) Dong Hai Ward
    Dong Hai Ward ensures consistent delivery of essential social services. Although specific
education statistics are still being consolidated following administrative reorganisation in July
2025, the ward maintains high coverage in basic services: 100% of households use clean water,
100% of solid waste is collected and treated, and full public lighting is installed on main roads.
Local health stations provide continuous access to basic medical care.
    (11) Luu Kiem Ward


                                                                                               112
                                                                                               Official Use Only
     Luu Kiem Ward continues to upgrade its education facilities and teaching equipment, although
detailed enrolment figures are still pending due to recent administrative merger. Healthcare
initiatives focus on disease prevention and public awareness, supported by waste segregation at
source reaching nearly 65% and an established contract with a professional waste collector to
manage solid waste safely.
     (12) Hong Bang Ward
     Hong Bang Ward demonstrates strong performance in both education and healthcare. The
ward maintains 100% enrolment for preschool and entry grades, and two newly constructed
schools—So Dau Preschool and Ngo Gia Tu Primary School—now meet national standards.
Twenty smart classrooms have also been implemented. Healthcare coverage is universal, with
100% participation in social insurance and comprehensive disease prevention measures in place.
     (13) Nam Trieu Ward
     Nam Trieu Ward is strengthening its education system through the reassignment and
appointment of managerial staff at preschools, primary schools and lower secondary schools,
although full education data are still being compiled post-merger. Preventive healthcare is well
implemented, with 436 health insurance cards issued for elderly residents and extensive social
support delivered through 3,470 welfare gifts. Public health communication is also regularly
strengthened to support disease prevention.
     (14) Hoa Binh Ward
     Hoa Binh Ward maintains excellent education indicators, with all public schools meeting
National Standard Level 1 and over 45% attaining National Standard Level 2. Healthcare services
are equally strong: 95.22% of the population is covered by health insurance, all three ward-level
health stations operate 24 hours a day with adequate staffing, and disease prevention measures
have been effectively implemented. Universal access to clean water and solid waste collection
further highlight the ward’s strong social infrastructure.

2.4.   EXISTING TECHNICAL INFRASTRUCTURE
       Existing Traffic Conditions
     External transport
     The external transport network of Hai Phong City is oriented along the primary North–South
and East–West axes to enhance regional connectivity with neighbouring provinces and cities. The
North–South axis is structured around National Highway 10 and the coastal expressway/national
highway system, while the East–West axis comprises key arterial routes such as the Ha Noi – Hai
Phong Expressway, the Hai Phong – Ha Long Expressway, National Highways 18, 5, 17B and 37.
This network has received substantial investment, with a total length of approximately 120.2 km,
including major routes such as: the 33-km Ha Noi – Hai Phong Expressway (six lanes, Class-A
expressway standard); the 25-km Hai Phong – Ha Long Expressway (four lanes); National
Highway 5—the main Ha Noi–Hai Phong corridor on the Ha Noi–Kunming economic axis; the
52.5-km section of National Highway 10 within Hai Phong now undergoing upgrading and
widening; National Highway 37, serving as the northern ring-route, fully upgraded in 2023; the


                                                                                             113
                                                                                             Official Use Only
interprovincial coastal route connecting Hai Phong and Thai Binh currently under construction;
the 41.5-km National Highway 17B linking Quang Ninh and Hai Phong; and the 15.63-km Tan
Vu – Lach Huyen road and bridge—the longest sea-crossing bridge in Viet Nam and Southeast
Asia.
     Internal transport
     Regarding internal transport, Hai Phong currently has approximately 380 urban roads with a
total length of more than 300 km, forming a dense and complex network, including 33 principal
radial corridors. Road widths vary significantly across the city, ranging from large interprovincial
corridors such as National Highway 5 and the coastal route to provincial roads and urban streets
of 2–4 lanes or narrower depending on location. Many of these routes are being upgraded to Class-
I urban standards, consistent with the city’s role as a regional centre and its integration into the
national transport network. Common road types include national highways (such as National
Highway 5—the main four-lane artery connecting Hai Phong with Ha Noi and Quang Ninh—and
National Highway 10 with 2–4-lane cross-sections connecting neighbouring provinces); the
coastal route of approximately 20.9 km serving both urban and regional mobility; an expanding
provincial road system upgraded toward Class III standards; and a diverse intra-urban street
network ranging from major boulevards (Lach Tray, Pham Van Dong) to smaller streets supported
by modern bridges such as Rao Bridge and Hoang Van Thu Bridge. Major corridors and ring-roads
typically have two to four lanes or more, with median strips, sidewalks and signalling systems,
while smaller branch roads primarily provide access to residential areas.
     Despite ongoing improvements, the urban road network still exhibits limitations. Most high-
traffic routes are radially oriented toward the city centre, while ring-roads and transversal
connections remain underdeveloped. North–South connectivity across the Cam River depends
heavily on a limited number of bridges (Hoang Van Thu, Binh, Kien), creating high traffic pressure
at key gateways. Severe congestion frequently occurs along major corridors such as Nguyen Van
Linh – Nguyen Binh Khiem, Cau Dat, Lach Tray, Dien Bien Phu, To Hieu and Tran Nguyen Han,
particularly during peak hours. In 2024, the city recorded 510 road traffic accidents, 4 railway
accidents and 2 waterway accidents, resulting in 229 fatalities and 380 injuries. Although accident
numbers decreased compared to the previous year, traffic overload and conflict points at strategic
intersections remain major challenges. Overall, Hai Phong’s urban transport network has expanded
in scale but remains incomplete and lacks adequate ring-road and cross-link connections,
generating significant pressure on existing radial routes.
     Waterway transport
     In terms of waterway transport, Hai Phong benefits from a dense river system within the Red
River – Thai Binh basin, including major rivers such as Cam, Bach Dang, Lach Tray, Van Uc and
Luoc, together with interconnected tributaries. These rivers form vital inland waterway corridors
linking Hai Phong’s major seaports with Quang Ninh, Hai Duong, Hung Yen, Thai Binh and other
provinces in the Northern Delta, creating key transport axes such as the Quang Ninh – Hai Phong
– Ha Noi – Viet Tri corridor. These waterways play an essential role in bulk cargo transport,




                                                                                                114
                                                                                                Official Use Only
connecting industrial zones and port complexes with inland regions, easing pressure on road
transport and contributing significantly to regional economic development.
     Traffic system around the project area
     Component 1:
     Construction access in Dong Hai Ward is primarily supported by the Ha Noi–Hai Phong
Expressway (CT.04) via its frontage roads, together with Bui Vien and Dang Kinh Streets, which
connect from the expressway overpass to local residential areas and the work sites of the
Expressway Overpass. Access to Ngo Quyen Bridge on the Dong Hai side is directly from National
Highway 5, while the Vu Yen side is reached via May Chai Bridge, Vu Yen 1 Bridge and the
internal road network of the Vin Royal Island urban area.
    On Vu Yen Island, access is facilitated through a well-developed internal road system (7–12
m wide) and the two main connections from the mainland—May Chai Bridge and Vu Yen 1
Bridge—which serve the construction of both Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge. For Ruot
Lon Bridge on the Vu Yen side, transportation is mainly through internal roads combined with the
Vu Yen 1 Bridge.
     In Nam Trieu, the alignment passes through agricultural and aquaculture areas, where access
relies on existing village roads and dike roads with limited widths (3–5 m), suitable mainly for
light trucks. Where the project corridor approaches National Highway 5 or Road 359, these major
routes can also be used to support access.
     In the areas of Thuy Nguyen, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh and Luu Kiem, the alignment traverses
established residential zones where local streets are narrow (4–6 m) and flanked by housing,
requiring careful traffic coordination. Additional access is provided by key regional routes such as
Tran Hung Dao Road, Road 359 and nearby sections of National Highway 5. For the Thuy Nguyen
side of Ruot Lon Bridge, access is via longitudinal service roads being graded within the project
ROW.

     For Component 3, construction activities are concentrated along existing urban streets in the
city centre, including Hoang Dieu – Nguyen Tri Phuong, Dien Bien Phu, Cau Dat, Nguyen Duc
