Tam Bac, Tam Bac – Hung Vuong, Phan Dinh Phung, The Lu and Ha Ly. These are high-density
urban corridors with heavy traffic and extensive residential and commercial activities. Traffic
management measures—such as barricading, lane narrowing, night-time construction and
restricted road occupation—must therefore be carefully planned and closely coordinated with local
authorities to ensure project progress while minimising congestion and disturbance to residents
and businesses.

       Existing Irrigation and Drainage Conditions
     In Component 1, the project area extends across Dong Hai, Thuy Nguyen, Nam Trieu, Hoa
Binh, Luu Kiem, and Bach Dang wards, where stormwater drainage conditions vary according to
existing land use. In Dong Hai Ward, areas adjacent to the Hanoi–Hai Phong Expressway and the



                                                                                              117
                                                                                              Official Use Only
Hai An Port area are served by a coordinated stormwater drainage system, collecting runoff
through roadside drains and discharging to the Cam River and nearby surface water bodies. Within
the Vinhomes Royal Island Urban Area in Thuy Nguyen Ward, the stormwater drainage system
has been constructed as part of the urban development project. In Nam Trieu, Hoa Binh, Luu Kiem,
and Bach Dang wards, agricultural and aquaculture areas rely on existing field drainage canals and
irrigation networks, while sections passing through residential areas and along Road 359, National
Highway 10, and National Highway 5 are equipped with stormwater drainage systems integrated
with the road infrastructure.
     For Component 2, drainage within the project area is associated with the Re River and related
structures of the An Hai drainage system, including the Cai Tat Canal and the An Kim Hai Canal.
Stormwater and surface runoff are conveyed through existing drains and canals and discharged
into the Re River. Along sections passing through An Duong Ward and Hong Bang Ward, the
drainage system has been established as part of the area-wide drainage network.
     In Component 3, project activities are carried out along existing urban roads, where
stormwater drainage has been constructed in the form of underground pipes and covered drains,
connected to the general drainage network of the city centre and operating in association with the
existing road infrastructure.

       Existing Power Supply
     In Component 1, the project area covers Thuy Nguyen, Dong Hai, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Luu
Kiem, and part of Nam Trieu wards, where electricity supply coverage varies by land use. In
existing residential areas, areas adjacent to major transport infrastructure, and within the Vinhomes
Royal Island Urban Area (Thuy Nguyen Ward), the electricity supply network is well established,
with high but not complete coverage provided through existing medium- and low-voltage
distribution lines. Electricity for project implementation will be sourced from the national power
grid, through the construction of new transformer stations for construction purposes,
complemented by temporary power solutions in locations where the existing grid does not extend
to construction sites. Following project completion, the newly constructed electrical infrastructure
will be utilised for road lighting and project facilities during operation.
     For Component 2, project activities are mainly located along Provincial Road 208, where the
electricity supply network has been developed in parallel with the road corridor and adjacent
residential areas. The area is served by existing medium- and low-voltage lines and transformer
stations, indicating a high level of electricity supply coverage along most sections of the alignment.
     In Component 3, construction works are carried out along inner-city roads in the central urban
area, where the electricity supply system is fully developed. The area is characterised by a
complete and continuous electricity distribution network, with dense coverage of distribution lines
and transformer stations along urban streets, allowing direct access to grid electricity for
construction and temporary power needs.



                                                                                                  118
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
       Existing Water Supply
     In Component 1, the project area covers Thuy Nguyen, Dong Hai, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Luu
Kiem, and part of Nam Trieu wards, where water supply coverage varies by land use. In existing
residential areas and within the Vinhomes Royal Island Urban Area, the domestic water supply
network has been installed with a high level of coverage, allowing existing pipelines to be used
for construction water demand. In contrast, in sparsely populated sections such as the Ruot Lon
Bridge area, as well as agricultural and aquaculture areas, water supply coverage is low or absent,
and water provision relies on temporary solutions such as water tankers or localized temporary
pipelines.
    For Component 2, project activities are mainly located along Provincial Road 208, where the
municipal water supply system has been developed in association with the road corridor and
adjacent residential areas. The area is characterised by a relatively high level of water supply
coverage, with existing distribution pipelines available along most sections of the alignment,
enabling water supply for construction and temporary facilities through existing service points or
temporary connections.
     In Component 3, construction works are carried out along inner-city roads in the central urban
area, where the water supply system has been developed in a coordinated manner. Water supply
coverage in this area is high and continuous, with a dense distribution pipeline network along urban
streets, allowing water for construction activities, temporary facilities, and workers’ use to be
supplied through the existing municipal water supply system.

       Solid Waste Management
    Municipal solid waste
     Hai Phong generates around 2,010 tons/day of municipal solid waste, including 1,030 tons/day
in urban areas and 980 tons/day in rural areas, of which roughly 970 tons/day is collected. Urban
waste is collected and transported by four service providers and treated centrally at the Trang Cat
and Dinh Vu complexes, achieving a 100% urban collection rate. Trang Cat operates sanitary
landfills and a composting line (70–100 tons/day), while Dinh Vu receives 300–450 tons/day with
remaining capacity of about 292,000 tons. Despite extensive citywide campaigns promoting
segregation at source, treatment still relies heavily on landfilling and faces increasing pressure
from rising waste volumes.
     Hazardous waste
     Hazardous waste in Hai Phong is primarily generated from industrial zones, healthcare
facilities and transportation services. Collection and treatment are undertaken by licensed units,
including Hai Phong Urban Environment Company (URENCO HP), MTV Environment and
Infrastructure JSC, and privately licensed operators such as VIMEXCOM, Thanh Nga
Environmental Services, and Green Earth Co. Hazardous industrial waste is transported in sealed
vehicles to regional treatment facilities—most commonly the VIMEXCOM Hazardous Waste
Treatment Centre (Trang Cat) and off-city facilities in Hung Yen, Hai Duong and Quang Ninh.


                                                                                                119
                                                                                                Official Use Only
Healthcare waste is segregated at source and treated by on-site autoclaves or transported to the
URENCO HP incineration line. Despite improved compliance, challenges remain: incomplete
segregation, periodic illegal dumping at peri-urban sites, and limited on-site storage capacity,
highlighting the need for expanded licensed treatment capacity and stronger regulatory oversight.

       Wastewater Drainage
     Hai Phong is gradually expanding its wastewater treatment infrastructure, although the current
system still falls short of urban needs. The Vinh Niem Wastewater Treatment Plant has a design
capacity of 36,000 m³/day and is planned to be expanded to 72,000 m³/day in Phase 2 under the
city’s 2020–2050 development roadmap, but it has not yet been put into operation. Other existing
facilities include the Trang Minh Plant (1,500 m³/day) and the Cat Ba Island Plant (2,800 m³/day).
Meanwhile, most urban and peri-urban areas continue to discharge domestic wastewater—after
basic septic tank pre-treatment—into combined sewers and directly into rivers and canals. As a
result, major receiving waters such as the Re, Da Do and Hon Ngoc Rivers have become
increasingly polluted.
     According to the city’s urban development planning, by 2025 Hai Phong must treat 505,000
m³/day of urban wastewater. To comply with Grade I urban standards—which require 40–50% of
wastewater to be properly treated—the city has approved the Urban Renovation Scheme 2020–
2025, including an investment of over VND 24 trillion to upgrade the drainage system. Of this,
VND 9.4 trillion is allocated specifically for stormwater and wastewater projects in two phases.
The 2020–2025 phase focuses on drainage improvement in Do Son, Kien An and Duong Kinh,
and the construction or upgrading of major wastewater treatment plants such as Bac Song Cam
WTP, Vinh Niem WTP (Phase 2) and Hoang Mai WTP (An Duong ward). The 2025–2030 phase
will continue with new centralised treatment plants, including Ho Dong (Hai An ward), Quan Tru
(Kien An ward) and Hai Thanh (Duong Kinh ward).
    In project area
     Within the project area—especially Component 2—there is currently no dedicated sewerage
system along the Re River. Wastewater from thousands of households in Hong Phong, Tan Tien,
Nam Son, An Duong Town and An Dong is discharged through local canals, culverts and sluices
directly into the Re River, generating an estimated 9,000 m³/day of untreated domestic wastewater.
    Major wastewater discharges into the Re River originate from multiple residential clusters
across Hong Phong, Tan Tien, Nam Son, An Duong Town and An Dong Commune, with a
combined untreated flow of approximately 9,000 m³/day. In Hong Phong Commune, Ho Dong
Hamlet contributes around 400 m³/day from 950 households. Tan Tien Commune has three
discharge points: Nong Xa Hamlet, where a craft-village treatment station handles 135 m³/day; the
Tan Tien Dam area, where wastewater is pumped via the Do Nha Station into drainage canals; and
Kinh Giao Hamlet, which discharges about 200 m³/day from 484 households and a local market.
In Nam Son Commune, Quynh Hoang Hamlet releases an estimated 500 m³/day from 450
households, while Luong Quan and My Tranh Hamlets discharge wastewater through three


                                                                                               120
                                                                                               Official Use Only
additional outfalls including Cong Gao Sluice, serving 521 households. An Duong Town
contributes two major flows: about 400 m³/day from the Hospital Canal Outfall serving 1,100
households, and 450 m³/day from Ba Go Sluice serving 1,150 households. In An Dong Commune,
Ba Nhi Sluice receives wastewater from 750 households, Bay Mau Sluice discharges
approximately 350 m³/day from 355 households, and the outfall near Cai Tat Market releases about
700 m³/day from 850 households.

2.5.   SENSITIVE RECEPTORS & PCR
       Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure
       Development
     Ring road No. 3 is a newly constructed urban arterial road forming a major transport axis in
the northern peri-urban belt of Hai Phong City. The alignment is approximately 18.68 km in length,
with a right-of-way width of 50–68 m, starting from a direct connection with the Ha Noi–Hai
Phong Expressway at the western end and extending eastward through Nam Trieu, Hoa Binh and
Bach Đang wards. Designed to modern urban standards for mixed traffic, the corridor traverses a
transitional landscape from rural farmland in the west to dense linear urban development with
continuous residential frontage in the east. Along the alignment, Road No. 3 crosses the Cam River
at Km 3+200 via the Ngo Quyen Bridge, a major tidal navigation bridge approximately 32 m wide
and 2,106.9 m long and spans the Ruot Lon River at Km 6+100 via the Ruot Lon Bridge,
approximately 32 m wide and 430 m long.
     Road construction will require the permanent acquisition of 684,000 m² of land including:
including (1) 289,400 m² of annual crop land, (2) 8,800 m² of perennial crop land, (3) 176,800 m²
of aquaculture land (including 16.32 ha of shrimp pond land in Nam Trieu Ward, from Km 6+500
to Km 8+900), (4) 7,580 m² of protection forest land, (5) 46,300 m² of residential land, (6) 1,170
m² of non-agricultural production and business land, (7) 2,930 m² of land for public facilities, (8)
331,000 m² of transport and irrigation land, (9) 4,890 m² of religious and belief land, (10) 25,870
m² of cemetery land, (11) 75,380 m² of specialized water surface, and (12) 660 m² of unused land.
The number of affected households and organizations is 2,007 households and 12 organizations,
of which 181 households are required to be relocated .
     The initial section from Km 0 – Km 6+500 is characterized by transport and hydraulic
intersections. Following the connection with the Hanoi–Hai Phong Expressway, the alignment
intersects National Highway No. 5 (Km 2+300) before crossing the Cam River via the Ngo Quyen
Bridge (Km 3+200). The Cam River is a major waterway with heavy commercial navigation
serving the downstream Hai An Port (3.0–3.5 km distance), making navigation clearance a critical
safety consideration. Proceeding northward, the route crosses the Ruot Lon River (Km 6+100).
This area is defined by a mosaic of agricultural land, aquaculture ponds, and a contiguous strip of
planted protection forest (Sonneratia caseolaris) approximately 1.2 ha in area, which functions as
a buffer for bank stabilization and erosion control.



                                                                                                121
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     Between the major bridge crossings (Figure 2.6 and 2.7) and residential clusters, specifically
sections Km 6+500 – Km 8+900 and Km 11+900 – Km 12+600, the corridor traverses low-lying
terrain dominated by rice cultivation and household-managed brackish aquaculture ponds. In these
sections, the road design elevation is approximately 1.0 m higher than the surrounding ground
level to meet flood protection standards, altering local drainage patterns.
    From Km 9+500 onwards, the alignment bisects established communities in Nam Trieu, Bach
Dang, and Hoa Binh wards. Settlement patterns vary from the scattered mixed housing of the Lap
Le cluster (Km 9+500 – Km 10+500) to the dense, continuous residential frontage found in the
Ngu Lao (Km 13+000 – Km 14+100) and Trung Ha (Km 15+000 – Km 15+500) areas. In these
dense sections, masonry "tube houses" and small businesses are located within 10–20 m of the
Right-of-Way (ROW), creating a receptor environment highly sensitive to construction vibration,
noise, and access disruption.

irrigation systems, including the Hau Long River (Km 12+000) (Figure 2.8) and Chu River (Km
16+090) (Figure 2.9), which receive domestic and agricultural runoff. The alignment terminates
at the intersection with National Highway 10 (Km 18+650), a high-traffic node where existing 5–
6 low-voltage distribution lines and utility infrastructure will require management during the
construction phase.

       Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement
     Component 2 comprises two subcomponents. Subcomponent 2.1 focuses on the rehabilitation
and upgrading of the Re River infrastructure, covering an approximately 9.5 km section from Re
2 Bridge (at the intersection with National Highway No. 10) to the Cai Tat sluice gate. The scope
of works includes the construction of a river management road along the Re River, rehabilitation
of existing embankments, and construction of additional river embankments in selected sections
(Figure 2.10). Subcomponent 2.2 aims to improve environmental sanitation in the Re River basin
through the construction of a sewer network along both riverbanks to collect wastewater currently
discharged directly into the river and convey it to the new An Duong 1 wastewater treatment plant
for treatment.




        Figure 2.11. Location of Quan Vinh Pumping station and embankment types
     The total land acquisition area is approximately 744,500 m² (of which the WWTP site
accounts for 2.99 ha), including (1) 97,600 m² of annual crop land, (2) 2,010 m² of perennial crop
land, (3) 15,420 m² of aquaculture land, (4) 2,790 m² of residential land, (5) 1,820 m² of non-
agricultural production and business land, (6) 13,180 m² of land for public facilities, (7) 334,220
m² of transport and irrigation land, (8) 1,370 m² of religious and belief land, (9) 240 m² of cemetery
land, (10) 416,350 m² of specialized water surface, and (11) 5,300 m² of unused land. The number
of affected households and organizations is 426 households and 15 organizations, and no
households are required to be relocated.
    Component 2 focuses on the environmental rehabilitation of the Re River corridor. The project
footprint extends approximately 13.77 km along the riverbanks through An Duong, An Bien, and



                                                                                                  127
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
Hong Bang wards, comprising embankment upgrades, management road construction, and the
installation of wastewater interceptors. The Re River supports typical lowland urban aquatic
habitats dominated by pollution-tolerant species, with no recorded rare or endangered biodiversity.
     A defining sensitivity of this corridor is the Quan Vinh raw-water intake pumping station
(located near the downstream section). This facility abstracts water directly from the Re River to
supply the An Duong Supply Water Treatment Plant. The intake is located approximately 200 m
downstream of proposed Neoweb embankment sections and 250 m upstream of stone revetment
works. Consequently, the river reach is highly sensitive to turbidity, sedimentation, and accidental
spills during construction, requiring stringent pollution controls. From National Highway 10 to the
Cai Tat Culvert, the alignment traverses a heavily modified landscape characterized by dense,
continuous linear settlements. The corridor passes through the Le Loi, An Dong (An Duong Ward),
and So Dau (Hong Bang Ward) residential clusters. These areas feature rows of 1–3 storey
masonry "tube houses" and mixed-use shopfronts located within 7–50 m of the embankment
footprint.
     In these residential sections, existing ground levels (typically +2.0m to +3.0m) are generally
higher than or equal to the designed road elevation, ensuring the project does not create an intrusive
elevated barrier relative to homes. The corridor is bisected by high-traffic intersections (e.g., Road
208) and is adjacent to sensitive social infrastructure, including the Re Market (~35 m from the
road) and several kindergartens (e.g., Be Thong Minh, Do Re Mon, Trung Ha) located within 150–
300 m of the construction zone. The riparian corridor contains a high density of religious and
cultural sites sensitive to vibration, noise, and access disruption. Key receptors located within 0–
30 m of the works include the An Tri Pagoda, Quynh Cu Pagoda, and the Pham Tu Nghi Shrine;
in some instances, the management road alignment traverses the garden yards of these facilities...
Additionally, the project impacts the An Duong Cemetery, requiring the relocation of
approximately 29 graves situated within the embankment right-of-way. The locations and
distances of these sensitive receptors are summarized in (Table 2.2).
     The proposed An Duong 1 WWTP is sited on 2.99 ha of agricultural land in An Hai Ward,
distinct from the dense riverbank settlements. This area is dominated by annual cropland and
aquaculture ponds, with the nearest residential cluster (Hoang Mai) located approximately 550 m
away, ensuring a substantial buffer zone. The receiving body for treated effluent is the Lach Tray
River, a major tidal watercourse (200–300 m wide) currently located approximately 6–8 km
upstream of the estuary. The water quality of the Lach Tray River shows signs of pollution, with
several parameters exceeding applicable standards at certain monitoring locations and during
specific sampling periods. In particular, BOD₅, COD, TSS, and ammonium concentrations have
exceeded the B1 limits of QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT (Column B1), primarily due to upstream
urban runoff as well as domestic and industrial wastewater discharges. These industrial discharges
are mainly associated with small- and medium-scale facilities and workshops, such as mechanical
repair services, warehouses, and port-related activities, which typically discharge indirectly
through the municipal drainage system. The river reach represents a highly modified urban aquatic



                                                                                                  128
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
environment, characterized by limited riparian vegetation and the absence of protected or sensitive
aquatic habitats, as confirmed by baseline surveys. Aquatic communities are dominated by
common freshwater–estuarine species that are tolerant of turbid and nutrient-enriched conditions.
Hydraulically, the river is influenced by tidal exchange and seasonal flows, resulting in fluctuating
water levels and flow velocities. The river serves multiple functions, including urban drainage and
navigation, with regular but low- to moderate-intensity traffic of small cargo barges and service
vessels. All construction materials and waste associated with the An Duong 1 WWTP will be
transported by road.
         Table 2.2. Consolidated Sensitive Receptors and PCRs under Component 2

       Component 3 – City Center Flood Reduction
     The Component 3 comprises: (i) construction of two new sluice gates (Figure 2.12) at the
junctions of the Tam Bac River with the Thuong Ly and Cam rivers, converting the river reach
into a 10 ha regulation lake to meet increasing stormwater retention needs. A new box culvert
connecting to the existing Tam Bac Lake will expand the total water surface area around 14-15 ha
³. A pumping station with a design capacity of 12 m³/s will be constructed to optimize stormwater
evacuation to the Cam River; and (ii) upgrading of about 4.2 km of primary stormwater drains
with cross-sections ranging from 1.0 × 1.0 m to 3.0 × 2.0 m. The component also includes
associated urban drainage and wastewater collection works along the Tam Bac corridor, consisting
of approximately 2.6 km of HDPE sewers (D300–D500), wastewater separation chambers, 06
pumping stations (including the upgrading of the existing Lan Be station), and tertiary sewers for
household connections, to enhance stormwater conveyance and urban flood control.




                                                                                               132
                                                                                               Official Use Only
     No permanent land acquisition is required. No households or organizations are permanently
affected by the Project and no resettlement is required.
    The Tam Bac River is a highly modified urban watercourse serving as the primary drainage
and flood-control channel for the city center. The river, with a width of approximately 50–90 m,
supports limited aquatic habitat dominated by pollution-tolerant species and has negligible
navigation function. Navigation along this reach is effectively constrained by the Tam Bac Bridge,
which prevents through traffic. Consequently, no regular commercial or transport navigation takes
place in this section, and only a small number of local boats are occasionally present for temporary
mooring and access by nearby residents.
     Two sluice gates are proposed: the Tam Bac Sluice at the upstream confluence with the
Thuong Ly River, located in a long-established inner-city area with continuous residential and
mixed-use development and reinforced riverbanks; and the Thuy Doi Sluice at the downstream
junction with the Cam River, located in dense residential neighborhoods with narrow plots and
limited access along 10–15 m-wide riverbank roads. The associated drainage and wastewater
collection network, comprising about 4.2 km of stormwater drains and 2.6 km of sewers, runs
through the high-density urban core of Hong Bang, Ngo Quyen, and Le Chan districts, following
major corridors such as Nguyen Thai Hoc, Dien Bien Phu, and Tam Bac streets, where buildings
are typically located only 4–10 m from the construction alignment. This creates a highly sensitive
receptor environment to construction-related vibration, dust, and access disruption. Given the
constrained urban setting, high traffic volumes, and dense underground utilities, suistable
construction methods will be applied to minimize surface disturbance to surrounding residential
and commercial areas.
     Tam Bac Lake, which is integrated into the regulation system through a new connecting box
culvert, serves both as a critical retention basin and as a cultural landmark of the city. Its perimeter
is defined by paved embankments, pedestrian walkways, and landscaped areas that function as
high-value public open spaces for daily recreation. The adjacent “The Lu” food walking street and
riverside promenades attract intensive pedestrian use, particularly in the evenings and on
weekends, requiring strict control of construction encroachment and visual impacts. Existing
technical infrastructure within the component corridor includes urban drainage pipes, combined
sewers, low-voltage power lines, street lighting, and municipal water supply pipelines running
along both riverbanks and crossing the river at bridge locations. According to the FS and the
Resettlement Plan (RP), these utilities will not be permanently relocated; any interaction with
existing infrastructure will be temporary and limited to the construction phase.
    The component corridor also contains a high concentration of sensitive social and cultural
receptors located within approximately 10–120 m of the works, including major healthcare and
educational facilities such as Viet Tiep Friendship Hospital and Gold Star General Hospital (10–
15 m from the alignment), which require uninterrupted emergency access, and Nguyen Tri Phuong
Primary School and Le Hong Phong High School (7–100 m from the alignment), which are
sensitive to noise during teaching hours. In addition, several active religious sites including Our


                                                                                                    133
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
Lady of Carmel Church (10–15 m from the alignment), Tam Ky Temple, and Tam Bac Pagoda
(approximately 30 m from the sluice) are located along the corridor and regularly host
congregations and festivals, making them particularly sensitive to construction-related noise and
vibration. The locations and distances of sensitive receptors of this component are summarised in
the Table 2.3

                        NATURAL, ENVIRONMENTAL AND
            SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS OF PROJECT AREA
     Analysis of project alternatives is a key stage of the environmental and social assessment. This
includes assessing the “with-project” and “without-project” scenarios and comparing design
options to support decision-making.

3.1.      WITH-PROJECT AND WITHOUT-PROJECT SCENARIOS
    The Project aligns with Hai Phong City's Master Plan to 2040 and supports green growth and
climate adaptation.
       Positive impacts under the With-Project scenario include:
   -      Improved transport connectivity;
   -      Economic development benefits;
   -      Flood reduction and enhanced drainage;
   -      Improved wastewater management and water quality;
   -      Enhanced urban resilience.
     Without the Project, flooding, poor sanitation, and weak connectivity persist, limiting
sustainable development.
    The environmental and social implications of the two scenarios are compared across multiple
dimensions.

            consistency with the current profile and reduced construction disturbance.

                                                                                              144
                                                                                              Official Use Only
                                ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT
                                     ASSESSMENT
     The impact assessment in this Chapter is structured by project components, consistent with
the project design and the arrangement of physical interventions. The chapter evaluates impacts at
the Component level (Components 1–3) and across the different project phases—Preparation,
Construction, and Operation—to ensure alignment with the project’s environmental and social
footprint.

   4.1.    Positive Environmental and Social Impacts of the Project
    This chapter assesses the positive environmental and social impacts that will result from the
implementation of the Hai Phong Urban Infrastructure and Environment Improvement Project.
The Project is expected to bring significant improvements to flood control, drainage, sanitation,
and environmental quality in the city’s central and western areas. The analysis below summarizes
the main benefits associated with each component during both construction and operation phases,
as well as overall socio-economic gains.

   4.1.1. Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban
          Infrastructure Development
      Component 1, consisting of the construction of Ring Road No. 3 together with Ngo Quyen
Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge, will generate substantial long-term socio-economic and
environmental benefits for Hai Phong City and the surrounding region. The strategic alignment of
Ring Road No. 3 creates a new high-capacity mobility corridor connecting the Ha Noi–Hai Phong
Expressway with National Highway No. 10, significantly improving urban connectivity between
Thuy Nguyen, Hai An, Hong Bang, and An Duong districts. This increased connectivity reduces
pressure on existing overloaded urban roads, shortens travel time, and improves logistics
efficiency for industrial zones located in the northern and eastern growth areas of the city.
      The construction of new river-crossing structures—Ngo Quyen Bridge over the Cam River
and Ruot Lon Bridge over the Ruot Lon River—provides critical missing links that enhance
regional accessibility and reduce detours of 8–12 km currently required to cross major waterways.
By integrating these new bridges with the urban road network, the project enhances resilience of
emergency access routes, facilitates movement during flood events, and supports expansion of
public transport services along the new corridor.
      Environmental benefits are also expected due to improved stormwater drainage and road-side
utilities integrated into the design. The installation of reinforced concrete box culverts and lighting
systems reduces local flooding risk along the new alignment and enhances road safety, especially
at intersections. The project also contributes to climate-resilient urban development by creating a
modern transport corridor designed to withstand increased rainfall, higher temperatures, and more
intense storm events under future climate scenarios included in the final FS(2026).



                                                                                                   145
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Overall, Component 1 will create substantial long-term positive impacts through improved
transport efficiency, reduced congestion, enhanced urban connectivity, and strengthened resilience
of critical mobility infrastructure serving both existing and future urban development in Hai
Phong.

   4.1.2. Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement
     Component 2 brings some of the most transformative environmental benefits of the project,
as it directly addresses long-standing problems of riverbank erosion, untreated wastewater
discharge, and low-capacity drainage systems across the Re River catchment. The construction
and rehabilitation of approximately 9.67 km of Re River embankment will significantly improve
riverbank stability, reduce erosion, and protect adjacent households, agricultural land, and local
infrastructure that are currently exposed to seasonal scouring and bank failures. The embankment
design—using Neoweb slopes, reinforced concrete walls, and stone revetments—follows the
cross-sections in the FS and is expected to markedly improve public safety during the rainy season.
     The management roads on both riverbanks (13.74 km) will further enhance long-term river
corridor accessibility and facilitate maintenance of flood-control infrastructure. These roads also
provide auxiliary social benefits by improving access for local residents, emergency services, and
future riverside recreational development.
     The wastewater collection system (3 route main sewers including 1a, 1b and 2; 12 pumping
stations; 12 separation chambers; and 8.42 km tertiary sewers) will greatly reduce direct discharge
of untreated wastewater into the Re River, thereby improving water quality and environmental
conditions along the entire corridor. By intercepting household wastewater, the project reduces
organic pollution, suspended solids, nutrients, oil and grease, and coliform contamination—
parameters that as exceeding national standards at several monitoring points.
     The construction of An Duong 1 WWTP (Phase 1) with a capacity of 10,000 m³/day
introduces essential wastewater treatment infrastructure for a rapidly urbanizing region currently
lacking centralized sanitation services. Effluent meeting QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT (Column A)
will markedly improve downstream water quality in the Lach Tray River and contribute to
achieving national and city-level water-environment targets.
     A further significant positive impact concerns the improvement of raw-water quality in the Re
River, which is the primary intake source for the An Duong Water Treatment Plant. According to
the city water-supply planning documents, the Re River is designated as a major raw-water source
for the An Duong and Vat Cach Water Plants, with a combined supply capacity of 340,000 m³/day,
serving more than 1 million residents and industrial enterprises in Hai Phong.
     At present, water quality in the Re River is degraded due to approximately 430 discharge
points, including wastewater from ~720 households located along both riverbanks that discharge
untreated wastewater directly into the river. The interception and conveyance of these pollution
sources to the An Duong 1 WWTP will significantly reduce organic load, suspended solids,
nutrients, and microbiological contamination entering the raw-water intake area.



                                                                                               146
                                                                                               Official Use Only
     As a result, Component 2 will yield a direct and measurable improvement in raw-water quality
for the An Duong Water Treatment Plant, thereby enhancing the reliability, stability, and long-
term safety of potable water supply for a large portion of Hai Phong’s population. This effect
extends beyond environmental restoration to broader public-health benefits by reducing treatment
burden, lowering risks of episodic contamination, and improving resilience under future climate
scenarios where pollutant loads could otherwise intensify.

   4.1.3. Component 3 – City Center Flood Reduction
     Component 3 provides direct and substantial benefits for flood reduction in the central urban
area of Hai Phong, particularly within the highly urbanized Tam Bac basin. The construction of
two regulating sluices (Tam Bac Bridge and Mui Thuy Doi) combined with a 12 m³/s pumping
station creates a robust flood-control system capable of reducing inundation frequency and
duration during storm events. This system transforms Tam Bac Lake and the newly formed 10.2-
ha regulating lake into a combined 14–15 ha flood-retention complex, which helps attenuate
stormwater peaks, reduce downstream hydraulic overload, and minimize urban street flooding.
     The project also includes significant improvements to the wastewater interception system
along the Tam Bac River, including 2.712 km of HDPE D300-D500 sewers, 16 separation
chambers, 5 new construction wastewater pumping stations and the rehabilitation of Lan Be
Pumping Station. These facilities help convey approximately 6,000 m³/day of wastewater to the
Vinh Niem WWTP instead of discharging directly into the river, thus improving the water quality
of Tam Bac River and surrounding canals. Better water quality contributes to enhanced
environmental conditions, reduced odor, improved aesthetics, and improved public health for
communities living along the riverbanks.
     The urban stormwater drainage network in the Tam Bac basin—approximately 4.252 km of
reinforced concrete culverts—is designed for a 5-year rainfall return period, replacing old,
undersized, or degraded drainage structures. The upgraded network is expected to significantly
reduce localized flooding along key streets such as Dien Bien Phu, Cau Dat, Nguyen Duc Canh,
Lan Ong, Hoang Dieu, Nguyen Tri Phuong, Ly Tu Trong, Hai Ba Trung, and Tran Nguyen Han.
Reduced flooding will improve traffic safety, protect household properties, and lower maintenance
costs for urban infrastructure.
     Together, the regulating sluices, pumping station, regulating lakes, wastewater interception
network, and upgraded drainage system produce a comprehensive and integrated flood-
management solution that directly supports Hai Phong’s climate resilience and urban
environmental quality goals.

   4.1.4. Cross-cutting long-term benefits of the whole Project
     Across all components, the project delivers strong cumulative environmental and social
benefits aligned with Hai Phong’s long-term development strategy. By combining modern
transport infrastructure, riverbank protection, wastewater treatment, and flood-control systems, the
project enhances the city’s resilience to climate change, improves public health, and strengthens



                                                                                                147
                                                                                                Official Use Only
the ecological quality of key waterways. Reduced flooding, reduced pollution loads, and stabilized
riverbanks create a safer and more attractive urban environment, supporting tourism, economic
activities, and improved living standards.
     The project also contributes to employment generation during construction, offers
opportunities for local suppliers, and lays the foundation for future investments in riverside
revitalization, green spaces, and urban redevelopment areas along the Re and Tam Bac rivers.
Collectively, these benefits position Hai Phong for cleaner, safer, and more sustainable urban
growth.

    4.2.    Impacts during Preparation Phase
     The main impacts during the preparation phase include (1) land acquisition and the related
social consequences on livelihood, permanent physical and economic displacement, business
activities and relocation of graves and (2) safety risks related to unexploded ordnances (UXOs).
The impacts due to dust and solid waste from site clearance will be mainly generated in
construction phase and will be presented in the sections of impacts during construction phase.

    4.2.1. Land acquisition & resettlement impacts
     The Project requires permanent land acquisition of 1,975,297.5 m3, across multiple communes,
affecting residential, agricultural, aquaculture, public, and special-use lands, as well as a small area
of protection forest (Table 4.1). A total of 2,433 households experience land, structure, livelihood,
or business impacts, and these are summarized in Table 4.2.

    4.2.1.1. Impact on Relocated Households
     Permanent acquisition of residential land and houses results in the physical displacement of
181 households, primarily under Components 1. Relocation disrupts established settlement
patterns, kinship ties, neighbourhood cohesion, and daily access to schools, health services, and
markets. Transitional difficulties may arise during the shift to new living environments, especially
for vulnerable households such as the elderly, poor, and women-headed families. These impacts
can be mitigated through replacement-cost compensation, resettlement support, and assurance that
resettlement sites are equipped with adequate infrastructure.
                 Table 4.2. Summary of Affected and Relocated Households
   Component               Affected HHs                HHs with affected        Relocated HHs
                                                       houses/structures
 Component 1       2,007                            230                       181
 Component 2       426                              91                        0
 Component 3       ~420 (mostly businesses; –                                 0
                   temporary impacts)
 TOTAL             2,433                            321                       181
                           Source: Consolidated land acquisition dataset (Dec 2025); drafted RP.
     Relocated households may opt for self-relocation or resettlement in one of two city
resettlement areas available for the project:
    1. Nam Trieu Ward Resettlement Area (13.54 ha, 536 lots – planned)
    2. Bach Dang Ward Resettlement Area (19.98 ha, 887 lots – under development)
     These sites were reviewed through environmental and social due diligence, confirming that
they will provide complete infrastructure—including water supply, drainage, electricity, and
access to schools, markets and health stations within short distances (0.5–2 km). This ensures that
relocated households can maintain essential daily activities.
     Although relocation causes temporary inconvenience, these impacts are mitigable through
replacement-cost compensation, transportation support, rental assistance for households needing
temporary accommodation, and self-relocation support. The impact significance is assessed as
Substantial.



                                                                                                149
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    4.2.1.2. Impact on Livelihoods
     The Project will permanently acquire approximately 1,975,297.5 m2 of land of different
categories as presented in Table 4.1, including residential land, agricultural land (paddy, annual
and perennial crops), aquaculture ponds, public land and a small area of protection forest. Land
acquisition of this scale will affect the livelihoods of households whose income and daily activities
depend on land-based production or land-related business.
     According to the socio-economic information consolidated in the RP, 2,433 households are
affected by various types of land losses, structures, trees, crops or business activities. Among these,
a large proportion lose agricultural or aquaculture land which contributes to household subsistence
and income security. In addition, seven ward-level authorities experience loss of public land and
unused land under their management. 27 organizations are also affected by loss of non-agricultural
land, structures or trees.
     A significant number of households will face livelihood challenges due to reduced agricultural
production. Loss of paddy fields, perennial crops and aquaculture ponds will directly reduce
income and indirectly affect household living conditions, particularly in peri-urban communes
such as Nam Trieu, Hoa Binh and Dong Hai, where many households still rely on rice cultivation
and aquaculture as regular sources of income. Although households will receive compensation at
replacement cost for the acquired land, the transition away from agricultural activities may be
difficult for groups with limited skills, elderly household heads, women-headed households, and
low-income families.
     In urban wards where land acquisition is more scattered, affected households generally rely
less on agriculture and have greater ability to shift to wage labour or service-sector jobs.
Nevertheless, livelihood impacts still occur when agricultural plots are fragmented or reduced
below economically viable size.
     Business-related livelihood impacts also arise in Component 3, where approximately 301
street-front shops and small vendors are expected to face temporary income disruption once
construction begins due to reduced customer access. These effects are not part of permanent land
acquisition but represent important associated livelihood risks that require mitigation under
subsequent construction-phase management.
     Considering the diversity of affected land types, the number of households depending on
agriculture or aquaculture, and the vulnerability of specific groups, the impact on livelihoods in
the pre-construction phase is assessed as Substantial. Mitigation will require not only cash
compensation but also the implementation of livelihood and business restoration programs
proposed in the RP, including vocational training, agricultural extension, job-placement assistance
and targeted support for vulnerable households.
    4.2.1.3. Impacts on Land Use Change
     The land use in the project area will be changed due to the permanent acquisition of residential
land (49,606.4 m²), agricultural land (820,702.9 m²) and aquaculture ponds (233,573.4 m²) for the
development of new roads, bridges, river embankments and wastewater infrastructure. These
conversions will alter the existing land-use structure in several wards, especially where large areas
of paddy land, aquaculture land and mixed-use land are acquired. Such land-use changes may


                                                                                                   150
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
influence income sources and livelihood patterns of 1.292 affected households, particularly those
who rely on agricultural or aquaculture production .
     However, the impact is rated as LOW, because:
    • land-use trends in the affected wards already show a gradual shift toward urban
        development, with increasing proportions of residential, commercial and service land and
        a corresponding reduction in agricultural land;
    • once infrastructure under Components 1 and 2 is completed, land values along the new
        corridors and riverbank areas are expected to increase, enabling improved land-use
        efficiency and opportunities for planned urban expansion; and
    • improved accessibility and connectivity brought by the project will support local economic
        development and provide affected communities with better access to services and
        employment opportunities.
     The long-term positive impacts of improved infrastructure and urban development outweigh
short-term disruptions, and potential negative impacts associated with land-use change can be
mitigated through compensation and livelihood support measures included in the Project’s
Resettlement Plan.
    4.2.1.4. Impacts on Cultural Assets (Graves)
     The Project will affect a total of 348 graves, as identified by the Inventory of Losses (IOL)
conducted in October 2025. The majority of affected graves are located in Component 1, including
245 graves in Hoa Binh Ward and 74 graves in Nam Trieu Ward, where permanent land acquisition
will require grave relocation. Component 2 accounts for a smaller number of affected graves, with
29 graves identified in An Duong Ward.
     No affected graves were recorded in Bach Dang, Dong Hai, Lưu Kiếm, An Hai, or Hong Bang
wards according to the IOL results. Grave relocation is a culturally sensitive process due to the
strong spiritual significance of ancestral worship in Vietnamese tradition. According to the
Resettlement Plan (RP), compensation will fully cover the costs of excavation, relocation, reburial,
and associated ritual practices in accordance with local customs.
     The RP further stipulates that arrangements for grave relocation, including identification of
receiving cemeteries and consultation with affected households, will be undertaken during the
detailed land acquisition process by the Ward People’s Committees. Given the cultural sensitivity
of ancestral graves and the need for careful coordination with affected families and local
authorities, the impact significance is assessed as Substantial during the pre-construction phase.
    4.2.1.5. Loss of planted protection forest due to construction of Ruot Lon Bridge
     The land acquisition process for construction of the Ruot Lon Bridge crossing the Ruot Lon
River under Ring Road No. 3 at Km 6+100 will require the acquisition and conversion of
approximately 0.76 ha of planted protection forest. The dominant tree species is Ban chua
(Sonneratia caseolaris), with a planting density of approximately 250–300 trees per hectare. The
affected forest area is located in Lot 1b, Compartment 26, Sub-zone TN2, Nam Trieu Ward, and
is currently managed by the Nam Trieu Ward People’s Committee.
     The acquired area accounts for approximately 0.5% of the total mangrove protection forest
strip along the Ruot Lon River, which covers about 165 ha, with an average width of approximately


                                                                                                151
                                                                                                Official Use Only
300 m and a length of about 5.5 km. This forest was established under riverbank protection
afforestation programs implemented between 1997 and 2015, with primary functions including
wave attenuation, riverbank stabilization, and erosion control along the Ruot Lon River corridor.
     In addition to planted trees, the area also provides habitat for several common aquatic species
such as tilapia (Oreochromis spp.), catfish (Clarias spp.), common carp (Cyprinus carpio), crucian
carp (Carassius auratus), goby (Glossogobius spp.), as well as common crustaceans and benthic
fauna including freshwater prawns (Macrobrachium spp.), small shrimps, brackish-water crabs
(Scylla spp.), and freshwater snails. Waterbird species commonly distributed along the riverbanks
include little egret (Egretta garzetta), cattle egret (Bubulcus ibis), purple heron (Ardea purpurea),
black-crowned night heron (Nycticorax nycticorax), and other common riparian bird species.
These species are generally tolerant of disturbed environments and are not listed as rare,
endangered, or protected species.
     According to the Feasibility Study (FS), tree clearance will not occur across the entire 0.76 ha
of legally acquired forest land, but will be confined to the direct construction footprint of the five
bridge piers Figure 4.1, including foundation pits and immediate working areas around each pier.
The direct construction area for each pier is estimated at approximately 400–600 m², using bored
piles with diameters of D = 1.5–2.0 m. Accordingly, the total area of forest actually cleared is
estimated at approximately 2,000–3,000 m² (equivalent to 0.20–0.30 ha), which is substantially
smaller than the legally acquired forest area defined in the RP. The remaining forest area within
the land acquisition boundary will not be cleared and will continue to provide vegetation cover
and riverbank protection functions.
     Site clearance activities will result in the loss of existing forest vegetation and may cause
temporary and localized degradation of site-specific ecological functions, including riverbank
stabilization and attenuation of flow and wave impacts, particularly during heavy rainfall or high-
tide events. The reduction of vegetation cover within foundation pits and pier construction areas
may locally increase the risk of surface erosion and localized sediment generation at the base of
bridge piers. However, because the cleared areas do not form a continuous open riverbank section
and remain surrounded by the remaining protection forest strip, these impacts will not significantly
alter wave-buffering and bank-stabilization functions at the river-corridor scale. Tree removal
within the pier construction footprint will also result in localized habitat loss for common aquatic
species and waterbirds that use the riparian forest for foraging and resting.
     Nevertheless, as the conversion of protection forest land is permanent and cannot be restored
in situ, the impact is considered irreversible. Based on (i) the relatively small area of forest land
acquired (0.76 ha, approximately 0.5% of the total protection forest strip in the area); (ii) the fact
that actual tree clearance is limited to approximately 0.20–0.30 ha and is dispersed across five
bridge piers; (iii) the planted nature of the forest and the absence of protected or threatened flora
and fauna; and (iv) the surrounding landscape context, which has already been significantly
influenced by agricultural activities and existing infrastructure, this impact is assessed as
Moderate. The assessment reflects primarily the permanent nature of land-use conversion, while


                                                                                                  152
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
the reduction in ecological function at the corridor scale is limited and can be offset through
compensatory afforestation programs.




                    Figure 4.1. Location of the acquired protection forest.

   4.2.2. UXO-related risks
     The Project is located in an area with a history of wartime activities. Hai Phong, including its
port, river corridors and industrial zones, was subjected to extensive bombing during past conflicts,
creating the potential for unexploded ordnances (UXOs) to remain buried within agricultural land,
residential areas, riverbeds and canal sediments. Although parts of the project area have been
developed or cultivated for many years, UXOs may still be present below ground and must be
cleared to ensure the safety of workers and local communities before construction begins.
     Most components of the Project will therefore require UXO survey and clearance by licensed
military units. Based on the characteristics of earthworks, the subprojects with higher likelihood
of encountering UXOs include the construction of new roads and bridges under Component 1,
excavation for river embankments and wastewater facilities under Component 2, and trenching for
box culverts and pipelines under Component 3. These activities involve excavation depths that
may reach 5–12 m in agricultural land and riverine deposits, where war remnants could be buried.
     If UXOs are not cleared prior to construction, they may pose direct risks to construction
workers, supervisory engineers and nearby residents, and could also damage surrounding
infrastructure. Consultation with Hai Phong’s military command indicates that no UXO accidents
or findings have been reported in the project wards in the past decade. Nevertheless, the presence
of potential UXOs cannot be ruled out, and clearance is required to meet national safety regulations
and safeguard requirements.
     Given the historical exposure of Hai Phong and the nature of excavation works proposed under
the Project, the UXO risk is assessed as Moderate. Clearance must be completed by qualified units
before any construction activities commence, and work should proceed only within certified UXO-
free areas to avoid possible threats to human safety and surrounding structures.


                                                                                                 153
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
   4.3.    Impact & Risk During Construction Phase
   4.3.1. Generic impacts & risks
     Generic impacts are those that arise from typical construction activities and are common to
most or all investment items of the Project. These impacts generally occur during site clearance,
demolition of existing structures, earthworks and excavation, foundation works, embankment
construction, road works, installation of drainage and wastewater pipelines, as well as the
construction, installation, and commissioning of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), including
associated mechanical and electrical equipment, pumping systems, and ancillary facilities. These
generic impacts are relevant to the following project components and key investment items:
    • Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure Development
       which includes construction of Road No. 3 as the main urban transport corridor, together
       with bridge structures along the route (Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge) and
       associated drainage, lighting and auxiliary works.
    • Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement, comprising the following two
       Subcomponents including Subcomponent 2.1: Construction and Upgrading of Re River
       Embankment and Management Roads and Subcomponent 2.2: Environmental Sanitation
       Improvement of the Re River Catchment, including the installation of wastewater
       collection and interceptor sewers, construction of pumping stations, and construction of the
       An Duong Wastewater Treatment Plant (Phase 1).
    • Component 3 – City Center Flood Reduction, which includes construction of regulating
       sluices and flood control structures, stormwater pumping facilities, rehabilitation of canals
       and box culverts, and associated urban drainage and wastewater collection works in the
       Tam Bac area. Generic impacts under this component are largely related to in-water and
       near-water construction activities, deep excavation for hydraulic structures, temporary
       dewatering, and construction works carried out within densely populated inner-city areas.
     Typical construction impacts include: (i) dust and air emissions; (ii) noise and vibration; (iii)
reduced water quality; (iv) solid-wastegeneration; (v) impacts on the ecological environment; (vi)
traffic congestion and temporary access obstruction; (vii) impacts associated with labor influx;
(viii) impacts due to improper access; (ix) local bussiness disruption; (x) relocation or interruption
of utilities and technical infrastructure; and (xi) Impacts on agricultural and aquacultural activities.
Typical construction risks include: (xii) local flooding; (xiii) embankment subsidence and
geotechnical instability; (xiv) exposure to natural hazards such as storms and heavy rainfall; (xv)
community and worker health and safety; (xvi) risks of labor violence, harassment and GBV; and
(xvii) Impacts on Physical Cultural Resources (PCR).
   4.3.1.1. Impacts on Ambient Air Quality
   a. Dust generation
    Dust emissions can arise from several main construction-related sources. Not only dust from
demolition during site clearance and subsequent construction activities needs to be considered, but
also dust generated by on-road vehicles serving the construction sites and off-road machinery
operating within work areas. For the Hai Phong Project, the principal dust-generating activities



                                                                                                   154
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
include: (i) demolition of existing structures within the land-acquisition boundaries of Road No.
3, the Re River embankment corridor and the Tam Bac riverfront; (ii) earth excavation, backfilling
and levelling for road embankments, bridge foundations, culverts, box-sewers, pumping stations
and the An Duong 1 WWTP; and (iii) loading, hauling, unloading and stockpiling of construction
materials, excavated soil and dredged material, together with vehicle and machinery movement
over unpaved or partially paved surfaces. These activities occur predominantly in urban and peri-
urban areas and therefore pose direct exposure risks to construction workers, supervisors and
nearby communities—particularly children, elderly people and residents living, studying or
working along construction corridors or haul routes.
    Dust generated from earthworks, demolition and material handling can adversely affect the
health of nearby communities. Larger particles (TSP – Total Suspended Particulates) tend to settle
more quickly and primarily cause mechanical irritation to the eyes, skin and upper respiratory tract.
Dust generation can occur in any direction from a site; however, dispersion is more likely
downwind of the prevailing wind direction or in close proximity to active work fronts. Dust
impacts are also more significant during drier periods, as rainfall acts as a natural dust suppressant.
   (i)     Dust from demolition
     Dust generation to air may occur during site clearance and demolition activities including the
removal of houses, yards and other architectural structures distributed across multiple wards of
Hai Phong City. These activities are implemented in a phased and location-specific manner
depending on land acquisition progress and site readiness, rather than occurring simultaneously
across all locations. Such impacts are relevant to Component 1, Component 2 where clearance of
existing structures is required prior to construction of roads, embankments, drainage corridors,
wastewater systems, flood control structures and associated facilities.
     According to the RP (2026), a total of 481 households will have houses or other architectural
structures affected, with a combined affected structural area of approximately 54,114 m². Of this,
the affected residential house area alone is 28,379 m². In terms of housing types, 95 households
have temporary houses, 265 households have single-storey houses, 104 households have two-
storey houses, and 17 households have three- to four-storey houses. corresponding to an average
of about 59 m² per household.
     For Component 1 (covering the five wards of Hoa Binh, Bach Dang, Dong Hai, Lưu Kiếm
and Nam Trieu), demolition works will affect houses and auxiliary structures with a combined
affected house area of approximately 29,105 m². In addition, the affected areas of other structure
types in Component 1 include: residential houses: 21,668 m²; yards: 1,364 m²; other structures:
1,192 m²; fences/walls: 1,267 m²; toilets: 967 m²; kitchens: 2,365 m²; and ancestral worship
houses: 282 m².
    Within Component 2, affected structures are distributed between the two key investment
items. Under Construction and Upgrading of Re River Embankment and Management Roads (An
Duong and Hong Bang wards) and An Duong 1 WWTP, the total affected area is approximately



                                                                                                   155
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
25,009 m², inlcuding: Residential houses: 6,709 m²; yards: 4,191 m²; other structures: 8,230 m²;
fences/walls: 1,475 m²; toilets: 2,982 m²; kitchens: 1,422 m².
     The demolition and removal of these structures across Components 1 and 2 will generate
localized and temporary dust emissions, which may cause short-term deterioration of ambient air
quality in the immediate vicinity of clearance and demolition sites. While no demolition of houses
or architectural structures is recorded under Component 3 in the Tam Bac basin area.
           Table 4.3. Summary of the affected houses and architectural structures

                                                                structure area
                                                                          (m²)

     The highest dust risk is associated with wards where demolition volumes are greatest and
where settlement density is high, notably Bach Dang under Component 1 and An Hai and Hong
Bang under Component 2, where houses, small shops and community facilities are located close
to construction boundaries along narrow internal streets. In contrast, in less densely populated or
peri-urban wards such as Luu Kiem and parts of Nam Trieu, affected structures are more dispersed
and access roads are wider, resulting in lower levels of localized dust disturbance despite the
presence of demolition activities.
     Demolition activities are implemented in a phased manner following land acquisition and site
clearance schedules and do not occur simultaneously across all wards. Based on the scale and
distribution of affected structures, demolition works at individual sites are expected to last for
several weeks to a few months, with longer cumulative demolition periods under Component 1
due to the larger number of affected structures, and shorter periods under Component 2.
Considering the scale of demolition activities, their phased implementation, and the proximity of
sensitive receptors in certain wards, dust impacts prior to mitigation are assessed as Moderate.
(ii) Dust from earthworks (excavation, filling, trenching)
    The quantitative approach well recognized in many ESIAs funded by WB following the
bank’s ESF, using project-specific data from the FS (2026). Dust emission coefficient E for
construction earthworks is calculated following the World Bank Environmental Assessment
Sourcebook (1991) and US EPA AP-42 (1995):



                                                                                               156
                                                                                               Official Use Only
                    E = k x 0.0016 x (U/2.2)1.4 ÷ (M/2)1.3, kg/ton     [1]
    Where:
   • E: emission coefficient (kg/ton);
   • U: average wind speed (m/s) (2.4 m/s in dry season; 3.6 m/s in rainy season according to
      local meteo data);
   • k: particle size multiplier (k = 0.35 for PM₁₀ as per AP-42, Table 13.2.4-4);
   • M: average surface moisture content of material (%), here 11%.
    Average dust mass generated for each activity is then:
                                   W = E x Q x d (kg) [2]
     Where:
    • W: total dust mass generated;
    • Q: volume of material handled (m³), taken from FS for each key investment item;
    • d: bulk density of soil/rock (1.45 t/m³ under Decision 1329/QĐ-BXD).
     Dispersion from linear/surface sources is estimated using a surface-source diffusion model
(Tran Ngoc Chân, 2000):

                              E L       −ut
                           C=  s   (1 − e L ) (mg/m3)            [3]
                              uH
    Where:
   • C: one-hour average dust concentration at breathing height (mg/m³);
   • E_s = M / (L x W) (mg/m2.s)emission rate per unit area (mg/m²·s);
   • u: wind speed perpendicular to plume (m/s);
   • H: mixing height (m);
   • L, W: Length and width of air diffusion (m).
    All three components require large-scale earthworks for road embankments, riverbank
shaping, culverts, sewers, sluices, pumping stations and the WWTP. The FS estimates a total
excavation/backfill volume of each Component presented in Table 4.4.

    Air pollution from dust generated during construction is a location-specific impact,
concentrated mainly at sections where large-scale earthworks are carried out in close proximity to


                                                                                                         160
                                                                                                         Official Use Only
residential areas and other sensitive receptors, particularly within Component 1. The impact is
temporary in nature and occurs sequentially along the construction corridor, with exposure at each
receptor cluster typically lasting several weeks, while the overall earthworks period extends for
several months. Based on the magnitude of dust emissions under the worst-case scenario, the short
separation distance between sources and receptors as residential houses and other sensitive
receptors are typically located within approximately 7–50 m along dense inner-city corridors and
10–100 m along peri-urban road and embankment sections, the impact is assessed as moderate and
can be effectively mitigated through appropriate construction and dust control measures.
(iii) Dust from construction material and waste transportation
    The transportation of raw materials, fuels, and construction materials from supply sources to
the project sites, and the transportation of excavated soil, demolition and construction waste to
designated disposal or reuse sites, can generate dust due to vehicle movement, loading, and
unloading activities. The transport of loose materials such as sand, soil, and aggregates is the main
cause of dust generation associated with construction logistics. All transportation activities—
covering raw materials, backfilling, demolition debris, and excavated soil—will occur in parallel
throughout the 24-48 month construction period.
     Based on the updated earthwork and material estimates (Table 1.4 and Table 1.5), the Project
will handle approximately 3.9 million tons of construction materials over the construction period.
A substantial proportion of excavated soil and demolition materials will be reused within key
investment item boundaries, particularly for the ring road No.3, river embankment and
management road works along the Re River, thereby reducing the need for long-distance material
transport. Nevertheless, Component 1 and Componen 2 will require the import of aggregates, base
course materials, cement and concrete products from licensed quarries, batching plants and
material suppliers. The updated estimates indicate an average of approximately 500–520 material
transport trips per day across all investment items during peak construction periods, reflecting the
scale and logistical intensity of construction activities.
    The number of outward truck movements (10-ton capacity) was estimated based on total soil
and material volumes and indicative construction durations (Chapter 1). Average daily truck trips
by key investment items are presented below.

    These trips represent vehicle movements on both paved and unpaved internal roads, where
trucks accumulate mud and dust that can be tracked onto public streets. Additional emissions arise
from vehicle passage over construction yards, material stockpile areas, and access ramps.
According to IAQM Guidance (2016), the magnitude of dust emissions from transportation is
categorized as follows:
   •   High: > 50 (Heavy-duty vehicle) HDV (10-ton) outward movements per day, potentially
       dusty surface material (e.g., clay or fine sand), unpaved haul length > 100 m;
   •   Moderate: 10–50 HDV (10-ton) per day, moderately dusty surface material, unpaved haul
       length 50–100 m;
   •   Low: < 10 HDV (10-ton) per day, low dust-release material, unpaved haul length < 50 m.

     In the IAQM model, the road source represents all emissions generated along construction
transport routes, including: (i) exhaust gases from trucks and machinery (CO, NOₓ, SO₂, VOCs,
PM₂.₅); (ii) resuspended dust from vehicle movement on unpaved or disturbed surfaces; and (iii)
fugitive dust and spillage from uncovered material-haul trucks. All these emission components are
incorporated to assess the overall impact of construction traffic on ambient air quality. Using
IAQM criteria combined with truck-trip estimates, the dust emission magnitude differs across key
investment items is showned in Table 4.8

    Dust impacts arising from material and waste transport during construction are assessed as
moderate overall which occur across all components during periods of active material delivery and
waste haulage, particularly along construction access routes and in areas adjacent to residential
and commercial receptors.. These impacts are location-specific, temporary, and can be effectively
mitigated through appropriate transport management and dust control measures.
   b. Emissions generated from operation of construction equipment
     Construction equipment is a significant source of air emissions during site preparation,
earthworks, embankment construction, culvert and sewer installation, bridge works and road
paving. Combustion of diesel fuel in excavators, loaders, compactors, cranes and generators
produces exhaust gases such as CO, NOₓ, SO₂ and unburned hydrocarbons (HC), together with a
small fraction of particulate emissions that contributes to the overall TSP load at construction sites.
These particulates are released from low-height exhaust points and disperse rapidly in open areas,
but may accumulate around work fronts during periods of intensive machinery use, especially
under dry and stagnant conditions. Potentially affected receptors include workers operating within
10–20 m of equipment and nearby residents along component 1 (Lập Lễ mixed housing cluster
(Km 9+500–Km 10+500), Ngũ Lão residential cluster (Km 13+000–Km 14+100), Trung Hà
residential strip (Km 15+000–Km 15+500), and 10 Hamlet residential area (Km 17+300–Km
17+500) within Nam Trieu, Hoa Binh, and Bach Dang wards), component 2 (Lê Lợi, Group 1, and
An Dong residential areas in An Duong Ward and Sở Dầu residential area in Hong Bang Ward &
Component 3 (along along Nguyen Tri Phuong, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tam Bac, Tran Nguyen Han,
Hai Ba Trung, Lan Ong, Cau Dat, Dien Bien Phu, Hoang Dieu, Lan Be, Nguyen Cong Hoa, Hoang
Minh Thao, and The Lu streets) together with local famers in agricultural land and aquaculture
ponds located adjacent to the ring road No.3 alignment, particularly paddy fields in Nam Trieu,
Hoa Binh, and Bach Dang wards and along Re river corridor in An Hai, An Duong and Hong Bang
wards.
     For the Hai Phong Project, the FS (2026) estimates the total fuel demand for construction
machinery and trucks as about 237,000 L of diesel/gasoline, broken down by key investment items
as in Table 1.7. Fuel mass is obtained using a diesel density of 0.85 kg/L. Pollutant loads are then
calculated using WHO emission factors for diesel combustion, assumed conservative for mixed



                                                                                                   164
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
construction equipment: CO: 0.03 kg / kg fuel; SO₂: 0.04 kg / kg fuel; NO₂: 0.055 kg / kg fuel;
HC: 0.012 kg / kg fuel; and TSP: 0.0045 kg/kg fuel. The resulting estimated emissions are
summarised in Table 4.9

    Emissions from diesel-fuelled construction equipment generate low concentrations of CO,
SO₂, NO₂, HC and TSP at receptor distances of 5–10 m, with all predicted values remaining far
below the applicable QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT limits. Dispersion modelling indicates that
pollutant levels decrease rapidly with height and distance, and exposure is largely confined to
workers operating near machinery rather than the surrounding community. Given the low emission
magnitude and limited spatial extent of impact, exhaust-related air pollution from diesel use across
the Hai Phong Project is assessed as a Low impact.
   4.3.1.2.    Noise Generation and Impact
     During the construction phase of the project, noise generated mainly from the following source (i)
transportation of construction materials, solid waste; (ii) construction and dismantling works items
and (iii) operation of construction equipment at the construction site. Construction noise in the
community may not only pose directly risks of worker’s health and /or damage worker' sense of
hearing, but can indirectly affect peoples' quality of life, reduce the people’s working performance
and the children’s concentration. To some level, construction noise can be an indirect contributing
factor to the degradation of someone's health in that it can cause people to be irritated and stressed
and can interrupt their ability to sleep - all of which may lead to higher blood pressure, anxiety,
and feelings of animosity toward the people or agencies responsible for producing the noise.
Construction noise has the potential to disturb people at home in their residences, in office
buildings or retail businesses, in public institutional buildings, at locations of religious services,
while attending sporting events, or when on vacation.
    Noise propagation attenuates with distance following the empirical formula:
                                   Lp = Lp(Xo) + 20log10(Xo/X)


                                                                                                  166
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
      Where:
 Lp(Xo): noise level at a distance of 1.5m from Lp(X): noise level at the calculated position
 the source (dBA)                                 (dBA)
 Xo = 1.5m                                        X: calculated position (m).

    Compnent 1, subcomponent 2.1 and Component 3 all produce the substaintal noise levels due

densely populated corridors. Machinery noise exceeds daytime limits for residential and sensitive
receptors at distances up to about 20–30 m, and nighttime limits at distances beyond 100 m.
