Receptors include residential clusters, shopfronts, schools, markets and roadside vendors along
PR351, PR10, the Tran Hung Dao–An Dong–An Duong corridor and dense inner-city streets such
as Dien Bien Phu, Cau Dat, Lan Ong, Hai Ba Trung and Tran Nguyen Han. The construction of
subcomponent 2.2 generates low noise impacts because most construction activities occur around
the An Duong WWTP site and along internal roads in sparsely populated peri-urban areas, where
only a limited number of scattered households and on-site workers are exposed and noise levels
drop quickly with distance.
     Noise generated during construction is a location-specific impact, temporary in nature and
occurs sequentially along construction routes, with noise exposure at each receptor cluster
typically lasting several weeks, while overall construction activities extend over several months.
Based on the intensity of machinery operation, the close proximity between noise sources and
residential or other sensitive receptors, and the potential for exceedance of applicable daytime and
nighttime noise limits at short distances, the impact is assessed as moderate but it can be effectively
mitigated through appropriate construction scheduling, equipment management, and noise control
measures.
     4.3.1.3.   Vibration Impact
     Vibration can be generated mainly from equipment operating with large capacity and often on
sites such as bulldozers, excavators, rollers, pile drivers, etc. from activities of leveling,
compacting and foundation construction. When assessing ground-borne vibration, there are
typically two major concerns: building damage and potential to cause disturbance.
    Refer to the vibration level of vehicles, machinery and equipment in the report Transit Noise
and Vibration Impact Assessment (US, Federal Transit Administration, 2006), to forecast the
amount of vibration decreasing by distance, the following formula is used:
                               L = Lo – 10lg (r/ro) – 8.7a (r – ro) (dB)
     Where:
     •   L is vibration in dB at a distance r meters away from the source;
     •   L₀ is vibration in dB measured at a distance r₀ meters from the source. Vibration at a
         distance of r₀ = 10 m is generally acknowledged as source vibration;
     •   a is the intrinsic vibration reduction coefficient on clay ground of about 0.5.

     The forecasting results are presented in the following table.

     Construction-related vibration is a location-specific impact arising from the operation of
heavy construction equipment, based on the vibration levels of typical construction machinery
presented in Table 4.12, the vibration impact by Subcomponent 2.1 is assessed as substantial due
to the use of pile driving and vibratory hammer equipment for the construction of vertical concrete
river embankments. These activities generate clearly perceptible ground vibration at close
distances, particularly where residential buildings, shopfronts and other sensitive receptors are
located in An Dong residential area of An Dương ward within approximately 5–15 m of the works.
Although predicted peak particle velocity values remain below thresholds associated with
structural damage, vibration levels are noticeable and may cause nuisance during active piling and
compaction periods.
     Moderate vibration impacts are expected for Component 1 and Component 3. In Component
1, rollers, compactors and heavy trucks operate along road sections passing through residential and
mixed-use areas, where receptors are typically located in Ngu Lao, Trung Ha residential areas
within 10–30 m of the construction corridor, resulting in perceptible vibration during earthworks
and pavement works. In Component 3, jackhammers, rollers and compactors are used within
narrow inner-city streets for drainage and sewer construction, with buildings along Nguyen Tri


                                                                                               169
                                                                                               Official Use Only
Phuong, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tam Bac, Tran Nguyen Han, Hai Ba Trung, Lan Ong, Cau Dat, Dien
Bien Phu and Hoang Dieu Tam Bac, Lan Be, Nguyen Cong Hoa, Hoang Minh Thao, The Lu
streets located 5–10 m from trenching activities.
     Low vibration impacts are expected for Subcomponent 2.2, as construction activities are
largely confined to the wastewater treatment plant site and peri-urban areas with sparse receptors
and larger setback distances. Vibration exposure is limited primarily to on-site workers and short-
duration activities, with minimal effects on surrounding communities.
     Overall, construction vibration impacts of the Project are assessed as moderate which are
temporary, localized, restricted to daytime, remain below thresholds associated with non-structural
or structural damage, and can be effectively mitigated through appropriate construction methods,
scheduling and vibration control measures.
    4.3.1.4. Impacts on Water Quality
     Construction activities of the Project will generate wastewater that may affect the quality of
surrounding rivers, canals, artificial ponds, and agricultural irrigation systems. These include: (i)
Domestic wastewater from workers’ camps and daily worker activities; (ii) Construction
wastewater from concrete mixing, curing, pile installation, cofferdam dewatering, and equipment
washing and (iii) Stormwater runoff from exposed soils, stockpiles, and machinery areas that
mobilizes suspended solids, sediments, and oil residues.
    (i) Domestic Wastewater from Workers’ Camps and daily worker activities
      Domestic wastewater generated under the Project originates from two primary activities. The
first source is the workers’ camps established during construction, which accommodate workers
and generate domestic wastewater from bathing, washing, cooking, and sanitary use. The second
source is daily on-site worker activities at work fronts. These include temporary toilets, portable
sanitary cabins, and uncontrolled urination or defecation near excavation zones when sanitary
facilities are insufficient or not properly arranged. Both sources produce wastewater with high
concentrations of organic matter, suspended solids, nutrients, oils, and pathogenic
microorganisms. If not properly managed, domestic wastewater may enter nearby rivers, drainage
canals, and agricultural ponds, degrading water quality along the alignment.
      Domestic wastewater is estimated based on a consumption rate of 100 L/person/day for all
workers during on-site working hours (TCXDVN 33:2006). For the 20% of workers who reside in
camps, an additional 50 L/person/day is applied to account for evening domestic use in accordance
with QCVN 01:2021/BXD. All water used is assumed to be fully converted to wastewater.

    Based on the estimated workforce and domestic wastewater volumes summarized by
component and subcomponent (Table 4.14), daily domestic wastewater generation during
construction ranges from approximately 5.5 m³/day for Subcomponent 2.2 to about 26.95 m³/day
for Component 1, with additional wastewater generated at worker camps accounting for



                                                                                              171
                                                                                              Official Use Only
approximately 10% of on-site flows. Although the absolute daily wastewater volumes at each
construction area are relatively limited, pollutant concentrations in untreated domestic wastewater
are extremely high, with BOD₅, TSS, nutrients, oil and grease, and coliform levels exceeding the
limits of QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT (Column B) by factors ranging from several times to several
orders of magnitude, as presented in Table 4.15. Consequently, any direct discharge of untreated
wastewater from construction sites or worker camps would result in immediate and localized
deterioration of receiving water quality.
     Potential receptors include roadside drainage canals, internal irrigation ditches, and nearby
surface water bodies, including the Hau Long River, Chu River, Re River, Ruot Lon River, Cam
River, and the Tam Bac river, as well as low-lying residential clusters and agricultural and
agricultural areas adjacent to construction areas where wastewater may overflow or infiltrate
during wet weather conditions. Exposure occurs throughout the construction period, which extends
over multiple months for each component, with higher daily wastewater loads associated with
Component 1, Subcomponent 2.1 and Component 3, where workforce size and construction
duration are greatest. Worker camps represent an additional continuous risk due to concentrated
discharge and high pathogen loads.
     Given the high pollutant concentrations in untreated domestic wastewater, the continuous
nature of wastewater generation during construction, and the close proximity of sensitive receiving
waters and residential receptors, as well as the relatively limited volume of domestic wastewater
generated by the construction workforce, the temporary and localized nature of construction
activities, and the fact that wastewater generation is confined to worker camps and site facilities
rather than dispersed along the entire construction corridor the impact from domestic wastewater
during the construction phase is assessed as moderate but can be effectively mitigated through
appropriate wastewater collection, treatment and management measures.
    (ii) Construction wastewater
    Construction activities across all key investment items generate various types of wastewater
with differing volumes and pollutant characteristics depending on the specific work being
undertaken. Key wastewater-generating activities include concrete mixing, aggregate washing,
excavation dewatering, bored-pile drilling, equipment washing and general site cleaning. These
wastewaters typically contain high suspended solids, alkaline pH, and traces of oil and grease, and
may reach nearby rivers, canals or urban drainage systems if not properly managed. Table 4.16
summarises the main construction activities, estimated water use, wastewater volumes, pollutant
characteristics and receiving water bodies for each key investment item.

     Based on the wastewater-gerating activities and estimated volumes summarized by
component and subcomponent (Table 4.16), construction wastewater is generated continuously
across all components, typically ranging from about 10–30 m³/day at each active workfront, with
higher flows occurring locally during cofferdam dewatering, excavation dewatering and bored-
pile operations (up to approximately 30–40 m³/day). Although the overall wastewater volumes are
moderate, the pollutant characteristics are highly adverse: concrete-related activities generate
strongly alkaline effluent (pH ≥11) with high TSS and cementitious solids; excavation and
dewatering produce sediment-laden flows containing clay, silt and dissolved iron and manganese;
and equipment washing contributes oil and grease. When discharged untreated, these wastewater
streams would exceed the limits of QCVN 40:2025/BTNMT by wide margins.
     Potential receptors include the Hau Long, Chu, Cam, Ruot Lon, Re and Tam Bac Rivers;
agricultural canals and field drainage systems in Nam Trieu, Hoa Binh, Bach Dang and An Hai;
and the dense urban drainage network within the Tam Bac basin. Many construction fronts under
Component 1, Subcomponent 2.1 and Component 3 are located within approximately 5–20 m of
receiving waters or connected drainage systems, resulting in direct hydraulic connectivity between
construction areas and surface water bodies. Uncontrolled discharge of construction wastewater
could therefore cause rapid increases in turbidity, suspended solids and alkalinity, as well as
localized sediment deposition and short-term ecological stress in rivers and canals.
    Construction wastewater is a location-specific impact, concentrated mainly at construction
fronts where excavation dewatering, concrete works and cofferdam operations are carried out in


                                                                                                        174
                                                                                                        Official Use Only
close hydraulic connection with rivers, canals and urban drainage systems, particularly under
Component 1, Subcomponent 2.1 and Component 3. The impact is temporary in nature and occurs
sequentially along construction sections, with wastewater generation at each location lasting for
limited periods as works progress, while the overall construction period extends over several
months.
     Based on the high pollutant concentrations in untreated construction wastewater, the short
separation distance between sources and receiving waters, and the presence of sensitive surface
water bodies and residential drainage systems near active workfronts, as well as the relatively
limited volumes of construction wastewater, the temporary and localized nature of construction
activities, and the fact that wastewater generation is confined to discrete work sites rather than
occurring continuously along the entire project corridor the impact from construction wastewater
during the construction phase is assessed as moderate and can be effectively mitigated through
appropriate wastewater management and treatment measures.
(iii) Rainwater Runoff
     During construction, rainwater runoff poses a potential source of water pollution as
stormwater can mobilize loose soil, fine particles, cement residues, oils, greases, and general
construction debris from exposed surfaces, stockpiles, and work areas. When runoff flows across
unprotected ground or partially completed works, it can carry significant sediment loads into
nearby rivers, canals, and artificial ponds. The degree of impact depends on the size of the exposed
area, rainfall intensity, topography, and the dilution capacity of the receiving water bodies.
     The volume of runoff generated at each key investment item is estimated based on the standard
hydrological formula:
                                     W = ψ x q x F/1000 (m3/day)
    Where:
   •   W = rainwater runoff (m³/day),
   •   ψ = runoff coefficient (0.2 for mixed soil, vegetation, and semi-pervious surfaces),
   •   q = maximum daily precipitation (160 mm), q_min = 20 mm/day
   •   F = construction footprint area (m²).

    Runoff water quality is typically characterised by low pollutant concentrations compared with
construction wastewater, but sediment loads (TSS) can be significant when soil is disturbed.
According to WHO, typical concentrations include COD 10–20 mg/L, TSS 10–50 mg/L, nitrogen
0.5–1.5 mg/L, and phosphorus 0.004–0.03 mg/L. While these concentrations are low, the total
mass of pollutants transported depends on the volume of runoff, which in turn depends on the
footprint area. Runoff impacts therefore vary across key investment items.
                Table 4.17. Estimated Rainwater Runoff at Construction Sites
    Component /           Surface         Runoff              Main Receiving Water Body
   Subcomponent          Area (m²)        Volume
                                         (m³/day)

                                                                                                Official Use Only
 Component 1               510,689           16,350 Hau Long, Chu; Cam River; Ruot Lon Rivers;
                                                    aquacultural ponds, agricultural drainage and
                                                    irrigation canals

                                                                                     Source: FS 2026
    Note: Runoff presented as minimum–maximum range based on low and high rainfall
conditions
     Rainwater runoff is generated across all construction components, with the magnitude and
potential impact varying by construction scale, exposed surface area and proximity to receiving
waters. Under Component 1, runoff volumes are highest due to the extensive construction footprint
of road and bridge works and the direct drainage connection to rivers, agricultural canals and
aquacultural ponds, making this component the main contributor to sediment-laden runoff during
rainfall events. Subcomponent 2.1 generates moderate runoff associated with riverbank excavation
and management road works, with runoff discharged directly to the Re River and connected
agricultural drainage systems. Subcomponent 2.2 produces comparable runoff volumes within the
WWTP site, where drainage ditches convey runoff to the Lach Tray River and surrounding
irrigation canals. Component 3 generates lower runoff volumes per unit area, but works are located
within dense urban catchments, resulting in direct discharge to the Tam Bac River, Tam Bac Lake
and associated canal systems.
     Construction runoff is a location-specific impact, concentrated at construction sections where
large exposed surfaces are hydraulically connected to sensitive rivers, canals and ponds. The
impact is temporary and occurs intermittently during rainfall events, shifting sequentially along
construction fronts as works progress. While runoff during storm events may mobilize sediments
from exposed surfaces and affect sensitive receiving waters, the extent and duration of such
impacts are limited by the confined spatial footprint of active construction areas, the short-term
exposure associated with individual work sections, and the episodic nature of rainfall-driven runoff
rather than continuous discharge. On this basis, the impact from construction runoff during the
construction phase is assessed as moderate and can be effectively mitigated through appropriate
erosion and runoff control measures..
    4.3.1.5. Solid waste generation and impacts
     Solid waste generated during construction of the Project components can be grouped into three
main types: (i) construction solid waste; (ii) domestic solid waste from workers; and (iii) hazardous
solid waste associated with machinery maintenance and material use. In line with WB ESS3
(Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management) and good international practice,
these wastes may create adverse environmental, social and health impacts if not properly managed.



                                                                                                   176
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
Besides contributing to dust already assessed in the air-quality section, mismanaged waste can
contaminate soil and surface water, obstruct drainage canals and riverbanks, degrade the urban
landscape and generate indirect health and safety risks for workers and communities.
(i) Construction solid waste (including demolition waste)
     Construction solid waste generated during the Project arises from (i) demolition of existing
structures and temporary works; (ii) surplus excavated materials including stripped organic topsoil;
and (iii) residual construction waste associated with material handling, formwork, packaging and
breakages during construction activities.
     Based on the FS (2026), the Project will handle approximately 3.907 million tons of
construction materials across. In addition, earthworks will generate about 303,240 tons of surplus
excavated materials, including stripped organic topsoil and unusable soil, as identified in the
updated earthwork balance. Demolition waste volumes are derived directly from RP-verified
affected structures. The resulting indicative quantities of construction solid waste by components
are presented in Table 4.18.
                  Table 4.18.Estimated volume of construction solid waste (tons)
 Component          / Construction           Residual             Demolition       Surplus soil &
 Subcomponent         materials handled      construction         waste            stripped topsoil
                                             waste
 Component 1           3,504,001             38,250               32,502           125,314
 Subcomponent 2.1      173,038               1,903                837              88,591
 Subcomponent 2.2      104,798               1,153                165              76,575
 Component 3           125,647               1,382                0                12,760
 TOTAL                 3,907,483             44,688               33,504           303,240
                                                                                 (Source FS 2026).
     Table 4.18 shows that solid waste is generated mainly from residual construction waste,
limited demolition activities, and large volumes of surplus excavated soil and stripped topsoil
associated with earthworks and site clearance. Across all components, surplus soil and topsoil
constitute the dominant waste stream, while demolition waste and residual construction waste
account for a relatively small proportion and are confined to specific locations.
     Component 1 generates the largest quantities of construction materials and surplus soil due to
extensive road and bridge works, with waste transport occurring along urban and peri-urban
corridors. Subcomponent 2.1 and Subcomponent 2.2 generate moderate volumes of residual
construction waste and substantial quantities of excavated soil associated with river embankment
works and WWTP construction, respectively, with disposal routed through designated haul roads.
Component 3 produces comparatively smaller waste volumes, primarily from excavation and civil
works within urban drainage and pumping station areas.
     Construction solid waste impacts are location-specific and occur intermittently as construction
progresses by section, with exposure at each receptor group limited to short periods during active
earthworks and waste transport. The majority of generated waste comprises inert excavated soil
and topsoil, while demolition waste is limited in quantity and spatial extent and generated


                                                                                                177
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
progressively over the construction period rather than simultaneously across the Project area. On
this basis, the overall impact of construction solid waste during the construction phase is assessed
as moderate and can be effectively mitigated through appropriate segregation, storage, transport,
and disposal measures.
(ii) Domestic solid waste
     Domestic solid waste during construction consists primarily of food packaging, nylon bags,
bottles, cans, paper, and biodegradable organic residues. According to the WHO Rapid
Assessment Method, domestic waste generation is estimated using a rate of 0.5 kg/person/day,
representing total daily household-type waste per worker. It is assumed that 80% of workers
commute home, generating waste only at construction sites, while 20% reside in worker camps,
generating waste at both construction sites and camps. Using this assumation, the total domestic
waste volume for each key investment item is calculated as shown in the table below.

     Total domestic solid waste generated during the construction phase is estimated at
approximately 300 tons over the entire construction period. At the level of individual workfronts,
daily domestic waste generation remains relatively small and is associated mainly with routine
activities of construction workers at sites and worker camps. The waste stream consists primarily
of food residues, packaging materials, plastics, paper, bottles and cans, and does not contain
hazardous constituents.
    Component 1 and Component 3 generate the largest quantities of domestic waste due to higher
workforce numbers and longer construction durations, while Subcomponent 2.1 and
Subcomponent 2.2 contribute smaller but continuous amounts over their respective construction
periods. Receptors potentially affected include nearby households, shopfronts, markets, schools
and roadside drainage canals located adjacent to construction sites and worker camps, surface
water bodies that could be affected in the event of improper domestic waste management include
the Cam River, Ruột Lợn River, Chu River, Hậu Long River, and Re River, together with
connected urban drainage canals, irrigation canals, and aquaculture water-supply channels located
along Road No. 3, the Re River embankment, and inner-city drainage corridors. These canals serve
surrounding paddy fields and household-managed aquaculture ponds, particularly in peri-urban


                                                                                                178
                                                                                                Official Use Only
sections and riverbank areas. If not properly managed, domestic waste may cause localized odour,
attract insects and rodents, create visual nuisance, and contribute to temporary blockage of local
drains, especially during hot or wet conditions. These impacts, however, are confined to the
immediate vicinity of work sites and camps.
     Hai Phong City operates an established municipal solid waste collection and disposal system
with sufficient capacity to accommodate the additional domestic waste generated by the Project,
which is negligible at the city scale. Accordingly, potential impacts are not related to disposal
capacity but depend primarily on the effectiveness of on-site waste management practices,
including segregation, containerisation and timely collection by municipal services. Exposure at
each location is temporary and limited to the duration of nearby construction activities, typically
several months.
     Considering the relatively small daily quantities, the localized and reversible nature of
potential nuisance impacts, the availability of an existing municipal collection system, and the
limited exposure duration at each site,the impact related to domestic solid waste generated during
the construction phase is assessed as small and can be mitigated through the appropriate
segregation, storage, transport and disposal measures.
(iii) Hazardous solid waste
     Hazardous solid waste from the Hai Phong Project will be generated in small but continuous
quantities at all construction sites. Order-of-magnitude estimates based on equipment fleets and
construction duration indicate that each Component/subcomponent produces about 5–8 kg/month
of oily rags, used oil filters and contaminated absorbents from machinery maintenance. In addition,
Component 1, Subcomponent 2.1 and Component 3 generate approximately 10–30 kg/month of
asphalt residues, bitumen and paint containers associated with road, embankment, riverbank and
urban drainage works. Welding-related hazardous waste, including electrode stubs and rejected
welding rods, is generated mainly under Component 1 and Component 3, amounting to
approximately 5–10 kg over the entire construction period of each bridge or sluice structure.
Hazardous office waste, such as batteries, fluorescent tubes and toner cartridges, is generated at a
rate of about 1–3 kg/month at each site office across all components.
     Over the full 24–48-month construction period across Component 1, Subcomponent 2.1,
Subcomponent 2.2 and Component 3, the total hazardous solid waste volume is expected to be on
the order of 4–6 tons, which is very small compared with the licensed hazardous waste treatment
capacity available in Hai Phong.
     Potential receptors include construction workers, nearby households and shopfronts located
along the Ring Road No.3 corridor, the Re River embankment area and the Tam Bac basin, as well
as adjacent surface water bodies that could be affected by accidental leakage or runoff. These
include the Hau Long, Chu, Cam, Ruot Lon, Re and Tam Bac Rivers, together with local irrigation
canals. If hazardous waste is not properly segregated and stored on impermeable surfaces, oil,
grease and heavy metals could locally contaminate soil and sediments, form surface sheens on



                                                                                                179
                                                                                                Official Use Only
water bodies and cause minor impacts on aquatic organisms, while scattered containers and asphalt
residues could degrade site aesthetics and create localized safety hazards.
    However, given the small quantities involved, the localization of potential impacts to
construction sites and camps, the availability of licensed hazardous waste collection and treatment
service providers in Hai Phong, and the fact that hazardous waste generation is limited to the
construction phase only, hazardous solid waste impacts of the Project are assessed as low.
   4.3.1.6.    Impacts on Ecological Environment
     Construction activities under the Project including vegetation clearance, site preparation,
earthworks, dredging, embankment stabilization, bridge works, and increased human presence
may affect both terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems.
    The Project area is highly urbanised and agriculturally modified, and most terrestrial and
aquatic habitats have been substantially altered. Common, disturbance-tolerant species dominate
throughout the alignment, and no critical habitats, special-use forests, or protected species under
Decree 06/2019 (amended by Decree 84/2021) occur within the construction footprints. Ecological
impacts therefore vary mainly with habitat condition and proximity to waterways.
A. Terrestrial Ecology
(i) Flora
     Terrestrial vegetation within the Project area is highly modified and fragmented, largely
confined to urban roadsides, riverbanks, drainage corridors, agricultural field margins and vacant
lots. No natural or secondary forests or ecologically valuable terrestrial habitats occur within the
project footprint, except for a small area of designated protection forest at the Ruot Lon Bridge,
which is assessed separately as a site-specific impact.
     Component 1, Subcomponent 2.2 and Component 3 are located predominantly within urban
and peri-urban corridors, where vegetation is dominated by common grasses, including Panicum
spp., Cynodon dactylon and Axonopus compressus, together with ruderal herbs and shrubs such as
Bidens pilosa, Ageratum conyzoides, Chromolaena odorata, Eupatorium spp. and Lantana
camara. Scattered planted trees and ornamentals (e.g. mango, jackfruit, banana and ornamental
shade trees) occur around houses, along streets and within institutional compounds. These plant
assemblages are typical of disturbed urban environments, are largely non-native or opportunistic,
and regenerate rapidly after disturbance. Vegetation clearance for trenching, road works, drainage
installation and access to riverbanks results only in the removal of low-value, degraded cover.
Impact significance is assessed as Low.
     Component 2 extends into semi-rural and agricultural fringes, where terrestrial vegetation
includes mixed grasses and reeds (Phragmites australis, Saccharum spontaneum), shrubs along
field boundaries, and seasonal crops such as rice and vegetables. Vegetation along irrigation canals
and embankments is sparse and routinely managed by local farmers. These habitats are already
subject to frequent human disturbance and do not support natural or sensitive plant communities.



                                                                                                180
                                                                                                Official Use Only
Vegetation removal for embankment formation, slope stabilization and WWTP construction
results in minor and localized loss of common species only. Impact significance is assessed as
Low.
(ii) Fauna
    Terrestrial fauna within the Project area is limited to common, disturbance-tolerant species
associated with urban settlements, agricultural land and roadside habitats.
     Component 1, Subcomponent 2.2 and Component 3, which pass through dense urban areas or
mixed residential–commercial corridors, support fauna typical of built-up environments. Recorded
or expected species include common birds such as house sparrow (Passer montanus), common
myna (Acridotheres tristis), bulbuls (Pycnonotus spp.), swallows and pigeons; small reptiles such
as house geckos (Hemidactylus spp.) and skinks; amphibians including common frogs and toads
in drains and gardens; small mammals such as rats and mice; and ubiquitous insects (ants, flies
and mosquitoes). Construction activities may cause temporary displacement or avoidance
behaviour, but habitats are readily recolonized once works move on. No species of conservation
concern are present. Impact significance is assessed as Low to Negligible.
     Component 2 supports a slightly broader but still low-diversity fauna assemblage, including
field rats, squirrels, insectivorous and water-associated birds such as egrets, herons, swallows and
kingfishers, as well as common reptiles and amphibians inhabiting fields and canal edges. These
species are widespread, adaptable and not dependent on the Project area for breeding or long-term
survival. Habitat loss is minor, localized and does not affect regional populations. Impact
significance is assessed as Low.
     Across all components, terrestrial ecosystems are highly modified, fragmented and of low
ecological value, dominated by common plant and animal species adapted to urban, roadside and
agricultural environments. Construction activities will cause only localized and temporary
disturbance, mainly through vegetation clearance and short-term displacement of common fauna.
No critical habitats, protected species or biodiversity-sensitive areas are affected.
B. Aquatic Ecology
(i) Flora
     Aquatic ecosystems within the Project area are associated primarily with major rivers
including the Cam River, Tam Bac River, Re River, Ruot Lon River, Hau Long River and Chu
River and their connected agricultural canals, urban drainage channels and aquaculture ponds. All
these water bodies are highly modified, with regulated banks, altered flow regimes and long-term
exposure to urban and agricultural discharges.
    Aquatic flora in the large tidal rivers (Cam River and Tam Bac River) is generally poor and
unstable. Vegetation is dominated by floating macrophytes such as Eichhornia crassipes (water
hyacinth) and Pistia stratiotes (water lettuce), which occur sporadically in low-velocity zones,
together with filamentous algae and biofilms attached to concrete embankments. Submerged



                                                                                                181
                                                                                                Official Use Only
macrophytes are rare due to high turbidity, fluctuating water levels and disturbed substrates. These
rivers exhibit limited ecological structure, and aquatic plants are frequently removed naturally by
tidal flushing or manually as part of river management.
    The Lach Tray River shows clear spatial differentiation in aquatic and wetland ecology.
Midstream urban sections are ecologically degraded and dominated by disturbance-tolerant
species, similar to the Cam and Tam Bac rivers, while the lower Lach Tray River retains more
natural estuarine and wetland features, including mangroves, tidal mudflats and brackish-water
vegetation, which support higher biological productivity.
     The lower Lach Tray River is of particular ecological and livelihood importance as it supports
polychaete (Tylorrhynchus heterochaetus, “ruoi”) farming and associated estuarine species such
as Tenualosa reevesii, mainly concentrated in estuarine sections within Duong Kinh and Hai An
wards. The main polychaete farming zones are located approximately 20 km downstream along
the river course from the proposed discharge point of the An Duong 1 Wastewater Treatment Plant
(WWTP)
    In the secondary rivers and tributaries (Re, Ruot Lon, Hau Long and Chu Rivers) and
associated irrigation canals, aquatic flora is more visible but remains ecologically low-value.
Common plant groups include emergent grasses and reeds along banks (e.g. Typha spp.,
Phragmites spp.), floating vegetation (Eichhornia, Lemna), submerged or semi-submerged species
such as Hydrilla and Ceratophyllum in sheltered reaches, and periphyton and algae attached to
canal beds and structures. These assemblages reflect eutrophic conditions and slow-flow
environments typical of agricultural and peri-urban waterways.
     Construction activities may cause temporary increases in turbidity and localized sediment
deposition, which can partially smother attached algae or floating vegetation. However, given the
dominance of fast-growing, disturbance-tolerant species, recovery is expected to be rapid once
construction ceases. No rare, endemic or protected aquatic plant species are present. Impact
significance on aquatic flora is assessed as Low to Moderate, depending on the hydraulic capacity
of each water body.
(ii) Fauna
     Aquatic fauna across all project-related water bodies is dominated by common freshwater and
brackish species adapted to turbid, nutrient-rich and human-influenced environments.The Cam
River and Tam Bac River, as large tidal rivers, support fish communities consisting mainly of
tilapia (Oreochromis spp.), mullet (Mugil spp.), small catfish (Clarias spp.), gobies, and juvenile
brackish-water species tolerant of salinity fluctuation. Benthic fauna is dominated by snails (e.g.
Bellamya spp.), bivalves and tubificid worms, which are typical indicators of organically enriched
sediments. These rivers do not function as spawning grounds or migratory corridors for sensitive
species due to intensive navigation, embankment hardening and degraded water quality.
    In the Re, Ruot Lon, Hau Long and Chu Rivers, irrigation canals and aquaculture-connected
ponds, fish assemblages are composed mainly of common freshwater species such as carp


                                                                                                182
                                                                                                Official Use Only
(Cyprinus carpio), tilapia, snakehead (Channa spp.), climbing perch (Anabas testudineus) and
mud loach (Misgurnus spp.). Aquatic invertebrates include snails, small crustaceans, insect larvae
and oligochaete worms, all of which are tolerant of low dissolved oxygen and high nutrient levels.
These species are commonly associated with agricultural drainage systems and fish ponds.
     Construction-related sediment release, dewatering and runoff may temporarily disturb feeding
activities and benthic habitats near active works. However, species present are highly resilient,
populations are already accustomed to fluctuating water quality, and impacts are expected to be
localized, short-term and reversible. No endangered, protected or ecologically sensitive aquatic
fauna species are recorded within the Project area.
     Aquatic ecosystems within the Project area are artificial or heavily modified, dominated by
common, disturbance-tolerant flora and fauna associated with urban rivers, irrigation canals and
aquaculture systems. Construction impacts are limited to temporary increases in turbidity,
localized sediment deposition and short-term disturbance to common species, with no long-term
alteration of ecological function. Excluding the site-specific impact related to protection forest
acquisition at the Ruot Lon Bridge (assessed separately), given the absence of critical habitats,
protected species or ecologically sensitive areas within the construction footprints, the dominance
of common and disturbance-tolerant flora and fauna, and the localized and reversible nature of
construction-related disturbance, the overall ecological impact of the Project during the
construction phase is assessed as moderate.
    4.3.1.7. Impacts on Local Road Traffic
     The construction activities such as transportation of materials and equipment, excavation,
trenching for drainage and sewer installation, and roadbed construction will take place mainly
along existing transport corridors and urban streets within Hai Phong City. These works will
temporarily occupy part of the road surface and introduce a considerable number of heavy vehicles
and construction machinery into the existing traffic system. As a result, the following potential
impacts are expected during the construction phase: (i) Traffic congestion caused by the
occupation of road sections for construction of drainage and sewer networks, particularly during
peak commuting hours; and (ii) Conflicts between construction vehicles/equipment and regular
road users due to shared use of narrow urban streets; and increased risk of traffic accidents
resulting from reduced visibility, dusty surfaces, and temporary detours.

     Traffic impacts during construction vary substantially depending on truck volumes, baseline
traffic conditions, road width and construction duration. Component 1 generates substantial
construction traffic impacts due to very high heavy-truck volumes, averaging approximately 390
trips per day over a 720-day construction period. These movements occur along PR351, PR359,
NH10, NH5A and associated urban and local roads that already experience medium to high
baseline traffic, including mixed residential, commercial and port-related traffic as well as high
proportions of heavy trucks and container vehicles on the national highways. At the interchange
areas, construction activities and intensive truck movements are expected to cause localized
congestion, lane restrictions and temporary traffic diversions, further reducing travel speeds and
increasing delay and traffic safety risks for local residents and road users. Subcomponent 2.1 and
Subcomponent 2.2 generate moderate traffic impacts. Subcomponent 2.1 involves around 42 truck
trips per day along river management roads and connecting corridors with low to medium baseline
traffic, where additional construction traffic may intermittently affect local circulation and access.
Subcomponent 2.2 generates approximately 30 truck trips per day, mainly on internal agricultural
roads and a planned access road to the WWTP, where baseline traffic is very low and impacts
remain localized and manageable with standard traffic management measures. Component 3
generates low construction traffic impacts, as truck volumes are limited to approximately 10 trips
per day over a longer construction duration of about 1,440 days. Although works are undertaken
along narrow streets and busy inner-city roads with very high baseline traffic, the low frequency



                                                                                                  184
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
of heavy vehicles results in short-term and localized disturbances mainly during material delivery
or removal activities.
    Considering the construction truck volumes associated with project’s components and the
overall duration of construction activities, construction traffic impacts are characterized by
temporary, location-specific increases in traffic volume and associated disturbances along
construction access routes. Exposure at any given location is limited to the period when active
construction and material transport are underway. On this basis, the overall construction traffic
impact of the Project is assessed as moderate.
   4.3.1.8.    Impacts related to labor influx
     Construction will require temporary mobilisation of workers, creating localized pressures on
domestic waste and wastewater management, temporary accommodation, and community
interactions at all contruction sites. These impacts are temporary and reversible, and depend mainly
on workforce size, construction duration, and the location and management of worker
accommodation.
    Higher labor-influx pressure occurs where workforce size and construction duration are
greatest (notably Components 1 and 3), which together account for over two-thirds of total
domestic waste during construction (about 105 tons of 150 tons). In these areas, workers are mainly
accommodated in peri-urban or urban fringe zones; although local waste loads increase (e.g. about
53 t under Component 1), existing municipal services reduce community sensitivity. Moderate
pressure occurs in river-corridor and peri-urban areas where temporary camps may be established
(mainly Subcomponent 2.1), potentially increasing demand for sanitation and waste services and
causing minor, short-term social disturbance in nearby communities if camp management is
inadequate. Low pressure is expected where workforces are smaller and most workers commute
or use existing rental housing (mainly Subcomponent 2.2), resulting in limited additional waste
generation and minimal community disturbance.
     Overall, labor-influx impacts are assessed as Moderate, driven mainly by works with large
workforces and long durations (especially Components 1 and 3). Affected receptors are households
near camps and access routes, which may experience short-term pressure on sanitation, waste
management and minor social disturbance during construction (about 24–48 months). With proper
camp management, codes of conduct and waste-handling measures (per the LMP), these impacts
are expected to remain manageable, localized and fully reversible.
   4.3.1.9.    Impacts due to improper access
     During construction, temporary access restrictions will occur wherever works occupy parts of
existing roads, sidewalks, pedestrian corridors or building frontages (Components 1, 2 and 3).
These restrictions result from fencing, hoarding, machinery placement, trenching and partial
laneclosures, and may temporarily affect access to houses, small shops, markets and community
facilities.




                                                                                                185
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     The magnitude of impact depends mainly on street width, traffic and pedestrian density, and
the extent to which works overlap with existing access routes. Along major roads and bridge
approaches (notably under Component 1), road and bridge construction and associated drainage
or utility works may temporarily narrow or block entrances to houses, shops and local alleys,
especially in narrow urban sections. Along river corridors and management roads (mainly under
Subcomponent 2.1), sewer trenching and embankment works may reduce the usable width of local
lanes serving residential clusters and agricultural plots, intermittently affecting household
driveways, small businesses and pedestrian movement. In contrast, where works are largely
confined within facility sites and adjacent agricultural land (Subcomponent 2.2), interaction with
residential and commercial access routes is limited and access disruption is minimal.
     In the most spatially constrained urban areas of the Tam Bac basin (Component 3), excavation
for culverts, box drains, sluices and pumping-station structures requires partial lane closures along
narrow streets and riverfront corridors, temporarily affecting access to shop-houses, markets,
eateries and riverside pedestrian paths. Although inconvenience is more noticeable in these high-
density areas, works progress in short rolling sections (typically about 150–200 m), and temporary
bridges, walkways and ramps are provided to maintain movement.
    Overall, access obstruction is localized, short-term (usually about 2–3 months per frontage)
and fully reversible once surfaces are reinstated. With proper phasing, advance notice and
provision of safe alternative access, access-related impacts during construction are assessed as
Moderate.
   4.3.1.10. Disruption of business activities along streets
     During construction, temporary business disruption will occur due to access restrictions,
partial lane closures, trenching, fencing and occupation of sidewalks and road shoulders at all
locations where works are carried out. These disturbances may reduce customer visibility, restrict
direct shop access, limit parking and loading space, and temporarily decrease pedestrian flow,
thereby affecting turnover of street-front businesses.
     The magnitude of impact depends mainly on commercial density, street width, traffic intensity
and the duration of access obstruction. In corridors with continuous rows of shops and mixed-use
frontage such as sections of Ring Road No. 3 and inner-city streets (notably under Component 1
and especially Component 3) construction activities may affect groceries, cafés, retail shops, repair
stalls, food vendors and market-related businesses. In these areas, excavation and utility works
may temporarily block or narrow shopfront access and sidewalks, creating short-term congestion
and reducing customer access, particularly in wet-market areas and dense retail or food-service
streets.
    In areas with lower commercial density or more peri-urban characteristics such as bridge
approach zones, riverbank corridors and access roads to major facilities (mainly under parts of
Component 1 and Component 2) business activities are fewer and more scattered. Here, any




                                                                                                 186
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
disruption is generally localized to small household-based or service-type businesses and is of
shorter duration and lower intensity.
     Across the Project, business-disruption impacts are confined to active construction fronts
(typically about 150–200 m at a time), temporary in nature (often around 2–3 months per frontage),
and fully reversible once works move on and surfaces are reinstated. Considering the concentration
of commercial activities along several major corridors and the rolling construction approach
(especially under Component 3 and parts of Component 1), the overall significance of business-
disruption impacts during construction is assessed as Moderate.
   4.3.1.11. Impacts from the Relocation of Existing Infrastructure
     The relocation of existing technical infrastructure including electricity lines, water supply
pipelines, telecom and fiber-optic cables, street-lighting systems, and irrigation/drainage ditches
will occur where construction intersects existing utility corridors. This may cause temporary
service interruptions, access constraints and localized safety risks during trenching, diversion and
reinstatement works. Based on the RP and engineering layout, the table below summarises for each
componennt: (i) the specific types of infrastructure requiring relocation, (ii) the receptors indirectly
affected by service interruptions or access constraints, and (iii) the typical duration of disruption
associated with each utility type. This provides a consolidated basis for assessing the magnitude
and significance of relocation-related impacts during construction.

     Impact magnitude depends mainly on utility density, urban context and receptor sensitivity.
In densely built-up and commercially active areas (notably under Components 2 and 3), where
multiple networks are co-located in narrow corridors, short shutdowns (typically several hours per
intervention) may inconvenience households, disrupt small businesses and affect services reliant
on uninterrupted electricity, water and internet. Constrained working space also increases traffic
and pedestrian safety risks. In peri-urban and semi-rural sections (mainly under Component 1),
networks are more dispersed and fewer users depend on continuous services; interruptions are
shorter, access constraints more manageable, and overall disturbance is lower.
     Overall, utility relocation impacts are temporary, localized and fully reversible, but given the
essential nature of these services and the presence of high-density, sensitive areas (especially under
Components 2 and 3), the significance of this impact is assessed as Moderate, reflecting the
likelihood of repeated but short-duration disruptions during construction.
   4.3.1.12. Impacts on aquaculture and fishing activities
    The Project affects aquaculture and small-scale fishing through: (i) permanent loss of
aquaculture ponds due to land acquisition, and (ii) temporary disturbance to fishing activities and
aquatic conditions during construction.
     Permanent impacts on aquaculture assets occur mainly under Components 1 and 2, where
household-operated ponds (typically 200–1,000 m²) cultivating common freshwater species (e.g.
tilapia, carp, catfish, mudfish) are fully or partially acquired. These ponds are part of integrated


                                                                                                  188
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
household livelihood systems in peri-urban areas; their acquisition therefore represents a
permanent loss of productive assets at the household level. This impact is most significant along
the Hau Long and Chu river corridors and sections of Ring Road No. 3 (Component 1), and along
the Re River corridor and within the An Duong 1 WWTP footprint (Component 2). More
livelihood-sensitive shrimp farming areas in Nam Trieu Ward are addressed separately under the
site-specific assessment. Overall, this permanent impact is assessed as Moderate and is addressed
through compensation and livelihood restoration measures under the RP.
     Temporary impacts on fishing and aquatic conditions may occur along the Hau Long, Chu,
Cam, Ruot Lon, Re and Tam Bac rivers under Components 1, 2 and 3, due to riverbank works,
dredging, cofferdams and slope protection. These activities may cause short-term increases in
turbidity, reducing fishing efficiency within limited downstream distances (typically 100–300 m).
Fishing is small-scale, non-commercial and supplementary; therefore, these impacts are localized,
short-term and reversible. In urban waterways under Component 3, which function mainly as
drainage channels, no aquaculture is present and fishing is only occasional; related impacts are
assessed as Low.
    Overall, impacts on aquaculture and fishing are assessed as Moderate, driven mainly by
permanent pond acquisition under Components 1 and 2, while construction-related disturbances
elsewhere remain temporary and low in significance.
   4.3.1.13. Impacts on agricultural production
     Construction activities under Component 1 and Component 2 interface with agricultural areas
to varying degrees, ranging from fragmented household garden plots to active rice paddies and
mixed-crop systems in peri-urban zones. Potential impacts include temporary obstruction of field
access, disturbance of irrigation ditches, localised soil compaction along access routes, and short-
term sediment deposition during heavy rainfall events. These effects are location-specific,
seasonal, and linked to the timing of earthworks relative to cropping cycles. Detailed assessment
of agricultural impacts under Component 1 and Component 2, including site-specific conditions,
livelihood sensitivity, and mitigation measures, is therefore addressed separately under the site-
specific impact assessment section. Accordingly, impacts at this level are characterised only in
general terms.
     By contrast, Component 3 is located entirely within the dense inner-city environment of the
Tam Bac basin, where agricultural land has already been fully converted to residential,
commercial, and service uses. No rice paddies, seasonal crops, household gardens, or other forms
of agricultural production are present adjacent to construction areas. As a result, construction
activities under Component 3 do not interfere with agricultural land use, crop production, irrigation
systems, or farming access. No pathways exist for construction runoff, soil deposition, or access
restriction to affect agricultural activities.




                                                                                                 189
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    In summary, while agricultural impacts under Component 1 and Component 2 may occur
locally and are assessed in detail under the site-specific impact analysis. At the project-wide level,
impacts on agriculture are therefore considered Moderate.
   4.3.1.14. Risks of Local Flooding
    Construction activities across the Project may temporarily disrupt existing surface drainage
paths and small local channels due to excavation, trenching, installation of culverts and manholes,
and the construction of embankments, foundations, and utility corridors. If temporary drainage
arrangements are not adequately maintained, these obstructions may reduce runoff conveyance
capacity during heavy rainfall events, leading to short-term and localized surface water ponding
in construction areas and adjacent low-lying locations. Such temporary inundation could affect
construction mobility, slow down traffic, and cause short-lived inconvenience to nearby
households and businesses, as well as pose a risk to unfinished works and equipment in open
excavation areas.
    This impact is most likely to occur in the following situations:
   -   Along roadside and urban streets where open-cut trenching and utility installation works
       temporarily interrupt roadside drains and small agricultural or urban ditches (notably under
       Component 1 and parts of Component 2 and 3);
   -   Along the Re River embankment corridor and at major structures (Component 2), where
       dredging, excavation, backfilling, and the construction of pumping stations and the An
       Duong 1 WWTP foundations may temporarily obstruct natural overland flow paths or
       create low points that retain stormwater;
   -   Within the dense urban drainage network of the Tam Bac basin (Component 3), where
       installation of regulating sluices, pumping stations, canal edge reconstruction, and new
       stormwater conduits may temporarily narrow or block existing drainage channels that
       already operate close to capacity, particularly during high-intensity rainfall and tidal
       influence.
    The risk is highest during the rainy season (typically June–October), especially in urban areas
with limited spare hydraulic capacity and in locations with multiple concurrent excavation fronts.
The Project design and construction approach incorporate appropriate construction sequencing and
method selection in constrained urban and riverine environments, as reflected in the Feasibility
Study. With these considerations, the potential for flooding is expected to remain localized and
short-term. Overall, the significance of this impact across all components is assessed as Moderate,
temporary, and reversible.
   4.3.1.15. Risks of Embankment Subsidence and Bank Erosion during Heavy Rains
     During construction, the project may face geotechnical risks related to embankment
instability, riverbank erosion and local ground settlement, especially in areas with soft alluvial
soils and strong river influence (mainly Subcomponent 2.1, and to a lesser extent Components 1
and 3). These risks depend on soil strength, groundwater conditions, surcharge loading, excavation
methods and the duration of exposure of temporary slopes and unfinished embankments.



                                                                                                  190
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
     The highest risk occurs along the Re River embankment (Subcomponent 2.1), where works
are carried out on soft clay and silty sand (undrained shear strength typically 20–40 kPa and lower
in the rainy season). Elevated pore pressures, fill loading and temporary removal of vegetation
may cause surface sloughing, shallow failures and differential settlement. Given the linear extent
of works and direct hydraulic connection to the river, the pre-mitigation risk of localized instability
during heavy rainfall is Substantial until permanent revetment (RC walls, Neoweb, rock lining) is
completed.
     Moderate, localized risks occur near river-adjacent works near Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot
Lon Bridge (Component 1), temporary clearance of riparian vegetation increases susceptibility to
erosion until permanent works are in place; minor, short-term erosion may also occur near the Cam
and Ruot Lon Rivers during cofferdam and excavation works. At the regulating sluice gates and
pumping station (Component 3), excavation and dewatering close to the Tam Bac River and urban
structures, combined with tidal fluctuations, may induce minor soil movement, local erosion or
short-term settlement, but these are manageable with staged excavation, shoring and drainage
control.
     Low geotechnical risk applies to works on engineered or relatively stable ground, including
the Ring Road No. 3 (Component 1), the subcomponent 2.1 and the urban drainage and wastewater
collection system (Component 3), where excavation is shallow, groundwater interaction is limited
and no natural riverbanks are involved.
     Considering soil conditions, construction methods and exposure duration, and noting that risks
reduce rapidly once permanent structures and revetment are in place, the overall impact of
embankment instability, bank erosion and ground settlement is assessed as Moderate, and can be
effectively managed through proper construction sequencing, temporary stabilization, drainage
management and erosion-control measures.
   4.3.1.16. Risks of Natural Hazards
     Component 3 carries the highest natural-hazard risks in the Project. These works are located
within the densely built, low-lying Tam Bac basin, which is highly sensitive to intense rainfall,
tidal surges and typhoon-induced flooding. Construction activities take place immediately adjacent
to urban waterways where water levels can rise rapidly during storm events. Heavy rainfall
coinciding with high tides may cause overtopping of temporary works, rapid inflow into
excavations, and short-term inundation of surrounding streets. Narrow construction corridors,
typically 5–7 m wide, constrain temporary drainage capacity and increase the likelihood of water
accumulation around excavation pits, formwork and temporary shoring. In addition, strong winds
during typhoons pose risks to cranes, scaffolding, sheet piles and other temporary structures
concentrated along riverfront works. Given the dense urban setting and limited buffer distances to
surrounding receptors, natural-hazard risks under Component 3 are assessed as Moderate.
    Component 1 and Subcomponent 2.1 also present Moderate natural-hazard risks associated
with heavy rainfall, seasonal storms and typhoons typical of Hai Phong between July and October.



                                                                                                   191
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
Along Component 1, prolonged rainfall may flood open excavations for drainage, utilities and road
structures, temporarily reducing soil bearing capacity and requiring pumping and slope protection
to maintain workability. Bridge works under Component 1, including cofferdam installation,
abutment excavation and pile driving near riverbanks, may experience short-term flooding or
elevated flow velocities during storm events, necessitating reinforcement of temporary works and
staged construction. Under Subcomponent 2.1, construction along the Re River embankment and
management roads is exposed to rapid river-level rise during heavy rains or typhoon-driven surges,
potentially affecting unfinished embankment sections and temporary working platforms. In these
areas, risks remain localized, short-term and manageable through seasonal scheduling, controlled
excavation, temporary drainage measures and pre-storm preparedness.
     Subcomponent 2.2 exhibits Low natural-hazard risks. Construction is undertaken on relatively
flat reclaimed ground, away from active riverbanks and major tidal influences. While heavy
rainfall may cause temporary water accumulation in open excavations for tanks and foundations,
the site is not exposed to tidal surges or strong river currents. Potential impacts are limited to short-
term waterlogging, pumping requirements and minor construction delays. With standard
stormwater management, excavation support and equipment protection measures, natural-hazard
risks at the WWTP site remain Low and readily mitigated.
     Based on the above analysis, natural-hazard risks during construction are primarily associated
with heavy rainfall, tidal influence and typhoon events and vary spatially across the project. The
highest risks arise in low-lying, hydraulically active urban areas where construction interfaces
directly with rivers and drainage systems, while risks elsewhere are more localized and episodic.
Overall, these risks are temporary and largely predictable. Accordingly, the level of impact is
assessed as Moderate, and they can be effectively managed through appropriate construction
sequencing, seasonal planning, temporary drainage and stabilization measures, and emergency
preparedness procedures.
    4.3.1.17. Community and Worker Health and Safety
(i) Risks of traffic accidents
    Construction activities will increase traffic-accident risks due to reduced road space,
temporary traffic arrangements and the interaction between construction vehicles and existing
mixed traffic (Components 1, 2 and 3). The level of risk depends mainly on traffic density, street
width, presence of vulnerable road users and construction traffic intensity.
     The most critical risk arises in dense inner-city areas (Component 3 and Subcomponent 2.2),
where works are carried out along very narrow urban streets, typically about 3–7 m wide, with
intense pedestrian and motorcycle flows, continuous shopfronts, markets and roadside activities.
In these areas, background traffic is dominated by motorcycles and pedestrians, with limited
physical separation and few alternative routes. Even with modest construction-truck volumes
(typically around 5–10 truck trips per day), fencing and hoarding along the work fronts, reduced
visibility, and frequent access and turning movements markedly increase the likelihood of



                                                                                                    192
                                                                                                     Official Use Only
pedestrian conflicts, motorcycle side-swipe collisions, reversing-vehicle accidents and rear-end
crashes. Given the very high concentration of vulnerable road users and the prolonged construction
duration in inner-city sections, pre-mitigation accident risk in these areas is assessed as Substantial.
     Moderate accident risk applies in river-corridor and peri-urban sections (mainly
Subcomponent 2.1), where background traffic volumes are generally low to moderate, but
construction generates frequent heavy-vehicle movements for fill, rock and revetment materials.
Localized conflicts may occur with motorcycles, agricultural vehicles and nearby residents,
particularly at constrained intersections and access points, where limited shoulder width and
turning radii increase the risk of side-swipe and turning-movement accidents. Although
Component 1 generates the highest overall construction traffic volume across the Project, accident
risks at major junctions and strategic nodes (including intersections with national and provincial
roads) are addressed separately under site-specific impact assessments. At a corridor level,
increased interaction with mixed traffic near village access points and local road connections
remains a relevant concern.
     Overall, because a significant share of works takes place in highly constrained urban streets
with large numbers of vulnerable road users (notably Component 3 and Subcomponent 2.2), the
traffic-accident risk for the Project during construction is assessed as Substantial prior to
mitigation.
(ii) Risks of working on or near water
    Occupational health and safety risks associated with working on or near water differ markedly
across components and are strongly influenced by hydrological conditions and seasonal weather.
     Subcomponent 2.1 (Construction and Upgrading of Re River Embankment and Management
Roads) carries the highest water-related OHS risk. Construction proceeds along soft alluvial
riverbanks subject to rainfall, rapid river-level rise and seasonal flooding. During the June–October
rainy and typhoon season, excavation zones and temporary platforms may be inundated, slopes
may slough, and saturated ground conditions can destabilize machinery. Workers operating on
narrow river-edge platforms face elevated risks of slips, falls and drowning. Prior to mitigation,
storm-related OHS risk for this subcomponent is assessed as Substantial.
     Component 1 (Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge) and Component 3 (Regulating sluice
gates and stormwater pumping station) present Moderate risks associated with over-water and
river-adjacent construction. Bridge works involve cofferdams, barges, scaffolding and lifting
operations above water, while sluice and riverbank works occur in tidal urban corridors where
rapid water-level fluctuations are common. Sudden storms or combined rainfall–tide events may
inundate work areas, destabilize temporary structures and increase fall and equipment-overturn
risks, particularly during July–September.
     Component 1 (Ring Road No.3 road sections), Subcomponent 2.2 (An Duong WWTP) and
inland portions of Component 3 (urban drainage) involve predominantly terrestrial works.
Although heavy rainfall may cause temporary trench flooding, slippery surfaces or localized runoff


                                                                                                    193
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
accumulation, construction fronts are generally stable and not directly exposed to dynamic river
or tidal processes. With basic drainage control, housekeeping and weather-responsive work
scheduling, water-related OHS risks for these components remain Low.
(iii) Labor accidents (OHS risks)
     The construction activities across all key investment items involve a wide range of
occupational health and safety (OHS) hazards arising from heavy equipment operation, confined
and deep excavations, work at height, lifting operations, vehicle–worker interaction, and proximity
to water bodies. To clearly identify where key risks occur and how they may affect workers, a
consolidated OHS risk matrix has been developed. The matrix lists: (i) the major OHS risks that
are reasonably expected during construction; (ii) the corresponding potential consequences for
worker safety; and (iii) the specific key investment items where each risk is most relevant. This
structured presentation allows for consistent assessment of risk significance, prioritisation of
mitigation measures, and alignment of contractor OHS obligations across all project sites.

     Across all key investment items, construction works involve multiple high-risk activities—
heavy machinery operation in constrained corridors, deep excavation and trenching, lifting
operations, work at height, and vehicle–worker interface along busy transport routes. These risks
are heightened at bridge works, sewer corridors, the Re River embankment, and the
sluice/pumping-station sites where space is limited, soil conditions are unstable, or interaction with
public traffic is unavoidable. Although most OHS risks are localized and short-term, their
frequency and severity potential result in an overall Substantial risk rating for the Project. With
strict application of OHS management systems, worker training, confined-space procedures,
traffic control measures, and engineering safeguards, residual risks can be reduced to acceptable
levels during implementation
(iv). Community health and safety (CHS) risks
     The table below consolidates the principal community health and safety (CHS) risks
associated with construction activities across all key investment items in Component 1,2 & 3. The
assessment considers the diverse socio-spatial contexts of the Project, ranging from densely
populated urban corridors around the Tam Bac Basin (Regulating sluice gates and stormwater
pumping station–Urban stormwater drainage and wastewater collection system), to mixed
residential–agricultural areas along the Ring Road No.3, to riverside settlements and narrow alleys
adjacent to the Re River embankment and sewer trenches (Construction and Upgrading of Re River
Embankment and Management Roads). Potentially affected community receptors include roadside
households, shopfront businesses, schoolchildren, pedestrians, market users, commuters,
riverbank users, and vulnerable groups such as the elderly and children living near excavation or
canal improvement sites.
     The risk matrix identifies, for each major hazard, the corresponding community-level
consequences and the specific key investment items where exposure is expected. These risks
derive from interactions between construction activities and public circulation areas, changes in
traffic patterns, temporary obstruction of access, proximity to excavations or waterways, and the
constrained nature of many work fronts within residential neighbourhoods.

     Component 1 involves extensive linear construction works along mixed residential–
commercial corridors and peri-urban areas, where construction traffic, open excavations and heavy
machinery interact with local traffic, pedestrians and roadside households. Community health and
safety risks mainly relate to traffic conflicts, temporary access obstruction, dust, noise and
vibration, and falls into open trenches. Risks are generally localized and moderate, with higher
exposure at busy road sections and shared traffic corridors.
    Component 2, particularly Subcomponent 2.1, is implemented within narrow riverbank and
sewer corridors adjacent to dense residential clusters and agricultural settlements. Construction
takes place close to houses, local access paths and waterways, increasing the likelihood of traffic
conflicts, falls into excavations, exposure to contaminated sediments, temporary flooding and risks
associated with working near water. These conditions result in the highest concentration of
community health and safety risks within the Project.
    Component 3 is carried out in the most spatially constrained and densely populated urban
environment of the Tam Bac basin. Construction fronts are located along narrow inner-city streets
and riverbanks with very high pedestrian and motorcycle flows, limited working space and close
proximity to shops, markets and public spaces. As a result, risks related to traffic conflicts, falls
into open trenches, exposure to machinery, flooding of excavations and working near tidal
waterways are pronounced during active construction.
    Overall, community health and safety impacts for the Project are assessed as Moderate. This
assessment reflects the concentration of risks in densely populated and spatially constrained areas
under Component 2 and Component 3, combined with more moderate but widespread risks along
Component 1 corridors. All identified risks are temporary and location-specific, occurring mainly
during active construction phases and moving progressively as work fronts advance. With rolling
construction, reinstatement of roads and drainage, and implementation of standard traffic



                                                                                                 197
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
management, site safety and community protection measures, community exposure is expected to
reduce rapidly once construction moves beyond each locality.
   4.3.1.18. Risks of Labor Violence and Hard-Working Conditions
(i) Use of Child Labor and Forced Labor
     In some construction projects, contractors may attempt to reduce costs by employing underage
or unqualified workers for simple tasks. Under Vietnamese law, child labor refers to employment
of persons under 15 years of age. The use of child or forced labor is strictly prohibited under both
national regulations and international standards (Vietnam Labor Code 2019; ESS2 – Labor and
Working Conditions). The Project is assessed as having Low risk of child or forced labor because:
   •  no previous cases were reported in Bank-financed or state-budget infrastructure projects in
      Hai Phong;
   • socio-economic surveys conducted during the ESIA preparation revealed no evidence of
      child labor in the area;
   • contractors are contractually obligated not to employ workers under the minimum legal
      age or any form of forced labor;
   • PMU and local authorities will monitor compliance through regular inspections; and
   • adherence to national labor laws and inclusion of clauses prohibiting child and forced labor
      are mandatory requirements in all bidding and contract documents.
    Therefore, the likelihood of this impact is minimal and the risk is assessed as Minor.
(ii) Labor Violence and Hard-Working Conditions
    Types of workers. The project workforce will comprise direct workers (employed by the
PMU), contracted workers (hired by construction contractors, supervision consultants, or
independent monitoring consultants), and primary supply workers (employed by suppliers
providing construction materials such as sand, stone, cement, aggregates, bitumen, and pre-cast
concrete products). No community workers will be engaged.
    Direct workers. The PMU’s direct staff will include administrative, environmental, social,
and financial management officers, responsible for supervising and coordinating project activities.
These staff members will work mainly in office settings and are not exposed to significant
occupational risks.
    Contracted workers. Approximately seven civil works contractors will be engaged to
implement key investment items under Components 1, 2, and 3, with an estimated total of about
460–500 construction workers at peak period. Workers will include skilled and unskilled
laborers, machine operators, drivers, and site supervisors. They will be mobilized at different times
and locations across Road No. 3, bridge sites, Re River embankment, and Tam Bac river works.
    Primary supply workers. Materials for the project will be procured from licensed quarries,
batching plants, and suppliers within Hai Phong and nearby provinces. The number and type of
primary suppliers will be finalized during construction. Workers from these suppliers will be
subject to ESS2 requirements to ensure fair labor practices, adequate working conditions, and
occupational health and safety (OHS) compliance.
