                                                                                                 198
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
     Community workers. None are expected, as all construction will be performed by
professional contractors. Any local residents temporarily employed for simple tasks (e.g., site
cleaning, vegetation removal) will be treated as contracted workers under ESS2.
     Other stakeholders. Project-related government officials from departments such as DOC,
DAE, and DOF will remain under their existing employment governed by the Vietnam Labor Code
and Law on Civil Servants. They will not be exposed to construction-related OHS risks. Potential
risks include physical strain, fatigue due to long working hours, poor housing and sanitation at
worker camps, or conflicts between workers. However, these impacts are localized, short-term,
and manageable due to the following factors:
    • working sites are scattered and limited in size;
    • construction duration for each work package ranges from 6 to 24 months;
    • workers will be provided with protective equipment and rest breaks; and
    • the Environmental and Social Code of Practices (ESCOPs) and Contractor’s Occupational
        Health and Safety Plans include mandatory measures on labor safety, working hours,
        sanitation, and welfare.
     Regular safety training, health monitoring, and grievance redress mechanisms for workers will
further mitigate risks. Hence, the overall impact level is Low to Moderate.
    4.3.1.19. Gender-Based Violence (GBV)
     At each construction site, approximately 30–100 workers will be mobilized, with about 70–
80% being men. The temporary concentration of a male-dominated workforce may create risks of
gender-based violence (GBV), sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), and sexual harassment (SH)
towards women workers or nearby residents. Potential social consequences include tension in
families, domestic violence, and spread of communicable or sexually transmitted diseases
(HIV/AIDS, syphilis, hepatitis). These impacts may affect both women employed on-site and
women living near worker camps, particularly in urban areas under road, bridge works,
embankment, and sluices and pumping stations.
     Nevertheless, these risks are considered Minor, because:
    • the number of non-local workers is limited, and most are expected to be recruited locally;
    • previous socio-economic surveys and consultations revealed no existing evidence of GBV
        or SEA cases in similar construction contexts;
    • contractors are required to implement a GBV/SEA Action Plan, including training on
        gender sensitivity, awareness of social and communicable diseases, and sanctions for
        violations;
    • worker Codes of Conduct (CoC) will be enforced at all construction sites, clearly defining
        unacceptable behaviors; and
    • close coordination will be maintained between contractors, PMU, and local authorities to
        monitor worker behavior and respond to any complaints.
     Preventive measures will include awareness campaigns, confidential grievance channels, and
cooperation with local Women’s Unions to ensure protection and support for potentially affected
women.




                                                                                              199
                                                                                              Official Use Only
    4.3.1.20. Impacts on Physical Cultural Resources (PCRs)
     As identified in Section 2.6, there are no nationally or internationally recognized tangible or
intangible cultural heritage sites located within the direct or indirect area of influence of the
Project. None of the key investment items intersect with protected monuments, temples, pagodas,
archaeological relics, or other officially registered cultural properties in Hai Phong city.
     However, several local shrines, communal houses, temples, and cemeteries are situated in
proximity to project alignments, particularly along Road No. 3, Ngo Quyen Bridge, Re River
embankment, and Tam Bac River area. These sites are listed in Table 2.1, Table 2.2 and Table 2.3.
Construction activities may temporarily affect the operation and accessibility of these PCRs due
to:
    • temporary obstruction of entrance paths caused by fencing or excavation;
    • increased dust, noise, and vibration during road and drainage construction; and
    • exhaust gases and safety concerns affecting visitors and local residents.
     Such impacts are temporary, indirect, and of low significance, as works will not encroach
upon the PCRs themselves and can be fully mitigated through the following measures:
(i) maintenance of pedestrian and access routes to cultural sites during construction; (ii) advance
notice to local communities and site custodians prior to works; (iii) installation of noise barriers
and regular dust suppression; and (iv) prohibition of material storage or equipment parking near
temples or shrines.
Potential for Chance Finds
     Construction of roads, bridges, drainage and wastewater pipelines, embankments, regulation
lakes, and pumping stations will require excavation at various depths, including:
    • embankment piling at 8–12 m (Re River embankment);
    • bridge foundations and abutments at up to 20 m (Ngo Quyen bridge, Ngo Quyen bridge);
    • roadbed excavation at 0.5–1.0 m (the Ring Road No.3, Ngo Quyen bridge); and
    • excavation for the An Duong 1 WWTP and Tam Bac River improvements (2–3 m).
     Although these works involve substantial earthworks, the likelihood of encountering
archaeological or historical artifacts is considered Low, since most construction will take place
along existing rights-of-way, previously developed land, or agricultural areas that have been
disturbed over decades of urbanization. Nevertheless, a Chance Finds Procedure is included in the
Environmental and Social Code of Practices (ESCOPs) to ensure compliance with the Law on
Cultural Heritage (2001, amended 2009)

   4.3.2. Site-specific impacts of Component 1
   4.3.2.1.    Pollution risk due to improper management of saline organic topsoil from
               aquaculture areas
     A site-specific impact with potential risk may arise from stripping and handling of the organic
topsoil layer in acquired aquaculture areas along the section from Km 6+500 to Km 8+900.
According to the Resettlement Plan (RP), approximately 16.32 ha of shrimp ponds in Nam Trieu
Ward will be acquired for construction of Ring Road No. 3. These are brackish-water shrimp ponds
that have been operated for a long period, where pond-bottom sludge and organic topsoil have


                                                                                                200
                                                                                                Official Use Only
accumulated as a result of long-term deposition of organic matter, dissolved salts, and minerals
associated with water intake and discharge during aquaculture activities.
     The organic topsoil layer in this area will be stripped to an average thickness of approximately
0.3 m to facilitate road embankment construction. The total volume of wet sludge generated,
including pond-bottom sediment and organic topsoil, is estimated at approximately 48,960 m³
requiring disposal. This organic soil material will be temporarily stockpiled on site for a period of
1–2 weeks to allow moisture reduction, after which it will be transported to the disposal site
operated by Toan Thang Company, a licensed facility for treatment of degraded soil.
     Scientific studies indicate that organic topsoil in long-term brackish-water shrimp ponds
typically exhibits moderate to high salinity and elevated organic content. Boyd and Tucker (1998)
and Avnimelech (2015) report that electrical conductivity of shrimp pond soils commonly ranges
from 3–10 mS/cm, while domestic studies in the northern coastal region, including Hai Phong,
have recorded EC values of 3.8–9.5 mS/cm and chloride contents of 0.15–0.35 percent within the
0–30 cm soil layer. On this basis, the excavated organic soil is considered moderately to highly
saline.
     Under normal construction management conditions, the impact is not expected to occur.
Pollution risk becomes relevant only in the event of improper material management, such as
prolonged stockpiling, uncontrolled runoff, or discharge of leachate beyond the aquaculture area.
In such cases, localized salinization of soil, reduction in soil fertility, and temporary deterioration
of surface water quality may occur, particularly in areas not adapted to saline conditions. Indirectly
affected receptors would include households engaged in agriculture that relies on freshwater
sources and salt-sensitive crops. Impacts on on-site aquaculture activities are addressed in the
following section..
     Overall, the pollution risk associated with improper management of saline organic topsoil
from aquaculture areas during the construction phase is highly site-specific and strongly dependent
on excavation and material-handling practices. This impact is assessed as Moderate because: (i)
stripped organic sludge is managed within the aquaculture area; (ii) temporary storage duration is
short, limited to approximately 1–2 weeks; (iii) stripping activities are implemented in a rolling
manner along sections of approximately 100–150 m; and (iv) excavated sludge will be promptly
transported for disposal once moisture content meets handling requirements.




                                                                                                   201
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Figure 4.2. Location of the project section passing through shrimp farming areas.


   4.3.2.2.    Pollution risk to agricultural production and aquaculture due to road
               construction activities
     The Ring Road No. 3 project with a total length of 18.65 km traverses areas with mixed land-
use patterns, including residential areas, protection forest, rivers, agricultural land, and aquaculture
areas. Aquaculture ponds are concentrated along a section of approximately 2.4 km from Km
6+500 to Km 8+900, rice fields extend for approximately 1.6 km from Km 10+300 to Km 11+900,
freshwater fish ponds for approximately 0.8 km from Km 11+800 to Km 12+600, and vegetable
and cash crop areas for approximately 2.3 km from Km 14+300 to Km 16+600. These areas are
located immediately adjacent to the construction corridor and are served by small earthen irrigation
and drainage canals with typical widths of approximately 0.5–1.0 m.
     According to the FS, a rolling construction method will be applied, with typical segment
lengths of approximately 100–150 m. Prior to construction, organic topsoil within the acquired
area will be stripped, with a total volume of approximately 181,694 m³, including about 48,960 m³
of wet sludge from shrimp farming areas and approximately 55,930 m³ of soil meeting technical
standards for reuse as embankment fill and temporarily stockpiled on site. Given the total route
length of 18.65 km, the average volume of organic soil requiring management within each 100–



                                                                                                   202
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
150 m construction segment is estimated at approximately 300–450 m³ per segment, particularly
concentrated in sections crossing agricultural land and aquaculture ponds.
    During the period prior to reuse for embankment filling, this soil stockpile represents a
significant potential source of fine sediment to surrounding water bodies and cultivated land during
heavy rainfall events or when temporary drainage systems are not yet fully established.
     Earthworks, topsoil stripping, and material stockpiling activities are spatially confined and
temporally limited within each construction segment. However, in aquaculture sections located
immediately adjacent to the alignment, sensitivity to water quality changes is high due to the very
short receiving distance. The main impact mechanisms during construction include (i) surface
runoff carrying fine sediment and suspended solids directly from construction areas, slopes, and
temporary stockpiles into shrimp ponds or irrigation canals supplying ponds; (ii) dispersion of
small construction materials and construction waste from transport activities along the alignment
into adjacent ponds, as well as domestic waste from workers; and (iii) localized leakage of oil and
grease from construction machinery and vehicles, transported via surface runoff or the irrigation
canal network into aquaculture ponds. These impacts may lead to increased turbidity,
accumulation of fine sediment at pond bottoms, localized reduction in dissolved oxygen, and short-
term fluctuations in water quality over a period of approximately 4–6 months (FS, 2026).
    For the shrimp farming section from Km 6+500 to Km 8+900, higher water quality
requirements compared to freshwater fish farming result in increased impact sensitivity. Field
survey results indicate that the main shrimp stocking period typically occurs around March–April,
with a possible additional cycle around August–September. During the grow-out phase, shrimp
ponds are commonly subject to periodic water exchange at intervals of approximately 7–10 days
or 10–20 percent of pond volume per exchange, which increases hydrological connectivity
between ponds and the irrigation canal system. Even short-term water quality disturbances during
the grow-out period may affect shrimp growth, survival rates, and harvest yields, particularly if
they coincide with water intake events or heavy rainfall during July–September.
     In contrast, in the freshwater fish farming section from Km 11+900 to Km 12+800, which is
characterized by household-scale operations, and in adjacent rice fields and vegetable cultivation
areas, similar impact mechanisms mainly result in temporary increases in turbidity, localized
sedimentation, and short-term water quality effects, with lower sensitivity compared to shrimp
farming. For rice fields and crop areas, sediment and material deposition may cause localized
burial of crops and affect plant growth; if such events coincide with sensitive crop growth stages,
yield reduction may occur during that cropping season.
     Rice fields and crop areas in the project area are typically cultivated in two seasons per year,
with planting and irrigation periods concentrated in January–February (winter–spring crop) and
June–July (summer–autumn crop). Irrigation and drainage water for both agricultural fields and
aquaculture ponds is mainly sourced from the Ruot Lon River (Km 6+100), Hau Long River (Km
12+000), Chu River branch (Km 16+090), and the network of local irrigation canals intersecting
or running parallel to the alignment. Accordingly, if construction-related pollution coincides with


                                                                                                 203
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
key irrigation periods or aquaculture production cycles, the magnitude of impact on agricultural
and aquaculture production would be higher than at other times.
     Overall, the pollution risk to agricultural and aquaculture activities during construction of
Ring Road No. 3 is assessed as Moderate. This impact is site-specific, temporary, and segmented
along the alignment, with an expected duration of approximately 4–6 months per construction
section. The Moderate rating is based on the scale of sediment and material generation within each
construction segment, the low-lying terrain, and the immediate presence of sensitive production
receptors. The impact is considered manageable and can be effectively mitigated through
appropriate construction practices, material management, and drainage control measures.
   4.3.2.3.    Traffic safety risk at intersections with regional roads
     The Ring Road No. 3 project includes four main intersections, comprising two grade-
separated intersections with the Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway at Km 1+022 and with National
Highway 5A at Km 2+340, and two at-grade intersections with Provincial Road 359 at Km 13+680
and with National Highway 10 at Km 18+680. The Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway has a permitted
speed of 120 km/h, an average traffic volume of approximately 14,500 vehicles per day, and a
typical carriageway width of about 33–35 m. National Highway 5A has a maximum speed of 80
km/h, an average traffic volume of approximately 20,900 vehicles per day, a typical carriageway
width of about 24–26 m, and a high proportion of heavy trucks and container vehicles with loads
of up to 18 tons. National Highway 10 and Provincial Road 359 have permitted speeds of 80 km/h,
traffic volumes ranging from approximately 30,000 to more than 40,000 vehicles per day, mixed
traffic composition including trucks, cars, and motorcycles, and typical carriageway widths of
approximately 18–22 m.
     Traffic safety impacts may occur at both types of intersections due to increased interaction
between existing traffic streams and construction operations. During construction, material hauling
trucks, excavated soil transport, and equipment vehicles will access construction sites with an
estimated frequency of approximately 10–25 truck trips per day at intersection areas during peak
construction days. In parallel, installation of fencing, construction site layout, and temporary traffic
organization within intersection areas may alter traffic geometry and driver behavior, thereby
increasing collision risk, particularly under conditions of high traffic volume and high operating
speeds.




                                                                                                   204
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
 Grade-separated intersection with National     Flyover            crossing    HN-HP      Express
 Highway 5A                                 Highway




 At-grade intersection with Provincial 359         At-grade intersection        with     National
                                                   Highway 10
     For grade-separated intersections, traffic safety impacts mainly arise during construction of
flyovers and approach roads while existing traffic on the Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway and
National Highway 5A must be maintained. Activities such as construction of abutments and piers,
girder launching, and establishment of construction safety corridors typically require localized
narrowing of traffic lanes on these major roads. During these stages, the effective carriageway
width may be reduced by approximately 20–30 percent, increasing the likelihood of traffic
accidents and congestion at these intersections. Construction of flyovers on major transport
corridors is expected to last approximately 12–18 months per intersection, of which traffic-
affecting stages such as pier construction, girder erection, and superstructure completion typically
account for approximately 6–9 months. Additional accident risks may arise from falling
construction materials or equipment from elevated works during flyover construction.
    For at-grade intersections, traffic on Provincial Road 359 and National Highway 10 will
generally be maintained in its existing configuration during most of the mainline construction
period, with no significant reduction in carriageway width. Traffic narrowing and conflict mainly
occur during the stages of connecting Ring Road No. 3 to these existing roads, including
construction of roundabouts, turning lanes, and pavement completion within the intersection area.



                                                                                                205
                                                                                                Official Use Only
The period of direct traffic impact at each at-grade intersection is typically concentrated within
approximately 2–4 months, during which fencing, lane adjustments, and temporary traffic
arrangements are required.
     The primary receptors include road users on the Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway, National
Highway 5A, National Highway 10, and Provincial Road 359; heavy truck and container drivers;
motorcyclists and non-motorized road users at at-grade intersections; and construction workers
operating within intersection areas. Traffic safety risk is highest during peak traffic hours, typically
from 06:30–08:00 and 16:30–18:30, when traffic volumes increase significantly on National
Highway 5A, National Highway 10, and Provincial Road 359. On the Ha Noi–Hai Phong
Expressway, traffic volumes are more evenly distributed between approximately 08:00 and 19:00,
with off-peak hours from 20:00 to 07:00 the following day. In addition, Hai Phong experiences a
rainy season from approximately May to October, which reduces visibility, increases pavement
slipperiness, and further elevates accident risk at construction-affected intersections if adequate
daytime and nighttime warning signage and traffic control measures are not fully implemented.
     Overall, traffic safety risk at the four major intersections during construction of Ring Road
No. 3 is assessed as Significant. This impact is highly site-specific and time-bound. Although the
spatial extent of impact is relatively limited, sensitivity is high due to large traffic volumes and
high operating speeds. The impact is temporary and associated with construction at each
intersection, with an estimated duration of approximately 12–18 months for grade-separated
intersections and 2–4 months for at-grade intersections. Given the combination of traffic speed
traffic volume construction duration and direct interaction between construction activities and live
traffic a Significant rating is justified. The impact can be mitigated through appropriate traffic
safety management plans temporary traffic organization and strict construction safety controls.
   4.3.2.4.    Disruption and disturbance to waterway traffic on the Cam River in the Ngo
               Quyen Bridge area
     Ring Road No. 3 will include the construction of Ngo Quyen Bridge crossing the Cam River
at Km 3+200 and Ruot Lon Bridge crossing the Ruot Lon River at Km 6+100. Waterway traffic
on the Ruot Lon River is mainly composed of small boats and small barges below 50–100 DWT,
with typical vessel beam of approximately 4–8 m, used for household-scale transport of
construction materials, agricultural products, and local travel. According to the FS, Ruot Lon
Bridge is designed with a navigation opening width greater than 50 m and a navigation clearance
height of not less than 9.5 m; therefore, the impact of bridge construction activities on waterway
traffic on the Ruot Lon River is assessed as negligible.
     However, the Cam River is currently an important navigation corridor of Hai Phong. The river
section at the bridge location has a channel width of approximately 300–350 m and high vessel
traffic, with about 200–300 vessel movements per day, including material barges, inland cargo
vessels, and large seagoing vessels entering and leaving the Hai Phong port system. Large seagoing
vessels in this area are mainly cargo and container ships with deadweight tonnage of approximately
5,000–30,000 DWT and typical beam of approximately 18–32 m. These vessels travel along the


                                                                                                   206
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
main maritime channel and typically require traffic regulation when passing under or through
river-crossing structures.
     During construction, impacts on waterway traffic will mainly arise from in-river works and
erection of the main span, including construction of foundations and piers or pylons, installation
of cofferdams, deployment of barges and floating cranes, and establishment of on-water
construction safety exclusion zones. According to the FS, Ngo Quyen Bridge is designed with a
main navigation span of 230 m and a navigation clearance height of H ≥ 41 m. In the highest-
impact scenario, assuming that each pier or pylon during construction occupies approximately 40
m of river width across the channel, including the pier footprint, cofferdam, and safety corridor,
the total occupied water surface width for two in-river piers or pylons may reach approximately
80 m. Based on the 230 m main span, the effective navigable width during peak construction
periods may be reduced to approximately 150 m, equivalent to an approximate reduction of 35
percent compared to normal operating conditions. Under this degree of narrowing, large seagoing
vessels up to 30,000 DWT would still be able to pass the bridge area, but would need to reduce
speed, limit simultaneous passing and overtaking, and depend on traffic regulation.
     Navigation disturbance risk increases notably under adverse hydrological conditions, which
typically occur during the rainy season in Hai Phong from approximately May to October, when
stronger flows, tidal water level fluctuations, and reduced visibility due to rain and storms may
occur. For barges and inland cargo vessels with high passage frequency, the likelihood of localized
queuing and increased waiting time near the bridge area will be more evident during peak
construction stages. Construction of in-river components including foundations and piers or pylons
typically lasts approximately 6–9 months, followed by main-span erection and closure works over
approximately 3–6 months. Therefore, the period with potential direct influence on waterway
traffic at the Ngo Quyen Bridge area may extend for approximately 9–12 months within the overall
bridge construction schedule. Overall, disruption and disturbance to waterway traffic on the Cam
River in the Ngo Quyen Bridge area is assessed as Moderate, reflecting the high sensitivity of the
navigation corridor and vessel traffic volume, but also the temporary and manageable nature of the
spatial footprint and duration. The impact can be mitigated through appropriate waterway traffic
safety management measures during construction, including navigation channel regulation and
effective on-water safety controls.




                                                                                               207
                                                                                               Official Use Only
                Figure 4.3. Map of the main navigation span of Ngo Quyen Bridge.
   4.3.2.5.    Impacts on water quality and aquatic life at Cam River and Ruot Lon River
     Direct sources of impacts on water quality during bridge construction include: (i) pile drilling,
excavation of pile caps and foundations, and construction of pier bases and shafts within
cofferdam-isolated work zones, which disturb bottom sediments and generate dewatering effluent
with high total suspended solids (TSS). Based on cofferdam scale and effective excavation or
drilling depth, the volume of disturbed soil and sediment is estimated at approximately 1,800–
2,400 m³ per pier at the Ngo Quyen Bridge and 600–900 m³ per pier at the Ruot Lon Bridge. The
associated dewatering discharge is estimated at approximately 450–1,200 m³/day per work point
at the Ngo Quyen Bridge and 150–450 m³/day per work point at the Ruot Lon Bridge, consisting
mainly of turbid water containing fine silt and sand; (ii) localized riverbank erosion and leakage
of soil, sand, and cement from material stockpiles, working platforms, and temporary access roads
located near the water edge, particularly during rainfall events and tidal water level fluctuations,
potentially increasing localized TSS and turbidity; (iii) discharge of wash water from barges, pier
surfaces, and construction equipment at bridge sites, estimated at approximately 1–3 m³/day per
work point during peak construction periods; and (iv) domestic wastewater from construction
workers (approximately 25–40 workers per bridge, generating about 1.25–3.2 m³/day per bridge),
as well as the risk of oil and grease leakage from construction machinery and equipment.
    The Cam River at the bridge location has a width of approximately 300–350 m and an average
depth of about 7 m. Average river discharge during the rainy season reaches approximately 816.9
m³/s, while during the dry season it remains at around 334 m³/s, reflecting strong tidal influence
and high water exchange and dilution capacity, particularly in the rainy season. The Ruot Lon
River at the bridge location has a width of approximately 180–220 m and an average depth of
about 3 m. Discharge reaches approximately 491 m³/s in the rainy season, with flow velocities



                                                                                                  208
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
ranging from about 0.52–1.10 m/s near the bridge, decreasing to approximately 0.2–0.4 m/s in the
dry season.
    Baseline water quality in both the Cam River and Ruot Lon River is relatively good, with
monitored parameters within the permissible limits of QCVN 05-2023/BTNMT. Baseline TSS
concentrations are approximately 10.4 mg/L for the Cam River and 11.2 mg/L for the Ruot Lon
River. Bottom sediments in both rivers consist mainly of fine silt and sand, which are easily
resuspended during in-river construction activities.
     Based on the identified impact sources and the hydrological and baseline water quality
conditions of both rivers, the spatial extent of TSS dispersion is expected to be localized,
concentrated mainly within the river channel at pier locations and extending approximately 50–
200 m upstream and downstream, depending on tidal phase, and occurring only during in-river
pier and foundation construction. Each pier construction period is expected to last approximately
6–9 months; however, the most intense sediment disturbance is concentrated within shorter periods
of approximately 20–35 days corresponding to specific drilling, excavation, and dewatering work
packages.
     Temporary degradation of water quality may also have adverse effects on aquatic ecosystems
in both rivers through: (i) increased TSS and turbidity, reducing light penetration in the water
column, affecting fish feeding behavior, and causing localized sediment deposition on low-
mobility benthic organisms within the near-field construction zone (approximately 50–200 m);
and
(ii) underwater noise and vibration generated during drilling and pile installation. Noise levels
from pier construction activities are estimated at approximately 80–85 dBA at a distance of 30 m
and decrease rapidly with distance, while vibration levels from pile driving may reach
approximately 11 mm/s at 10 m. These effects are expected to cause short-term behavioral
disturbance to aquatic organisms within a limited radius (approximately 10–50 m) around
construction sites and are not expected to propagate over a wider area.
     Aquatic ecosystems in both rivers are common and adapted to tidal brackish–freshwater
conditions and fine silt–sand substrates. Typical species include common brackish and freshwater
fish (tilapia, catfish, carp, silver carp, mullet, goby) and common benthic organisms (polychaete
worms, small bivalves, benthic crustaceans). No rare, endangered, endemic, or protected aquatic
species have been recorded within the bridge construction areas based on available information.
The typical food web comprises organic detritus and plankton → benthic organisms → benthic
and small omnivorous fish, with low-mobility benthic organisms being the most sensitive group
to temporary increases in TSS and sediment deposition.
     On the basis of: (i) the dominant impact mechanism being temporary increases in TSS and
turbidity; (ii) the differing hydrological conditions and dilution capacities of the two rivers; (iii)
baseline water quality and aquatic ecosystems without sensitive or high conservation-value
species; and (iv) the localized and temporary spatial and temporal extent of impacts,
the impact on water quality and aquatic life in the Cam River is assessed as Low. For the Ruot Lon


                                                                                                  209
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
River, due to more limited dilution capacity during the dry season and higher sensitivity of the
receiving water body, the impact is assessed as Moderate. However, with the application of
appropriate construction wastewater management, sediment control, and material handling
measures, these impacts can be effectively mitigated and maintained within acceptable limits.
   4.3.2.6.    Impacts on adjacent protection forest at the Ruot Lon Bridge area
     In addition to the permanently acquired 0.76 ha of planted protection forest dominated by
Sonneratia caseolaris, which has been assessed under the impact category “Loss of protection
forest,” construction activities at the Ruot Lon Bridge (Km 6+100) may generate localized indirect
impacts on the remaining protection forest areas adjacent to the construction site. These activities
include bridge pier construction (foundation excavation, pile drilling, concrete casting, and
superstructure erection); temporary material stockpiling; establishment of machinery yards and
temporary access roads; movement of workers and construction vehicles in the vicinity of the
forest edge; and the generation of dust and the risk of accidental spillage of fuel or construction
materials into adjacent forest areas if construction organization and material or waste management
are not adequately controlled.
     Affected receptors include the existing planted protection forest vegetation outside the land
acquisition boundary; tree root systems located along the forest edge near the construction site; the
growth and natural regeneration capacity of trees within indirectly affected areas; and localized
landscape continuity and green corridor functions. The main impact mechanisms on the adjacent
protection forest are expected to include: (i) trampling and soil compaction within the root zones
of Sonneratia caseolaris trees along the forest edge due to construction machinery and vehicles
operating close to the construction boundary, reducing soil aeration and water infiltration and
increasing the risk of stress or decline in trees located immediately adjacent to the work area; (ii)
mechanical damage to trunks and branches caused by crane rotation, lifting and lowering of
structural elements, or maneuvering of dump trucks and excavators within confined spaces near
the forest edge; (iii) localized burial of root zones and intertidal surfaces at the forest margin due
to soil or mud overflow or runoff from temporary material stockpiles, temporary soil storage areas,
or disposal sites during heavy rainfall events or high tidal conditions, potentially resulting in
mortality of young trees or reduced growth at the forest edge; and (iv) localized soil contamination
at the forest margin due to improper management of construction waste (e.g., spillage of concrete
or cement mortar, fine aggregates) and leakage of oil or grease from equipment, which may alter
soil pH and cause root toxicity in sensitive edge trees.
     These impacts are expected to be spatially limited to the protection forest strip directly
adjacent to the construction site, typically within a few meters to a few tens of meters from the
construction boundary, and will not result in any additional loss of protection forest area beyond
the legally acquired land during the project preparation phase. The affected receptors are limited
to the remaining planted Sonneratia caseolaris protection forest at the site margin, including mature
trees and young or regenerating individuals.




                                                                                                  210
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
     The protection forest in this area is a planted forest with primary functions of bank
stabilization and wave attenuation. No rare, endangered, endemic, or protected flora or fauna
species have been recorded in the adjacent forest area. Localized impacts at the forest edge may
temporarily reduce site-specific protection functions; however, such effects are expected to be
short-term and reversible. Given the limited spatial extent, the absence of sensitive or high-
conservation-value species, and the temporary nature of construction activities (approximately 6–
9 months), the impact on adjacent protection forest at the Ruot Lon Bridge is assessed as Low.
With the implementation of appropriate construction site management, strict control of material
storage and waste handling, and clear demarcation of construction boundaries, these impacts can
be effectively mitigated and maintained within acceptable limits.
   4.3.2.7.    Impacts on community safety and accessibility in adjacent residential areas
     Site-specific impacts related to community safety and accessibility occur mainly along
sections where the alignment passes directly through existing residential areas. The severity of
impacts varies from sparsely distributed residential clusters such as the Lap Le area (Km 9+500–
Km 10+500) to dense and continuous residential stretches in Ngu Lao (Km 13+000–Km 14+100)
and Trung Ha (Km 15+000–Km 15+500), as well as at locations where the alignment crosses local
access roads connecting residential areas with agricultural land and aquaculture ponds on the
opposite side of the route, as described in the assessment of agricultural and aquaculture impacts
above.
     Under existing conditions, internal roads and local access roads within these residential areas
typically have a width of approximately 5–7 m and serve mixed traffic, including motorcycles,
bicycles, and pedestrians. These roads also function as direct access to houses and small shops,
and as connection routes to farmland and aquaculture areas across the alignment. During staged
construction (100–150 m working sections), activities such as roadbed excavation and
embankment works, construction of cross-drainage structures, and in particular the installation of
roadside drainage systems in front of residential properties will create continuous excavation
trenches approximately 1.5–2.0 m deep and 1.0–1.5 m wide. These trenches may remain open for
about 1–2 weeks per construction section, posing a direct risk of temporary interruption to
household and small business access.




     Lap Le residential area                         Trung Ha residential area


                                                                                                211
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     The combination of narrow local road cross-sections, un-reinstated drainage trenches, and the
frequent presence of dump trucks, excavators, and heavy construction equipment significantly
increases safety risks for pedestrians, motorcyclists, elderly people, and children at residential
access points. In addition, the need to detour, use temporary access paths, or wait for temporary
trench covers may cause inconvenience to daily activities and small roadside businesses,
particularly in densely populated areas such as Ngu Lao and Trung Ha.
      These impacts on community safety and accessibility do not occur simultaneously along the
entire alignment but move progressively with the construction sequence. The duration of direct
impact at each location is typically about 3–6 weeks for standard road construction sections and
may extend to approximately 1–2 months at locations requiring cross-drainage works or complex
utility installation. Given the localized spatial extent, temporary nature, and the presence of
exposed trenches and construction traffic in residential settings, the impact is assessed as Medium.
With the application of appropriate construction management measures, including safe trench
protection, temporary access provision, clear signage, and traffic control, these impacts can be
effectively mitigated.
     In contrast, along sections where the alignment runs behind residential areas and through
agricultural land, such as the Thon 10 area (Km 16+200–Km 17+000), houses are located
approximately 5–10 m from the right-of-way, but the project does not directly affect local access
roads. As a result, impacts on community safety and accessibility for residents in this area are
considered negligible.
   4.3.2.8.    Impacts on the operation of Hai An Port
During construction of the approach road to Ngo Quyen Bridge under Ring Road No. 3, the
operation of Hai An Port will be subject to site-specific impacts because the alignment passes
directly through the port area and runs very close to active berths along the Cam River. Hai An
Port is a dedicated container port that regularly handles container vessels and inland barges, with
a designed capacity of approximately 500,000–600,000 TEU per year. On average, the port
receives and handles about 10–15 vessel and barge calls per day, including small- to medium-sized
container vessels and inland barges. Port operations are conducted continuously on a 24/7 basis
and are therefore particularly sensitive to any disruption of berth access, cargo handling space, and
internal port traffic circulation.




                                                                                                 212
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
                     Hai An port                         Location of the alignment and the port
     The primary impact sources arise from construction of the bridge approach and elevated
structures passing above or immediately adjacent to active berths within the port area. In addition
to typical construction-related disturbances, these works require the establishment of temporary
safety exclusion zones at certain stages, which may restrict usable berth space and affect vessel
access, berthing, or cargo handling activities. During peak construction activities, such as girder
launching or transportation of oversized structural components, access channels to the berths may
need to be temporarily regulated by time windows, resulting in localized delays to vessel schedules
and a reduction in daily handling efficiency.
     In parallel, construction of the approach road through container yards and internal logistics
areas increases conflicts between construction traffic and port container trucks. During certain
stages, internal road cross-sections may be temporarily narrowed or reconfigured, leading to longer
truck turnaround times and reduced internal circulation capacity. Construction activities at
elevated levels above ongoing port operations also increase safety management requirements,
particularly with respect to risks of falling materials or equipment incidents. However, such risks
are spatially limited and can be effectively controlled through appropriate engineering measures
and construction management.
     The above impacts are spatially localized, concentrated at the approach road section and
adjacent berths, and temporally temporary, generally lasting about 6–9 months, with each pier or
span causing more pronounced effects for only several weeks to a few months. They do not persist
throughout the entire construction period and do not result in a complete interruption of port
operations. Based on the scale and frequency of port activities, the limited spatial extent, and the
temporary nature of construction-related disruptions, impacts on the operation of Hai An Port are
assessed as site-specific, localized, and temporary, with an overall impact level of Medium. With
close coordination between the Project Owner, Contractors, and the Port Authority, including
phased construction planning, scheduling of sensitive works during suitable time windows, and
maintenance of safe and functional berth access, these impacts can be effectively managed and are
not expected to affect the long-term operational role of Hai An Port.


                                                                                                213
                                                                                                Official Use Only
   4.3.2.9.     Impacts on Lap Le Ward Medical Station (Km 9+550) and Trung Ha Commune
                Kindergarten (Km 15+400)
     Ring Road No. 3 passes in very close proximity to Lap Le Ward Medical Station at Km 9+550
and Trung Ha Commune Kindergarten at Km 15+400, with a distance of only approximately 10–
20 m from the construction boundary to both facilities. Lap Le Ward Medical Station with about
6-8 staffs is a ward-level healthcare facility providing outpatient services only, typically staffed by
approximately 08 doctors, nurses and medical assistants per working shift operating mainly during
office hours from approximately 07:30–11:30 and 13:30–17:00 on weekdays, with an average of
30–60 outpatient visits per day, and peak activity occurring in the morning (08:00–10:30). Trung
Ha Commune Kindergarten with about 200-250 children accommodates approximately 200–250
children and is staffed by about 18–22 teachers and childcare assistants, operates from
approximately 07:00–16:30 from Monday to Saturday, with two daily peak periods during
morning drop-off (07:00–08:00) and afternoon pick-up (16:00–17:30).




              Lap Le medical station                         Trung Ha Kindergarten
     During construction of Ring Road No. 3 at these locations, key activities include earthworks
and roadbed compaction, pavement and foundation construction, transportation of construction
materials by trucks, and the installation of temporary traffic arrangements and site fencing. These
activities may generate dust, noise, localized vibration, temporarily occupy or narrow road space,
and increase conflicts between construction vehicles and local traffic.
     Due to the very short separation distance (10–20 m), noise generated by road construction
activities (excavators, vibratory rollers, and trucks) may reach approximately 80–85 dBA at a
distance of 30 m, and potentially higher at closer distances. These noise levels exceed daytime
permissible limits for healthcare and educational facilities under QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT, which
are more stringent than those for general residential areas. This represents a key site-specific
impact, given the high requirement for quiet conditions at healthcare facilities and kindergartens,
and may directly affect outpatient medical services and the learning and daily activities of
preschool children.
    Dust generated from excavation and material transport in close proximity may lead to
localized increases in TSP and PM₁₀ concentrations, particularly during the dry season. At


                                                                                                   214
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
distances of less than 20 m, dust levels at the medical station and kindergarten may temporarily
exceed the limits of QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT, potentially affecting patients, medical staff, and
young children, who are respiratory-sensitive receptors. In addition, direct construction activities
along the Ring Road corridor involve approximately 20–40 construction and material transport
vehicle movements per day, combined with temporary traffic arrangements on narrow access roads
(approximately 7–10 m wide). This may slow or temporarily obstruct access to the medical station
and increase safety risks during peak drop-off and pick-up hours at the kindergarten.
     Based on (i) the very close proximity of the Lap Le Ward Medical Station and Trung Ha
Commune Kindergarten to the Ring Road No. 3 alignment (10–20 m), (ii) the high sensitivity of
healthcare and educational facilities to noise, dust, and access disruption, and (iii) the localized
and temporary nature of the impacts, the site-specific impact is assessed as Medium. With strict
control of noise and dust emissions, maintenance of safe and continuous access, and avoidance of
high-noise construction activities during medical service hours and kindergarten teaching hours,
these impacts can be effectively managed and are not expected to result in long-term adverse
effects on the operation of either facility.

   4.3.3. Site-specific impacts of Component 2
   4.3.3.1.     Impacts on water quality and aquatic life of the Re River due to embankment
               construction and upgrading of the riverside road
     The construction and upgrading of the Re River embankment (approximately 4.24 km on the
right bank and 4.8 km on the left bank) combined with the riverside road (approximately 8.05 km
on the right bank and 5.72 km on the left bank) involve several construction activities that may
affect water quality and aquatic life. These activities include: (i) excavation and stripping of
riparian areas and riverbanks, localized dredging of bottom sediments, excavation of embankment
foundations and the riverside road subgrade, together with earthworks and soil transport within the
river corridor, resulting in disturbance of fine silt–sand sediments and generation of suspended
solids (TSS). According to the FS, the total volume of soil and sediment generated from
excavation, foundation works, and grading is approximately 137,867 m³, of which 59,800 m³ will
be reused and about 78,000 m³ will require disposal. During temporary stockpiling, this soil and
sediment constitutes a major source of fine sediment runoff into the river during rainfall events or
when temporary drainage systems are not yet fully established; (ii) wastewater generated during
construction, including stormwater runoff from construction sites, material stockpiles, and
riverside access roads carrying fine sediments, as well as domestic wastewater from construction
workers (approximately 30–50 workers per section, generating an estimated 1.5–4.0
m³/day/section); and (iii) the risk of localized pollution from accidental spillage of construction
materials (soil, sand, cement) and leakage of oil, grease, or fuel from machinery and vehicles
operating close to the riverbank and within the river, particularly during peak construction and
equipment maintenance periods.
     The Re River at the embankment construction sections has a relatively narrow channel width
of approximately 30–60 m, with an average depth of 2–3 m. Flow velocity ranges from about 0.3–


                                                                                                215
                                                                                                Official Use Only
0.6 m/s in the rainy season to approximately 0.1–0.25 m/s in the dry season, corresponding to
discharges of 30–80 m³/s and 10–30 m³/s, respectively. These hydraulic conditions indicate a
limited local dilution and self-purification capacity, particularly during the dry season. Baseline
water quality of the Re River generally complies with QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT (Class B1);
however, COD and BOD₅ exceed allowable thresholds, with COD ranging from 10.9–26.2 mg/L
and BOD₅ from 6–15.3 mg/L, indicating moderate organic pollution pressure. Riverbed sediments
are dominated by fine silt–sand, which are easily resuspended during excavation, dredging, and
embankment construction.
     Based on the identified impact sources and the prevailing hydrological and baseline water
quality conditions, changes in TSS and turbidity during embankment and riverside road
construction are expected to be localized, concentrated mainly within the active construction
section and approximately 100–300 m downstream of the embankment section, depending on tidal
and flow conditions (USACE, 2004; Van Rijn, 1993; World Bank Group, 2007). Under the staged
construction approach defined in the FS, each active embankment section is approximately 200 m
in length. Intensive excavation and dredging activities typically occur over 2–3 months per section,
with the most significant sediment disturbance concentrated within about 3–6 weeks, after which
impacts rapidly decline once the embankment structure, slope stabilization, and temporary
drainage measures are completed.
      The directly affected receptors are the Re River water environment and its aquatic ecosystem.
Degradation of water quality during embankment and riverside road construction may affect
aquatic life through: (i) increased TSS and turbidity, reducing light penetration in the water
column, impairing fish foraging efficiency, and causing localized sediment deposition on low-
mobility benthic organisms, primarily within 100–300 m downstream of the active construction
section; and (ii) localized noise and vibration generated by construction machinery and equipment
(pile driving, foundation excavation, operation of heavy machinery) near and within the river,
which may disturb aquatic organism behavior within a very limited area around the construction
site.
     The aquatic ecosystem of the Re River is typical and common, adapted to urban freshwater
conditions, fine silt–sand substrates, and a water body that has been subject to long-term
anthropogenic influence. Dominant species include common freshwater fish such as tilapia
(Oreochromis spp.), catfish (Clarias spp.), common carp (Cyprinus carpio), crucian carp
(Carassius auratus), grass carp (Ctenopharyngodon idella), and common benthic fauna such as
freshwater snails, bivalves (Unionidae, Corbiculidae), small crustaceans (Macrobrachium spp.,
freshwater shrimp), and benthic aquatic insects. No rare, endangered, endemic, or conservation-
value aquatic species have been recorded within the embankment construction area based on
available information. The typical food web consists of detritus and plankton → benthic organisms
→ benthic and omnivorous fish, with low-mobility benthic organisms being the most sensitive
group to temporary increases in TSS and fine sediment deposition during construction.




                                                                                                216
                                                                                                Official Use Only
     Based on:(i) the fact that the dominant impact source during embankment and riverside road
construction is the temporary increase in TSS and turbidity due to excavation, dredging, soil and
sediment stockpiling, and sediment-laden runoff; (ii) the hydraulic characteristics of the Re River,
including its narrow width, shallow depth, and low flow velocity, resulting in limited dilution and
self-cleaning capacity, particularly in the dry season; (iii) baseline water quality conditions that
generally meet regulatory standards but with COD and BOD₅ values approaching or exceeding
thresholds, indicating moderate sensitivity of the receiving environment; and (iv) the localized and
temporary spatial and temporal extent of impacts, confined to the active embankment section and
approximately 100–300 m downstream, and mainly occurring during excavation and dredging
phases lasting about 3–6 weeks per active section (~200 m), the impact on water quality and
aquatic ecosystems of the Re River is assessed as Medium. However, with the application of staged
construction methods, strict control of stormwater runoff and construction wastewater, proper
management of soil and sediment stockpiles, slope protection and stabilization, and effective
prevention of oil, grease, and construction material leakage, these impacts are short-term,
reversible, and can be effectively mitigated, remaining within acceptable limits for the Re River
water environment and aquatic ecosystem.
   4.3.3.2.    Risk of sedimentation and turbidity affecting water intake at Quan Vinh pumping
               station
     Construction of river embankment sections near the water intake of Quan Vinh pumping
station will involve stripping of organic soil and riverbank sediments, excavation of embankment
foundations, construction of toe protection works, and transportation and handling of construction
materials along the riverbank. Two construction sections are located close to the intake area,
including: (i) an upgraded stone embankment section approximately 1,320 m in length located
about 250 m upstream of the intake; and (ii) an expanded stone embankment section approximately
60 m in length located about 200 m downstream of the intake.




                                                                                                217
                                                                                                Official Use Only
  Figure 4.4. Location of Quan Vinh pumping station and adjacent concrete embankment
                                       sections




                                    Quan Vinh pumping station
     These construction activities will generate substantial volumes of fine sediments and
excavated materials which, if not adequately controlled, may be washed into the river and
transported over short distances toward the intake area. The impact pathway is therefore defined
as: embankment construction activities → release of fine sediments and suspended solids →
localized increases in turbidity and sediment deposition in the river → potential obstruction and
operational disturbance at the raw water intake of Quan Vinh pumping station.
     The expected impact area is localized, mainly affecting the river reach within approximately
100–300 m upstream and downstream of the intake, consistent with the influence zone identified
for water quality impacts along the Re River.
     In terms of duration, impacts will occur only during construction of the embankment sections
adjacent to the intake. Construction is expected to last approximately 6–9 months for the upstream
section (1,320 m) and 1–2 months for the downstream section (60 m). Individual turbidity and
sedimentation events are short-lived (from several hours to a few days) but may recur
intermittently throughout these construction periods. The risk is higher during the dry season
(November to April), when river discharge is lower and dilution and natural self-cleansing capacity
are limited.
     The primary affected receptor is the raw water intake of Quan Vinh pumping station, which
supplies water to An Duong Water Treatment Plant with an operational capacity of approximately
160,000–180,000 m³/day, operating continuously 24 hours per day, 7 days per week. Baseline
water quality monitoring results indicate that the Re River in this section is already degraded, with
TSS (13.7–69.4 mg/L), COD (10.9–26.2 mg/L), and BOD₅ (6–15.3 mg/L) exceeding allowable
limits for raw water sources under QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT, and low dissolved oxygen (DO
≈ 4.9 mg/L), indicating limited assimilative capacity. As a result, the intake system is highly
sensitive to short-term increases in turbidity and fine sediment loads, which may clog trash racks
and intake screens, increase treatment requirements, and reduce the operational stability of the
water supply system.



                                                                                                 218
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
     The level of impact is assessed as Substantial. This assessment is based on the combined
effects of: (i) the large volume of fine sediments generated by construction activities located very
close (200–250 m) to a critical water intake; (ii) already degraded baseline water quality and
limited assimilative capacity of the Re River; and (iii) the strategic importance and continuous-
operation requirement of Quan Vinh pumping station for the municipal water supply system.
    Although the impact is temporary and spatially localized, there remains a significant risk of
operational disruption if sediment release and turbidity are not effectively controlled. In the
absence of mitigation measures, the impact is therefore considered Substantial. However, with the
implementation of well-designed and strictly managed mitigation measures—as specified in the
ESMP, including sediment control, staged construction, protection of stockpiles, and intake
safeguarding—the impact can be substantially reduced and maintained within acceptable limits.
   4.3.3.3.    Risk of residential building cracking and safety hazards associated with Re River
               embankment construction
    Along the 9.67 km Re River embankment on both riverbanks, the Project includes several
types of embankment structures, comprising: (i) approximately 6.06 km of newly constructed
embankments using Neoweb retaining walls combined with Neoweb grassed slopes (from
National Highway 10 to Re Bridge); (ii) approximately 1.32 km of rehabilitation of existing
damaged embankments (from Re Bridge to Cai Tat Bridge); (iii) approximately 0.06 km of newly
constructed stone revetment connections to link existing embankments where no embankment
currently exists; and (iv) approximately 2.23 km of new construction and rehabilitation of
embankments using reinforced concrete frames combined with Neoweb grassed slopes, including
a 690 m vertical embankment along the south bank from Re Bridge (Phan Dang Luu Road) to
address dense riverside housing and facilitate wastewater collection while minimizing river
channel narrowing.
     These vertical embankment sections require deep excavation and deep foundation systems,
with excavation and embedment depths of approximately 4–6 m below existing ground level,
combined with reinforced concrete or prestressed concrete piles of about 30 m in length to ensure
stability in soft, saturated Holocene alluvial soils. During construction, the primary impact
mechanisms include deep excavation and foundation works, soil deformation and horizontal
ground movement, differential settlement, and cracking of adjacent structures. Localized vibration
may also occur during pile installation; however, ground movement associated with deep
excavation is the dominant mechanism of potential damage.
     The impact is spatially limited and highly site-specific, primarily affecting residential
buildings located within approximately 10–30 m from the edge of vertical embankment sections.
Other embankment types (Neoweb embankments and stone embankments with shallow
foundations) are expected to have a much smaller influence zone, largely confined to the
embankment slope and the riverside management road, with negligible propagation toward
residential areas. In terms of duration, impacts occur only during construction of the vertical
embankment sections. For each such section, the potential impact period is estimated at


                                                                                                219
                                                                                                Official Use Only
approximately 5–6 months, corresponding to the phases of deep excavation, foundation
construction, and backfilling. No long-term impacts are anticipated once construction is completed
and ground conditions have stabilized.
     The primary affected receptors are residential houses and ancillary structures located close to
the embankment alignment, particularly in the An Dong – An Duong residential area. At several
locations, the minimum distance from the embankment centerline to residential buildings is only
about 5 m, separated solely by a narrow riverside management road.
     Affected structures are mainly 1–2 storey masonry houses with shallow strip foundations or
short pile foundations. Such structures typically have limited tolerance to differential settlement
and lateral ground movement, making them vulnerable to ground deformation caused by deep
excavation in soft, saturated alluvial soils. Although the impact is temporary and spatially
localized, it involves deep excavation and foundation works conducted in very close proximity to
existing residential buildings constructed on shallow foundations in weak ground conditions.
Consequently, there is a significant risk of structural damage (cracking, uneven settlement) and
associated safety hazards to residents if ground movement is not properly controlled. In the
absence of mitigation measures, this impact would be considered severe. However, with the
application of appropriate engineering design, construction methods, and monitoring and control
measures as specified in the ESMP, including ground movement monitoring, structural condition
surveys, and adaptive construction controls, the impact can be effectively managed and reduced to
an acceptable level.
   4.3.3.4.    Impacts on agricultural activities along agricultural land adjacent to the Re River
     The Re River embankment and the associated operation road will be constructed along both
riverbanks passing through An Duong, Hong Bang, and An Hai Wards, where agricultural land is
closely interwoven with the river corridor. In these areas, paddy fields, short-term crops, and small-
scale fish ponds are located immediately adjacent to the proposed embankment and operation road.
Agricultural production in this river section depends on a dense network of small irrigation and
drainage canals, typically 0.5–1.0 m wide, which provide water supply and drainage for
cultivation.
    Construction will be implemented in staged sections to avoid long-term occupation of large
areas. Nevertheless, a substantial volume of topsoil and surface sediment, estimated at
approximately 137,867 m³, will need to be stripped and handled to accommodate embankment and
road construction. During construction, agricultural land near the river will inevitably be
temporarily used for soil stockpiling, embankment fill placement, material storage yards, and
construction access roads. In parallel, a number of irrigation and drainage canals will need to be
crossed, narrowed, or temporarily diverted during embankment, road, and drainage construction.
     Overall, these activities create a clear impact pathway: temporary land occupation and
disturbance of irrigation and drainage canals → restricted access to fields and interruption of water
supply → disruption of normal farming activities and potential loss of a cropping cycle. The impact



                                                                                                  220
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
does not occur simultaneously along the entire alignment, but is limited to sections under active
construction where the embankment and road run immediately adjacent to agricultural land. At
each such section, the expected disturbance period is approximately 4–6 months, corresponding to
the direct construction duration. The impact is therefore localized and progressively shifts along
the river. It is temporary and of short- to medium-term duration at each affected location.
     However, the severity of impact depends strongly on whether construction timing coincides
with the local agricultural calendar, with higher risk during land preparation, transplanting, and
peak irrigation periods. The affected receptors are farming households in An Duong, Hong Bang,
and An Hai Wards, who cultivate small land parcels along the river. Most of this land is used for
two rice crops per year (typically the winter–spring crop around January–February and the
summer–autumn crop around June–July), as well as short-term vegetables and other annual crops.
These farming systems are tightly scheduled and highly dependent on reliable land access and
water availability. Plot sizes are generally small, and household livelihoods may be affected even
by short-term disruptions to cultivation or irrigation, particularly if such disruptions occur during
critical crop growth stages.
     Overall, this impact is assessed as Moderate. It does not result in permanent loss of agricultural
land and is limited in space and time to active construction sections. However, if construction
overlaps with sensitive periods in the agricultural calendar, disruptions may be clearly perceived
by affected households and could lead to reduced yields and temporary income loss for a cropping
season. Without mitigation, the impact could be significant for some households at the local level,
although it remains temporary and reversible.
     The impact can be substantially reduced through careful planning and management, including
scheduling construction to avoid peak agricultural periods where feasible, maintaining or
temporarily diverting irrigation and drainage flows, minimizing the extent and duration of
temporary land occupation, and promptly restoring affected areas after construction. Temporary
agricultural disruption nevertheless remains an important livelihood issue during construction,
requiring close coordination with local authorities and farmers, advance disclosure of construction
schedules, and active supervision by the CSC and PMU. With these measures, residual impacts
are expected to be minor and manageable.
   4.3.3.5.    Impacts on Pham Tu Nghi temple, An Tri and Quynh Cu pagodas
     During construction of the Re River embankment and the riverside operation road, Pham Tu
Nghi temple (An Hai Ward), An Tri Pagoda, and Quynh Cu Pagoda (Hong Bang Ward) are small-
to medium-scale community religious facilities serving local residents. These structures are not
classified as recognized heritage sites by the Hai Phong Department of Culture, Sports and
Tourism. The riverside operation road passes very close to, and in some cases cuts through, parts
of boundary walls, gardens, and auxiliary spaces of these facilities, with distances from the
construction boundary ranging from 0–20 m, giving rise to site-specific impacts that require
separate consideration.



                                                                                                   221
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Existing structures are mainly single-storey masonry buildings with thin brick walls and
shallow foundations, with limited tolerance to vibration. During embankment and operation road
construction, activities such as subgrade excavation, slope treatment, embankment construction,
road compaction, and heavy truck operation are the main sources of vibration. Construction-related
vibration at distances of 10–20 m may reach approximately 2–5 mm/s, particularly during
vibratory rolling and compaction. Given the existing structural characteristics, such vibration is
not expected to cause overall structural instability, but may increase the risk of wall cracking,
plaster detachment, and minor displacement of non-load-bearing elements, especially at boundary
walls and auxiliary structures intersected by the operation road.
     In addition to vibration, noise and dust from embankment and road construction represent
notable site-specific impacts due to the very short separation distance. Peak construction noise
levels may reach 75–85 dBA at 20–30 m, directly affecting the quiet environment required for
religious practices, chanting, and community activities, particularly on full-moon days, the first
day of the lunar month, and during religious festivals. Dust generated from excavation, material
transport, and pavement works may deposit within pagoda and communal house grounds, reducing
visual amenity and increasing cleaning and maintenance needs during construction. In the specific
case of An Tri Pagoda, the riverside operation road is constructed directly on the existing access
road, approximately 3–5 m wide, which serves as the primary entrance to the pagoda. During road
subgrade and pavement works, construction and material transport traffic, estimated at 20–30
vehicle trips per day (dump trucks, rollers, light trucks), may temporarily narrow or disrupt access
to the pagoda, particularly on days of religious activities. This impact is localized and temporary,
concentrated over approximately 1–2 months of direct construction in front of the pagoda gate
under the staged construction approach.
     These impacts are localized and temporary, occurring only during direct construction at each
embankment and operation road section adjacent to the religious facilities, with durations ranging
from several weeks to a few months depending on construction progress. Based on the very close
proximity of construction activities to the facilities, the site-specific impact on Pham Tu Nghi
temple, An Tri Pagoda, and Quynh Cu Pagoda is assessed as Medium. The impacts can be
effectively managed through limiting high-intensity vibratory compaction near structures,
vibration monitoring, temporary reinforcement of weak boundary walls, dust control measures,
and scheduling construction to avoid major worship periods, thereby ensuring structural safety and
the continuation of normal religious activities for the community.
   4.3.3.6.    Impacts on sensitive receptors.
    The construction of the component 2 is likely to adversely impact Re market, Bao Quang
pagoda, (refer to Table 2.1, Table 2.2 and Table 2.3– Chapter 2 for detailed information) that are
more vulnerable than other receptors. The impact may include (i) the inconvenience of local
people’s access to sensitive receptors; (ii) dust generation causing health problems to people
working and visiting the receptors, (iii) noise causing annoyances and distractions to religious and
working activities; (iv) risks of damages to the buildings of receptors by vibration and other

                                                                                                Official Use Only
construction activities, (v) risks of traffic safety and accidents to people visiting to the receptors.
