   4.3.4. Site-specific impacts of Component 3
   4.3.4.1.    Road surface disruption and traffic congestion caused by material transportation
               in high-density urban areas
     Stormwater drainage and wastewater collection works under Component 3 are implemented
under constrained urban conditions and mainly apply open-cut construction methods, including:
(i) 220 m of reinforced concrete box culverts (2.5 × 2.0 m) at the Tam Bac and Thuy Doi regulating
sluices; (ii) upgraded stormwater drains with cross-sections ranging from 1.0 × 1.0 m to 3.0 × 2.0
m, with a total length of approximately 4.252 km, constructed mainly along Nguyen Tri Phuong,
Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tam Bac, Tran Nguyen Han, Hai Ba Trung, Lan Ong, Cau Dat, Dien Bien Phu,
and Hoang Dieu streets; and (iii) HDPE wastewater collection sewers (D300–D500) with a total
length of approximately 2.721 km, installed along Tam Bac, Lan Be, Nguyen Cong Hoa, Hoang
Minh Thao, and The Lu streets.




                                                                                                   223
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     Affected central urban streets typically have a carriageway width of 10–15 m, flanked by
residential houses and commercial premises located only 4–10 m from the construction area.
    For large-diameter drains (DN ≥ 1000 mm) and box culverts, excavation trenches, temporary
slopes, shoring systems, and material stockpiles typically occupy 40–60% of the carriageway
width, significantly reducing usable road space and necessitating temporary traffic arrangements.
For smaller drains (DN ≤ 1000 mm), construction is mainly within sidewalks of approximately 3.5
m width or occupies part of the curbside lane, resulting in 20–30% road narrowing.
    Additional impacts arise from material and waste transportation, with an average frequency
of 20–30 trips per day by 10-ton trucks for each 200 m construction section. This creates conflicts
with background traffic at intersections and narrow road sections (10–15 m wide) experiencing
high traffic volumes of approximately 900–1,800 vehicles/hour/direction during peak hours
(06:30–08:30 and 16:30–18:30), and 350–900 vehicles/hour/direction during off-peak periods. In
accordance with Hai Phong urban traffic regulations, trucks over 3.5 tons are restricted or
prohibited during peak hours, meaning that 10-ton trucks operate only during off-peak periods.
     If site fencing is incomplete, warning signs and lights are not continuously installed, or
excavation trenches are not temporarily covered after working hours, traffic safety risks increase
significantly, including the risk of pedestrians, motorcycles, and bicycles falling into open
trenches; collisions between local traffic and construction vehicles at narrowed road sections; and
nighttime or rainy-condition accidents due to reduced visibility.
     Directly affected receptors include road users on the affected urban streets; roadside
businesses such as clothing shops, restaurants, and household goods and electronics stores along
the Nguyen Tri Phuong, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tam Bac, Tran Nguyen Han, Hai Ba Trung, Lan Ong, Cau Dat,
Dien Bien Phu and Hoang Dieu streets that depend on direct road access; residents along the
frontage; construction workers operating in confined spaces adjacent to moving traffic; and urban
service vehicles such as waste collection trucks, ambulances, and fire engines that must pass
through construction zones.
    The impact is localized to individual construction sections and does not occur simultaneously
across the entire urban road network. Open-cut works are implemented on a rolling (staged) basis,
with priority given to completing one section before moving to the next. At any given time, only
one to two construction fronts are deployed on major streets to limit overall disruption. For each
150–200 m section, direct road occupation typically lasts 1–2 months, and may be longer for large
box culverts or locations near intersections. Congestion levels and traffic safety risks increase
during urban peak hours and during periods when multiple drainage works are implemented
concurrently.
     Based on (i) the large proportion of road space occupied by open-cut drainage works under
narrow urban road conditions (10–15 m), with adjacent buildings only 4–10 m away; (ii) increased
traffic conflicts due to material transportation under high background traffic volumes; (iii)
heightened safety risks if fencing, signage, lighting, and trench covering are inadequate; and (iv)


                                                                                               224
                                                                                               Official Use Only
temporary but extended road occupation of 1–2 months per section, the impact on urban traffic
and commercial–daily activities is assessed as Moderate.
     However, because the impact is localized, staged in time, regulated by rolling construction,
limited numbers of work fronts, and restrictions on heavy trucks during peak hours, it is temporary
and manageable, and can be effectively mitigated through strict implementation of temporary
traffic management, safety fencing, and transport scheduling measures.
   4.3.4.2.    Restricted riverbank access and small-boat movement during construction of
               regulating sluices
    Construction of the Tam Bac and Thuy Doi regulating sluices is carried out within the river
channel and requires installation of cofferdams and larsen steel sheet piles to isolate foundation
and structural works. The construction method does not block the entire Tam Bac River flow
throughout the construction period; flow is maintained through the remaining channel or via
temporary diversion to ensure drainage capacity and hydraulic stability.
     The Tam Bac River section at the two sluices does not support commercial waterway transport
or fishing activities. Waterborne activity consists mainly of small wooden motorboats, canoes, and
small barges used by local residents for temporary mooring, typically 6–12 m in length, 2–3 m in
width, and with shallow drafts (<1.5 m). The number of temporarily moored vessels is estimated
at 20–30 boats per day, scattered along both riverbanks and concentrated near the Thuy Doi sluice.
     With a river width of approximately 50–90 m and no commercial navigation, the maintained
channel primarily serves hydraulic and environmental functions. However, during sluice
construction, cofferdams, safety fencing, and exclusion zones will reduce riverbank access from
the water side, temporarily restricting boat landing, mooring, and embarkation or disembarkation
at the sluice locations. In addition, approximately 1,200 m of HDPE D300–D500 intercepting
sewers constructed by open-cut methods along both riverbanks require continuous fencing, further
interrupting local riverbank access points.
     Directly affected receptors are local residents who temporarily moor small boats along the
Tam Bac River. As there is no commercial navigation, the affected group is local and small in
scale. The impact is localized, confined to cofferdam areas, fenced sluice construction zones, and
riverbank sections under sewer construction. At each sluice, restricted access and small-boat
movement occur throughout the period of direct construction (up to 24 months). Outside these
areas, river flow and general riverbank access are largely maintained.
     The impact is assessed as Low, reflecting the small-scale, non-commercial nature of river use.
Restrictions are limited to construction zones and durations and can be mitigated through advance
notification, temporary waterway guidance, and timely restoration of riverbank access after
construction.




                                                                                               225
                                                                                               Official Use Only
   4.3.4.3.    Risk of damage to underground utilities during drilling and subsurface works
     The risk of damage to underground technical infrastructure arises mainly from deep
excavation and subsurface works associated with stormwater drainage and wastewater collection
under Component 3 in the dense urban core of Hai Phong. Existing underground utilities include
urban water supply pipelines, existing drainage systems, medium- and low-voltage power cables,
telecommunications cables, and household connections, which are densely distributed, particularly
along Tam Bac, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tran Nguyen Han, and Hai Ba Trung streets. Available records
indicate that some utilities were constructed in different periods, with incomplete or locally
inaccurate as-built drawings, increasing construction risk.
     Most drains are constructed by open-cut methods with excavation depths typically 1.0–4.0 m,
increasing the likelihood of direct conflicts with existing utilities. The impact is localized and site-
specific, concentrated at open-cut sections for box culverts and large stormwater drains. The
highest risk occurs during alignment setting-out, excavation, pipe installation, and road
reinstatement stages. Under rolling construction, potential risk at each location typically lasts 1–2
months, corresponding to the period of direct works.
     Directly affected receptors are existing underground utilities managed by water supply, power,
telecommunications, and drainage authorities. Indirectly affected receptors include urban service
users along the affected streets including households, businesses, and sensitive facilities such as
hospitals, schools, and religious structures if incidents cause temporary interruption of water,
electricity, communications, or drainage services.
     This risk is site-specific, arising in a dense urban environment with complex underground
infrastructure. Although the probability of incidents can be controlled through appropriate surveys
and engineering measures, the potential consequences for urban services and sensitive receptors
are significant if an incident occurs. Based on localized spatial extent, short impact duration, but
potentially high consequences, the impact is assessed as Moderate and can be effectively mitigated
through detailed utility record review, supplementary surveys, appropriate construction methods,
and close coordination with utility operators throughout construction.
   4.3.4.4.    Risk of cracking/collapse or instability of surface structures due to open
               excavation works close to houses
     The main works under Component 3 are implemented within the existing urban core, where
excavation and underground construction must be carried out under constrained site conditions
with residential and commercial buildings closely lining both sides of streets. The key impact
source related to potential cracking or damage to structures is the excavation depth and the
associated change in ground stress conditions during construction. Open-cut excavations for box
culverts and stormwater drains typically reach depths of approximately 2.0–4.0 m below the
existing road surface. At the two regulating sluices, cofferdam construction and larsen sheet piling
require deeper excavation, pile installation, and ground treatment under weak and water-saturated
soil conditions. Some interceptor sewer sections apply trenchless methods to maintain traffic;



                                                                                                   226
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
however, localized ground deformation may still occur if excavation pressure and geotechnical–
hydrological conditions are not adequately controlled.
     The primary receptors are adjacent residential houses and shop-houses along the streets where
drains and sewers are installed, including Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tam Bac, Tran Nguyen Han, Hai Ba
Trung, Lan Ong, Cau Dat, Dien Bien Phu, Hoang Dieu, Lan Be, Nguyen Cong Hoa, and Hoang
Minh Thao. These buildings are predominantly 3–4 storey reinforced concrete structures built in
continuous rows, commonly with shallow foundations and limited settlement joints; therefore,
they are sensitive to differential settlement and lateral ground movement when the ground is
disturbed. Within a wider area, sensitive facilities such as schools, healthcare facilities, and
religious structures are also present; however, these receptors are generally located several tens of
meters (about 10–30 m) from deep excavations and underground works and therefore face lower
risk than buildings immediately adjacent to the works.
    Direct construction at each typical section (about 200 m of drain or a major sewer section)
generally lasts 1–2 months. The highest risk occurs during excavation, underground structural
works, and road reinstatement. Ground deformation may occur immediately during excavation and
may also manifest later as post-settlement if the ground is weakened and not properly stabilized
and controlled.
     This risk of cracking or instability of surface structures due to trenching and underground
works in Component 3 is a site- and phase-specific impact. Several sewer sections along urban
streets are located very close to buildings (approximately 4–10 m) and require deep excavation,
increasing receptor sensitivity. However, the FS specifies that where excavation walls are close to
houses, the contractor must “install excavation wall reinforcement before foundation excavation”
to maintain trench stability and reduce lateral displacement and localized settlement transmitted to
adjacent structures. Therefore, considering the localized influence area, the temporary nature of
works, the typical receptor type (3–4 storey reinforced concrete buildings), and the specified
temporary shoring/sheet-pile support measures, the impact is assessed as Minor and can be
effectively controlled through appropriate construction methods, pre-construction condition
surveys of adjacent buildings, and ground deformation monitoring throughout construction.
   4.3.4.5.    Impacts on aquatic life and water quality of Tam Bac River during construction
     Construction of the Tam Bac and Thuy Doi regulating sluices generates key impact sources
to the aquatic environment during construction, including: (i) installation of cofferdams and larsen
sheet piles, excavation of foundations, earthworks, and sluice structure construction within the
river and near the water edge, which disturb fine silt–sand sediments and generate suspended solids
(TSS). The volume of material directly associated with the Tam Bac River works (two regulating
sluices) is estimated at approximately 2,550–3,830 m³. During temporary stockpiling, this
soil/sediment becomes a primary source of fine sediment runoff into the river during rainfall events
or when temporary drainage is not yet fully established; (ii) construction wastewater, including
sediment-laden stormwater runoff from work areas, material stockpiles, and riverside access roads,
together with dewatering effluent and domestic wastewater from workers if site facilities are


                                                                                                 227
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
located near the river and are not properly collected and treated; and (iii) localized pollution risks
from spillage of construction materials (mortar, concrete, packaging), small volumes of
construction solid waste, and leakage of oil, grease, and fuel from diesel and hydraulic equipment
(crawler excavators, crawler cranes, vibro hammers for sheet piling, generators, and high-capacity
pumps) operating within or adjacent to the river, particularly during peak construction and
equipment maintenance periods.
     The Tam Bac River at the two sluice locations is approximately 50–90 m wide with an average
depth of about 2.5–4.0 m. Flow velocity in the rainy season is estimated at about 0.4–0.8 m/s,
corresponding to discharges of approximately 40–120 m³/s. In the dry season, velocity decreases
to about 0.1–0.3 m/s with discharges of only about 10–40 m³/s. These slow-flow conditions,
particularly in the dry season, indicate limited local dilution and self-cleaning capacity. Baseline
water quality monitoring indicates relatively good conditions, with all parameters meeting QCVN
08-MT:2023/BTNMT (Column B), specifically BOD₅ 4.2–5.4 mg/L, COD 7.8–9.4 mg/L, DO 5.0
mg/L, and TSS 10.2–10.4 mg/L.
    Bottom sediments are dominated by fine silt–sand with a high fine fraction and are easily
resuspended by mechanical disturbance. Under low dry-season flows, resuspended fine sediments
tend to settle slowly and accumulate locally around the construction area. In the rainy season,
higher velocities can transport suspended matter more rapidly, but impacts are still expected to be
concentrated mainly within the construction reach and the hydraulically connected near-
downstream section.
     Based on the identified impact sources (foundation excavation, cofferdam and sheet piling,
dewatering, temporary sediment stockpiling, and sediment-laden runoff) and the Tam Bac River
hydrological conditions, TSS increases during construction are expected to be localized and short-
term. TSS concentrations may increase noticeably during near-bank excavation and during heavy
rainfall events; however, the affected reach is expected to be largely confined to the river section
at the two sluices and approximately 100–300 m downstream. Once direct excavation activities
cease and cofferdams are stabilized, TSS levels are expected to decline rapidly toward baseline.
     The directly affected receptors are the Tam Bac River water environment and aquatic
ecosystems within the construction reach and adjacent riverbank sections where interceptor sewers
are installed. The aquatic ecosystem is already substantially modified and consists mainly of
common species adapted to fluctuating conditions and urban pollution, including tilapia
(Oreochromis spp.), common carp (Cyprinus carpio), catfish (Clarias spp.), crucian carp
(Carassius auratus), freshwater prawns/shrimp (Macrobrachium spp.), Corbicula clams
(Corbicula spp.), and freshwater snails (Gastropoda). No rare, endangered, endemic, or protected
aquatic species have been recorded within the project area. Water quality deterioration during
construction may affect aquatic life through (i) increased TSS and turbidity, reducing light
penetration, affecting fish feeding, and causing localized deposition on low-mobility benthic
organisms in the near-field area; and (ii) potential localized DO reduction and increases in organic



                                                                                                  228
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
pollution and mineral oil in still or semi-enclosed waters around cofferdams if construction
wastewater and oil control are inadequate.
     According to the construction schedule, direct construction for each regulating sluice is
expected to last approximately 6–8 months, with the highest-risk period for water quality
(increased turbidity/TSS and deposition) concentrated over about 3–4 months during excavation
and near-water structural works. Turbidity increase events are short-lived (hours to days) but may
recur multiple times throughout direct construction, particularly during the rainy season when
heavy rainfall generates runoff from work areas and stockpiles.
     Based on (i) the volume of excavated material directly related to Tam Bac River works being
relatively small compared with the total Component 3 volume; (ii) the limited number and scale
of in-river construction equipment; (iii) baseline water quality being relatively good but with
limited assimilative capacity under low-flow conditions; and (iv) the localized and temporary
spatial and temporal extent of impacts, the impact on Tam Bac River water quality and aquatic
ecosystems during construction of the two regulating sluices and associated sewers is assessed as
Medium. With the adoption of a construction approach that avoids complete river blockage, strict
cofferdam integrity control, silt control measures, collection and treatment of dewatering effluent
prior to discharge, effective management of soil/sediment stockpiles and temporary drainage, and
strict prevention of oil and construction material leakage near the riverbank, these impacts are
expected to be short-term, reversible, and manageable within acceptable limits.
   4.3.4.6.    Site-specific impacts on Gold Star International General Hospital and Viet Tiep
               Friendship Hospital
      Under Component 3, two large reinforced concrete box culvert routes pass directly in front of
the main entrance areas of two major hospitals in the central urban area, including:
(i) a culvert route along Nguyen Tri Phuong Street approximately 1,200 m in length with a cross-
section of 1,500 × 1,500 mm, passing the entrance area of Gold Star International General
Hospital; and (ii) a culvert route along Hai Ba Trung Street approximately 430 m in length with a
cross-section of 1,200 × 1,000 mm, passing the entrance area of Viet Tiep Friendship Hospital.
The distance from the excavation trench, shoring system, and site fencing to hospital boundary
walls and main entrance gates is only approximately 5–10 m, creating clear site-specific impacts
on hospital access and service environment.
     Gold Star Hospital is a private hospital; while its service scale may be smaller than large public
hospitals, it remains a common option for local residents and therefore maintains steady daily
patient and vehicle flows. In contrast, Viet Tiep Friendship Hospital is the city’s tertiary public
referral hospital, with consistently very high patient volumes, particularly among elderly and
insured patients. The fact that thousands of vehicle movements occur daily indicates very large
and continuous traffic demand, making uninterrupted access at the hospital entrance a high-
sensitivity issue.




                                                                                                   229
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
     During open-cut box culvert construction, key activities include removal of road pavement
structures, deep trench excavation and installation of shoring, lowering and installation of culvert
segments, concrete jointing works, and road reinstatement, together with transport of excavated
soil and materials by dump trucks. With a road width of approximately 15 m and works located
along the curbside lane/sidewalk zone (about 3.5 m), the curbside lane will be significantly
narrowed, reducing space for patient drop-off and pick-up, reducing discharge capacity at the
hospital gate, and increasing conflicts between background traffic and hospital access traffic. This
is particularly critical during peak periods (06:00–08:30 and 16:30–18:30), when traffic volumes
along Nguyen Tri Phuong and Hai Ba Trung streets may reach approximately 1,200–2,200
vehicles/hour/direction (dominated by motorcycles, followed by private cars and taxis). During
off-peak periods (09:30–11:00, 13:30–15:30, and after 19:30), volumes are approximately 500–
1,000 vehicles/hour/direction.
    Given the very short distance between noise/dust sources and hospital gates and boundary
walls, construction noise from excavators, concrete cutting, vibratory compaction, and trucks may
reach 80–90 dBA within 10–20 m, potentially exceeding the daytime limits for healthcare facilities
under QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT and directly affecting the treatment environment and patient
reception areas. Dust generated from excavation, temporary stockpiling, and material transport
near the entrances may increase localized TSP/PM₁₀ levels; during dry and windy conditions or
when vehicles enter and exit continuously, dust concentrations near hospital boundaries may
temporarily exceed QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT, with the most pronounced effects on elderly people,
respiratory patients, and those with underlying health conditions.
     Based on (i) direct open-cut construction of large box culverts along two key urban corridors
with a 15 m road cross-section and works located at the curbside lane; (ii) high background traffic
density and continuous hospital access demand, particularly at Viet Tiep; and (iii) the high
sensitivity of healthcare facilities to noise, dust, and emergency access constraints, the site-specific
impact is assessed as Moderate at the Gold Star Hospital entrance area and Substantial at the Viet
Tiep Hospital entrance area during periods of direct works. With staged construction, provision of
priority access lanes for ambulances and patient drop-off/pick-up, restrictions on heavy trucks
during peak hours, and strict dust and noise control in front of hospital gates, the impacts can be
controlled and are not expected to cause long-term disruption to hospital services.
   4.3.4.7.    Impacts on sensitive receptors
      The construction of the component 3 is likely to adversely impact Nguyen Tri Phuong primary
school, Our Lady of Carmel Church, Tam Ky Temple and Tam Bac Pagoda (refer to Table 2.2– Chapter
2 for detailed information) that are more vulnerable than other receptors. The impact may include
(i) the inconvenience of local people’s access to sensitive receptors; (ii) dust generation causing
health problems to people working, studying in and visiting the receptors, (iii) noise causing
annoyances and distractions to religious, studying and working activities; (iv) risks of traffic safety
and accidents to people visiting to the receptors. The impacts on the sensitive receptors within the
radius of 50 m around the component area are described in Table 4.25

                                                                                                  231
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
   4.4.    Impact & Risk During Operation Phase
   4.4.1. Impacts and risks during operation of Component 1
     The operation phase of Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Climate-Resilient Urban Infrastructure
Development will generate long-term environmental and social impacts directly associated with
the functioning of the new six-lane Ring Road No. 3, its interchanges, elevated structures, and the
two major bridges (Ngo Quyen and Ruot Lon). Most operational outcomes are expected to be
positive, particularly through improved regional connectivity, reduced travel times, enhanced
logistics efficiency, and better access to public services for communities in Thuy Nguyen.
However, several adverse impacts may arise if traffic management, road maintenance, and
environmental monitoring are not implemented effectively. These include increased traffic
emissions and noise affecting nearby sensitive receptors, road-safety risks at major junctions,
localized flooding due to blocked drainage, alterations to river flow patterns at bridge piers, and
long-term impacts on landscape and protection-forest habitats.
   4.4.1.1.    Noise, Emissions, and Road Safety from Traffic Growth
     During the operation of Component 1, increased traffic volume is the primary source of
environmental and road-safety impacts. According to the FS, traffic growth follows early uptake
patterns typical of new urban arterials in Viet Nam, with a mix of light vehicles and a sustained
proportion of heavy trucks serving logistics, port access and inter-district movement. Vehicle
operation generates continuous noise from engines and tire–road interaction, exhaust emissions
including PM₂.₅ and NOx, SOx and higher exposure to traffic accidents due to increased traffic
density and speed.
    Road traffic noise has been assessed using standard propagation relationships consistent with
the US Federal Highway Administration Traffic Noise Model, applied conceptually to the vehicle
speeds and traffic composition defined in the FS. Light vehicles travelling at approximately 50
km/h typically generate noise levels in the mid-60s dBA at a distance of 10 m from the road edge,
while heavy trucks travelling at around 60 km/h may generate levels in the upper-70s to low-80s
dBA at the same distance. Due to distance attenuation, these noise levels are reduced to
approximately the mid-50s to mid-60s dBA at distances of around 30 m. At this setback,
operational noise levels are generally within the daytime limits specified in QCVN
26:2025/BTNMT for residential areas.
     For air emissions, conceptual application of COPERT 5 emission factors indicates that light
vehicles emit on the order of a few tens of milligrams of PM₂.₅ per kilometre, while heavy-duty
trucks emit several grams of NOx per kilometre depending on engine technology and operating
conditions. In addition, COPERT-based factors indicate that SOx emissions are closely linked to
fuel sulfur content and are generally much lower than NOx on a per-kilometre basis, with the
highest contributions associated with diesel-powered heavy-duty vehicles. When combined with
the FS traffic forecast and typical roadside dispersion behaviour, incremental increases in near-
road PM₂.₅ concentrations during peak traffic periods are expected to be on the order of a few


                                                                                               232
                                                                                               Official Use Only
micrograms per cubic metre. Such increases are consistent with observations along comparable
urban arterial roads in Vietnamese cities and remain within national ambient air quality standards,
although they represent a continuous source of exposure for roadside receptors.
     The principal receptors include residential clusters, mixed-use shop-houses, schools, clinics
and other sensitive land uses located along Road No. 3 and bridge approach sections, particularly
where buildings are situated within approximately 10–30 m of the carriageway such as Lap Le,
Trung Ha, Ngu Lao and Group 10 residential areas. Additional receptors include road users
themselves such as motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians, especially at intersections with NH10,
and PR359, access points and bus stops. These receptors are already subject to traffic-related noise,
emissions and safety risks under existing conditions, and operational impacts represent an
incremental increase rather than a new exposure pathway.
     The duration of these impacts is long-term and effectively permanent over the operational life
of the road. Traffic-related noise, emissions and road-safety risks persist continuously once the
road is opened and stabilise as traffic volumes reach maturity. Spatially, impacts are confined to
the immediate road corridor and adjacent frontage areas, with rapid attenuation beyond short
distances (5-20 m) from the carriageway.
     Considering that (i) operational noise levels at typical residential setbacks are within
applicable standards, (ii) incremental air-quality impacts are small in magnitude and remain below
regulatory thresholds, (iii) affected receptors are largely located along an established transport
corridor, and (iv) road-safety risks can be managed through speed control, traffic enforcement,
signage and ongoing monitoring, the overall significance of noise, air-emission and road-safety
impacts from traffic growth is assessed as Moderate.
   4.4.1.2.    Vibration due to Heavy Trucks
    Operational vibration is generated primarily by heavy trucks (typically 10–30 tons gross
vehicle weight) travelling at speed, particularly on rigid pavement sections, bridge approaches,
and areas with pavement irregularities or expansion joints. Ground-borne vibration propagates
through pavement and subgrade and may be perceptible in nearby structures. The main receptors
are residential buildings, small commercial structures, and sensitive facilities located within
approximately 10–30 m of the road edge. Most buildings along the corridor are low-rise masonry
or reinforced concrete houses with shallow foundations, which are generally tolerant of low-
amplitude traffic-induced vibration.
    Vibration effects are chronic but low-intensity, occurring intermittently as heavy vehicles
pass. The spatial influence is limited to a narrow corridor adjacent to the road and does not
propagate over long distances. The FS specifies adequate pavement structural capacity, uniform
subgrade treatment, and smooth transition design at bridges and culverts to minimise dynamic
loads. Speed control, axle-load regulation, and routine pavement maintenance are key operational
controls that directly limit vibration levels. Based on comparable operational roads, traffic-induced
vibration levels from heavy vehicles are expected to remain below common building-damage



                                                                                                 233
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
thresholds (typically <2–3 mm/s PPV at 10–20 m distance). No structural damage is anticipated,
and nuisance effects are limited. The operational vibration impact is therefore assessed as Low.
   4.4.1.3.    Safety Risks for Waterway Navigation (Ngo Quyen Bridge)
     During operation, the Ngo Quyen Bridge becomes a permanent fixed structure across the Cam
River navigation corridor. The main navigation-related hazard is vessel allision with bridge
piers/abutments or misalignment through the navigation opening under adverse visibility and tidal-
current conditions. This risk is influenced by (i) the baseline intensity and mix of river traffic at
the crossing and (ii) the bridge’s designed navigation envelope and pier arrangement.
     The primary receptors are commercial and service vessels navigating the Cam River at the
bridge location (Km 3+200), including frequent barges and inland cargo vessels and larger
seagoing cargo/container ships accessing Hai Phong’s port system. Baseline conditions indicate
high waterway use (about 200–300 vessel movements/day) within a river section of roughly 300–
350 m width; larger ships in this reach are commonly in the 5,000–30,000 DWT range with typical
beam widths of 18–32 m, navigating along the main channel under traffic regulation where
required. Secondary receptors include bridge operators/maintenance teams and
downstream/upstream users exposed to accident spill-over consequences (temporary navigation
restriction, congestion, spill response).
     The FS indicates that the bridge configuration was selected to reduce the number of in-river
obstructions by adopting a large main span, thereby limiting interference with the main navigation
channel. The agreed navigation clearance at the bridge location is width B > 140 m and vertical
clearance H ≥ 41 m (agreed with the Viet Nam Maritime Administration), and the adopted cable-
stayed arrangement includes a 230 m main span (95+230+95 m) to keep piers away from the
principal channel to the extent practicable. The FS also notes the need to consider potential ship
collision loads (up to about 10,000 DWT) in selecting the span range and structural arrangement.
These design provisions substantially reduce (but do not eliminate) operational navigation risk.
     The Cam River is currently crossed by several existing bridges, including Hoang Van Thu
Bridge, Binh Bridge all of which have been in stable operation for many years and already impose
fixed horizontal and vertical constraints on navigation. Inland and coastal vessels transiting this
reach are therefore accustomed to navigating under permanent bridge structures, complying with
regulated navigation envelopes, tidal windows, and vessel traffic coordination implemented by the
inland waterway and maritime authorities. No systemic navigation safety issues have been reported
in association with the routine operation of these existing bridges.
     The impact is long-term and effectively permanent, persisting for the full operational life of
the bridge. Considering (i) the high baseline traffic volume and presence of large vessels at the
crossing, (ii) the fact that river traffic is already operating safely under multiple existing bridges
across the Cam River with permanent navigation constraints, such as Hoang Van Thu Bridge and
Binh Bridge, (iii) the permanent introduction of fixed structural constraints, and (iv) the substantial
reduction in risk achieved through the agreed navigation envelope and large main-span selection



                                                                                                   234
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
specifically intended to minimise channel obstruction, the operational safety risk for waterway
navigation at Ngo Quyen Bridge is assessed as Moderate.
   4.4.1.4.    Impact on the protective function of the protection forest at the Ruot Lon Bridge
               abutments
    During the operation phase, the impact on the protective function of the protection forest at
the Ruot Lon Bridge is limited to the permanent occupation of land by fixed bridge structures
located on the riverbank where the protection forest is present. Based on the final bridge
configuration described in the FS, the protection forest is located only on one riverbank, and
permanent forest loss during operation is therefore confined to the footprints of the bridge
abutment and a limited number of approach-bridge piers on that bank.
     While a total area of approximately 0.76 ha of planted protection forest was legally acquired
for the project, only a small portion of this area remains permanently unavailable for forest
regrowth during operation. The permanently affected area is limited to the footprints of the
abutment and approach piers, estimated at approximately 500 m², which represents a small fraction
of the acquired area and a very minor proportion of the overall riparian protection forest along the
Ruot Lon River.
     All other areas within the acquired corridor beneath and adjacent to the bridge deck are not
permanently sealed and retain suitable conditions for natural regeneration and/or replanting. Given
the planted and managed nature of the protection forest dominated by Sonneratia caseolaris,
natural regeneration and assisted reforestation are expected to occur progressively once
construction disturbance ceases. Under typical local estuarine conditions, partial vegetation
recovery is expected within 2–3 years, with recovery of functional canopy cover and bank-
stabilization capacity generally achieved within 5–7 years, depending on hydrological conditions
and management support.
     As a result, the long-term impact mechanism during operation is limited to a localized and
permanent reduction of vegetation cover only at the fixed structural footprints, which may slightly
weaken bank protection capacity at those discrete points by increasing localized exposure to flow
turbulence and wave action. However, the remaining protection forest belt upstream and
downstream of the bridge remains continuous, and no long, uninterrupted gaps are created along
the riverbank. At the corridor scale, the overall protective function of the riparian forest in terms
of wave attenuation and bank stabilization is retained.
     Considering (i) the very limited permanent area affected during operation (~500 m²); (ii) the
planted, non-natural character of the protection forest; (iii) the continuity of the forest belt along
the river outside the immediate bridge footprints; and (iv) the high potential for natural
regeneration and reforestation within a timeframe of approximately 5–7 years after construction,
the impact on the protective function of the protection forest at the Ruot Lon Bridge during the
operation phase is assessed as Low.




                                                                                                  235
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
   4.4.1.5.    Impacts related to community and production-area severance
     During the operation phase of the Ring Road No. 3, with a platform width of approximately
47–68 m and a continuous central median, forms a permanent linear transport corridor that creates
a physical and functional barrier across existing land-use patterns. The alignment passes through
dense residential clusters over about 3.1 km, notably at Km 9+500–Km 10+500 (Lap Le), Km
13+000–Km 14+100 (Ngu Lao), and Km 15+000–Km 15+500 (Trung Ha) (see detailed
description at Section 2.6), where houses and small businesses are located within 10–20 m of the
right-of-way. In these sections, the road separates residential frontage from local access roads,
community facilities, and daily activity spaces located on the opposite side.
    In addition, the alignment crosses agricultural and aquaculture areas over approximately 4.9
km, mainly at Km 6+500–Km 8+900 and Km 11+900–Km 12+800, where rice paddies and
household-managed aquaculture ponds are distributed on both sides of the corridor. In these low-
lying sections, the road embankment is typically about 1.0 m higher than surrounding ground
levels, increasing the difficulty of informal crossing for farmers accessing fields and ponds.
     The primary receptors affected by this severance effect are residential households, farmers,
and small-scale land users whose homes and production areas are located on opposite sides of the
alignment. Elderly residents, farmers transporting tools or produce, and pedestrians without
motorised transport are particularly sensitive to reduced accessibility. Operational traffic on Ring
Road No. 3 is characterised by higher speeds and volumes than pre-project local roads,
substantially increasing the risk associated with at-grade crossing attempts. Where pedestrian
crossings, agricultural underpasses, or service roads are absent or widely spaced, residents may
resort to informal crossing points, increasing the likelihood of traffic accidents involving
pedestrians, bicycles, and motorcycles.
     This impact is long-term and effectively permanent, persisting throughout the operational life
of the road and remaining spatially confined to sections where settlements and agricultural land
are directly split by the corridor. Unlike construction-related access disruption, the severance effect
does not diminish over time unless specific crossing and access-management measures are
implemented. Considering the high-capacity nature of the road, the direct effect on daily
movement and livelihood access, the disproportionate sensitivity of elderly residents and farming
households, and the permanent character of the barrier, community severance during operation of
Component 1 is assessed as Moderate.
   4.4.1.6.    Impacts on Irrigation and Water Supply for Agricultural Land along the
               Alignment
    During operation of Ring Road No. 3, the road embankment and associated drainage structures
permanently modify surface-water flow paths across agricultural areas. Potential impacts arise
where the road alignment intersects existing irrigation canals, field ditches, and small water-supply
channels serving rice paddies, vegetable plots, and aquaculture ponds. If cross-drainage
connections are insufficient, poorly maintained, or hydraulically mismatched, the road may act as



                                                                                                   236
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
a partial barrier, disrupting gravity-fed irrigation and drainage regimes that farmers rely on for
seasonal cropping.
     The primary receptors are agricultural households cultivating rice, mixed annual crops, and
small-scale aquaculture ponds located on both sides of the Ring Road No. 3 alignment. The site-
specific impact assessment for agriculture and aquaculture during construction, the most sensitive
areas are the low-lying agricultural and aquaculture zones between km 6+500–km 8+800, km
10+300–km 12+600, and km 14+300–km 16+600 (see detailed description at Section 2.5), where
irrigation depends on small canals and field ditches connected to the Hau Long and Chu river
systems. These areas require reliable bidirectional water exchange for both irrigation and drainage,
particularly during land preparation and peak growing periods.
     The FS indicates that Ring Road No. 3 is designed with a continuous longitudinal drainage
system on both sides of the carriageway, combined with frequent cross-culverts and box culverts
to maintain transverse water connectivity. Cross-drainage structures are designed to accommodate
local irrigation flows and stormwater for a minimum 10-year return period, and are aligned, where
practicable, with existing canals and ditches. In agricultural sections, culvert spacing is
intentionally denser than in urban segments, and the design includes restoration and re-connection
of irrigation canals after construction, with canal inverts and side slopes reinstated to pre-project
profiles. At locations where canals are realigned, the FS requires equivalent hydraulic capacity to
be maintained, ensuring no net loss of irrigation or drainage function compared to baseline
conditions.
     Any irrigation-related impact during operation is long-term in nature, as it is linked to the
permanent presence of the road embankment. However, unlike construction-phase disturbances,
operational impacts depend primarily on the performance and maintenance of the designed cross-
drainage system. Where culverts and canals function as intended and are kept free of sediment and
debris, water supply and drainage for agricultural land are expected to be maintained at levels
comparable to pre-project conditions. Localised impacts may still occur episodically during
extreme rainfall or if maintenance is inadequate, but these are not inherent to the design itself.
Considering that (i) the alignment crosses several low-lying agricultural and aquaculture areas that
are sensitive to irrigation disruption, (ii) the FS incorporates specific design measures to maintain
transverse irrigation and drainage connectivity, including frequent cross-culverts and canal
reinstatement, (iii) affected areas and locations have been clearly identified through the site-
specific agricultural and aquaculture impact assessment, and (iv) residual risks are mainly
associated with operation and maintenance rather than design limitations, the impact on irrigation
and water supply for agricultural land during operation of Ring Road No. 3 is assessed as Low to
Moderate. With proper maintenance of cross-drainage structures and coordination with local
irrigation authorities, long-term adverse effects on agricultural water supply are not expected.




                                                                                                 237
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
   4.4.1.7.   Risk of Riverbank Erosion, Pier Scour, and Flow Regime Alteration during
              Operation of Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge
     During the operation phase, the bridge piers and abutments of Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot
Lon Bridge interact permanently with river flow, creating a potential for local pier scour, bank
erosion, and minor alteration of flow patterns in the immediate vicinity of the structures. The
magnitude of these effects depends primarily on river width, flow velocity, discharge regime, and
the proportion of the flow section occupied by bridge foundations.
     At the Ngo Quyen Bridge crossing the Cam River, the river width at the bridge location is
approximately 300–350 m, with an average water depth of about 7–10 m. Typical flow velocities
range from 0.5–0.8 m/s during the dry season to approximately 1.2–1.8 m/s during flood
conditions, corresponding to average seasonal discharges of roughly 300–400 m³/s in the dry
season and up to 800–1,200 m³/s during major floods. At the Ruot Lon Bridge, the river width is
narrower, approximately 180–220 m, with average depths of 2.5–3.5 m and lower flow velocities,
typically 0.2–0.4 m/s in the dry season and 0.6–1.1 m/s during the flood season, with
corresponding discharges of about 150–250 m³/s (dry season) and 450–500 m³/s (flood season).
    In both cases, the bridge piers (with a typical plan dimension of approximately 22 × 60 m)
occupy only a small fraction of the effective flow. section, and hydraulic optimisation of span
arrangement and pier spacing maintains adequate conveyance width. Hydraulic–morphological
modelling undertaken for the FS indicates negligible head losses on the order of approximately
0.002–0.02 m, with flood water levels remaining below design thresholds and no formation of
hydraulic bottlenecks, including under extreme flood events and climate change–adjusted
scenarios.
     Model results further indicate that hydraulic impacts during operation are highly localised,
limited mainly to the immediate vicinity of the piers and abutments. Predicted local pier scour
depths are up to approximately 2.0 m, while minor bank erosion is expected to be limited to about
0.3–0.5 m along the Ruot Lon River and up to approximately 2.5 m at isolated, unprotected
sections of the Cam River. These effects are consistent with typical responses of large alluvial
rivers to fixed in-channel structures and are fully accounted for in the design.
    The FS incorporates permanent mitigation measures to control these risks, including deep pier
foundations extending below the calculated maximum scour depth, riprap or stone mattress
protection around pier bases and abutments sized for 1%–0.2% annual exceedance probability
flood velocities, and extension of scour protection upstream and downstream to cover predicted
scour-hole development zones. Along erosion-prone riverbanks, reinforced revetments are
provided; at the Ruot Lon Bridge, these are combined with eco-engineering measures that retain
mangrove vegetation and preserve the protective function of the riparian protection forest.
    With these measures in place, changes in flow velocity, riverbed level, and bank stability
remain confined to the immediate vicinity of the bridge structures and are controlled within the
design tolerance of the protection system. No progressive riverbank erosion, alteration of river



                                                                                             238
                                                                                             Official Use Only
alignment, or long-term degradation of flood conveyance capacity is expected during operation.
Accordingly, the operational-phase impact related to riverbank erosion, pier scour, and flow
regime alteration at Ngo Quyen Bridge and Ruot Lon Bridge is assessed as Low.
   4.4.1.8.    Flooding risks to nearby residential areas and agricultural land
     Increased stormwater runoff and localized flooding during operation of Component 1 is driven
by (i) the creation of a continuous impervious road surface along Ring Road No. 3 and (ii) localised
“barrier effects” where the finished road elevation is higher than surrounding low-lying land,
accelerating runoff and constraining natural overland drainage if cross-drainage structures are
undersized, blocked, or poorly maintained. The FS design basis reduces this risk through a dual-
side longitudinal drainage system, hydraulic connections to existing canals, and a cross-drainage
system (culverts) designed from hydrological and hydraulic calculations to ensure conveyance,
with culvert diameters generally not less than D1250 and accounting for climate-change
considerations (TCVN 9845:2013).
     Receptors are low-lying residential pockets and agricultural/aquaculture plots located
immediately adjacent to the alignment where surface drainage currently relies on shallow field
ditches and local canal gradients. The most sensitive built-up sections include Lap Le (Km 9+500–
Km 10+500), Ngu Lao (Km 13+000–Km 14+100), Trung Ha (Km 15+000–Km 15+500), and 10
Hamlet (Km 17+300–Km 17+500), where finished road levels are designed close to existing
access roads but still sit above natural ground by about 0.15–0.8 m (Lap Le ~0.8 m; PR359
intersection from Km 13+000 ~0.15 m; Trung Ha ~0.5 m; 10 Hamlet ~0.6 m). The more flood-
prone receptors occur along low-lying agricultural and aquaculture sections at Km 6+500–Km
8+800, Km 10+300–Km 12+600, and Km 14+300–Km 16+600, where the road formation is
designed at approximately 1.0 m above surrounding ground and where runoff ponding can occur
on the lower side during short-duration, high-intensity storms if outlets and cross-culverts are
obstructed or if local canals cannot freely receive peak flows.
     The impact is long-term and recurring, manifesting intermittently during heavy rainfall events
over the operational life of the road. It does not occur continuously, but the consequence
(temporary ponding/inundation) can recur seasonally, particularly during peak storm periods, and
is strongly conditioned by maintenance of inlets, side drains, and culvert crossings and by the
capacity of the receiving canal network.
     Considering that (i) the FS incorporates engineered longitudinal drainage and cross-culverts
designed to convey runoff to existing canals (including minimum culvert sizing and climate-
change considerations), which substantially reduces baseline flood risk, but (ii) low-lying
agricultural/aquaculture stretches with approximately 1.0 m formation height remain sensitive to
blockage and maintenance failures and could experience recurring localized ponding during
intense storms, the significance of localized flooding risk during operation of Component 1 is
assessed as Moderate.




                                                                                                239
                                                                                                Official Use Only
   4.4.1.9.    Accident and Emergency Risks
During operation, Ring Road No. 3 functions as a primary urban–peri-urban traffic corridor
carrying mixed traffic, including motorcycles, passenger cars, buses, and heavy trucks serving
industrial and logistics activities. Accident risk arises mainly from (i) increased vehicle speeds
compared to inner-city streets, (ii) interactions between through traffic and local access traffic at
intersections and property access points, and (iii) the presence of vulnerable road users
(motorcycles, bicycles, pedestrians) at sections passing near residential clusters, agricultural areas,
schools, and healthcare facilities.
Directly affected receptors include road users (especially motorcyclists and pedestrians), residents
living along the corridor, and emergency-response services (ambulance, fire, police) that rely on
uninterrupted road access. Sensitive receptors are concentrated at locations where the road passes
within approximately 10–20 m of facilities such as ward medical stations and kindergartens.
This is a long-term, continuous operational impact, occurring throughout the service life of the
road. Risk levels vary temporally, with higher probability during night-time, adverse weather, and
peak traffic hours, and spatially at junctions, U-turns, and access-heavy sections rather than along
uninterrupted segments.
The FS incorporates traffic-safety-by-design measures, including controlled access in peri-urban
sections, channelised intersections, median barriers to prevent random crossings, traffic-calming
measures and reduced design speeds at sensitive locations, dedicated sidewalks and crossings
where applicable, and full road lighting. Emergency lay-bys, clear shoulder widths, and continuous
pavement markings are provided to support emergency response and incident management.
Considering the permanent increase in traffic exposure but also the integration of modern road
safety design standards, the operational accident and emergency risk is assessed as Moderate,
acceptable with proper enforcement, traffic management, and routine safety audits during
operation.
   4.4.1.10. Operation and Maintenance-related Impacts
Operation and maintenance (O&M) activities include routine pavement inspection, pothole repair,
resurfacing, drainage cleaning, vegetation control, and periodic maintenance of lighting, signage,
and safety barriers. These activities generate temporary disturbances through short-term lane
closures, maintenance vehicles, noise, and minor emissions. Affected receptors include road users
(temporary delays), roadside residents and businesses (short-term noise and access disturbance),
and maintenance workers exposed to traffic hazards.
O&M impacts are short-term, intermittent, and highly localised, typically affecting road sections
of a few hundred metres for durations ranging from a few hours (routine works) to several days
(resurfacing), occurring periodically over the operational life. The FS incorporates O&M-friendly
design, including service shoulders, drainage access points, durable pavement materials to reduce
maintenance frequency, and traffic management provisions for safe maintenance under live traffic.
Maintenance activities are planned to be scheduled during off-peak hours where possible. Given
their temporary nature, limited spatial extent, and the integration of safe O&M design and traffic
management measures, operation- and maintenance-related impacts are assessed as Low.




                                                                                                   240
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
   4.4.2. Impacts and risks during operation of Component 2
   4.4.2.1.    Noise, Emissions, and Road Safety from Traffic Growth
During the operation phase of the management road with a typical carriaway width of 3.5m – 7m,
two narrow lanes, no median along the Re river embankment, noise, air emissions, road-safety
impacts and minor traffic-induced vibration arise primarily from traffic on the road. Projected
operational traffic volumes are low, consisting mainly of motorcycles, bicycles, light passenger
vehicles, and occasional maintenance or inspection vehicles. Average daily traffic is estimated to
remain below 1,000 vehicles/day, with peak-hour flows typically <100 vehicles/hour, and heavy
trucks are not expected to use the road under normal operating conditions. Population density along
the corridor is low to moderate, characterised by scattered riverside housing, small residential
clusters, and agricultural land, rather than continuous urban frontage.
Traffic-related noise is generated by engine operation and tyre–road interaction at relatively low
travel speeds typical of embankment and riverside roads. Based on standard traffic-noise
relationships and the design operating conditions defined in the FS, operational noise levels at
residential receptors located along the Re River corridor are expected to remain close to existing
background levels and within applicable daytime limits of QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT. Noise
attenuation is rapid due to low speeds and the linear, open nature of the corridor.
Air emissions during operation are limited in magnitude due to the modest traffic volume and short
travel distances. Conceptual application of standard emission factors indicates that incremental
concentrations of PM₂.₅ and NOx, SO2 near the road edge would be small and unlikely to cause
measurable deterioration of ambient air quality. These emissions represent a continuation of
existing urban and peri-urban traffic exposure rather than a new or intensified pollution source.
Road-safety risks during operation are associated mainly with mixed use of the embankment roads
by motorcycles, pedestrians, cyclists and local residents accessing riverside paths and agricultural
plots. However, traffic speeds are relatively low (<40 km/h), junction density is limited. With
standard traffic management measures, signage and speed control, accident risks remain
manageable.
The principal receptors include riverside households, small residential clusters, local road users
and pedestrians along the Re River embankment. Exposure is continuous and long-term over the
operational life of the subcomponent but is spatially confined to the immediate road corridor.
Considering that (i) the management road has a limited width (ii) operational traffic volumes and
speeds are low compared with major urban arterials, (iii) noise, vibration and air-emission levels
remain close to baseline conditions and within regulatory standards, (iv) road-safety risks are
typical of local access roads, and (v) impacts represent incremental rather than transformational
change, the overall significance of noise, air-emission and road-safety impacts from traffic growth
during operation of the management road is assessed as Low


   4.4.2.2.    Localised Flooding Caused by Embankment and Management Road Blocking
               Natural Drainage
During operation, the river embankment and the associated 3.5-7m management road along the Re
River form a continuous linear structure that may locally modify pre-existing surface drainage
patterns. In several low-lying sections, the embankment crest is 1m higher than adjacent


                                                                                                241
                                                                                                Official Use Only
agricultural lands creating a potential “barrier effect” if stormwater outlets, cross-culverts or
connecting canals are undersized, obstructed or poorly maintained. The risk is most relevant during
short-duration, high-intensity rainfall events coinciding with high tide, when inland runoff cannot
freely discharge to the Re River.
The primary receptors are low-lying agricultural plots garden land in An Duong, Hong Bang and
An Hai wards, whose drainage depends on local canal systems discharging to the Re River. These
include areas connected to the Bắc Nam Hưng, Tân Hưng Hồng, Quỳnh Hoàng, Bệnh Viện, Gạo
and Ba Mẫu canals, which collect runoff from Bắc Sơn, Nam Sơn and parts of An Dương. In these
locations, temporary ponding may occur upstream of the embankment if cross-drainage culverts
beneath the management road are hydraulically constrained or blocked by sediment and debris.
The FS addresses this risk through an integrated drainage design, including longitudinal side drains
along the embankment, hydraulic connections to existing canals, and cross-drainage culverts
located at all major canal crossings. Culvert sizing is based on hydrological and hydraulic
calculations, taking into account design storm conditions and climate-change considerations, with
the intent to maintain existing drainage directions and discharge capacity. Where properly
maintained, this system substantially reduces the likelihood of persistent waterlogging behind the
embankment.
The impact is long-term but intermittent, occurring only during heavy rainfall events over the
operational life of the embankment. Any inundation is localised, typically limited to upstream low-
lying plots and internal canal networks, and recedes once rainfall subsides and drainage pathways
function as designed.
Considering that (i) Subcomponent 2.1 introduces a continuous embankment and management
road across low-lying terrain, which can locally constrain natural drainage, but (ii) the FS
incorporates engineered longitudinal drainage and cross-culverts designed to preserve runoff
conveyance to the Re River, and (iii) residual flooding risk is mainly associated with extreme
rainfall events and maintenance performance rather than systemic design failure, the significance
of localised flooding during operation of Subcomponent 2.1 is assessed as Low.
   4.4.2.3.    Risks of falling into the Re river
During operation, the Re River embankment and the associated management road run continuously
along the river edge, creating permanent human access close to the water. At sections with steep
slopes or vertical revetments, accidental slips or loss of balance may lead to falls into the river,
particularly during rainfall, flooding events, or at night when surfaces are wet and visibility is
reduced.
Receptors include maintenance workers, local residents using the management road, fishermen,
and riverbank users from adjacent residential and agricultural areas. Elderly people and children
are more vulnerable, especially at locations where informal access paths or riverbank activities
remain common. The FS includes stabilized embankment slopes and reinforced revetments, with
edge protection (such as parapets or guardrails) provided at sections with steep or vertical banks
to physically separate users from the river edge.
The risk is long-term and persists throughout the operational life of the embankment, but is
spatially limited to sections where the management road and public access run directly adjacent to
the river. Given the permanent proximity of users to the river but the presence of built-in physical
protection measures in the design, the risk of falling into the Re River during operation of



                                                                                                242
                                                                                                Official Use Only
Subcomponent 2.1 is assessed as Low, subject to proper implementation and maintenance of the
safety features.


   4.4.2.4.    Impact on Raw Water Quality at Quan Vinh Pumping Station during Operation
During operation, potential impacts on raw water quality at the Quan Vinh pumping station may
arise from two main sources: (i) malfunction of the wastewater collection and conveyance system
(leakage, blockage, or surcharge), leading to untreated domestic wastewater re-entering the Re
River; and (ii) local changes in flow regime following completion of river embankment works,
which may promote sediment deposition near the intake zone.
The primary receptor is the Quan Vinh raw water abstraction point and the downstream water-
supply system relying on this intake. Secondary receptors include domestic water users served by
the treatment plant, who may be indirectly affected if raw water quality deteriorates.
If wastewater sewers operate inefficiently, leakage or overflow during peak flows or maintenance
failures could introduce organic pollutants, nutrients, and coliforms into the river, causing short-
term degradation of raw water quality near the intake. In addition, the embankment may slightly
reduce near-bank flow velocity, thereby increasing the likelihood of fine sediment accumulation
in the vicinity of the intake. Such sedimentation can elevate turbidity and reduce intake efficiency,
particularly during low-flow conditions following the rainy season. These mechanisms are
consistent with the construction-phase impact pathways identified in the site-specific assessment,
but occur at lower frequency and intensity during operation.
The impact is long-term but intermittent, occurring only when system failures, inadequate
maintenance, or unfavorable hydrological conditions coincide. Effects are localized around the
intake and reversible through timely system repair, flushing, and sediment management.
Considering that (i) the wastewater collection system is designed for controlled conveyance and
separation, (ii) operational failures are not continuous but episodic, and (iii) The FS incorporates
a separated and closed wastewater collection system with adequate hydraulic capacity, inspection
manholes and maintenance access, which substantially reduces the risk of leakage or backflow of
untreated wastewater into the Re River near the Quan Vinh raw-water intake. In addition, the
riverbank revetment design maintains the effective flow section and allows for periodic inspection
and localized dredging at the intake area, limiting the potential for post-revetment sediment
deposition. With these design and operational measures in place, adverse effects on raw-water
quality at Quan Vinh Pumping Station are not expected to be frequent or persistent, the impact on
raw water quality at Quan Vinh pumping station during operation is assessed as Low.
   4.4.2.5.    Blockages and Wastewater Overflow
During the operation phase, blockage, malfunction or structural damage of the drainage pipelines
and pumping stations may lead to localized flooding and secondary environmental pollution if the
system is not adequately maintained. Potential sources of risk include sediment accumulation in
sewers, debris clogging at inlets and screens, mechanical failure of pumps, and power outages
during heavy rainfall events. In such cases, stormwater and mixed flows may surcharge the system
and overflow onto adjacent roads, residential areas or agricultural land, causing temporary
inundation, waterlogging and possible contamination from accumulated pollutants.




                                                                                                 243
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
The primary receptors include roadside commercial and agricultural areas, residential clusters such
as An Dong, Le Loi, Group 1 in An Duong ward and So Dau in Hong Bang ward, located
downstream or adjacent to the drainage network and pumping stations. These receptors are
particularly sensitive during the rainy season when peak runoff coincides with high groundwater
levels or tidal influence, increasing the consequences of system failure. The impact may recur
intermittently over the operational life of the system if maintenance is inadequate; however, each
occurrence is short-term in duration and localized in extent.
The FS incorporates a closed drainage and pumping system designed with sufficient hydraulic
capacity, standby pumps, trash screens, inspection manholes, and provisions for routine operation
and maintenance, including periodic desilting and emergency response during extreme rainfall.
With these design and management measures in place, the likelihood and severity of system
blockage or failure are substantially reduced. Therefore, after implementation of the FS-design
mitigation measures, the operational impact related to blockage or damage of drainage pipelines
and pumping stations is assessed as Low.
   4.4.2.6.    Sludge, Chemicals, Hazardous Waste, Odour and Noise from An Duong 1
               WWTP
During the operation phase, the main environmental and community health impacts associated
with An Duong 1 WWTP arise from: (i) generation and handling of sludge; (ii) use and storage of
treatment chemicals and management of hazardous waste; and (iii) odour and noise emissions from
WWTP process units.
Sludge generation is inherent to biological wastewater treatment. With a maximum design capacity
of approximately 10,000 m³/day and influent characteristics defined in the FS, biological sludge
production is estimated at about 2–3 tonnes of dry solids per day. Dewatered sludge, with an
expected dry solids content of approximately 15–20%, will be temporarily stored in a designated,
covered sludge storage area within the WWTP, designed with impermeable flooring and leachate
collection to prevent secondary pollution. The estimated quantity of dewatered sludge is
approximately 15–25 tonnes per day. Sludge will be regularly transported off-site by licensed
contractors for treatment and disposal in accordance with applicable Vietnamese regulations.
The sludge is primarily composed of organic matter, nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus) and
microorganisms typical of municipal domestic wastewater. Potential trace contaminants may be
present depending on influent quality and upstream discharges; therefore, sludge quality will be
periodically monitored to determine appropriate treatment and disposal options, in compliance
with national standards.
Operational chemicals include disinfectants (e.g. chlorine or hypochlorite), pH adjustment
chemicals, and polymer for sludge conditioning and dewatering. Hazardous wastes generated
intermittently include used lubricating oils, oil-contaminated materials, chemical containers, spent
lamps, batteries, and discarded electrical or electronic components. The quantities of hazardous
waste are expected to be small, estimated at several tens to a few hundred kilograms per year,
depending on maintenance frequency and equipment servicing requirements. these wastes require
strict control due to their hazardous properties.
Odour emissions at the WWTP originate mainly from early treatment stages and sludge handling
units, including screening, grit removal, sludge thickening and dewatering, where anaerobic
conditions may intermittently occur. Typical odorous compounds identified in the FS include



                                                                                                244
                                                                                                Official Use Only
reduced sulfur compounds and nitrogenous gases, primarily hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) and ammonia
(NH₃). Noise is generated by mechanical and electrical equipment within the WWTP, such as
pumps, screens, grit removal equipment, sludge dewatering units, blowers and auxiliary systems.
These sources operate continuously or periodically depending on treatment demand.
Based on these criteria and the final site layout, potential off-site receptors are limited to land uses
located within approximately 150–200 m from the WWTP boundary, corresponding to the distance
to the nearest Hoang Mai residential area 200 m to the WWTP in accordance with QCVN
01:2025/BTNMT). No concentrated residential clusters are located within this distance. The most
sensitive receptors are therefore WWTP workers and operational staff, who experience the highest
exposure to odour, noise, sludge and chemicals during routine operation, inspection, sludge
handling and maintenance activities. Within the same vicinity, several small-scale household
livestock sheds are present near the WWTP boundary. While not residential receptors, these
facilities may be sensitive to nuisance odour or hygiene impacts if sludge, chemicals or odorous
air streams are not adequately managed, and therefore require attention in operational management
in line with QCVN 01:2025/BTNMT requirements.
Sludge generation, chemical use, odour emission and noise generation are long-term and recurring
over the entire operational life of the WWTP. Sludge handling, chemical use, and the generation
of hazardous waste at An Duong 1 WWTP are managed, stored, and controlled entirely within the
WWTP boundary (including sludge dewatering areas, chemical storage rooms, and hazardous
waste storage areas). As a result, these activities do not cause adverse impacts on the nearest
residential area. With respect to odour and noise, the main sources (e.g. H₂S and NH₃ from
preliminary treatment and sludge handling units, and noise from pumps, blowers, and other
electromechanical equipment) are expected to be confined largely within the WWTP site and its
immediate surroundings, with rapid attenuation beyond the plant boundary. At a distance of 150–
200 m, potential odour and noise impacts are therefore assessed as minor and intermittent to the
nearest residential area.
The FS incorporates permanent engineering and management measures at An Duong 1 WWTP,
including enclosed process units, centralized odour collection and treatment using a biofilter–
activated carbon system, controlled sludge thickening and dewatering with off-site licensed
disposal, and dedicated storage and handling procedures for chemicals and hazardous waste. These
measures are complemented by appropriate equipment selection, facility layout, and operational
management to limit odour, noise, and secondary environmental risks during long-term operation.
Considering that (i) sludge generation, chemical use, odour and noise are intrinsic to the operation
of an urban WWTP of this scale; (ii) the FS integrates permanent odour treatment, sludge
management, chemical containment and hazardous-waste control measures; (iii) no dense
residential areas are located within the inferred environmental safety distance of approximately
150–200 m; and (iv) residual impacts are largely confined to the facility boundary and immediate
surroundings, the combined impacts related to sludge, chemicals, hazardous waste, odour and
noise during operation of An Duong 1 WWTP are assessed as Moderate. With proper operation,
maintenance and regulatory compliance, these impacts are controllable and are not expected to
cause significant off-site environmental or community health effects.
   4.4.2.7.    Odour Emissions and Noise at Separation Chambers and Pumping Stations
Odour emissions from separation chambers and pumping stations arise from wastewater retention
in wet wells and chambers, where anaerobic conditions may intermittently occur, generating H₂S,


                                                                                                   245
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
NH₃, and other malodorous gases. Noise is generated by pumps, electrical equipment, and
ventilation systems, particularly during peak-flow operation and maintenance activities.
Receptors are limited to households, shop-houses, pedestrians, and road users located immediately
adjacent to separation chambers and pumping stations along urban sewer corridors in An Duong,
An Hai and Hong Bang wards, as well as on-site maintenance workers. These facilities are
embedded within existing urban environments where background noise and odour are already
present.
Impacts are long-term but intermittent, occurring mainly during peak wastewater flows, hot
periods, and maintenance operations. The FS provides for covered chamber designs, appropriate
ventilation, routine cleaning, and maintenance to limit anaerobic conditions and odour build-up.
Pumping stations are designed with enclosed equipment and standard electrical noise controls.
Given the small spatial footprint of these facilities and the application of standard design and
operational controls, residual odour and noise impacts from separation chambers and pumping
stations are localised and short-range. The overall impact significance is assessed as Low.
   4.4.2.8.    Effects on the Receiving Water Body (Lach Tray River)
The treated effluent from the An Duong 1 WWTP is discharged to the Lach Tray River, a tidal-
influenced watercourse forming part of the lower estuarine system of Hai Phong. Baseline
monitoring data presented in the national EIA, FS (2026) indicate that the river segment near the
discharge point is affected by urban runoff and upstream wastewater inputs, with two parameters,
COD (23.2-33.8 mg/l), BOD₅ (13.8-19.6 mg/l), exceeding the recommended thresholds for QCVN
