    At the FS/ESIA stage, quantitative estimation of operational GHG emissions is not undertaken
due to the absence of consolidated and verified data on (i) long-term traffic activity (vehicle-
km/year) for each road component and (ii) annual electricity consumption (kWh/year) for the
WWTP and pumping facilities. These parameters will only be finalized at detailed design and
operation planning stages.




                                                                                            265
                                                                                            Official Use Only
                                ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL
                             MANAGEMENT PLAN (ESMP)
Based on the assessments of the potential adverse environmental impacts and mitigation measures
proposed in Chapter 5 of the report, this Chapter presents an Environmental and Social
Management Plan (ESMP) for the Project. The ESMP identifies the measures to be carried out for
the project, including environmental monitoring program and implementation arrangements, taken
into account the needs to comply with the government’s EIA regulations and WBG’s ESF and
ESSs, including Environmental, Health, and Safety (EHS) Guidelines of the WB.
This ESMP consists of main contents as follows:
   -   The measures to minimize the potential environmental and social impacts to be
       incorporated into the Feasibility Study and Detailed Design, and the mitigation measures
       to address the adverse impacts during pre-construction, construction, and operation phases
       together with implementation responsibilities;
   -   Environmental Monitoring Program;
   -   The project Compliance Framework, including environmental and social supervision
       arrangements, fines applicable to non-compliance;
   -   Capacity building programs;
   -   Cost estimation; and
   -   Grievance Redress mechanism.

5.1.   Mitigation Measures
This section proposes mitigation measures for the key project impacts anticipated during the
feasibility study, detailed design, site preparation, construction, and operation phases.
The mitigation measures for the detailed design phase for all components are provided in Section
5.1.1, while Section 5.1.2 details the mitigation measures for the preparation period.
Regarding the layout of mitigation measures during construction, there are two fundamental parts
to this ESMP. Given that most key impacts will occur due to civil works and the transportation of
construction and waste materials, many potential negative impacts on the physical, biological, and
social environments (e.g., noise, dust, water pollution, waste generation, and social disturbances)
could be mitigated through a set of general measures typically applied to most construction
projects. As part of the project’s ESMP, these generic measures have been translated into standard
environmental and social specifications, namely the Environmental and Social Codes of Practice
(ESCOPs). The ESCOPs will be applied to mitigate the typical impacts of civil works across all
components and will be included in all construction contracts. The content of the ESCOPs is
detailed in Section 5.1.3.
In addition to these general measures, each of components presents site-specific impacts that
require site-specific measures during construction. Section 5.1.3 discusses the site-specific
measures to be implemented during construction for each component where additional mitigation
measures are needed beyond those identified in the ESCOPs. These site-specific construction
measures will be incorporated into the relevant construction contracts.




                                                                                               266
                                                                                               Official Use Only
Similarly, Section 5.1.4 details the mitigation measures to be applied during the operational phase.
This section is divided into two subsections: Section 5.1.4.1 describes the measures applicable to
the operation of all components, while Section 5.1.4.2 presents the site-specific operational
measures relevant to each individual component.
5.1.1.     Design-phase mitigation measures
     During the detailed design phase, the following mitigation measures shall be incorporated into
the final detailed design packages for each component to avoid or minimize environmental, social
and safety impacts and risks identified in Chapter 4, and to translate the feasibility-level solutions
in FS 2026 into implementable, verifiable design requirements.
    5.1.1.1.   Component 1 – Thuy Nguyen Integrated and Resilient Urban Infrastructure
               Development
    For Component 1 (Ring Road No. 3 and associated bridge works, including the bridge
crossings over Cam River and Ruot Lon River), the detailed design shall incorporate the following
measures:

•   Confirm and optimize detailed alignment, vertical profile and cross-sections for the full
    corridor to minimize permanent land acquisition and reduce severance of
    agricultural/aquaculture production areas and local access routes, while maintaining
    compliance with the FS staging approach (full ROW planned with staged construction of the
    main carriageway in phase 1).
•   Integrate a complete road drainage and cross-drainage system in the detailed design to prevent
    localized flooding and uncontrolled runoff to adjacent fields/aquaculture ponds, including (i)
    adequate inlet spacing and longitudinal drainage, and (ii) cross-culverts at natural drainage
    lines and canal/ditch crossings to maintain existing drainage connectivity.
•   Where the alignment traverses areas with brackish/organic topsoil and aquaculture land, the
    detailed design shall specify (i) topsoil stripping boundaries, (ii) segregation and handling
    requirements for brackish/organic materials, and (iii) dedicated temporary/permanent storage
    locations and lining/containment needs to avoid secondary pollution of agricultural land and
    receiving waters.
•   For intersections and access points with regional roads and local roads, the detailed design
    shall include road-safety-by-design features: geometric design review, lane arrangements,
    sight distances, speed management measures, safe turning/merging provisions, and safe
    crossing facilities for vulnerable road users where applicable.
•   For bridge structures and approaches, the detailed design shall verify hydraulic capacity, scour
    protection and bank stability measures to prevent riverbank erosion and pier scour risks, and
    shall include robust outlet/abutment protection to avoid concentrated flow erosion.
•   For the Cam River crossing area, the detailed design shall incorporate navigational safety
    provisions into bridge design (clearance, pier protection, and navigational marking/signage
    interface requirements) and define safe waterway passage constraints to be used in the
    construction planning requirements.
•   For the Ruot Lon bridge area where impacts on the accquisition of a protection forest, the
    detailed design shall (i) minimize the permanent footprint at abutments/approaches, (ii) define
    clear limits of work and protection measures at the forest boundary, and (iii) integrate erosion



                                                                                                  267
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
    and sediment control features and drainage outlet protections to prevent runoff impacts to the
    forest edge.
•   Provide design provisions to maintain safe access and connectivity for adjacent residential
    areas during staged implementation, consistent with Chapter 4 risks on community safety and
    accessibility.

    5.1.1.2.   Component 2 – Re River Environmental Improvement
     For Component 2 (Re River corridor works, including embankment upgrading, management
roads, and the wastewater collection/transfer system with An Duong 1 WWTP), the detailed design
shall incorporate the following measures:

•   Undertake and integrate comprehensive geotechnical and river-hydraulic investigations to
    support stable embankment and riverside road designs, including settlement risk, slope
    stability, and bank erosion under heavy rainfall and flood conditions.
•   Confirm embankment type selection and structural details in locations where space constraints
    exist and where the FS notes use of solutions to “create sufficient space for wastewater
    infrastructure”; the detailed design shall demonstrate that the selected embankment
    configuration does not create unacceptable erosion/deposition impacts and remains stable
    under design flood/tidal conditions.
•   For sections near sensitive cultural sites the detailed design shall incorporate
    avoidance/minimization by layout refinement and define structural/vibration control design
    requirements for works in proximity (e.g., allowable vibration criteria to be reflected in
    construction method specifications, and minimum offset distances where feasible).
•   For areas with closely spaced residences along the Re River, the detailed design shall include
    requirements for excavation support and ground-movement control at embankment/road
    interfaces and at any deep structures, including the need for retaining systems and staged
    excavation constraints where close to houses.
•   Address the risk of sedimentation/turbidity affecting the Quan Vinh pumping station intake by
    incorporating design measures that reduce near-bank sediment accumulation and protect intake
    water quality, including bank protection detailing near the intake zone and requirements for
    localized flow/sediment assessment at/near the intake reach.
•   For the wastewater collection and transfer system along the Re River, the detailed design shall:
         o optimize alignment/depth to reduce community disruption and utility conflicts;
         o specify pumping station layouts to minimize odor/nuisance and ensure safe access;
         o include redundancy and overflow prevention features to address Chapter 4 risks of
             blockages and wastewater overflow.
•   For An Duong 1 WWTP , the detailed design shall:
         o confirm siting and buffer/offset requirements relative to nearby receptors;
         o specify enclosed/odor-control features and sludge handling layout to minimize odor
             and secondary pollution;
         o incorporate safe access, drainage, emergency response and operational safety
             provisions.
•   Ensure the management road design along the Re River maintains local access and safe
    riverbank use where relevant, including safe access points with handrails/guarding where there
    is a risk of falling into the river.



                                                                                                268
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    5.1.1.3.   Component 3 – City Center Flood Reduction
    For Component 3, the detailed design shall incorporate the following measures:

•   Verify and refine hydraulic design of the flood regulation system through detailed modelling
    to confirm performance under combined rainfall–tide conditions, and to avoid adverse
    backwater effects and unintended localized flooding impacts highlighted in Chapter 4.
•   For the two regulating sluices and associated impoundment to form the Tam Bac water
    regulation system, the detailed design shall define:
         o operational levels and gate operation envelopes;
         o navigation/small-boat access requirements and any necessary safe passage
             arrangements where Chapter 4 notes restricted riverbank access and small-boat
             movement risks;
         o structural and safety provisions for public interface areas in dense urban settings.
•   For the stormwater pumping station integrated within the regulating structure, the detailed
    design shall incorporate:
         o redundancy/maintainability and safe access for O&M;
         o emergency shutdown and safe isolation features;
         o noise/odor and aesthetic screening requirements appropriate for inner-city receptors.
•   For the connector box culvert between the existing Tam Bac lake and the new regulating area,
    the detailed design shall confirm structural integrity, constructability constraints and
    utility/interface management to minimize risks of ground settlement and impacts on adjacent
    structures.
•   For the primary stormwater drainage upgrades, the detailed design shall:
         o confirm open-cut excavation design requirements and support systems in narrow streets
             and near houses;
         o include utility detection/interface design requirements to minimize risks of damaging
             underground utilities;
         o incorporate permanent access points for maintenance and safe traffic interface
             arrangements at key road crossings.
•   For the associated wastewater collection works within the Tam Bac corridor, the detailed
    design shall:
         o integrate odor control and overflow prevention features;
         o optimize alignment to reduce disruption to sensitive receptors and business activities
             in dense inner-city areas;
         o include safe siting and access for pumping stations to minimize nuisance and safety
             risks.
•   Where works pass near sensitive receptors identified in Chapter 4, including hospitals and
    schools in the dense urban core, the detailed design shall incorporate receptor-specific
    avoidance/minimization requirements (e.g., construction frontage limits to be enabled by
    design staging, access maintenance requirements, and physical safety separation provisions to
    be specified in the design drawings and technical specifications).

5.1.2.     Preparation-phase mitigation measures




                                                                                             269
                                                                                             Official Use Only
   5.1.1.4.    Mitigation measures due to land acquisition activities
The Resettlement Plan (RP) has been developed for the project based on the FS (2026), and the
RP report also provides specific measures for compensation, assistance, and resettlement for
affected persons. The RP establishes mechanisms for project information disclosure, public
consultation and participation, as well as a grievance redress mechanism (GRM). Compensation
and assistance for affected households are based on the following principles:
   - All affected people who own assets or reside within the project land area must be fully
     compensated for their losses before land is acquired for construction. People who lose
     income and/or housing will be provided support to reinstate livelihoods based on eligibility
     criteria determined by the project in consultation with those affected. If, by the end of the
     project, livelihoods are proven not to have been reinstated to pre-project levels, additional
     measures will be provided.
   - Methods used to assess losses in WB-financed projects are based on full replacement costs.
     For this project, losses include land, structures, and other assets, and these replacement costs
     will be assessed as follows: Full replacement cost of land includes the value of land
     determined according to prevailing market prices plus administrative fees. For houses and
     other affected structures, valuation is based on the market price of construction materials
     and labor costs to build replacement houses of equivalent or better quality and size than the
     affected ones.
   - Mitigation of unavoidable economic and social impacts due to land acquisition or restriction
     of land use will be achieved by: (a) timely compensation at replacement cost for lost assets,
     and (b) supporting displaced persons in efforts to improve, or at least reinstate, their
     livelihoods and living standards, under practical conditions, to pre-displacement levels or
     prevailing levels prior to project implementation, whichever is higher.
   - To ensure that all complaints and grievances of affected persons regarding any aspect of land
     acquisition, compensation, and resettlement are addressed in a timely and satisfactory
     manner, and that all available channels for affected people to resolve grievances are in place,
     a clearly defined grievance redress mechanism must be established. All PAPs (Project-
     Affected People) may submit any inquiries to the implementing agencies regarding their
     rights. PAPs are not required to pay any fees related to seeking grievance resolution.
Preliminary estimates of compensation and support costs for PAPs have been prepared and are
included in the RP based on (a) current provincial regulations regarding compensation, assistance,
and resettlement, and (b) a rapid replacement cost assessment conducted to support the process.
Compensation and assistance plans for resettlement and livelihood restoration for each
component are detailed in the RP report.
Measures for relocated households
Land acquisition and resettlement will be compliance with the Resettlement Action Plan (RAP),
which will be prepared to establish the resettlement principles, eligibility requirements for
compensation, valuation methods, describe the legal and institutional framework, organizational
arrangements, mechanisms, and community consultation and participation, and grievance redress
mechanism to be applied to the project during the project implementation. Resettlement Action
Plan (RAP) will be prepared and submitted to the World Bank for approval before construction
activities will be started.


                                                                                                 270
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
The RAP will be prepared in compliance with the relevant World Bank’s SES - ESS5: Land
acquisition, restrictions on land use, involuntary resettlement and the Vietnam’s laws and
regulations.
The basic guiding principles of the World Bank’s resettlement policy are: Involuntary resettlement
should be avoided where feasible, or minimized after exploring all viable alternatives in project
design;
Where resettlement cannot be avoided, resettlement activities should be conceived and executed
as sustainable development programs, providing sufficient investment resources to enable the
people affected by the Project to share in benefits. Affected persons should be meaningful
consulted and should have opportunities to participate in planning and implementing resettlement
programs.
Measures for land use change
Mitigation measures for impacts related to land-use change focus on reducing adverse effects
arising from the conversion of agricultural, aquaculture and mixed-use land to urban infrastructure
and supporting affected households in adapting to new land-use patterns. In line with the
Resettlement Plan, the project has applied design optimization and alignment adjustments to limit
the scale of permanent land conversion, particularly in areas with concentrated agricultural and
aquaculture activities.
For unavoidable land-use change, affected land users are compensated at replacement cost for lost
land and land-based production assets, ensuring financial capacity to transition away from
agriculture or aquaculture where these activities are no longer viable. Households experiencing
income impacts due to the loss of productive land are supported through targeted livelihood
restoration measures, including job transition support and skills training, to facilitate a shift toward
non-agricultural livelihoods consistent with ongoing urbanization trends in the project area.
Through these measures, potential negative effects of land-use change on household income and
livelihood patterns are mitigated, while affected communities are supported to benefit from
improved accessibility, higher land-use efficiency, and emerging economic opportunities
associated with urban development.
Measures for livelihood
All Project affected people (PAP) doing business or farming in the project areas before the cut-off
date will be provided with rehabilitation measures, sufficient enough for them to improve or at
least maintain their living standards, income earning and production capacity the same as their pre-
project conditions. For affected crops, trees, regardless of the legal status of the land, compensation
will be paid to households who cultivate on the affected land at full replacement cost. For trees
which have not been harvested yet but can be brought to another location, the transportation cost
and the actual damage due to the transportation and re-planting must be compensated. For livestock
(including aquatic livestock), PAPs will be compensated in cash at replacement cost at the time of
land acquisition. In case the aquatic livestock can be brought to another location, the transportation
cost and the damage caused by the transportation must be compensated.
In addition, livelihood restoration program is vital to help severely affected households, vulnerable
households improve or at least restore their income and living standard as equal to or better than
pre-project level. Apart from financial support, vocational training is also provided to AHs.



                                                                                                    271
                                                                                                    Official Use Only
Measures for relocation of graves
The Project will affect a total of 348 graves. These graves are owned by local people and will be
relocated to the local’s cemetery. However, during the implementation of the project, there may
be additional finding of graves, especially derelict graves. Therefore, relocation of graves will be
carried out as follows:
    •   The affected graves will be relocated based on full consultation with affected households
        to ensure local people’s customs and procedures. Compensation for affected graves
        includes all costs related to a) land for burial, b) excavation, c) relocation, d) renovation,
        e) construction of new tombs and f) other necessary related costs following people’s
        customs and habits.
    •   In case of unidentified owners of the affected graves, notification on the relocation will be
        disclosed publicly (on TV, popular newspapers) several times to search for the owner of
        the grave. Within a prescribed period, if no owner shows up, the grave will be relocated by
        a specialized unit in consultation with the Commune People's Committee. The
        geographical location and condition of the graves (with detailed photographs), relocation
        procedure and the new location of the graves shall be carefully documented for later use
        by the owner. During the relocation process, the PMU shall promptly notify affected
        households so they have proper preparation in terms of spiritual activities and the
        compensation shall be made to the affected households as stipulated in the project’s RP
        and ESMP.
    •   Consultation with households whose graves have to be relocated: During project
        preparation, households whose graves had to be relocated were consulted with project-
        affected households. Affected households have been generally informed about the project's
        policies, implementation procedures, grievance redress mechanism, and expected location
        for the grave locations. The consultation process will continue during the detailed
        measurement survey of the project.
Measures for the loss of planted protection forest due to construction of Ruot Lon Bridge
    To minimize and properly manage impacts associated with the permanent conversion of
planted protection forest for the construction of the Ruot Lon Bridge, and in compliance with
Vietnamese environmental and forestry legislation, the following mitigation measures shall be
implemented during the preparation phase:

•   During project preparation, the exact boundaries of land acquisition and permanent works for
    the Ruot Lon Bridge shall be clearly defined and approved, ensuring that vegetation clearance
    is strictly limited to the direct construction footprint of bridge piers, foundations and immediate
    working areas, as assessed in Chapter 4.
•   The conversion of planted protection forest land shall be carried out in full compliance with
    the Law on Forestry (2017) and its implementing regulations, including requirements for
    approval of forest land conversion by competent authorities prior to site clearance.
•   In accordance with the Law on Forestry (2017) and Decree No. 156/2018/ND-CP (as
    amended), Circular No. 25/2022/TT-BNNPTNT, the conversion of forest land to other land
    uses is subject to a defined approval and implementation procedure. For the conversion of 0.76
    ha of protection forest (Sonneratia caseolaris), the key procedural steps include: (i) preparation


                                                                                                   272
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
    of a dossier for forest land reclamation and conversion, including justification of necessity,
    affected forest area, forest status, and a replacement forest planting plan or payment option;
    (ii) appraisal of the dossier by the competent forestry authority to confirm compliance with
    forest planning, protection forest functions, and replacement planting requirements; and (iii)
    issuance of a decision approving the forest land conversion by the competent authority, prior
    to land recovery and construction activities. The project owner is responsible for preparing and
    implementing a replacement forest plan or, subject to approval by the Hai Phong People’s
    Committee, paying replacement planting fees into the Forest Protection and Development
    Fund. The Hai Phong People’s Committee will approve the form and location of replacement
    planting, while the provincial forestry authority will appraise, supervise and certify the
    implementation. Replacement planting will apply appropriate mangrove species consistent
    with protection forest functions and will be completed in accordance with the schedule
    approved by the competent authority prior to or concurrently with project implementation.
•   Where direct replacement planting is not feasible, the project shall fulfill its obligation for
    forest replacement through payment to the Forest Protection and Development Fund
    administered by DAE, in accordance with prevailing regulations, prior to commencement of
    construction activities.
•   The preparation of the Resettlement Plan and land acquisition documentation shall explicitly
    include compensation arrangements for affected protection forest land and trees, consistent
    with the approved forest replacement plan and institutional responsibilities defined under
    forestry legislation.
•   During preparation, coordination shall be undertaken with the local forest management
    authority and Nam Trieu Ward People’s Committee to confirm the status, boundaries and
    management arrangements of the affected planted protection forest, and to ensure that
    remaining forest areas outside the construction footprint are protected from secondary
    disturbance.
•   In line with the Law on Environmental Protection (2020) and Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP,
    environmental management requirements related to forest conversion and compensatory
    afforestation shall be integrated into the project’s environmental approval and reflected in
    subsequent detailed design and construction planning documents.
•   Early consultation and information disclosure shall be conducted during the preparation phase
    with relevant local authorities and stakeholders regarding the scope of forest conversion,
    approved forest replacement measures, and long-term management responsibilities, to ensure
    transparency and regulatory compliance.

    5.1.1.5.   Measures for the impact of residual UXO
     To address risks associated with unexploded ordnance (UXO) identified during the
preparation phase of the Hai Phong Urban Infrastructure and Environment Improvement Project,
the following mitigation measures shall be implemented:

•   UXO survey and clearance shall be carried out after completion of compensation, land
    acquisition and site clearance, and prior to any construction activities, including land leveling,
    excavation, dredging, piling or trenching works under Components 1, 2 and 3.
•   The Project Owner/PMU shall contract licensed and authorized military units in accordance
    with national regulations to conduct UXO survey, clearance and safe disposal within all
    construction sites, access roads, material yards and other areas subject to ground disturbance.


                                                                                                  273
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
•   No construction activities shall be permitted in any project area until UXO clearance has been
    completed, clearance certification has been issued by the competent authority, and the site has
    been formally handed over to the PMU.
•   In the event that suspected UXO is discovered during subsequent construction activities, all
    works shall be immediately suspended in the affected area, the site secured, and the competent
    UXO clearance authority notified for further investigation and safe disposal before works
    resume.

5.1.3.     Construction-phase mitigation measures
    5.1.3.1.   General construction mitigation
The proposed mitigation measures for the construction phase include: (i) Environmental and Social
Codes of Practice (ESCOP), (ii) Worker Code of Conduct, and (iii) Labor Management Procedures
(LMP).
Component-specific mitigation measures will be presented for each component, along with
supplementary plans (in the annex).
Relevant elements will be incorporated into the Terms of Reference (TORs) for Consultancy
Services and/or Civil Works Contracts as guided below. In addition, the PMU and the Construction
Supervision Consultant (CSC) will be responsible for coordinating activities among different
contractors working simultaneously, sharing construction sites and access routes, etc., to ensure
that construction works are implemented in a synchronized manner while controlling
environmental and social impacts and risks.
1. Environmental and Social Code of Practice (ESCOP)
The ESCOP will be applied to all civil works contracts and, therefore, will be included in the
relevant sections of the construction contract and bidding documents. Note that, in addition to the
ESCOP, the bidding documents for each specific civil works package will also include relevant
component-specific mitigation measures and plans tailored to the natural conditions and location
of the civil works under that package.


    The following table summarizes the ESCOP-based mitigation measures designed to control
and minimize common construction-phase impacts, including:
i.Dust and emissions
ii. Noise and vibration
iii. Wastewater management
iv. Domestic solid waste
v. Hazardous waste management
vi. Impacts on biological resources
vii. Traffic management
viii. Risks of erosion, sedimentation, and localized flooding
ix. Risks of Natural Hazards


                                                                                               274
                                                                                               Official Use Only
x. Impacts due to impropor access
xi.Impacts on existing infrastructure and related services
xii. Impacts on Aquaculture and fishing activities
xiii. Impacts on Agricultural production
xiv. Gender Based Violence
xv. Community health and safety
xvi. Occupational health and safety risks
xvii. Impacts on cultural and historical resources and chance-find procedures.

                 • Ensure the use of pile driving equipment with appropriate load

                   vibrations at one point.

                   especially in areas with existing structures nearby.
                Compensation:
                 • For structures at high risk of damage, consider reinforcement
                   measures such as installing retaining walls, bracing, or shock-
                   absorbing layers to protect them from the impact of vibrations.

                  labeled waste classification on-site.
                • Sort waste at the source and concentrate it in temporary storage
                  areas.
                • Contract qualified units for collection, transportation, and treatment
                  in accordance with regulations.
                • Cover outdoor materials and provide temporary storage with roofs
                  to prevent wind or rain dispersion.
                • Arrange material stockpile areas at each component, with each area
                  approximately 100–200 m², impermeable surface, fencing, and
                  signage.
                • Inspect and maintain equipment and machinery to ensure good
                  condition and renew registration before expiry.
                • Major maintenance or repair of excavators is not permitted on-site;
                  such activities must be conducted at specialized maintenance
                  workshops.
               Restoration
                • If solid waste causes environmental or community impacts, the
                  contractor is responsible for Restoration and compensation
                  according to regulations.

                                                                                                                            286
                                                                                                                             Official Use Only
                                                                                           Responsibility for     Regulations of
 E&S issues                             Mitigation measures                                Implementation/       Vietnam and the
                                                                                             Supervision           World Bank

                Mitigation Measures for Spills
                Emergency response:
                 • The person detecting the spill must ensure safety and immediately
                   notify the supervising engineer and PMU.
                 • Use sawdust, sandbags, or shovels depending on the scale of the
                   spill.
                 • Do not discharge chemicals into the drainage system; contaminated
                   soil must be treated according to hazardous waste procedures.
                Incident reporting:
                 • The contractor must prepare a detailed report on the cause,
                   consequences, remedial actions, and preventive measures.
                 • Submit the report to the Supervision Consultant, PMU, and
                   Department of Agriculture & Environment (if required).
                Restoration (if any)
                 • Environmental damage Restoration: If hazardous waste incidents
                   cause environmental pollution or affect public health, the contractor
                   is responsible for Restoration and compensation according to
                   regulations.

                                                                                                                               290
                                                                                                                                Official Use Only

 E&S issues                             Mitigation measures                                Implementation/       Vietnam and the
                                                                                             Supervision           World Bank

               Minimization
                 • Provide sufficient signal lights, life buoys, firefighting equipment,
                   and 24/7 VHF communication devices.
                 • Install buoys and signal lights marking the construction boundary.
                 • Carry out works within the inside edge of the boundary buoys,
                   avoiding obstruction to existing waterway traffic.
                 • Do not conduct construction during adverse weather conditions
                   (thunderstorms, whirlwinds, fog, etc.).
               Mitigation
                 • Develop a maritime safety plan and submit it to the competent
                   authority for approval.
                 • Coordinate in advance with local medical stations for emergency
                   support.
                 • Install warning signs and signal lights at dredging locations along
                   the channel.
                 • Prepare an emergency response plan for contractors in case of
                   incidents or waterway traffic accidents.
               Restoration (if any)
                 • In case of collision, sinking, or disruption to waterway traffic, the
                   contractor is responsible for Restoration and compensation.

                                                                                                                                  295
                                                                                                                                  Official Use Only
                                                                                            Responsibility for     Regulations of
 E&S issues                             Mitigation measures                                 Implementation/       Vietnam and the
                                                                                              Supervision           World Bank

               Mitigation
                • Develop a Service Disruption Plan for each type of service if shut-
                  off is unavoidable, including: start–end time; affected areas;
                  temporary or alternative supply measures (clean water, temporary
                  Wi-Fi, generators, etc.); and contact points for incident handling.
                • Post public notices at community points (health stations,
                  commune/ward People’s Committee offices, households, schools,
                  etc.) at least 5 days in advance, in addition to direct notifications.
                • Provide alternative water supply to affected residents if service
                  interruption lasts more than one day.
                • Assess the level of impact (number of households, healthcare
                  facilities, schools, etc.) to prioritize support.
                • Clearly specify forms of notification (paper-based, electronic,
                  loudspeaker, etc.).
               Restoration (if any)
                • Sign a tripartite agreement (project owner – contractor –
                  infrastructure management unit) on responsibilities for
                  compensation, repair, and restoration in case of damage to existing
                  systems.
                • Maintain an incident logbook recording all damages to
                  infrastructure, including: date and time; description of damage;
                  cause; corrective measures; completion time of repair; confirmation
                  minutes signed by the supervising engineer and infrastructure
                  management unit.
