                                                                                                                          305
                                                                                                                           Official Use Only
The Labour Code of Conduct, Labour Management Procedures (LMPs) presented below, will be
applied to the entire project (3 components) and will therefore be incorporated into the relevant
sections of any consultancy or construction tender or contract documents.
1. Workers’ Code of Conduct
    The Workers’ Code of Conduct shall address, but not be limited to, the following measures:
    a. Compliance with laws and regulations
   •   All workers must comply with the laws and regulations of the Socialist Republic of Viet
       Nam.
   •   The list of workers must be registered with the local authorities in accordance with current
       regulations.
   •   Children under 18 years old are not permitted to work under the Project.
    b. Civilized behavior and respect for the community
   •   Behave properly when interacting with the local community, respecting local customs,
       practices, and traditions.
   •   Do not cause disturbance, disorder, or engage in fighting (physical or verbal) within or near
       the community.
   •   Workers residing in temporary camps, when visiting local communities, must conduct
       themselves in a manner consistent with the Code of Conduct.
    c. Prohibited behaviors and restricted items
   •   Prohibit the use or possession of illegal substances, weapons, and firearms (except for
       authorized security personnel).
   •   Prohibit pornography, gambling, and alcohol consumption during working hours.
   •   Prohibit hunting, fishing with explosives/chemicals, trading wildlife, or keeping animals
       (particularly birds).
   •   Prohibit cutting trees, collecting firewood, or harvesting plants beyond approved areas.
   •   Prohibit disturbing architectural/historical sites, burning waste, or clearing vegetation
       without authorization.
    d. Hygiene, safety, and environmental protection
   •   Maintain personal hygiene and living areas to appropriate standards.
   •   Smoking is only permitted in designated areas.
   •   Do not wash clothes, clean vehicles, or maintain equipment in rivers/streams or
       unauthorized locations.
   •   Do not dispose of garbage or construction waste indiscriminately; prevent the spread of
       pollutants such as oil or grease.
   •   Toilets must be used only in designated places.



                                                                                                306
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    •   Do not work without personal protective equipment such as boots and helmets.
    e. Disciplinary measures
    •   Any violation of the Code shall be subject to disciplinary action, ranging from a warning
        to termination of the labor contract, depending on the severity of the violation.
    •   Any construction worker, office staff, contractor’s employee, or any other person involved
        in the project found violating prohibitions shall be subject to disciplinary measures, which
        may range from a simple reprimand to dismissal, depending on the severity of the violation.
2. Labor management process (LMP)
A Labor Management Procedure (LMP) was developed as a stand-alone document for addressing
the impacts on and of labor and working conditions of the project. The LMP identifies the main
labor requirements and risks associated with the project and help the PMU and Contractor to
determine the resources necessary to address project labor issues. The LMP will enable different
project-related parties, for example, staff of the project management unit, contractors and sub-
contractors and project workers, to have a clear understanding of what is required on a specific
labor issue. The LMP is a living document, which is initiated early in project preparation, and is
reviewed and updated throughout development and implementation of the project. The LMP has
12 chapters. Chapter 1 served as Introduction. An overview of labor use in the project is presented
in Chapter 2. Key potential labor risks are listed in Chapter 3. Legislative Framework governing
labor employment in Vietnam is discussed in Chapters 4 and 5. Implementation Arrangements,
Age Requirement, Policies and Procedures and Timing of labor requirements follows in the
subsequent chapters. Grievance Redress Mechanism and Contractor Management are presented in
the last two chapters 10 and 11 respectively, while Chapter 12 relates to primary supply workers.
Details are presented in the Project’s LMP.
    5.1.3.2.   Component 1 – Site-specific mitigation
5.1.3.2.1.     Mitigation measures for the pollution risk due to improper management of
               brackish organic topsoil from aquaculture areas
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid mixing brackish organic topsoil with clean spoil including marking and isolating
    excavation zones and using separate trucks and stockpiles to prevent contamination of clean
    materials.
•   Avoid discharging wet brackish organic sludge into irrigation canals or agricultural fields
    including prohibiting temporary dumping on farmland or unlined open ground and establishing
    clear no-dumping zones around aquaculture ponds.
•   Avoid excavation and transport during heavy rainfall including applying a weather-based
    construction schedule to reduce uncontrolled runoff and spillage.
•   Avoid using brackish organic topsoil for road embankment construction or permanent
    backfilling works to prevent long-term soil and water contamination.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Manage brackish organic topsoil strictly in accordance with the construction method defined
    in the Feasibility Study, whereby the topsoil layer is excavated and removed directly during
    linear construction activities without on-site drying or pond-based storage.



                                                                                                307
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Segregate brackish organic topsoil from general excavation material at the point of excavation
    to prevent mixing with freshwater soils or inert fill materials.
•   Excavate topsoil in short sections consistent with the rolling construction approach, and load
    material immediately onto covered trucks to minimize exposure time, odour generation and
    runoff.
•   Avoid temporary stockpiling of brackish organic topsoil within the project area; where short-
    term placement is unavoidable, limit storage duration to less than 24 hours and place material
    on compacted ground with peripheral earth bunds to prevent runoff.
•   Control leachate and moisture by scheduling excavation during low-rainfall periods, shaping
    temporary placement areas to direct runoff away from drains and water bodies, and preventing
    uncontrolled discharge to the surrounding environment.
•   Control odour through rapid removal, avoidance of prolonged exposure, and, where necessary,
    light application of lime to freshly excavated material prior to loading.
•   Ensure safe handling and transport by using sealed or canvas-covered trucks, preventing
    dripping and spillage along haul routes, and implementing wheel washing at site exits to avoid
    salt and organic residue deposition on public roads.
•   Transport all excavated brackish organic topsoil directly to the licensed Toàn Thắng disposal
    facility in accordance with the approved waste management plan and official acceptance
    documentation.
•   Maintain waste tracking records including excavation location, quantity, transport time and
    disposal confirmation to ensure full traceability.
•   Prohibit any on-site reuse, treatment or drying of brackish organic topsoil unless explicitly
    revised and approved by the PMU and the competent authority.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of accidental spillage, runoff or contamination of surrounding land or water bodies,
    immediately remove contaminated material and implement clean-up measures to restore
    affected areas.Clean and reinstate temporary drying ponds after completion including removal
    of residual sludge, levelling of pond bases, repair of embankments and restoration of any
    disturbed access tracks.
•   Monitor soil salinity in temporarily used ponds before handover including measurement of
    electrical conductivity in the top 20-30 cm of soil, targeting values below 10 mS/cm or baseline
    conditions where available.
•   Conduct post-use drainage flushing including a single flushing with clean water where
    necessary to reduce residual salinity prior to returning ponds to owners.
Offset measures
•   Implement compensation and livelihood restoration measures for affected aquaculture
    households including compensation for temporary land acquisition and loss of one aquaculture
    production season in accordance with the approved Resettlement Plan.
•   Coordinate with local authorities and affected households to agree on disposal locations, access
    arrangements and restoration measures that minimise long-term impacts on surrounding
    aquaculture production systems.




                                                                                                 308
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
5.1.3.2.2.     Mitigation measures for the pollution risk to agricultural production and
               aquaculture due to road construction activities
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid conducting earthworks, topsoil stripping and stockpiling activities adjacent to shrimp
    farming ponds during sensitive production periods, particularly the main grow-out phases
    around March–April and August–September, as far as practicable through construction
    scheduling.
•   Avoid simultaneous opening of multiple construction segments in aquaculture and agricultural
    sections by strictly applying rolling construction with segment lengths limited to
    approximately 100–150 m.
•   Avoid discharging any untreated runoff, construction wastewater, dewatering effluent or
    domestic wastewater from workers into irrigation canals, shrimp ponds or agricultural fields.
•   Avoid locating temporary soil stockpiles, material storage areas or machinery maintenance
    points directly adjacent to aquaculture ponds, irrigation canals or low-lying agricultural plots.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Strip and manage organic topsoil separately from subsoil and construction materials, and
    stockpile it in clearly demarcated areas equipped with perimeter bunds, drainage ditches and
    temporary       covers    to    prevent     erosion     and     wash-off     during     rainfall.
    Limit the volume and duration of on-site soil stockpiling within each construction segment by
    reusing suitable material for embankment filling as early as possible in the construction
    sequence.
•   Install temporary drainage control measures along construction sections crossing or adjacent
    to agricultural land and aquaculture areas, including diversion ditches, sediment traps and
    controlled discharge points, to intercept sediment-laden runoff before it enters ponds or
    irrigation canals.
•   Provide physical separation between construction areas and aquaculture ponds, such as low
    earth bunds or temporary barriers, to prevent accidental inflow of sediment, construction debris
    or wastewater.
•   Ensure all construction machinery operating near aquaculture ponds and agricultural land is
    properly maintained to prevent oil and grease leakage, and keep spill response materials readily
    available on site.
•   Manage construction solid waste and domestic waste from workers through collection in
    designated containers and regular removal to licensed facilities, preventing dispersion into
    surrounding ponds and fields.
•   Coordinate construction schedules and temporary drainage arrangements with local farmers
    and aquaculture households, informing them in advance of planned works and potential short-
    term disturbances to water management.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of sediment runoff, turbidity increase or contamination affecting aquaculture
    ponds or agricultural land, immediately stop the source of pollution and implement corrective
    actions, such as reinforcing drainage controls, removing deposited sediment or isolating
    affected canals.



                                                                                                 309
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•   Restore affected irrigation and drainage canals, pond embankments and field boundaries to
    their pre-construction condition after completion of works in each segment.
    Where construction activities cause localized crop burial, soil compaction or water quality
    deterioration affecting aquaculture production, implement site-specific rehabilitation
    measures, including soil loosening, removal of contaminated material and restoration of
    normal water circulation.
Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts result in demonstrable loss of agricultural yield or aquaculture
    production despite mitigation measures, compensate affected households in accordance with
    the approved Resettlement Plan, including compensation for crop loss, production interruption
    or reduced harvest.
•   Maintain a grievance redress mechanism accessible to farmers and aquaculture operators to
    allow timely reporting of water quality or sedimentation issues and ensure prompt response
    during the construction period.


5.1.3.2.3.     Mitigation measures for the traffic safety risk at intersections with regional roads
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid conducting traffic-affecting construction activities at intersections during peak traffic
    hours (06:30–08:00 and 16:30–18:30) on National Highway 5A, National Highway 10 and
    Provincial Road 359, and during daytime peak periods on the Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway,
    as far as practicable through construction scheduling.
•   Avoid simultaneous construction works that affect traffic flow at more than one major
    intersection at the same time, particularly for the two grade-separated intersections with the
    expressway and National Highway 5A.
•   Avoid narrowing live traffic lanes on high-speed corridors without prior installation of physical
    separation systems and approval of detailed traffic safety measures by traffic police and road
    management authorities.
•   Avoid girder launching, heavy lifting or overhead works above live traffic lanes during periods
    of reduced visibility, heavy rainfall or strong winds, unless full or partial lane closures with
    approved safety arrangements are implemented.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Prepare and implement intersection-specific traffic safety management plans for each of the
    four intersections, distinguishing clearly between grade-separated intersections and at-grade
    intersections, and obtain approval from relevant road authorities and traffic police prior to
    construction.
•   For grade-separated intersections with the Ha Noi–Hai Phong Expressway and National
    Highway 5A, apply staged construction of abutments, piers and superstructures so that lane
    reductions are minimized and limited to the shortest feasible duration. Install rigid safety
    barriers, crash cushions and protective screens between construction zones and live traffic
    lanes to prevent vehicle intrusion and falling objects. Provide advance warning signage, speed
    reduction measures and lane guidance well upstream of work zones. Restrict heavy lifting,




                                                                                                 310
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    girder erection and overhead works to off-peak periods or night-time under police supervision
    and approved traffic control schemes.
•   For at-grade intersections with Provincial Road 359 and National Highway 10, maintain
    existing carriageway widths during most of the construction period, limiting lane narrowing to
    short durations during connection works, roundabout construction and pavement completion.
    Install temporary channelization measures such as cones, barriers and temporary markings to
    clearly define traffic paths. Deploy trained traffic controllers at intersection approaches during
    stages involving lane shifts, turning lane construction or high construction-vehicle movements.
•   For all intersections, control construction vehicle access by designating specific entry and exit
    points and limiting truck movements to off-peak hours. Install adequate daytime and night-
    time lighting, reflective signage and warning devices, particularly during the rainy season
    when visibility and pavement friction are reduced. Ensure construction workers operating
    within intersection areas receive specific traffic-safety training and use high-visibility personal
    protective equipment at all times.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of traffic accidents, near-miss incidents or recurrent congestion at construction-
    affected intersections, immediately review the effectiveness of traffic safety arrangements and
    revise lane layouts, signage, work sequencing or working hours as necessary.
•   Repair any damage to pavement surfaces, road markings, barriers or safety facilities caused by
    construction activities promptly to restore safe driving conditions. Suspend or reschedule high-
    risk construction activities when adverse weather conditions or unexpected traffic conditions
    significantly increase accident risk.
Offset measures
•   Where unavoidable lane restrictions or traffic delays at intersections cause prolonged
    disruption despite mitigation measures, implement additional actions such as extended
    working hours during off-peak periods to shorten the overall duration of traffic impacts.
• Maintain continuous coordination with traffic police, road authorities and local governments,
    and provide advance public information on traffic changes, construction schedules and
    expected delays to allow road users to plan alternative routes.
5.1.3.2.4.     Mitigation measures for the disruption and disturbance to waterway traffic on
               the Cam River in the Ngo Quyen Bridge area
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid construction activities that require significant narrowing of the main navigation channel
    during peak navigation periods for large vessels entering and leaving the Hai Phong port
    system, as identified by the inland waterway authority and port operators.
•   Avoid simultaneous construction works at multiple in-river pier or pylon locations that would
    cumulatively reduce effective navigable width below safe operational limits for large seagoing
    vessels.
•   Avoid conducting critical in-river works such as pier construction, cofferdam installation, and
    main-span erection during periods of severe weather, storms, or extreme tidal conditions when
    navigation safety risks are elevated.
Minimization and mitigation measures



                                                                                                   311
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
•   Prepare and implement a detailed waterway traffic management plan for the Cam River section
    at the Ngo Quyen Bridge, including navigation channel arrangements, vessel speed limits,
    passing rules, and communication protocols, and obtain approval from the relevant inland
    waterway and maritime authorities prior to construction.
•   Maintain a clearly defined and continuously open navigation corridor beneath the main span,
    ensuring that effective navigable width and clearance are sufficient for vessels up to 30,000
    DWT to pass safely under regulated conditions.
•   Establish clearly marked on-water exclusion zones around cofferdams, piers, pylons, and
    floating construction equipment using buoys, lights, reflective markers, and warning signage
    visible during both day and night.
•   Deploy on-water traffic control and coordination measures, including real-time
    communication with vessel operators and, where required, escort or guidance for large vessels
    passing the construction zone.
•   Schedule the movement and positioning of barges, floating cranes, and material transport
    vessels to minimize interference with through-navigation, particularly during periods of high
    vessel traffic density.
•   Implement strict control of construction materials, equipment, and lifting operations to prevent
    objects from falling into navigation channels during elevated works.
•   Coordinate construction activities with port authorities, shipping agents, and vessel operators
    to provide advance notice of temporary navigation restrictions, reduced speeds, or one-way
    traffic arrangements.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of navigation incidents, near-miss events, or unexpected congestion near the bridge
    construction area, immediately review and adjust navigation channel arrangements, exclusion
    zone layouts, and construction sequencing in coordination with waterway authorities.
    Promptly remove temporary obstructions, floating debris, or construction-related hazards from
    the river channel to restore safe navigation conditions.
•   Repair or replace damaged navigation aids, buoys, or signage caused by construction activities
    without delay.
Offset measures
•   Where unavoidable navigation delays or temporary restrictions result in measurable
    operational inconvenience to vessel operators despite mitigation measures, coordinate with
    relevant authorities to implement compensatory scheduling measures or alternative traffic
    arrangements.
    Maintain regular communication with waterway users and stakeholders throughout the
    construction period to address concerns and implement additional practical measures if
    required.
5.1.3.2.5.     Mitigation measures for Impacts on water quality and aquatic life at Cam River
               and Ruot Lon River
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid carrying out in-river excavation, pile drilling, and foundation works during extreme
    hydrological conditions such as floods, storms, or periods of exceptionally low flow,
    particularly in the Ruot Lon River during the dry season when dilution capacity is limited.


                                                                                                312
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Avoid simultaneous in-river construction at multiple piers within the same river reach to
    prevent cumulative increases in TSS and turbidity beyond localized and manageable levels.
•   Avoid direct discharge of untreated dewatering effluent, construction wastewater, wash water,
    or domestic wastewater into the Cam River and Ruot Lon River.
•   Avoid placing soil, sand, cement, or construction material stockpiles within the riverbank zone
    subject to tidal influence or surface runoff into the river.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Install fully enclosed cofferdams around all pier foundations and in-river excavation areas to
    physically isolate sediment-disturbing activities from the surrounding water body and limit
    sediment dispersion.
•   Collect all dewatering effluent from cofferdams and foundation pits and treat it through
    sedimentation tanks, settling ponds, or equivalent systems to reduce TSS prior to controlled
    discharge back to the river, ensuring compliance with applicable Vietnamese standards.
•   Stabilize riverbanks, working platforms, and temporary access roads near the river using
    compaction, temporary surfacing, and drainage controls to minimize erosion and sediment
    runoff during rainfall and tidal fluctuations.
•   Cover and bund temporary soil and sediment stockpiles, and install peripheral drainage ditches,
    silt fences, or sediment traps to intercept runoff before it reaches the river.
•   Sequence drilling, excavation, and foundation works so that periods of intense sediment
    disturbance are limited to the shortest practicable duration (approximately 20–35 days per pier)
    and do not overlap unnecessarily between work points.
•   Prohibit washing of construction equipment, barges, concrete mixers, and pier surfaces directly
    in the river; collect all wash water for treatment or off-site disposal. Install spill prevention and
    control measures at all locations where construction machinery operates near or over water,
    including drip trays, secondary containment, and readily available absorbent materials.
•   Collect domestic wastewater from construction workers using mobile toilets or temporary
    treatment systems, ensuring no untreated discharge into the river. Implement regular
    monitoring of turbidity and TSS upstream and downstream of active construction areas, with
    increased frequency during dry-season works at the Ruot Lon River, and adjust construction
    methods if elevated levels persist.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of accidental releases of sediment-laden water, oil, fuel, or construction materials
    into the river, immediately stop the source, deploy containment and recovery measures, and
    notify the relevant environmental and waterway authorities.
•   Remove accumulated construction-related sediment, debris, or temporary structures from the
    riverbed and riverbanks upon completion of in-river works to restore natural flow conditions
    and substrate characteristics.
•   Reinstate disturbed riverbank areas and temporary working platforms to stable conditions
    through grading and appropriate surface restoration once construction activities cease.
Offset measures
•   Where temporary water quality deterioration results in observable impacts on aquatic resources
    or river uses beyond the predicted localized zone, implement additional mitigation or
    restoration measures as required by environmental authorities.


                                                                                                    313
                                                                                                     Official Use Only
•   Maintain communication with local authorities and relevant stakeholders regarding
    construction schedules, in-river works, and environmental protection measures, and address
    concerns related to water quality and aquatic life throughout the construction period.
5.1.3.2.6.     Mitigation measures for impacts on adjacent protection forest at the Ruot Lon
               Bridge area
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid any construction activities, material storage, machinery parking, or worker movement
    outside the approved construction boundary and legally acquired land area adjacent to the
    protection forest.
•   Avoid locating temporary access roads, machinery yards, material stockpiles, or waste storage
    areas within or immediately adjacent to the remaining protection forest strip.
•   Avoid unnecessary operation of heavy machinery within the root protection zones of
    Sonneratia caseolaris trees along the forest edge, particularly during periods of high soil
    moisture or tidal inundation when soil compaction risks are elevated.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Clearly demarcate the construction boundary along the protection forest edge using fencing,
    stakes, or warning tape prior to commencement of construction to prevent accidental
    encroachment into forest areas.
•   Establish designated access routes and turning areas for construction vehicles and machinery
    that maintain a safe buffer distance from the forest edge and tree root zones.
•   Implement strict controls on crane operation, lifting activities, and equipment maneuvering
    near the forest boundary to prevent mechanical damage to tree trunks, branches, and crowns.
•   Stabilize temporary soil stockpiles and working platforms near the forest edge using
    compaction, containment bunds, and drainage controls to prevent soil or mud runoff into forest
    areas during rainfall or high tidal conditions.
•   Prohibit disposal of construction waste, concrete washout, cement mortar, or fine aggregates
    near the forest edge, and provide designated collection areas located away from the protection
    forest.
•   Implement spill prevention and response measures for fuel, oil, and lubricants, including
    secondary containment for refueling areas and readily available absorbent materials, to prevent
    soil contamination at the forest margin.
•   Control dust generation near the forest edge through appropriate watering or surface
    stabilization measures, ensuring that watering does not cause runoff or sediment flow into the
    forest area.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of accidental soil deposition, waste spillage, or contamination affecting the forest
    edge, immediately remove contaminated material, clean affected areas, and restore soil
    conditions to the extent practicable
•   Repair localized ground disturbance at the forest margin by regrading and reinstating surface
    conditions to support tree stability and natural regeneration
•   Replace or rehabilitate damaged individual trees or seedlings at the forest edge in coordination
    with the local forest management authority, using appropriate native or locally suitable species
    where required.


                                                                                                 314
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
Offset measures
•   Where indirect construction impacts result in measurable damage or loss of protection forest
    functions at the site margin beyond predicted levels, implement supplementary planting or
    enrichment measures within the adjacent protection forest area in consultation with the
    competent forestry authority.
• Maintain coordination with local forest management agencies throughout construction to
    monitor forest edge conditions and agree on any additional corrective actions needed to ensure
    no net loss of protection forest functions.
5.1.3.2.7.      Mitigation measures for impacts on community safety and accessibility in
                adjacent residential areas
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid simultaneous construction activities at multiple residential access points within the same
    neighborhood to prevent cumulative disruption of local mobility and daily activities.
•   Avoid leaving excavation trenches open overnight or during non-working hours in front of
    residential houses, shops, and local access roads wherever technically feasible.
•   Avoid scheduling construction activities that block household access during peak daily activity
    periods, particularly early morning and evening hours in dense residential areas such as Ngu
    Lao and Trung Ha.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement a rolling construction approach with short working sections (100–150 m) and
    prioritize completion and reinstatement of one residential frontage before moving to the next.
•   Provide safe and continuous temporary access to all affected houses, shops, and local roads
    during construction, including steel plates, prefabricated trench covers, temporary footbridges,
    or compacted access ramps across drainage trenches.
•   Install rigid barriers, fencing, and warning tape around open trenches and excavation areas,
    with adequate reflective materials and warning lights for nighttime visibility.
•   Clearly mark pedestrian routes and motorcycle access paths through or around construction
    zones using signage and ground markings, ensuring safe separation from construction vehicles
    and equipment.
•   Control construction traffic within residential areas by enforcing low speed limits, designated
    haul routes, and restricted truck movements during periods of high pedestrian activity,
    particularly near schools, shops, and markets.
•   Assign on-site safety personnel or flagmen at locations with high pedestrian and motorcycle
    movement to guide residents safely through construction zones and manage interactions
    between local traffic and construction vehicles.
•   Maintain regular communication with affected households and small businesses regarding
    construction schedules, expected duration of access disruption, and temporary access
    arrangements.
Remediation measures
•   Promptly reinstate road surfaces, sidewalks, and residential access points after completion of
    drainage installation and embankment works, ensuring surfaces are level, stable, and safe for
    daily use



                                                                                                315
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Repair any damage to household access roads, driveways, fences, or small roadside structures
    caused by construction activities.
•   Remove all temporary barriers, debris, and construction materials from residential areas once
    works at each section are completed.
Offset measures
•   Where temporary loss of access or safety risks result in demonstrable disruption to household
    activities or small roadside businesses beyond normal construction impacts, implement
    appropriate compensation or support measures in accordance with the project’s grievance
    redress mechanism and relevant resettlement provisions.
• Maintain an accessible grievance mechanism at community level to receive and promptly
    address complaints related to safety, access, and construction-related inconvenience
    throughout the construction period.
5.1.3.2.8.      Mitigation measures for Impacts on the operation of Hai An Port
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid construction activities that require full closure or long-term restriction of active berths
    or access channels serving Hai An Port.
•   Avoid scheduling girder launching, installation of oversized structural components, or other
    high-risk overhead works during peak port operation periods with high vessel and container
    throughput.
•   Avoid simultaneous construction works affecting multiple berths or internal port traffic
    corridors at the same time to prevent cumulative disruption to port operations.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Prepare and implement a detailed Construction Coordination Plan for works within the Hai An
    Port area, developed in close consultation with the Port Authority and port operators, covering
    construction sequencing, safety zoning, access arrangements, and communication protocols.
•   Phase construction of the bridge approach and elevated structures so that impacts on berth
    access and container handling areas are spatially limited and temporary, with only one berth
    or internal circulation section affected at a time wherever feasible.
•   Maintain continuous and safe access to all operational berths, navigation channels, and cargo
    handling areas, with any temporary restrictions clearly defined in time and space and
    communicated in advance to port operators and vessel agents.
•   Establish temporary safety exclusion zones beneath elevated works using rigid barriers,
    overhead protection, and warning signage to prevent falling object risks while allowing
    adjacent port operations to continue safely.
•   Coordinate construction schedules with the Port Authority to implement time-window
    restrictions for vessel movements or berth access only when strictly necessary, minimizing
    impacts on daily vessel calls and cargo handling efficiency.
•   Implement traffic management measures within the port area to manage interactions between
    construction vehicles and container trucks, including designated construction haul routes,
    temporary lane markings, speed limits, and on-site traffic controllers.
•   Ensure that all lifting operations, material handling, and elevated works above active port areas
    comply with strict safety procedures, including secure lifting plans, secondary retention
    systems, and real-time supervision.


                                                                                                 316
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    Remediation measures
•   Promptly reinstate any temporarily affected internal port roads, container yard surfaces, and
    berth-side working areas to their original condition or better after completion of each
    construction stage.
•   Remove temporary barriers, exclusion zones, and construction-related obstructions
    immediately after the relevant works are completed to restore full operational capacity.
    In the event of accidental damage to port infrastructure, utilities, or equipment caused by
    construction activities, carry out timely repair or replacement in coordination with the Port
    Authority.
    Offset measures
•   Where construction activities result in unavoidable short-term disruption to port operations or
    measurable delays in vessel handling despite mitigation measures, coordinate with the Port
• Authority to implement compensatory operational arrangements, such as adjusted scheduling
    or temporary reallocation of handling capacity.
• Maintain regular coordination meetings with the Port Authority and key port stakeholders
    throughout the construction period to review impacts, address operational concerns, and agree
    on additional corrective measures if required.
5.1.3.2.9.     Mitigation measures for Impacts on Lap Le Ward Medical Station (Km 9+550)
               and Trung Ha Commune Kindergarten (Km 15+400)
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid locating construction access points, material stockpiles, machinery parking areas and
    worker facilities directly in front of or adjacent to the main entrances of the Lap Le Ward
    Medical Station and Trung Ha Commune Kindergarten.
•   Avoid carrying out high-disturbance activities such as demolition, heavy compaction or
    operation of large construction machinery in close proximity to these sensitive receptors during
    critical operating hours, particularly consultation hours at the medical station and teaching, rest
    and pick-up/drop-off times at the kindergarten.
•   Avoid routing construction traffic through internal access roads or frontage areas used by
    patients, children, parents and staff where alternative routes are available.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Prepare site-specific protection and access management arrangements for each sensitive
    receptor, clearly defining construction boundaries, buffer distances, temporary access routes
    and safety zones, and coordinate these arrangements in advance with the medical station and
    kindergarten management
•   Implement enhanced dust control measures at these locations, including frequent water
    spraying of active works and access roads, covering of stockpiles and trucks, and prompt
    removal of debris, to prevent dust intrusion into buildings.
•   Apply stricter noise and vibration control measures near the medical station and kindergarten,
    including use of low-noise equipment, avoidance of simultaneous operation of multiple noisy
    machines, and scheduling of unavoidable noisy activities outside sensitive time periods.
    Install continuous physical barriers, fencing and warning signs to separate construction areas



                                                                                                   317
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
    from pedestrian routes used by patients, children and caregivers, ensuring that barriers are
    stable and without gaps.
•   Provide safe and clearly marked pedestrian access to both facilities at all times, including
    temporary walkways, ramps or plates over trenches, and ensure accessibility for ambulances,
    emergency vehicles and parents with children. Deploy trained safety personnel or flaggers at
    entrances during periods of construction traffic to manage interactions between construction
    vehicles, motorcycles and pedestrians. Ensure adequate lighting, visibility and cleanliness
    along access routes to both facilities, particularly during early morning and late afternoon
    hours.
    Remediation measures
•   In the event of dust, noise, vibration or access-related complaints from the medical station or
    kindergarten, immediately investigate the cause and adjust construction methods, schedules or
    protective measures to restore acceptable conditions.
•   Repair any damage to access roads, pavements, fences or facility frontage caused by
    construction activities and restore them to pre-construction condition without delay.
•   Where construction activities inadvertently disrupt access to emergency services or school
    operations, implement immediate corrective measures to reinstate safe and functional access.
    Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on the operation of the medical station or kindergarten cannot be fully
    avoided despite mitigation measures, provide additional temporary support in coordination
    with local authorities, such as adjusted construction schedules, enhanced safety supervision or
    supplementary access arrangements.
• Maintain regular communication with facility managers and local authorities to inform them
    of construction progress, upcoming activities and any temporary changes, thereby minimizing
    disruption to healthcare and education services.
    5.1.3.3. Component 2 – Site-specific mitigation
5.1.3.3.1.      Mitigation measures for impacts on water quality and aquatic life of the Re River
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid extending embankment works, temporary access roads, material stockpiles and
    construction platforms beyond the approved riverside footprint, ensuring all activities remain
    within the land acquisition boundary assessed in Chapter 4.
•   Avoid conducting sediment-disturbing activities concurrently along long continuous reaches
    of the Re River by phasing works so that only short sections are opened at any given time.
•   Avoid refuelling, equipment maintenance and storage of fuels, oils or chemicals within the
    river corridor or directly on the riverbank where accidental releases could enter the river.
•   Avoid scheduling major riverbank excavation during periods of high flow or heavy rainfall to
    reduce uncontrolled sediment runoff into the river.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement staged embankment construction methods, limiting excavation length and exposure
    time of riverbanks, and promptly stabilizing completed sections to reduce erosion and sediment
    input.



                                                                                               318
                                                                                               Official Use Only
•   Install appropriate erosion and sediment control measures along the riverbank, including
    temporary silt fences, geotextiles, toe protection and stabilized drainage outlets, to intercept
    sediment-laden runoff before it enters the Re River.
•   Provide temporary containment measures where necessary, such as localized silt curtains or
    equivalent near-bank controls, to confine turbidity increases to the immediate work area during
    critical activities.
•   Ensure all construction machinery operating near the river is well maintained and free of oil or
    fuel leaks, with spill prevention and response materials readily available on-site.
•   Collect and manage construction wastewater, dewatering effluent and concrete wash water so
    that no untreated discharges enter the river, using settlement or collection systems as
    appropriate prior to reuse or disposal.
•   Clearly demarcate no-go zones along sensitive near-bank areas to prevent unnecessary vehicle
    movement, equipment parking or stockpiling that could destabilize the riverbank or disturb
    aquatic habitats.
•   Monitor visual turbidity and sediment plume extent during embankment works and adjust
    construction methods, sequencing or controls if excessive spreading beyond the near-bank
    zone is observed.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of accidental sediment release, bank collapse, oil spill or other pollution incident
    affecting the Re River, immediately stop the source, contain the affected area and implement
    clean-up measures in accordance with approved emergency response procedures.
•   Remove unintended sediment deposits that adversely affect local water quality, intakes or near-
    bank habitats, and restore bank profiles where erosion or instability has occurred due to
    construction activities.
•   Rehabilitate temporarily disturbed riverbank sections after completion of works, including
    regrading, stabilization and revegetation to prevent ongoing erosion and sediment input.
Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on aquatic habitats or water quality cannot be fully avoided despite
    mitigation measures, implement compensatory actions in coordination with relevant
    authorities, such as supporting riverbank vegetation restoration or aquatic habitat enhancement
    at agreed locations along the Re River.
•   Provide timely information to local communities, river users and aquaculture operators
    regarding construction schedules and expected periods of disturbance to allow adjustment of
    activities and minimize socio-economic impacts.


5.1.3.3.2.     Mitigation measures for risk of sedimentation and turbidity affecting water
               intake at Quan Vinh pumping station
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid undertaking sediment-disturbing construction activities in the immediate vicinity of the
    Quan Vinh pumping station intake beyond the minimum footprint required for embankment
    and riverside road works.



                                                                                                 319
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•   Avoid simultaneous riverbank excavation or earthworks both upstream and downstream of the
    pumping station intake to prevent cumulative increases in turbidity near the intake zone.
•   Avoid scheduling intensive near-bank works close to the intake during periods of high
    pumping demand or low river flow, when the intake is more sensitive to sediment and turbidity
    increases.
•   Avoid positioning material stockpiles, access routes or temporary drainage outlets in locations
    where runoff could be directly conveyed toward the pumping station intake.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement staged construction along the riverbank near the pumping station, limiting the
    length of exposed bank at any given time and stabilizing completed sections promptly to reduce
    sediment release.
•   Install localized sediment control measures near the intake area, such as silt curtains, geotextile
    barriers or equivalent near-bank controls, to confine turbidity increases to the immediate
    construction zone.
•   Design and maintain temporary drainage and runoff interception measures along the riverside
    road and embankment works to prevent sediment-laden runoff from entering the river near the
    intake.
•   Ensure construction machinery operating near the intake is well maintained and free from oil
    or fuel leaks, with spill prevention and response equipment readily available on-site
•   Monitor visual turbidity and sediment plume movement near the pumping station intake during
    critical construction activities, and adjust construction methods or sequencing if plume
    encroachment toward the intake is observed.
•   Coordinate construction scheduling and sequencing with the pumping station operator to
    ensure awareness of high-risk periods and enable operational adjustments if necessary.
    Remediation measures
•   In the event of excessive turbidity or sediment deposition affecting pumping station operation,
    immediately suspend the relevant construction activities and implement corrective measures,
    including reinforcement of sediment controls or modification of construction methods.
•   Remove accumulated sediments near the intake where monitoring indicates adverse effects on
    intake performance or water quality, in coordination with the pumping station operator.
•   Restore disturbed riverbank sections near the intake following completion of works, including
    regrading and stabilization to prevent ongoing sediment inputs.
    Offset measures
•   Where residual risks to pumping station operation remain despite mitigation measures, support
    additional protective or maintenance measures in coordination with the pumping station
    operator, such as enhanced intake maintenance or supplementary sediment management
    actions during the construction period.
• Maintain regular communication with the pumping station operator and relevant authorities to
    provide advance notice of construction activities and ensure timely response to any operational
    concerns.
5.1.3.3.3.     Mitigation measures for risk of residential building cracking and safety hazards
    Avoidance measures


                                                                                                   320
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
•   Avoid extending embankment construction works, temporary access roads, excavation pits and
    material stockpiles beyond the approved riverside footprint, ensuring that works are kept at the
    maximum feasible distance from existing residential structures as identified in Chapter 4.
•   Avoid deep excavation, slope cutting or rapid loading in sections where houses are located
    very close to the embankment line unless protective structural measures are incorporated in
    advance.
•   Avoid simultaneous excavation and embankment loading along long continuous riverbank
    sections adjacent to dense residential areas by phasing works into short, manageable segments.
•   Avoid carrying out high-risk activities such as deep excavation or heavy compaction during
    periods of high groundwater levels or prolonged rainfall, when the risk of ground instability
    and building settlement is increased.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Conduct detailed geotechnical investigations along residential sections of the Re River
    embankment, including boreholes and soil strength testing, to inform safe embankment design,
    slope angles and construction methods.
•   Adopt construction methods that minimize ground movement near houses, such as staged
    excavation and filling, controlled compaction, and use of temporary retaining or support
    structures where required.
•   Install appropriate temporary retaining systems, sheet piles or bracing solutions in sections
    where embankment excavation is close to residential foundations to prevent lateral soil
    movement and collapse.
•   Implement systematic condition surveys of residential buildings prior to construction to
    document baseline conditions and identify particularly vulnerable structures. Monitor ground
    movement and visible cracking in nearby houses during embankment construction, and adjust
    construction methods or sequencing if signs of excessive settlement or distress are observed.
•   Clearly demarcate construction zones and install protective fencing and warning signs along
    residential interfaces to prevent accidental entry and enhance public safety. Maintain safe and
    clearly marked access routes for residents during construction, ensuring that entrances, paths
    and emergency access are not obstructed by works.
    Remediation measures
•   In the event that building cracking, settlement or other structural damage is observed and
    attributable to construction activities, immediately suspend the relevant works and investigate
    the cause in coordination with qualified engineers.
•   Implement prompt repair, strengthening or stabilization measures for affected residential
    structures, including foundation underpinning, crack repair or temporary support, as
    appropriate.
•   Restore ground and embankment stability in affected sections through additional
    reinforcement, regrading or drainage improvements before resuming construction.
    Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on residential buildings cannot be fully avoided despite mitigation
    measures, provide compensation or repair support to affected households in accordance with
    the approved Resettlement Action Plan and applicable regulations.



                                                                                                321
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Maintain regular communication with affected residents and local authorities regarding
    construction schedules, identified risks and mitigation actions, and provide a clear grievance
    mechanism to address safety concerns and damage claims promptly.
5.1.3.3.4.     Mitigation measures for impacts on agricultural activities adjacent to the Re
               River
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid extending construction activities, temporary access roads, material stockpiles and
    equipment parking beyond the approved riverside footprint into adjacent agricultural land,
    ensuring all works remain within the land acquisition boundary identified in Chapter 4.
•   Avoid scheduling construction activities that block access to agricultural plots during critical
    farming periods, including planting, harvesting and irrigation cycles, where practicable.
•   Avoid discharging construction wastewater, sediment-laden runoff or spoil onto adjacent
    agricultural land or into irrigation channels serving farmland.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain continuous and safe access to agricultural plots by providing temporary access paths,
    crossings or ramps where construction works interrupt existing routes used by farmers.
    Strip and store agricultural topsoil separately from subsoil and construction spoil in clearly
    demarcated and protected stockpiles, and reinstate topsoil promptly after completion of works
    to preserve soil fertility.
•   Implement erosion and sediment control measures along the interface with agricultural land,
    including temporary bunds, silt fences and controlled drainage outlets, to prevent sediment
    deposition on crops and farmland.
•   Limit the duration and extent of temporary land occupation for construction by phasing works
    in short sections and restoring completed sections as early as possible.
•   Ensure construction machinery operating near agricultural land is properly maintained to
    prevent fuel, oil or hydraulic leaks that could contaminate soils or crops.
•   Coordinate construction schedules with local farmers and commune authorities to inform them
    in advance of work timing, access changes and expected duration of impacts on adjacent
    agricultural land.
Remediation measures
•   In the event that agricultural land is accidentally damaged, compacted or contaminated by
    construction activities, immediately stop the source of impact and implement corrective
    actions, including soil loosening, removal of contaminated material and reinstatement of
    affected plots.
•   Restore irrigation channels, field boundaries and drainage structures affected by construction
    activities to their pre-construction condition to enable resumption of agricultural production.
•   Rehabilitate temporarily used agricultural land following completion of works, including
    regrading, topsoil replacement and stabilization to prevent ongoing erosion.
Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on agricultural production or loss of farming income cannot be fully
    avoided despite mitigation measures, compensate affected households in accordance with the



                                                                                                322
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    approved Resettlement Action Plan, including compensation for crop losses and temporary
    production disruption.
• Coordinate with local authorities and affected farmers to agree on restoration schedules and
    support measures to ensure timely recovery of agricultural activities along the Re River
    corridor.
5.1.3.3.5.     Mitigation measures for Impacts on Pham Tu Nghi temple, An Tri and Quynh
               Cu pagodas
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid locating construction access points, material stockpiles, equipment parking areas and
    worker facilities within the immediate vicinity of the temples and pagodas, particularly near
    main gates, courtyards and congregation spaces.
•   Avoid high-vibration or high-impact construction activities, such as heavy compaction or deep
    excavation, in close proximity to the temples and pagodas unless protective measures are in
    place.
•   Avoid scheduling disruptive construction activities during important religious days, festivals,
    ceremonial events or peak worship periods identified in coordination with temple and pagoda
    management committees.
•   Avoid routing construction traffic through internal access roads or pathways customarily used
    by worshippers where alternative routes are available.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Conduct pre-construction condition surveys of the temples and pagodas, including
    photographic records and basic structural assessments, to document baseline conditions and
    identify vulnerable elements such as old walls, columns or decorative features.
•   Adopt construction methods that minimize vibration and ground movement near these
    structures, including staged excavation, controlled compaction and use of low-vibration
    equipment.
•   Install physical separation measures, such as fencing, barriers and buffer zones, to clearly
    delineate construction areas and protect temple and pagoda grounds from accidental
    encroachment.
•   Implement enhanced dust and noise control measures in the vicinity of the temples and
    pagodas, including frequent water spraying, covering of stockpiles and trucks, and limiting
    simultaneous operation of noisy equipment.
•   Maintain safe and dignified access to the temples and pagodas at all times by providing clearly
    marked temporary access routes, walkways or ramps where existing access is affected by
    construction.
•   Provide advance notice to temple and pagoda management and local communities regarding
    construction schedules, changes in access arrangements and anticipated disturbance periods.
•   Monitor vibration, visible cracking or other signs of structural stress at the temples and pagodas
    during nearby construction activities and adjust methods or sequencing if adverse effects are
    observed.
Remediation measures




                                                                                                  323
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
•   In the event of cracking, structural damage or other adverse impacts to the temples or pagodas
    attributable to construction activities, immediately suspend the relevant works and investigate
    the cause in coordination with qualified engineers and heritage specialists where appropriate.
•   Implement prompt repair, strengthening or restoration measures for affected structures,
    ensuring that materials and methods are compatible with the original architectural and cultural
    characteristics.
•   Restore any disturbed temple or pagoda grounds, pathways or boundary features to pre-
    construction condition upon completion of nearby works.
Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on the use, integrity or setting of the temples and pagodas cannot be
    fully avoided despite mitigation measures, provide appropriate support in coordination with
    local authorities and religious management committees, such as contributions to site
    maintenance, minor restoration works or improvement of access facilities.
• Maintain ongoing consultation with temple and pagoda representatives and local authorities to
    address concerns promptly and ensure that cultural and religious practices are respected
    throughout the construction period.
5.1.3.3.6.     Mitigation measures for impacts on sensitive receptors
(a) Re Market – An Duong Ward
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid locating construction access points, material stockpiles, machinery parking areas or
    temporary facilities directly in front of Re Market entrances and internal circulation areas.
•   Avoid scheduling high dust- and noise-generating construction activities during the main
    operating hours of Re Market, from early morning to late afternoon.
•   Avoid routing construction vehicles through market access roads and areas intensively used by
    vendors and customers where alternative routes are available.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain continuous and safe access to Re Market by clearly marking temporary pedestrian
    and motorcycle access routes and ensuring that market entrances are not obstructed by
    construction activities.
•   Implement enhanced dust control measures within a radius of at least 50 m around Re Market,
    including frequent water spraying of active construction areas, exposed surfaces and access
    roads.
•   Ensure all trucks transporting soil and materials near the market are covered and cleaned to
    prevent dust deposition affecting vendors, customers and food hygiene conditions.
•   Apply noise control measures near the market by limiting simultaneous operation of noisy
    equipment and scheduling unavoidable noisy activities outside peak trading hours.
•   Install clear warning signs, temporary barriers and traffic control measures near market access
    points to reduce traffic safety risks to vendors, customers and pedestrians.
•   Deploy trained personnel to manage construction traffic and pedestrian movements near Re
    Market during busy periods to minimize congestion and accident risks.
•   Maintain clean conditions around the market frontage by regularly removing mud, debris and
    spilled materials caused by construction activities.

    construction activities and restore them to pre-construction condition without delay.
Offset measures
•   Where temporary loss of trading convenience or reduced customer access results in economic
    impacts to market vendors despite mitigation measures, provide compensation or support in

    or festivals identified in coordination with pagoda management.

•   Implement dust control measures near Bao Quang Pagoda, including regular water spraying of
    construction areas and access roads, to reduce dust intrusion into the open-fronted worship
    space.
•   Apply noise management measures by limiting operation of noisy equipment near the pagoda
    and scheduling unavoidable noisy works outside prayer and meditation periods.
•   Install physical barriers, fencing and warning signs to clearly separate construction areas from
    pagoda grounds and prevent accidental encroachment.
•   Maintain safe and dignified access to the pagoda at all times by clearly marking temporary
    access routes if existing access is affected by construction activities.

    disturbance periods and any changes to access arrangements.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of dust, noise or vibration causing unacceptable disturbance to religious activities
    at Bao Quang Pagoda, promptly review and adjust construction methods, schedules or
    protective measures.

    construction activities to pre-construction condition after completion of nearby works.

    5.1.3.4.   Component 3 – Site-specific mitigation
5.1.3.4.1.     Mitigation measures for the Road surface disruption and traffic congestion.
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid simultaneous open-cut construction on multiple adjacent sections of the same street by
    strictly applying rolling construction, limiting active works to one construction front
    (maximum two in exceptional cases) per major street at any given time.
•   Avoid full carriageway occupation by optimizing trench alignment, shoring systems and
    material storage layouts so that the minimum possible road width is occupied, particularly on
    streets with carriageway widths of 10–15 m.
•   Avoid scheduling material transport by 10-ton trucks during urban peak hours (06:30–08:30
    and 16:30–18:30) in compliance with Hai Phong traffic regulations restricting vehicles over
    3.5 tons.
•   Avoid locating material stockpiles, excavated spoil or construction equipment at intersections,
    bus stops, market access points or in front of major roadside businesses where they would
    cause severe circulation bottlenecks.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Prepare and implement a site-specific temporary traffic management plan for each 150–200 m
    construction section, including lane allocation, traffic diversion, pedestrian routing, signage,
    barriers and lighting, approved by the local traffic police prior to construction.
    Install continuous, rigid fencing along excavation trenches occupying the carriageway or
    sidewalks, combined with reflective warning signs and night lighting, to clearly delineate
    construction zones and prevent vehicles or pedestrians from entering hazardous areas.
    Cover excavation trenches with steel plates or equivalent load-bearing covers after working
    hours, during rain events, and at locations where pedestrian crossings or vehicle access must
    be maintained.
•   Organize material transportation in off-peak periods only, with controlled daily truck
    movements (approximately 20–30 trips/day per 200 m section), and stagger truck arrivals to
    avoid queuing and conflicts at narrow road sections and intersections.
•   Designate fixed truck routes for material and waste transport that avoid narrow streets, schools,
    markets and high pedestrian activity areas as far as practicable.
•   Deploy trained traffic controllers and safety personnel at narrowed road sections, intersections
    and access points during material loading/unloading and peak background traffic periods to
    manage conflicts between construction vehicles and local traffic.
•   Maintain road cleanliness by promptly removing spilled soil, mud and construction debris from
    carriageways and sidewalks, especially at the end of each working day and after rainfall.



                                                                                                 326
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•   Ensure safe pedestrian access along affected streets by providing clearly marked temporary
    walkways, protected crossings and ramps, particularly where sidewalks are occupied by
    construction works.
•   Coordinate construction schedules and access arrangements with roadside businesses and
    residents, providing advance notice of expected road occupation duration and access changes
    for each construction section.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of severe congestion, traffic incidents or safety hazards attributable to construction
    or material transportation, immediately review and adjust traffic management measures,
    including revising work sequencing, reducing active work width or increasing traffic control
    personnel.
•   Repair any damage to road surfaces, sidewalks, curbs or drainage structures caused by
    construction activities or heavy truck movements, restoring them to pre-construction condition
    promptly after completion of works in each section.
•   Remove all temporary traffic control installations, fencing and stockpiles as soon as a
    construction section is completed and reopen the full carriageway without delay.
Offset measures
•   Where temporary access disruption results in demonstrable economic losses to roadside
    businesses despite mitigation measures, implement compensation or support measures in
    accordance with the approved Resettlement Action Plan.
• Maintain a clear grievance redress mechanism for road users, residents and business owners to
    report traffic, access or safety issues, and ensure timely response and corrective action
    throughout the construction period.
5.1.3.4.2.      Mitigation measures for restricted riverbank access and small-boat movement
                during construction of regulating sluices
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid extending cofferdams, exclusion zones and safety fencing beyond the minimum
    footprint required for foundation and structural works at the Tam Bac and Thuy Doi regulating
    sluices.
•   Avoid simultaneous restriction of both riverbanks at the same cross-section where practicable,
    maintaining at least one side with limited access for small boats outside active construction
    zones.
•   Avoid blocking informal landing and mooring points outside the immediate cofferdam and
    fenced areas, particularly along riverbank sections not directly affected by sluice construction.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain a continuous navigable water corridor during construction by ensuring that remaining
    channel width and depth are sufficient for small boats and canoes to pass safely outside
    exclusion zones.
•   Clearly demarcate cofferdams, exclusion zones and restricted areas on the water using floating
    buoys, reflective markers and warning signage visible both day and night.




                                                                                                   327
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
•   Provide advance notification to local residents who use small boats, informing them of
    construction schedules, restricted areas, safe navigation routes and expected duration of access
    limitations at each sluice location.
•   Establish designated temporary mooring and landing points outside fenced construction zones,
    where feasible, to allow local residents to safely embark, disembark and secure small boats
    during construction.
•   Ensure fencing along riverbank sewer construction sections is arranged to minimize the
    number of permanently blocked access points and includes controlled openings where safe and
    feasible.
•   Coordinate construction activities with local authorities and community representatives to
    address access needs and adjust arrangements if significant inconvenience is reported.
Remediation measures
•   In the event that access restrictions or cofferdam arrangements cause unsafe conditions for
    small boats or riverbank users, promptly review and modify exclusion zone layouts, signage
    or navigation guidance.
•   Restore affected riverbank access points, landing areas and informal mooring locations
    immediately after completion of works at each sluice and along sewer construction sections.
•   Remove all temporary waterway markers, fencing and construction-related obstructions from
    the river channel and banks once construction activities are completed.
Offset measures
•   Where temporary loss of riverbank access or mooring causes inconvenience or minor
    livelihood impacts to local residents despite mitigation measures, provide appropriate support
    or compensation in accordance with the approved Resettlement Action Plan and local
    regulations.
• Maintain ongoing communication with affected residents throughout the construction period
    to receive feedback and implement additional practical measures if required to minimize
    residual impacts.
5.1.3.4.3.      Mitigation measures for risk of damage to underground utilities during drilling
                and subsurface works
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid commencing excavation or drilling works until all available utility records, as-built
    drawings and relocation requirements have been reviewed and verified in coordination with
    relevant utility operators.
•   Avoid alignment adjustments or changes to trench depth during construction without prior
    confirmation of utility locations and approval from the supervising engineer and utility
    authorities.
•   Avoid using heavy mechanical excavation directly above suspected utility corridors until their
    exact position and depth have been confirmed by field investigation.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Carry out comprehensive utility surveys prior to construction, including review of utility maps,
    field reconnaissance, ground-penetrating radar (GPR) surveys and trial pits at high-risk



                                                                                                328
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    locations, particularly along Tam Bac, Nguyen Thai Hoc, Tran Nguyen Han and Hai Ba Trung
    streets.
•   Clearly mark the confirmed locations and depths of underground utilities on the ground and
    construction drawings, and maintain these markings throughout the construction period.
    Adopt controlled excavation methods near identified utilities, including manual excavation,
    vacuum excavation or reduced-depth mechanical excavation, to minimize the risk of direct
    damage.
•   Implement step-by-step excavation and pipe installation procedures, with continuous
    supervision by qualified engineers, especially in sections with dense or overlapping utilities.
•   Coordinate construction schedules and methods with utility operators, ensuring their presence
    or on-call support during high-risk activities such as excavation near live power cables or water
    mains.
•   Provide protective measures for exposed utilities, such as temporary supports, casing or
    bridging, to prevent damage due to soil movement, vibration or accidental loading.
•   Train construction workers on utility identification, safe excavation practices and emergency
    response procedures for utility incidents.
•   Conduct formal consultation with relevant underground utility owners and operators (water
    supply, wastewater, electricity, telecommunications, gas) prior to drilling and subsurface
    works to obtain updated information on utility location, depth and condition.
•   Review available as-built drawings and utility maps and carry out joint site verification with
    utility operators where necessary to confirm the presence of underground infrastructure in
    sensitive sections.
•   Agree in advance with utility owners on protection measures, minimum clearance distances,
    temporary service arrangements and emergency response procedures before commencing
    works.
•   Provide advance notification to utility operators prior to drilling or subsurface works within
    their service corridors and coordinate site supervision where required.
•   Immediately inform the relevant utility operator in case of unexpected discovery or damage
    to underground utilities and suspend works until corrective measures are agreed and
    implemented.
•   Maintain up-to-date records of discovered or relocated utilities during construction and
    incorporate them into revised as-built drawings.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of accidental damage to any underground utility, immediately stop work in the
    affected area, secure the site and notify the relevant utility operator and local authorities.
    Implement temporary service continuity measures where feasible, such as bypass connections
    or temporary supplies, to minimize disruption to affected households, businesses and sensitive
    facilities.
•   Repair or replace damaged utilities promptly in accordance with technical standards and under
    the supervision of the responsible utility authority before resuming construction activities.
•   Restore affected road surfaces, sidewalks and service corridors to pre-construction condition
    following utility repair works.
Offset measures




                                                                                                 329
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•   Where unavoidable utility incidents result in temporary service interruption despite mitigation
    measures, provide timely notification and coordination with affected users and institutions,
    particularly hospitals, schools and other sensitive receptors.
• Where service disruption leads to demonstrable losses or inconvenience beyond normal
    construction impacts, implement compensation or support measures in accordance with
    applicable regulations and project agreements, in coordination with utility operators and local
    authorities.
5.1.3.4.4.       Mitigation measures for Risk of cracking/collapse or instability of surface
                 structures
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid deep open-cut excavation adjacent to buildings without prior installation of temporary
    excavation wall reinforcement where the trench edge is within 10 m of house foundations, in
    accordance with the FS requirement.
•   Avoid simultaneous excavation of long continuous sections by applying rolling construction,
    limiting active excavation length (typically about 150–200 m) and completing structural works
    before opening the next section.
•   Avoid rapid excavation, over-deepening or sudden removal of lateral support that could induce
    abrupt ground stress changes near adjacent structures.
•   Avoid conducting deep excavation during periods of prolonged heavy rainfall or high
    groundwater levels that increase the risk of trench instability and ground deformation.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Carry out detailed geotechnical investigations along streets with closely spaced buildings to
    confirm soil stratigraphy, groundwater conditions and appropriate temporary support solutions
    for each section.
•   Install appropriate trench support systems prior to excavation, such as steel sheet piles, soldier
    piles with lagging, braced shoring or equivalent systems, designed to limit lateral displacement
