    and settlement transmitted to adjacent buildings.
•   Adopt staged excavation and backfilling methods, excavating in short lifts and promptly
    constructing permanent structures and backfilling to restore ground support as early as
    practicable.
•   Control construction-induced vibration by selecting suitable equipment, limiting compaction
    energy near buildings and avoiding simultaneous operation of multiple heavy machines
    adjacent to sensitive façades.
•   Conduct pre-construction condition surveys of adjacent houses and shop-houses, including
    photographic documentation of existing cracks, tilting or structural defects, to establish
    baseline conditions.
•   Implement ground deformation and structural monitoring for buildings adjacent to deep
    excavations, including visual crack gauges or settlement markers, and review results regularly
    to detect early signs of distress.
•   Maintain clear separation between construction zones and building frontages using fencing and
    barriers to protect residents and prevent accidental loading or access to unstable areas.
Remediation measures




                                                                                                  330
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
•   If abnormal settlement, cracking or structural distress is observed in adjacent buildings during
    construction, immediately suspend excavation in the affected section and investigate the cause
    with qualified geotechnical and structural engineers.
•   Strengthen temporary support systems, adjust excavation sequencing or implement additional
    ground stabilization measures, such as strutting or local grouting, before resuming works.
•   Repair construction-induced damage to affected buildings, including crack repair, local
    underpinning or structural strengthening, as appropriate and under professional supervision.
•   Restore road surfaces, sidewalks and building access areas affected by remedial works to pre-
    construction condition.
Offset measures
•   Where residual damage or loss cannot be fully avoided despite mitigation and remediation
    measures, provide compensation or repair support to affected households in accordance with
    the approved Resettlement Action Plan and applicable regulations.
• Maintain transparent communication with affected residents regarding construction schedules,
    identified risks, monitoring results and available grievance mechanisms to address concerns
    promptly.
5.1.3.4.5.      Mitigation measures for impacts on aquatic life and water quality of Tam Bac
                River during construction
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid complete blockage of the Tam Bac River during construction by maintaining sufficient
    open channel width and flow capacity outside cofferdam areas, in accordance with the
    approved construction method.
•   Avoid simultaneous high-disturbance in-river works at both Tam Bac and Thuy Doi sluices
    during periods of low dry-season flow to limit cumulative turbidity and sediment
    accumulation.
•   Avoid locating temporary soil/sediment stockpiles, construction materials and worker facilities
    directly on the riverbank or within areas prone to runoff entering the river.
•   Avoid refuelling, equipment maintenance and storage of fuel, oil or hazardous materials within
    the river channel or immediate riverbank zone.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Install and maintain cofferdams and Larsen sheet piles with adequate embedment depth and
    sealing to minimize seepage, sediment leakage and collapse during excavation and structural
    works.
•   Limit the footprint and duration of in-river excavation by applying staged construction and
    completing foundation works promptly, followed by early stabilization of cofferdam interiors.
•   Implement effective sediment and erosion control measures along riverbanks and work
    platforms, including silt fences, geotextiles, toe protection and stabilized temporary drainage,
    to intercept sediment-laden runoff before it enters the river.
•   Provide localized turbidity control near active works, such as silt curtains or equivalent
    measures, particularly during near-bank excavation and sheet piling in slow-flow conditions.
•   Collect and treat construction wastewater, dewatering effluent and stormwater runoff from
    work areas and stockpiles through sedimentation or settling systems prior to discharge,
    ensuring no untreated effluent enters the river.


                                                                                                331
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Manage temporary stockpiles of excavated soil and sediment by covering, compacting and
    providing perimeter drainage and bunds to prevent wash-off during rainfall events.
•   Ensure all construction equipment operating within or near the river is well maintained and
    free from oil and fuel leaks, with spill kits and absorbent materials readily available on-site.
•   Prohibit discharge of concrete wash water, mortar residues, packaging waste and domestic
    wastewater from workers into the river, and provide adequate collection and disposal facilities
    away from the riverbank.
•   Monitor visual turbidity and sediment plume extent during high-risk activities and after heavy
    rainfall, and adjust construction sequencing or control measures if excessive spreading beyond
    the near-field zone is observed.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of excessive turbidity, sediment deposition, oil spill or other pollution incidents
    affecting the Tam Bac River, immediately suspend the relevant construction activities, contain
    the source and implement clean-up measures in accordance with the approved emergency
    response plan.
•   Remove unintended sediment accumulations around cofferdams or near-bank areas where they
    adversely affect local water quality or aquatic habitats, in coordination with relevant
    authorities.
•   Rehabilitate disturbed riverbank sections and cofferdam footprints after completion of works,
    including regrading, stabilization and removal of temporary structures, to prevent ongoing
    erosion and sediment release.
Offset measures
•   Where residual short-term impacts on aquatic habitats cannot be fully avoided despite
    mitigation measures, support compensatory actions in coordination with local authorities, such
    as riverbank vegetation restoration or habitat improvement at agreed locations along the Tam
    Bac River.
• Provide timely information to local communities and river users regarding construction
    schedules and expected periods of increased turbidity to allow temporary adjustment of
    activities.
5.1.3.4.6.      Mitigation measures for impacts on Gold Star International General Hospital
                and Viet Tiep Friendship Hospital
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid full obstruction or prolonged narrowing of the hospital entrance frontage by applying
    staged, short-section construction in front of each hospital gate, ensuring that only the
    minimum necessary length of curbside lane is occupied at any time.
    Avoid scheduling high-noise and high-dust activities, such as pavement breaking, concrete
    cutting, vibratory compaction and intensive truck movements, during hospital peak access
    hours (06:00–08:30 and 16:30–18:30), especially at Viet Tiep Friendship Hospital.
    Avoid routing heavy construction trucks past hospital gates during peak hours, in compliance
    with Hai Phong regulations restricting trucks over 3.5 tons, and where possible divert truck
    routes away from hospital entrance areas.
Minimization and mitigation measures



                                                                                                332
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Maintain uninterrupted emergency access to both hospitals at all times by providing a clearly
    marked priority lane for ambulances and emergency vehicles in front of hospital entrances,
    with physical separation from construction zones.
•   Provide dedicated and clearly signed temporary drop-off and pick-up areas for patients, taxis
    and private cars near hospital gates, and ensure these areas remain accessible throughout
    construction.
•   Prepare and implement a hospital-specific traffic management plan for each entrance area,
    including lane allocation, temporary markings, signage, traffic controllers and coordination
    with hospital security staff.
•   Install continuous rigid fencing and solid dust screens along the construction boundary facing
    hospital entrances to reduce dust dispersion and improve safety separation between
    construction works and hospital activities.
•   Implement enhanced dust control measures at hospital frontages, including frequent water
    spraying of excavation areas, access roads and stockpiles; covering of trucks; and prompt
    removal of excavated materials.
•   Apply stricter noise control measures in front of hospital gates, including use of low-noise
    equipment where feasible, avoidance of simultaneous operation of multiple noisy machines,
    and temporary noise barriers if required to reduce noise exposure at reception and waiting
    areas.
•   Deploy trained traffic controllers and safety personnel at hospital entrances during working
    hours to manage conflicts between construction vehicles, background traffic and hospital
    access traffic.
•   Ensure adequate lighting, visibility and cleanliness at hospital access points, particularly during
    early morning, evening and night-time periods.
•   Establish regular coordination with hospital management to provide advance notice of
    construction schedules, upcoming high-disturbance activities and any temporary changes to
    access arrangements.
Remediation measures
•   In the event that construction activities cause unacceptable noise, dust or access disruption
    affecting hospital operations, immediately review and adjust construction methods, schedules
    or control measures in consultation with hospital representatives.
•   Promptly repair any damage to hospital access roads, pavements, curbs or entrance facilities
    caused by construction activities and restore them to pre-construction condition without delay.
•   Where monitoring or complaints indicate exceedance of applicable noise or air quality
    standards at hospital boundaries, implement additional temporary controls, such as enhanced
    screening, rescheduling of activities or suspension of specific works until conditions are
    improved.
Offset measures
•   Where residual impacts on hospital operations cannot be fully avoided despite mitigation
    measures, provide additional temporary support in coordination with hospital management,
    such as extended construction working hours outside sensitive periods to shorten the overall
    duration of disruption.
•   Maintain a dedicated communication and grievance channel with hospital management to
    promptly address access, safety or environmental concerns throughout the construction period.


                                                                                                   333
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
5.1.3.4.7.     Mitigation measures for Impacts on sensitive receptors
A. Nguyen Tri Phuong Primary School – Nguyen Tri Phuong Street
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid scheduling high-dust and high-noise activities, including pavement breaking, deep
    excavation, culvert installation and intensive truck movements, during school hours from 07:00
    to 17:00, particularly during arrival and dismissal periods.
•   Avoid routing construction trucks and material transport past the school gate during peak
    school access times in the morning and afternoon where alternative routes are available.
•   Avoid placing construction access points, stockpiles or equipment parking areas directly in
    front of the school entrance or along pedestrian access routes used by students.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain continuous and safe access to the school by clearly marking and protecting pedestrian
    routes for students, parents and teachers, and ensuring that temporary road narrowing does not
    block school gates.
•   Implement enhanced dust control measures within a radius of at least 50 m around the school,
    including frequent water spraying of excavation areas, exposed surfaces and access roads, and
    covering of trucks transporting materials.
•   Apply strict noise management measures near the school, including limiting simultaneous
    operation of noisy equipment, using well-maintained machinery, and scheduling unavoidable
    noisy activities outside teaching hours as far as practicable.
•   Install continuous fencing, warning signs and night lighting along construction boundaries near
    the school to prevent students from entering hazardous areas.
•   Deploy trained traffic controllers at the school frontage during arrival and dismissal times to
    manage interactions between construction vehicles, background traffic and pedestrians.
•   Coordinate construction schedules and access arrangements in advance with the school
    administration and local authorities.
Remediation measures
•   In the event of excessive dust, noise or access disruption affecting teaching and learning
    activities, promptly review construction methods and schedules and implement corrective
    measures to restore acceptable conditions.
•   Repair any damage to school access roads, sidewalks or frontage areas caused by construction
    activities and restore them to pre-construction condition without delay.
Offset measures
•   Where residual disturbance to school activities cannot be fully avoided despite mitigation
    measures, provide additional temporary support in coordination with the school, such as
    adjusted construction schedules or enhanced safety supervision during critical periods.
•   Maintain a clear communication and grievance channel with school representatives to address
    concerns promptly throughout construction.
B. Religious and spiritual structures



                                                                                               334
                                                                                               Official Use Only
(Our Lady of Carmel Church, Tam Ky Temple and Tam Bac Pagoda)
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid scheduling disruptive construction activities during major religious services,
    ceremonies, festivals and peak worship days, including Sundays, lunar 1st and 15th days, and
    major Catholic and traditional festivals, as identified in coordination with religious
    management committees.
•   Avoid locating construction access points, material stockpiles, machinery parking or worker
    facilities adjacent to main gates, courtyards or worship spaces of the church, temple and
    pagoda.
•   Avoid routing construction vehicles through access paths commonly used by worshippers
    where alternative routes are available.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement dust control measures around religious structures, including regular water spraying
    of construction areas and access roads, covering of stockpiles and trucks, and prompt removal
    of excavated materials.
•   Apply noise control measures near religious structures by limiting operation of noisy
    equipment, avoiding simultaneous use of multiple heavy machines, and scheduling
    unavoidable noisy works outside prayer and worship periods.
•   Install physical barriers, fencing and clear warning signs to separate construction zones from
    religious premises and prevent accidental encroachment into worship areas.
•   Maintain safe and dignified access to the church, temple and pagoda at all times by clearly
    marking temporary access routes where existing access is affected by construction activities.
•   Provide advance notice to religious representatives and local communities regarding
    construction schedules, expected disturbance periods and any temporary changes to access
    arrangements.
Remediation measures
•   In the event that construction activities cause unacceptable dust, noise or access disruption
    affecting religious services or ceremonies, promptly review and adjust construction methods,
    schedules or protective measures.
•   Restore any disturbed courtyards, access paths, boundary walls or surrounding areas of the
    church, temple and pagoda to pre-construction condition after completion of nearby works.
Offset measures
•   Where residual disturbance to religious activities cannot be fully avoided despite mitigation
    measures, coordinate with religious management committees and local authorities to provide
    appropriate support measures, such as scheduling adjustments or minor improvements to
    access facilities.
•   Maintain ongoing consultation with representatives of the church, temple and pagoda to
    address concerns promptly and ensure respect for religious and cultural practices throughout
    the construction period.




                                                                                              335
                                                                                              Official Use Only
5.1.4.     Operation-phase mitigation measures
    5.1.4.1.   Mitigation Measures of componnent 1
5.1.4.1.1.     Mitigation measures for noise, emissions, and road safety from traffic growth
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid locating new sensitive land uses such as schools, clinics, kindergartens, and elderly-care
    facilities immediately adjacent to the carriageway during future land-use planning along Ring
    Road No. 3, particularly within the first 10–20 m from the road edge.
•   Avoid operating the road at design speeds in sections passing through dense residential areas,
    mixed-use frontage, and areas with frequent pedestrian crossings, where lower operating
    speeds are necessary to limit noise, emissions, and accident risk.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Apply appropriate operational speed limits by road function and surrounding land use,
    including reduced speed zones in residential stretches such as Lap Le, Trung Ha, Ngu Lao, and
    Group 10 areas, supported by fixed signage and road markings.
• Maintain smooth pavement conditions through routine inspection and maintenance to
    minimize tire–road noise and excessive vibration caused by pavement irregularities.
    Install and maintain clear horizontal and vertical traffic signage, road markings, lighting, and
    reflective devices at intersections, access points, and pedestrian crossing locations to reduce
    collision risk.
• Provide physical traffic-calming measures where appropriate, such as lane narrowing, median
    treatments, and channelization at intersections with QL10 and PR359, to control vehicle speed
    and improve driver awareness.
• Strengthen enforcement of axle-load limits and vehicle technical standards to reduce excessive
    noise, emissions, and vibration from overloaded or poorly maintained trucks.
• Coordinate with local authorities to enhance roadside vegetation and tree planting in
    appropriate sections as a supplementary noise and dust buffer, without obstructing visibility or
    road safety.
• Implement periodic monitoring of operational noise levels and ambient air quality at
    representative roadside receptors to confirm compliance with applicable standards and to
    support adaptive management if traffic conditions change.
5.1.4.1.2.      Mitigation measures for vibration due to heavy trucks
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid allowing heavy trucks to operate at high speeds on bridge approaches, rigid pavement
    sections, and areas with known sensitivity to vibration, particularly near closely spaced
    residential buildings.
•   Avoid introducing uneven pavement transitions, abrupt expansion joints, or poorly compacted
    repair works that could increase dynamic loads and vibration during vehicle passage.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain high pavement evenness standards through regular inspection and timely
    maintenance of rigid pavement slabs, expansion joints, and bridge deck transitions.
•   Apply and enforce operational speed limits for heavy trucks, particularly at bridge approaches
    and sections with nearby residential structures.


                                                                                                336
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Coordinate with traffic police and transport authorities to enforce axle-load regulations and
    prevent overloaded trucks, which are a primary source of excessive vibration.
• Conduct periodic visual inspection of nearby residential structures in sections with high heavy-
    truck traffic to identify any emerging vibration-related nuisance issues and address them
    through maintenance or operational adjustments.
5.1.4.1.3.      Mitigation measures for safety risks for waterway navigation at Ngo Quyen
                Bridge
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid placing any permanent auxiliary structures, cables, lighting fixtures, or maintenance
    equipment within the designated navigation clearance envelope approved for Ngo Quyen
    Bridge.
•   Avoid changes to navigation alignment, pier geometry, or river training works that would
    reduce the effective navigation width or create unexpected flow patterns near the bridge piers.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Install permanent navigation aids at the bridge location, including illuminated navigation
    lights, pier markings, clearance boards, and reflective devices in accordance with requirements
    of the Viet Nam Maritime Administration and inland waterway authorities.
• Provide clear daytime and nighttime signage indicating navigation spans, horizontal clearance,
    and vertical clearance to assist vessel operators, particularly under reduced visibility
    conditions.
• Maintain unobstructed sightlines to navigation openings through regular inspection and
    maintenance of bridge lighting, markings, and surrounding structures.
• Coordinate with maritime and inland waterway authorities to integrate Ngo Quyen Bridge into
    existing vessel traffic management, navigation notices, and pilotage procedures applied along
    the Cam River.
• Establish routine inspection and maintenance programs for bridge piers, fenders (if any), and
    protective elements to ensure long-term structural integrity and navigational safety.
• Implement emergency response and incident management procedures for vessel allision or
    near-miss events, including communication protocols with port authorities, river traffic
    control, and emergency services.
5.1.4.1.4.       Mitigation measures for impact on the protective function of the protection forest
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid expansion of permanent structural footprints beyond the approved abutment and
    approach-pier boundaries within the protection forest area.
•   Avoid hard paving, concrete sealing, or permanent storage of materials in areas beneath and
    adjacent to the bridge deck that are designated for forest regeneration
•   Avoid alteration of riverbank alignment, toe protection geometry, or flow-training structures
    that could increase flow concentration or wave impact on remaining forest edges.

    Minimization and mitigation measures




                                                                                               337
                                                                                               Official Use Only
•   Implement assisted natural regeneration and supplementary planting of Sonneratia caseolaris
    and other suitable native mangrove species within all acquired areas not permanently occupied
    by bridge structures.
• Stabilize exposed riverbank sections adjacent to abutments using bioengineering solutions (e.g.
    planted revetments, geotextile-reinforced soil with vegetation cover) to compensate for
    localized loss of forest protection function.
• Establish a vegetation management and maintenance plan for the regenerated forest belt,
    including replanting of failed seedlings, control of invasive species, and protection against
    trampling or unauthorized use.
• Monitor bank erosion, vegetation recovery, and canopy continuity at the abutment areas during
    the first 5–7 years of operation, with corrective measures implemented if localized erosion or
    vegetation loss is observed.
• Coordinate with local forestry authorities to integrate the regenerated areas into the official
    protection-forest management regime and routine inspection programs.
5.1.4.1.5.      Mitigation measures for impacts related to community and production-area
                severance
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid closure or removal of existing local access roads, farm tracks, and customary crossing
    points unless equivalent or improved alternatives are provided.
•   Avoid long spacing between formal crossings in residential and agricultural sections where
    daily cross-road movement is frequent.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Provide adequately spaced grade-separated crossings, including pedestrian underpasses,
    agricultural underpasses, and service roads, at locations identified as high-demand crossing
    points in residential and farming areas.
• Design underpasses and crossings with sufficient width, headroom, lighting, and drainage to
    safely accommodate pedestrians, bicycles, motorcycles, and small agricultural vehicles.
• Install continuous roadside fencing and median barriers in severance-prone sections to
    discourage unsafe informal crossings, while clearly guiding users toward designated crossing
    points.
• Provide clear, durable signage indicating the locations of nearest crossings, particularly near
    residential clusters, schools, and agricultural access points.
• Implement traffic-calming measures near crossing facilities, such as speed-limit enforcement,
    warning signs, rumble strips, and high-visibility road markings.
• Monitor crossing usage and accident data during operation, and retrofit additional crossings or
    safety features if informal crossing behavior persists.
5.1.4.1.6.      Mitigation measures for Impacts on irrigation and water supply for agricultural
                land along the alignment
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid blocking, downsizing, or misaligning existing irrigation canals and field ditches during
    operation through improper maintenance or modification of drainage structures.




                                                                                              338
                                                                                              Official Use Only
•   Avoid changes in road drainage operation that divert runoff away from established agricultural
    water-supply routes without compensation.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain all cross-culverts, box culverts, and canal crossings in a fully functional condition
    through regular inspection, desilting, and debris removal, particularly before and during peak
    irrigation seasons.
• Ensure that culvert inverts, slopes, and hydraulic capacity remain consistent with original
    design parameters and pre-project canal profiles.
• Coordinate routine operation and maintenance activities with local irrigation management
    units and commune authorities to promptly address blockages or hydraulic mismatches.
• Establish a grievance and rapid-response mechanism for farmers to report irrigation or
    drainage problems related to the road, with defined timelines for inspection and corrective
    action.
• Implement periodic post-operation monitoring during the first 2–3 years to verify that
    irrigation and drainage performance across the road is comparable to baseline conditions, with
    targeted remedial works if localized waterlogging or water shortage is detected.
5.1.4.1.7.      Mitigation measures for risk of riverbank erosion, pier scour, and flow regime
                alteration
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid any post-construction modification to pier geometry, abutment alignment, or river-
    training works that could reduce effective flow conveyance or alter local flow concentration
    patterns around the bridge structures.
•   Avoid removal, thinning, or degradation of designed scour-protection layers (riprap, stone
    mattress, revetment systems) during routine inspection or maintenance activities.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement periodic underwater and bank-condition inspections at bridge piers and abutments,
    particularly after major flood events, to verify scour depth, integrity of riprap, and bank
    stability.
• Maintain scour protection systems (stone mattress, riprap, toe protection) in accordance with
    design specifications, with timely repair or reinforcement if displacement, settlement, or
    exposure of foundation elements is observed.
• Monitor riverbank conditions upstream and downstream of bridge structures for early signs of
    localized erosion or vegetation loss, and implement targeted bank stabilization where needed.
• Retain and manage riparian vegetation, including mangrove stands at the Ruot Lon Bridge, to
    support natural bank stabilization and dissipation of flow energy.
• Coordinate with river management and flood-control authorities to integrate bridge
    monitoring data into broader river maintenance and flood-risk management programs.
5.1.4.1.8.      Mitigation measures for flooding risks to nearby residential areas and
                agricultural land
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid obstruction, partial closure, or downsizing of designed cross-drainage structures, side
    drains, and outlets during operation and maintenance.


                                                                                              339
                                                                                              Official Use Only
•   Avoid changes to land use, access roads, or ancillary developments that could block or reduce
    the capacity of receiving canals and drainage paths connected to the road system.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Establish a routine inspection and maintenance program for all inlets, side drains, culverts, and
    outfalls, with increased frequency prior to and during the rainy season.
• Ensure timely removal of sediment, debris, vegetation, and solid waste from drainage
    structures to maintain full hydraulic capacity.
• Coordinate drainage operation and maintenance with local irrigation and canal-management
    authorities to ensure downstream conveyance capacity is not compromised.
• Monitor flood-prone residential and agricultural sections after heavy rainfall events and
    implement localized corrective measures (e.g. inlet enlargement, additional culverts, improved
    outlet protection) if recurring ponding is observed.
• Provide a reporting and response mechanism for local communities and farmers to notify
    authorities of drainage or flooding issues, with defined timelines for inspection and remedial
    action.
5.1.4.1.9.      Mitigation measures for accident and emergency risks
    Avoidance measures
•    Avoid uncontrolled access openings, informal crossings, or unauthorized median breaks that
    increase conflict between through traffic and local movements.
•    Avoid reduction or removal of safety features (median barriers, guardrails, lighting, signage)
    during maintenance or roadside activities.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain full functionality of road-safety infrastructure, including signage, pavement
    markings, lighting, guardrails, median barriers, and pedestrian facilities.
• Implement regular road safety audits during operation, focusing on high-risk sections near
    residential clusters, schools, healthcare facilities, intersections, and U-turn locations.
• Apply speed management measures at sensitive locations, including speed-limit enforcement,
    warning signage, and traffic-calming devices where appropriate.
• Ensure emergency lay-bys, shoulders, and access points are kept unobstructed to facilitate
    rapid response by ambulance, fire, and police services.
• Coordinate with traffic police and emergency services to establish and periodically review
    incident response procedures, communication protocols, and access arrangements along the
    corridor.
5.1.4.1.10.     Mitigation measures for operation and maintenance-related impacts
    Avoidance measures
•    Avoid scheduling maintenance works that require lane closures during peak traffic hours
    unless unavoidable for safety reasons.
•   Avoid prolonged occupation of carriageway or shoulders for maintenance activities beyond
    the minimum area and duration required.
    Minimization and mitigation measures



                                                                                                 340
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•   Plan routine maintenance activities during off-peak hours and, where practicable, at night to
    minimize traffic disruption and exposure of road users and workers.
•    Implement temporary traffic management measures for all maintenance works, including
    advance warning signs, lane delineation, speed reduction, and worker protection barriers.
•    Ensure maintenance vehicles are clearly marked and equipped with warning lights and
    reflective devices.
•   Control noise and dust from maintenance activities through proper equipment selection, work
    methods, and site housekeeping, particularly near residential areas.
•    Provide training for maintenance personnel on working safely under live traffic conditions
    and on emergency procedures in case of accidents or breakdowns.

    5.1.4.2.   Mitigation Measures of componnent 2
5.1.4.2.1.     Noise, emissions, and road safety from traffic growth
Avoidance measures
•    Avoid permitting heavy trucks, freight vehicles, or non-essential through-traffic to use the
    management road under normal operating conditions.
•   Avoid upgrading the management road to a standard that would encourage higher travel speeds
    or attract regional traffic.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Apply permanent speed limits (≤40 km/h) along the management road, with additional speed-
    reduction signage near residential clusters, access paths, and riverbank activity areas.
• Install clear warning signage for mixed traffic conditions, including motorcycles, bicycles,
    pedestrians, and agricultural users, particularly at access points and curves.
• Maintain smooth pavement conditions through routine inspection and timely repair to
    minimize traffic-induced noise and vibration.
• Restrict maintenance and inspection vehicle movements to daytime hours where practicable
    to limit noise disturbance to nearby households.
• Promote the use of low-emission vehicles for routine inspection and maintenance activities
    along the embankment road.
5.1.4.2.2.      Localised flooding caused by embankment and management road blocking
                natural drainage
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid permanent blockage, narrowing, or realignment of designed cross-drainage culverts and
    canal connections beneath the management road.
•   Avoid any post-construction land-use changes or informal developments that obstruct drainage
    outlets, canals, or side drains discharging to the Re River.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement a routine inspection and maintenance program for cross-culverts, side drains, and
    outlet structures, with increased frequency before and during the rainy season.
•   Ensure timely removal of sediment, vegetation, and debris from drainage structures to maintain
    full hydraulic capacity.



                                                                                              341
                                                                                              Official Use Only
•   Coordinate with local irrigation and drainage authorities to manage canal maintenance and
    ensure downstream conveyance capacity is preserved.
• Monitor low-lying agricultural sections for recurrent ponding after heavy rainfall events and
    implement localized corrective actions (e.g. inlet cleaning, culvert extension, or minor channel
    improvement) where needed.
• Establish a community reporting mechanism to allow farmers and residents to promptly notify
    authorities of drainage or waterlogging problems along the embankment.
5.1.4.2.3.      Risks of falling into the Re River
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid leaving unprotected edges or abrupt drop-offs along sections where the management
    road runs immediately adjacent to steep slopes or vertical revetments.
•   Avoid removal or deterioration of edge-protection features (guardrails, parapets, barriers)
    during routine operation or maintenance.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Maintain continuous physical barriers (guardrails, parapets, or equivalent edge protection) at
    sections with steep or vertical riverbank profiles.
• Provide non-slip surface treatment and adequate cross-fall on the management road,
    particularly in areas prone to wet conditions.
• Install warning signage at locations with higher fall risk, including informal access points,
    fishing areas, and bends with limited visibility.
• Ensure adequate lighting at critical sections used by local residents and maintenance staff
    during early morning or evening hours.
• Conduct regular inspections of safety features and promptly repair damaged or missing
    barriers to maintain user protection.
5.1.4.2.4.      Impact on raw water quality at Quan Vinh pumping station during operation
Avoidance measures
•   Avoid any direct discharge of untreated wastewater, maintenance effluent, or contaminated
    runoff into the Re River upstream or near the Quan Vinh intake.
•   Avoid operational modifications that reduce near-bank flow capacity or restrict access for
    inspection and maintenance at the intake zone.
Minimization and mitigation measures
•    Implement routine inspection, cleaning, and maintenance of the wastewater collection and
    conveyance system to prevent leakage, blockage, or surcharge.
•    Establish rapid-response procedures for detecting and repairing sewer failures, with
    immediate containment and clean-up measures to protect river water quality near the intake.
•    Monitor sediment accumulation and flow conditions near the intake area and carry out
    localized dredging or sediment removal if excessive deposition is observed.
•   Coordinate regularly with the water supply operator to exchange information on river
    conditions, intake performance, and any observed changes in raw water quality.
•   Maintain access to the intake zone for inspection and emergency intervention, ensuring that
    embankment structures do not impede operational management of the pumping station.



                                                                                                342
                                                                                                Official Use Only
5.1.4.2.5.     Blockages and wastewater overflow
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid operating the drainage and pumping system beyond its designed hydraulic capacity by
    preventing unauthorized connections or changes to inflow characteristics.
•   Avoid long-term accumulation of sediment and debris in sewers, inlets, screens, and wet wells
    through continuous operation without scheduled maintenance.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement a routine inspection and preventive maintenance program for drainage pipelines,
    inlets, trash screens, wet wells and pumping stations, with increased frequency during the rainy
    season.
• Carry out periodic desilting of sewers and chambers based on sediment accumulation rates
    observed during operation.
• Ensure standby pumps and backup power supply (generators) at pumping stations are
    maintained in operable condition and tested regularly.
• Establish emergency response procedures for system surcharge or overflow events, including
    rapid removal of blockages, temporary pumping, and on-site containment of overflowed
    wastewater.
• Coordinate with local authorities and communities to promptly report unusual flooding,
    surcharge, or odour incidents linked to drainage malfunction.
• Restore affected areas (roads, agricultural plots, residential frontage) promptly after overflow
    events through cleaning, disinfection where necessary, and reinstatement of drainage function.
5.1.4.2.6.       Sludge, chemicals, hazardous waste, odour and noise from An Duong 1 WWTP
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid open storage, uncontrolled handling, or on-site disposal of sludge, chemicals, or
    hazardous waste within the WWTP premises.
•   Avoid operating odour-generating units (screens, grit chambers, sludge handling areas)
    without functional odour collection and treatment systems.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•    Operate sludge thickening and dewatering systems within design parameters to minimise
    sludge retention time and prevent anaerobic conditions.
•   Transport dewatered sludge off-site to licensed treatment or disposal facilities in sealed
    vehicles, following approved sludge management plans.
•    Store treatment chemicals and hazardous waste in dedicated, bunded, and clearly labelled
    storage areas with spill containment and access control.
•    Implement standard operating procedures for chemical handling, sludge management, and
    hazardous-waste collection, including staff training and use of personal protective equipment.
•   Operate enclosed process units and maintain the odour collection and treatment system
    (biofilter–activated carbon or equivalent) to control H₂S, NH₃ and other odorous compounds
    before discharge to the atmosphere.



                                                                                                343
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•    Conduct regular inspection and maintenance of blowers, pumps, screens and mechanical
    equipment to minimise abnormal noise generation.
• Monitor odour and noise levels within and at the boundary of the WWTP and address
    complaints through operational adjustment, maintenance or targeted mitigation measures.
5.1.4.2.7.    Odour emissions and noise at separation chambers and pumping stations
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid prolonged wastewater retention in separation chambers and pumping-station wet wells
    by preventing hydraulic bottlenecks and operational delays.
•   Avoid installation or operation of pumping stations without covers, ventilation control, or
    noise-dampening measures.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•    Ensure separation chambers and pumping stations are fully covered and designed to minimise
    air exchange with the surrounding environment.
• Implement routine cleaning and flushing of chambers and wet wells to reduce sediment build-
    up and anaerobic conditions.
• Maintain ventilation systems, seals and covers in good condition to prevent uncontrolled
    odour release.
• Operate pumps and electrical equipment within design limits and maintain noise control
    features, including enclosure panels and vibration isolation.
• Schedule major maintenance works during daytime hours where feasible to reduce nuisance to
    nearby residents and road users.
• Respond promptly to odour or noise complaints by inspecting equipment, adjusting operation,
    and implementing corrective maintenance actions.
5.1.4.2.8.      Effects on the receiving Water Body (Lach Tray River)
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid routine or intentional discharge of untreated or partially treated wastewater to the Lach
    Tray River under all normal operating conditions.
•   Avoid operation of the WWTP without a valid environmental permit, completed trial
    operation, and demonstrated compliance with QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT (Column A, K = 1).
•   Avoid locating any additional unplanned discharge points or emergency bypasses outside the
    approved outfall and monitoring locations.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Operate the An Duong 1 WWTP strictly within its approved hydraulic and treatment capacity,
    including proper control of inflow during storm events to prevent hydraulic overload.
•   Maintain continuous operation of the complete biological treatment line, including aeration,
    secondary clarification and final disinfection, to ensure stable effluent quality under variable
    loading conditions.
•    Ensure the online automatic effluent monitoring system (flow, BOD₅/COD surrogate, NH₄⁺–
    N, TSS) is fully functional at all times and continuously transmits data to the Hai Phong
    Department of Agriculture and Environment in accordance with permit requirements.




                                                                                                344
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•    Implement a preventive maintenance and redundancy program for critical equipment
    (blowers, pumps, power supply, control systems), including standby units and backup power
    generators, to minimise the probability of prolonged treatment failure.
• Establish and implement an emergency response procedure for abnormal discharge events,
    including immediate flow reduction, diversion to available balancing or retention capacity,
    rapid repair of failed units, and notification of relevant authorities.
• Conduct periodic monitoring of water quality upstream and downstream of the discharge point
    on the Lach Tray River, focusing on BOD₅, COD, NH₄⁺–N, DO and TSS, to verify actual
    mixing and recovery behaviour under operational conditions.
• Restrict sludge handling, chemical storage and maintenance activities to designated areas with
    full containment to prevent secondary contamination of the effluent or accidental release to the
    river.
• In the event of an accidental discharge of untreated or partially treated wastewater, implement
    short-term mitigation actions such as temporary flow throttling, increased aeration in
    downstream reaches where feasible, and enhanced monitoring until river water quality returns
    to baseline conditions.
5.1.4.2.9.      Environmental incident risks
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid operation of the WWTP, pumping stations and separation chambers without fully
    functional monitoring, alarm and control systems for flow, power supply and key treatment
    parameters.
•    Avoid routine manual handling of sludge, chemicals or hazardous materials outside designated
    containment areas and approved operational procedures.
•   Avoid storage of chemicals, fuel or lubricants in locations without secondary containment,
    impermeable flooring and controlled drainage.
•    Avoid bypassing treatment units or discharging wastewater outside the approved outfall under
    normal operating conditions.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Operate a real-time monitoring and alarm system for critical parameters (flow, power supply,
    pump status, blower status, pH, COD/BOD surrogate, NH₄⁺), with automatic alerts to on-duty
    operators in case of abnormal conditions.
•   Provide redundancy for critical systems, including standby pumps, blowers and backup power
    generators, to minimise the likelihood and duration of off-spec discharge during equipment
    failure or power outages.
•    Implement a preventive maintenance programme for pumping stations, separation chambers
    and WWTP process units, including routine inspection, cleaning and desilting to reduce
    blockage and overflow risks.
•   Establish and regularly update an Environmental Incident Response Plan covering off-spec
    discharge, wastewater overflow, sludge spills and chemical spills, with clear roles, response
    steps, internal reporting lines and notification procedures to local authorities.
•    Provide spill containment kits (absorbents, neutralising agents, temporary barriers) at the
    WWTP, pumping stations and chemical storage areas, and train staff in their proper use.




                                                                                                345
                                                                                                Official Use Only
•   Manage sludge through a closed and controlled chain, including mechanical dewatering, sealed
    container storage, licensed transport and disposal, to prevent leakage during handling and
    transfer.
• Conduct periodic emergency drills simulating overflow, sludge leakage and chemical spill
    scenarios to verify response readiness and coordination among operators, contractors and local
    emergency services.
5.1.4.2.10.     Occupational health and safety (OHS) risks of An Duong WWTP and associated
                facilities
    Avoidance measures
•    Avoid entry into confined spaces (wet wells, separation chambers, tanks, enclosed channels)
    without formal confined-space entry permits, atmospheric testing and authorised supervision.
•   Avoid working near open tanks, channels or elevated platforms without installed guardrails,
    covers or fall-prevention systems.
•   Avoid manual handling of heavy sludge containers, equipment or chemical drums where
    mechanical lifting or handling systems are provided.
•    Avoid assignment of untrained or unauthorised personnel to operational, maintenance or
    emergency-response tasks involving wastewater, sludge or chemicals.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Implement and enforce detailed OHS procedures under the approved Labour Management
    Plan, including confined-space entry procedures, lockout/tagout for electrical and mechanical
    systems, and safe-work permits.
• Install fixed and portable gas detection devices (H₂S, NH₃, oxygen deficiency) at confined
    spaces and high-risk areas, with audible and visual alarms linked to the control room.
• Provide continuous ventilation or forced-air ventilation systems for wet wells, pumping
    stations and enclosed process units to prevent accumulation of toxic or explosive gases.
• Equip all operational and maintenance staff with appropriate personal protective equipment
    (PPE), including respiratory protection, gas monitors, chemical-resistant gloves, protective
    clothing, safety harnesses and fall-arrest systems where required.
• Ensure all open tanks, channels and basins are fitted with permanent guardrails, toe boards and
    non-slip walkways, and that covers are installed where routine access is not required.
• Provide regular OHS training and refresher courses for WWTP operators, pumping-station
    staff and contractors, focusing on confined-space hazards, fall prevention, chemical handling,
    biological exposure and emergency response.
• Establish on-site emergency preparedness measures, including rescue equipment for confined
    spaces, eyewash and safety showers, first-aid facilities, and clear communication protocols for
    medical emergencies.
• Conduct routine OHS inspections and safety audits of WWTP units, pumping stations and
    separation chambers to identify and correct unsafe conditions before incidents occur.
    5.1.4.3. Mitigation Measures of componnent 3
5.1.4.3.1.      Flooding risk and operational upset risks
    Avoidance measures




                                                                                               346
                                                                                               Official Use Only
•    Avoid operation of sluice gates and pumping stations without a validated operational rule
    curve linking rainfall intensity, basin water level, downstream tide level and pump availability.
•   Avoid reliance on single power supply or single pump unit for storm-event operation; ensure
    no single-point failure during peak drainage demand.
•   Avoid manual gate operation during storm–tide events except under predefined emergency
    procedures and authorised supervision.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Provide full redundancy for critical components, including standby pumps, dual power feeds
    and on-site emergency generators sized to operate essential pumping and gate-control systems
    during extended outages.
• Implement real-time monitoring of rainfall, basin water level, downstream tide level, pump
    status and gate position, integrated into a central control system with alarm thresholds and
    decision-support prompts.
• Adopt standard operating procedures (SOPs) for coincident storm–tide conditions, including
    predefined gate-opening sequences and pump dispatch rules to minimise operator error and
    response delay.
• Conduct routine inspection, testing and preventive maintenance of gates, hoists, pumps and
    backup power systems, with documented readiness checks before and during the rainy season.
• Establish an emergency response protocol for uncontrolled discharge or pump/gate failure,
    including communication with city drainage authorities and rapid mobilisation of mobile
    pumps where required.
5.1.4.3.2.     Water-level modification and ecological/bank-stability effects from sluice
               opening/closure
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid abrupt or unnecessary rapid gate opening/closure that would generate excessive local
    velocities or turbulence near sluice structures.
•   Avoid routine operation regimes that maintain prolonged extreme water-level differentials
    across the sluices under non-storm conditions.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Apply gradual gate-opening and closing rates under normal and near-normal conditions to limit
    sudden hydraulic disturbances and local bank stress.
• Restrict high-frequency gate cycling to storm-response periods only, based on operational
    thresholds defined in the FS.
• Regularly inspect revetments, toe protection and bed protection near sluices for early signs of
    erosion or instability, and repair promptly.
• Maintain existing bank protection and engineered edges near the sluices to ensure local
    hydraulic effects remain confined to designed areas.
5.1.4.3.3.      Water-quality deterioration risk due to retention/accumulation under low-flow or
                closed-gate conditions
    Avoidance measures




                                                                                                 347
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•    Avoid prolonged full closure of sluices during non-critical tidal periods when basin water
    levels and downstream conditions permit partial flushing.
•   Avoid accumulation of trash and sediment in upstream canals and box culverts that would
    further reduce effective flushing capacity.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•   Operate sluices and pumps periodically during low-flow periods to provide controlled flushing
    where water-level and tidal conditions allow.
• Implement routine monitoring of key water-quality parameters in the Tam Bac system (DO,
    pH, TSS, COD, NH₄⁺, and H₂S as an odour indicator) to provide early warning of deterioration
• Increase frequency of trash removal and desilting in upstream drainage lines and near sluice
    inlets to reduce organic accumulation and anaerobic conditions.
• Adjust operational schedules during hot seasons to reduce water residence time where feasible,
    especially following rainfall events.
5.1.4.3.4.       Localised operational noise, odour and solid-waste–related nuisance from
                 pumping station and drainage structures
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid installation or operation of uncovered trash racks, open sumps or poorly sealed access
    openings that allow direct odour escape.
•   Avoid routine operation of auxiliary equipment (ventilation fans, generators) during night-time
    except when required for storm response.
    Minimization and mitigation measures
•    Maintain enclosure and acoustic shielding of pumping-station structures, ventilation openings
    and electrical rooms to limit noise propagation.
• Operate submerged pumps and associated equipment in accordance with manufacturer
    specifications to minimise vibration and noise peaks.
• Implement regular trash removal, sediment cleaning and wet-well flushing after storm events
    to prevent odour build-up and solid-waste accumulation.
• Provide localised odour control measures (natural ventilation optimisation, periodic chemical
    dosing where necessary) at wet wells and enclosed drainage sections.
• Schedule intensive maintenance and cleaning activities during daytime hours to reduce
    nuisance to nearby residents and street users.
5.1.4.3.5.      Operational safety risks from mechanical and electrical systems at sluices and
                pumping station
    Avoidance measures
•   Avoid unrestricted public access to sluice structures, pump stations and electrical/mechanical
    zones through permanent fencing and controlled entry points.
•   Avoid maintenance or inspection work during live storm-operation conditions unless required
    for emergency response and formally authorised.
    Minimization and mitigation measures




                                                                                               348
                                                                                               Official Use Only
•    Install permanent physical barriers, warning signage and lockable access gates around all
    moving mechanical parts, gate hoists and electrical installations.
•   Implement detailed operational and maintenance procedures covering gate movement, pump
    start-up/shutdown, lockout–tagout, and wet-weather work.
•    Provide mandatory training and certification for operators and maintenance staff on
    mechanical, electrical and hydraulic hazards specific to sluice and pump operation.
•    Equip facilities with adequate lighting, non-slip surfaces, handrails and emergency stop
    systems to reduce accident risk during routine and emergency work.
•    Carry out regular safety inspections and audits of mechanical and electrical systems,
    particularly before the rainy season and after major storm events.
5.1.5.     Mitigation for induced impacts
    5.1.5.1. Mitigation measures for land-use change and urban expansion
     • Ensure strict enforcement of approved land-use plans and zoning regulations along Ring
Road No. 3, particularly in peri-urban sections vulnerable to induced development.
     • Coordinate with district and ward authorities to monitor spontaneous conversion of
agricultural and aquaculture land adjacent to the corridor and prevent unauthorized construction.
     • Control access points and roadside frontage connections during operation to avoid ribbon
development and uncontrolled roadside commercial expansion.
     • Integrate Ring Road No. 3 into the city’s broader urban development and transport
planning framework to ensure that future land-use changes are guided by statutory planning
decisions rather than ad hoc development pressure.
     • Disclose corridor management requirements to local authorities and communities to clarify
that the Project does not authorize automatic land-use conversion along the road alignment.
    5.1.5.2. Mitigation measures for increased wastewater and solid waste loads
     • Coordinate with the municipal water and sanitation authorities to align future wastewater
and drainage capacity upgrades with projected population growth in areas influenced by improved
accessibility.
     • Require new residential and commercial developments induced by improved access to
connect to approved sewerage systems and comply with applicable wastewater discharge
standards.
     • Strengthen routine inspection and maintenance of local drainage canals to prevent
blockage by solid waste, particularly in peri-urban transition areas.
     • Enhance solid waste collection coverage and frequency in developing roadside settlements
through coordination with local environmental service units.
     • Prohibit informal wastewater discharge and illegal dumping into canals, rivers and ponds
through local enforcement and community awareness measures.
    5.1.5.3. Mitigation measures for induced traffic growth and air quality impacts
     • Promote traffic management measures along Ring Road No. 3, including speed control,
lane discipline, and enforcement of vehicle emission standards.
     • Restrict heavy truck movements during peak hours in residential sections to limit
congestion, noise and air pollution exposure.



                                                                                             349
                                                                                             Official Use Only
     • Maintain roadside greenery and buffer strips where feasible to help reduce dust and
improve local microclimate conditions.
     • Encourage modal shift to public transport and non-motorized transport through integration
of Ring Road No. 3 with city-level transport planning.
     • Monitor traffic volumes and air quality trends in sensitive sections and adjust traffic
management measures as part of routine urban transport management.
    5.1.5.4. Mitigation measures for rising land values, speculation and economic
                displacement
     • Enforce approved land-use plans, zoning controls and construction permitting
requirements to prevent speculative and unauthorized land conversion along upgraded corridors.
     • Strengthen local monitoring of informal construction, particularly in flood-prone or
drainage-sensitive areas, to avoid safety and infrastructure conflicts.
     • Coordinate with district authorities to manage roadside development density and prevent
premature or fragmented urban expansion.
     • Promote transparent land-information disclosure and public communication to clarify that
infrastructure upgrading does not automatically authorize land-use conversion.
     • Integrate social housing and affordable housing considerations into local urban
development planning to mitigate indirect economic displacement of low-income households and
renters.
    5.1.5.5. Mitigation measures for in-migration and pressure on public services
     • Coordinate with local authorities to anticipate population growth trends in peri-urban areas
influenced by improved accessibility and flood protection.
     • Phase expansion of public services (schools, healthcare, water supply, sanitation and solid
waste services) in line with approved urban development plans.
     • Require new developments to demonstrate adequate service connections and capacity
before approval.
     • Strengthen community-based management and ward-level planning to support social
integration of in-migrants and reduce pressure on existing communities.
     • Monitor service demand in rapidly developing areas and adjust municipal investment
priorities accordingly.
    5.1.5.6. Mitigation measures for transformation of traditional livelihoods
     • Support voluntary livelihood transition through access to vocational training, skills
development and local employment opportunities linked to urban and service-sector growth.
     • Encourage integration of traditional livelihood practices (e.g. small-scale aquaculture,
river-related activities) into compatible urban uses where feasible.
     • Prioritize livelihood support for vulnerable groups, including older residents and
households with limited adaptive capacity.
     • Promote community participation in local economic planning to ensure that livelihood
transitions are gradual and socially inclusive.
     • Avoid forced or abrupt displacement of livelihood activities through enforcement of
planned land-use change processes.


                                                                                               350
                                                                                               Official Use Only
    5.1.5.7. Mitigation measures for increased road-safety risks
     • Implement traffic management and safety measures at high-risk locations, including
intersections, bridge approaches and densely populated roadside sections.
     • Provide adequate pedestrian crossings, signage, speed-calming measures and lighting in
residential and mixed-use areas along Road No. 3.
     • Strengthen enforcement of traffic regulations, particularly speed limits and heavy-vehicle
controls in peri-urban communities.
     • Conduct road-safety awareness campaigns targeting residents, schools, and frequent road
users.
     • Monitor accident trends during operation and adjust traffic control measures as part of
routine road management.




5.1.6.      Mitigation for cumulative impacts
Cumulative impacts under the Project arise when multiple construction fronts operate
simultaneously, and when the Project overlaps with other city-led road, drainage, and embankment
works. The mitigation strategy therefore focuses on coordination, phasing, and city-wide
management rather than only site-specific measures. Measures below have been grouped into
Avoidance, Mitigation, and Restoration/Offset, in line with ESS1 and international good
practice.
                    Table 5.2. Mitigation Measures for Cumulative Impacts

5.2.    Institutional Arrangements & Responsibilities
        Institutional arrangements
    This section outlines the institutional framework, coordination mechanisms, and
responsibilities of all entities involved in the implementation of the Environmental and Social
Management Plan (ESMP) for the Hai Phong Project. The ESMP implementation structure follows
the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Framework (ESF), and integrates both government
management systems and project-specific compliance requirements.
    Mitigation measures included in the ESMP will be incorporated into the detailed design,
bidding documents, construction contracts, and supervision procedures. The Contractor is
responsible for the implementation of mitigation measures; the Construction Supervision
Consultant (CSC) carries out daily supervision; and the Independent Environmental and Social
Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) performs periodic compliance monitoring and provides capacity
support to the PMU.
    The organizational structure for managing and implementing environmental and social
safeguards during the pre-construction and construction phases of the project is shown in Figure
5.1

                                                                                                 Official Use Only
               Figure 5.1. Organizational chart of ESMP implementation
