5.3.     Environmental and Social Compliance Framework
5.3.1.     Environmental and Social Responsibility of the Hai Phong PMU and Detailed
           Design Consultants
During the preparation of TORs for consulting services and bidding documents for construction,
the PMU will also work closely with consultants to ensure that: i) contract package division and
cost estimates include the implementation of the ESMP, including independent monitoring
services, environmental sampling/monitoring and compliance monitoring, reporting, etc.; ii)
ESCOPs and relevant general as well as site-specific mitigation measures are incorporated into the
bidding documents; iii) environmental monitoring and included in the scope of work assigned to
the construction supervision consultant.
At the detailed design stage, the PMU will coordinate closely with detailed design engineers to
ensure that green/landscape, environmentally friendly solutions, and relevant mitigation measures
proposed in the ESIA/ESMP are reviewed and integrated into the technical design where
appropriate.
During construction, the PMU will work closely with the supervision consultant to monitor
contractors’ compliance and report to relevant authorities. The PMU will also instruct the
supervision consultant and contractors on actions to be taken in case of issues, incidents, or
accidents, etc.
The PMU will assign at least one qualified staff member as an Environmental Officer (EO) and at
least one qualified staff member as a Social Officer (SO) throughout project implementation.




                                                                                              358
                                                                                              Official Use Only
•   The EO will monitor environmental issues and oversee the component’s safety compliance.
    The EO will be supported by the independent environmental supervision consultant,
    environmental officers of the construction supervision team, as well as the contractors.
•   The SO will monitor and coordinate the social/resettlement aspects of the Project. The SO will
    be supported by the independent resettlement supervision consultant, social officers of the
    construction supervision team, as well as the contractors.
5.3.2.      Environmental Responsibility of Contractors

The contractor is responsible for implementing measures to control and mitigate adverse impacts
arising from construction activities. The mitigation measures specified in the ESMP must be fully
applied to prevent impacts on the local community and the environment throughout the
construction phase.
When remedial measures cannot be effectively implemented during construction, the contractor
must carry them out during the completion stage (and prior to issuing the completion acceptance
certificate).
The contractor’s responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following tasks:
    -    Comply with relevant legislative requirements on environmental management, community
         health, and safety.
    -    Perform the work within the scope of the contract requirements and other bidding clause
         sons
    -    Assign a representative of the construction team to participate in site supervision organized
         by the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC.
    -    Implement remedial actions under the instructions of the Environmental Officer (EO),
         Social Officer (SO) of the PMU, and the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC.
    -    In case of non-compliance or non-conformance, conduct investigation and propose
         mitigation measures, as well as implement corrective actions to minimize E&S impacts;
    -    Suspend construction activities when they generate adverse impacts beyond the guidance
         provided by the EO and ES. Propose and implement corrective activities or apply
         alternative construction methods, if necessary, to mitigate environmental impacts. Failure
         to comply may result in suspension of construction or other penalties until the issue is
         satisfactorily resolved as required by the EO of PMU and ES of CSC
    -    If the contractor proposes to use raw material sources not mentioned in the ESIA, the
         contractor shall report to the CSC and PMU and coordinate with them to conduct an
         environmental assessment of these sources to evaluate their compliance with national
         environmental requirements. Only compliant sources may be used under this project.
    -    The contractor shall be responsible for implementing remedial measures at its own cost.
         The contractor will also be liable for paying damages arising from non-compliance with
         the ESMP and/or applicable environmental regulations.
5.3.3.      Contractor’s Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)
After signing the contract, the contractor will be required to prepare and submit a Contractor’s


                                                                                                  359
                                                                                                  Official Use Only
Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) for each contract package and submit it
to the CSC and PMU for review and approval.
The objective of the C-ESMP is to provide guidance for environmental and social management of
proposed works/activities at the Project construction sites. This is to ensure that contractors – as
well as subcontractors (if any) – carry out their work with the minimum possible environmental
impacts. The C-ESMP will detail how the contractor will mitigate impacts from construction
activities and record the contractor’s responses for inspection, supervision, verification, internal
review, and corrective actions or improvements of environmental compliance conditions. The C-
ESMP must be site-specific and include details of control measures to be implemented on-site to
minimize any potential environmental impacts from proposed works/activities. If proposed
works/activities in the C-ESMP are modified in the Contract, the contractor shall revise the C-
ESMP to reflect such changes or modifications.
The contents of the C-ESMP must include the following:
(i) Applicable policies/regulations, providing a definition of the contractor’s environmental policy
and a statement of commitment to implementing the C-ESMP.
(ii) A brief description of the document; Date of issuance; Revision status; Distribution list; and
details of staffing and access.
(iii) Applicable laws and regulations related to the requirements in the Project ESMP.
Identification of contractor permits, licenses, and approvals relevant to the C-ESMP.
(iv) Details of how the environmental and social impacts identified in the ESIA will be managed
on-site, including: 1) specific measures to mitigate impacts during construction; 2) ESCOP; 3) the
Contractor’s ESMP to be developed after contractor selection and prior to construction
commencement; and 4) the Contractor’s Excavated Material Management Plan, which the
contractor is required to prepare.
(v) Detailed environmental and social training that all contractor staff (including subcontractors)
must undertake. At a minimum, all contractor personnel working at project sites must: i) be
familiar with and understand the C-ESMP for the works; ii) be aware of their environmental
responsibilities and legal obligations on-site; iii) comply with the workers’ Code of Conduct; and
iv) participate in health, safety, and emergency response training.
(vi) Specific capacities, support mechanisms, and resources necessary for adequate
implementation of the C-ESMP. The detailed environmental responsibilities of all contractor staff,
including subcontractors working on-site, with appropriate knowledge, skills, and training for
specific tasks, shall be defined.
(vii) The contractor shall be responsible for preparing monthly environmental–social reports, as
part of the Progress Report required in the bidding documents, including ad-hoc reports if any, for
submission to the project owner. The contents of these reports may include the following details:
   -   Implementation of the Contractor’s C-ESMP in compliance with the agreed program.
   -   Difficulties encountered in implementing the C-ESMP and recommendations for future
       improvement.
   -   Identification of non-compliances and non-conformances, with proposed corrective
       actions.


                                                                                                360
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    -    Reports from subcontractors related to C-ESMP implementation, including minutes of
         meetings and discussions organized by the contractor.
5.3.4.      Responsibilities of the Contractor’s Safety and Environment Officer (C-ES)
The contractor is required to appoint a qualified individual as the Contractor’s Environmental and
Safety Officer (C-ES) at the construction site. The C-ES must be trained in environmental
management and possess the necessary skills to communicate environmental management
knowledge to all relevant staff involved in the contract. The C-ES shall be responsible for
monitoring compliance with the contractor’s ESMP and environmental technical specifications.
The responsibilities of the C-ES include:
    -    Conducting on-site environmental monitoring to assess the contractor’s ESHS
         performance, equipment, and construction methods related to pollution control, as well as
         the adequacy of mitigation measures implementation.
    -    Monitoring compliance with environmental protection solutions, pollution prevention
         methods, control measures, and contractual requirements.
    -    Supervising the implementation of environmental impact mitigation measures.
    -    Preparing report on E&S data and site environmental conditions.
    -    Investigating complaints and recommending corrective actions as required.
    -    Advising the contractor on environmental improvement, awareness, and proactive
         implementation of pollution prevention solutions.
    -    Proposing appropriate mitigation measures to the contractor in cases of non-compliance
         and conducting additional monitoring as instructed by the PMU’s ES/ESO and the CSC.
    -    Reporting environmental issues to the contractor, ESO and ES (from PMU and CSC),
         submitting the contractor’s ESMP implementation reports to the PMU’s ESO, CSC’s ES,
         and other relevant agencies as required.
    -    Maintaining detailed records of on-site activities related to environmental matters.
    -    Utilizing or appointing qualified staff to implement required actions and measures to
         ensure labor-related issues are addressed, such as occupational health and safety (OHS)
         specialists to manage OHS matters.
    -    Preparing the Contractor’s Labor Management Procedures (LMP) and Contractor’s ESMP
         (C-ESMP), including OHS provisions, applicable to contracted workers under the project.
    -    Submitting the contractor’s LMP and C-ESMP to the PMU/CSC for review and approval
         before mobilizing staff to the project site.
    -    Supervising subcontractors’works, including their compliance with the contractor’s LMP
         and C-ESMP.
    -    Keeping employment records for contracted workers (including subcontractors), including
         documentation verifying minimum working age as stipulated in the LMP.
    -    Providing regular training for contracted workers on topics including, but not limited to,
         occupational health and safety, social risks such as GBV, SEA/SH, and the Code of
         Conduct to maintain good relations with local communities.


                                                                                                361
                                                                                                Official Use Only
    -    Requiring primary suppliers to identify and address risks related to child labor, forced
         labor, GBV, SEA/SH, and OHS for their workers.
    -    Establishing and implementing a grievance mechanism for contracted workers based on
         the GRM outlined in this LMP, ensuring that worker complaints are addressed promptly,
         and reporting the status of grievances and resolutions to the CSC and PMU. This grievance
         mechanism shall be part of the contractor’s LMP.
    -    Ensuring that all contractors and subcontractor workers understand and sign the Code of
         Conduct before commencing work.
    -    Taking all necessary measures to address risks of sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and
         sexual harassment (SH) as stipulated in the contractor’s LMP/C-ESMP and ensuring full
         compliance with these measures.
    -    Reporting incidents at the construction site and labor/OHS implementation status to the
         PMU in a timely manner.
Incident Reporting
Contractors shall notify the Construction Supervision Consultant and the Project Owner of any
incidents listed below within the agreed timeframe:
    -    Any violation of national laws, regulations, or international agreements.
    -    Any serious accidents or fatalities.
    -    Significant impacts resulting in personal property damage, such as traffic accidents,
         damage to houses/roads, or other incidents.
    -    Severe surface water/groundwater pollution.
    -    Failures of dikes/embankments at temporary disposal sites causing serious environmental
         pollution.
    -    Any fire incident linked to worker behavior or labor influx.
    -    Any complaints related to sexual harassment, child abuse, or other child-related incidents.
    -    Complaints received regarding pollution or damage.

5.3.5.      Responsibilities of the Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring
            Consultant (IESMC)
Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) will be contracted by the
PMU to provide several environmental protection training courses for relevant parties and to carry
out environmental inspections. The IESMC will perform monitoring tasks, including:
    -    Providing support to the PMU in establishing and operating the environmental and social
         management system (EMS), coordinating with the CSC team to train Contractors on the
         Project’s ESHS management requirements.
    -    Organizing training courses and implementing capacity building activities for the PMU,
         CSC, Contractors, and relevant stakeholders on ESHS during Project implementation. This




                                                                                                362
                                                                                                Official Use Only
         includes conducting training sessions to raise awareness on Gender/Labor Influx issues for
         Contractor staff, CSC staff, and PMU officers.
    -    Carrying out random field inspections to certify compliance or propose timely
         corrective/improvement actions.
    -    Assessing environmental quality in areas affected by construction activities (including site
         observations, reviewing environmental quality data provided by the CSC, reviewing other
         available documents, and conducting additional sampling if necessary)
    -    Reviewing the Contractor’s environmental compliance, including the implementation of
         mitigation measures and related documentation
    -    Reviewing the compliance of the PMU and CSC with the ESMP
    -    The IESMC will also provide guidance and technical support to the PMU and EO regarding
         environmental and social issues.
5.3.6.      Responsibilities of the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC)
During the construction phase, the CSC, qualified as required by the PMU, will carry out regular
environmental supervision. The CSC will assign Environmental and Social (ES) staff, who will be
responsible for inspecting and supervising all construction activities to ensure that the mitigation
measures set out in the ESMP are properly implemented and that the Project’s adverse E&S
impacts are minimized. The CSC will recruit a sufficient number of Environmental Supervision
Engineers with adequate knowledge of environmental protection and construction project
management to perform the required tasks and supervise the Contractor’s implementation.
Specifically, the CSC’s ES staff will:
    -    Review and assess, on behalf of the PMU, whether the construction design meets the
         requirements of mitigation measures and impact management under the ESMP
    -    Review and approve C’ESMPs
    -    Coordinate with the PMU’s Environmental Officer (EO) in reviewing environmental
         compliance at proposed new material and quarry sites, and advise the PMU on whether
         these extraction sites qualify for Project use
    -    Verify and confirm with the PMU’s environmental monitoring procedures, including
         parameters, monitoring locations, equipment, and monitoring results
    -    Supervise the Contractor’s implementation of the C-ESMP, including enforcement,
         proposing timely additional measures to improve mitigation efforts, and providing
         corrective guidance.
    -    Prepare monthly reports on the Contractor’s ESHS compliance status.
    -    Review payment requests related to environmental mitigation costs, if any.
5.3.7.      Compliance With Laws and Contractual Requirements
Construction activities will comply not only with the contractual requirements on pollution control
and environmental protection but also with the laws on pollution control and environmental
protection of the Socialist Republic of Viet Nam.




                                                                                                 363
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
All reports on construction methods submitted by the Contractor to the CSC for approval will also
be sent to the ES for review to determine whether pollution control and environmental protection
measures are adequately included.
The ES will also review the progress of work items to check for any violations of relevant
environmental laws, thereby preventing potential breaches.
The Contractor will periodically provide copies of relevant documents to the E&S staff of PMU
and CSC. These documents shall at least include Construction Progress Reports, updated
Construction Method Statements, applications for permits/registrations under environmental
protection laws, and all valid permits/registrations. The Contractor’s on-site E&S staff (C-ES) and
the CSC’s ES will also have the right to access the Site Logbook upon request.
Upon receipt of these documents, the SEO and ES will advise the PMU’s Environmental Officer
(EO) and the Contractor on cases of non-compliance with laws and contractual requirements on
environmental protection and pollution control, in order to guide subsequent actions. If the SEO
and ES conclude that a permit/registration application or any preparatory work for pollution
control and environmental protection may not comply with the method statement or may
potentially lead to violations of pollution control and environmental protection requirements, they
will provide recommendations to the Contractor and the PMU.
5.3.8.     Environmental Complaints and Sanctions System
The bidding documents will require the Contractor to arrange an Environmental, Social, Health,
and Safety (ESHS) Performance Security in the amount of 1–3% of the contract value. In the event
of serious non-compliance, environmental pollution, or social issues that the Contractor fails to
remedy within the permitted timeframe, funds from the ESHS Performance Security will be used
to pay a third party mobilized by the PMU to resolve these issues.
On the other hand, if CSC/IESMC/PMU detect non-compliance by the Contractor with the ESMP
and environmental regulations during site supervision, up to 2% of the Contractor’s monthly
interim payment will be withheld. The Contractor will be granted a grace period (determined by
CSC / PMU) to correct the violation. If the Contractor satisfactorily implements corrective actions
within the grace period (confirmed by CSC / PMU), no penalties will be applied, and the withheld
funds will be released to the Contractor. However, if the Contractor fails to carry out the necessary
corrective measures within the grace period, the Contractor shall bear the costs of engaging a third
party to rectify the damage (deducted from their payments).
In the event that IESMC/CSC/PMU fails to detect the Contractor’s non-compliance with
environmental regulations, these units shall be responsible for covering the costs of remedying the
violations.
Based on this Environmental and Social Compliance Framework, which includes the penalty
system, CSC will develop a detailed penalty system for environmental and social non-compliance
and submit it to the PMU for approval and implementation.
5.3.9.     Reporting Mechanism
The PMU is responsible for conducting internal supervision of the implementation of the reporting
regime. In addition, the PMU will hire an independent monitoring agency (EMA) to carry out
independent monitoring of the implementation of the Resettlement Plan (RP) and to assess the
living standards of those affected during and after the completion of resettlement.


                                                                                                 364
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
Both internal and independent monitoring will be regular (monthly for internal monitoring and six
months for independent monitoring). A final project assessment of the implementation of the
resettlement is mandatory and a report will be prepared to confirm whether the ESF's objectives
are being achieved. Tools for monitoring and reporting on ESMP implementation are summarized
in the Table below.
                                     Table 5.1. Reporting Mode

5.4.       Environmental and Social Monitoring Program
     Environmental and social monitoring for the Hai Phong Urban Infrastructure and Environment
Improvement Project will be implemented throughout the construction and operation phases.
Monitoring focuses on key receptors and impact pathways identified in Chapter 4, including
communities along the project’s components. The Hai Phong PMU is responsible for overall
management of the monitoring program, with technical support from the CSC and IESMC as
specified in the ESMP.
5.4.1.      Construction-phase monitoring
(1) Air Quality and Noise Monitoring
•       Monitoring locations: 04 locations, including:
           o Residential areas along the Re River
           o Residential areas near Tam Bac Bridge
           o Residential areas near the intersection of Ring Road No. 3 and DT359 Road
           o Gate area of Trung Ha Kindergarten
•       Monitoring parameters: Noise, Total Suspended Particulates (TSP).
•       Monitoring frequency: Once every 06 months.
•       Applicable standards for comparison: QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT; QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT.

(2) Surface Water Quality Monitoring
•       Monitoring locations: 04 locations, including surface water of the Re River, Tam Bac River,
        Ruot Lon River, and Cam River:
           o Re River – Construction areas of road and embankment along Re River.



                                                                                                      365
                                                                                                      Official Use Only
         o Downstream of Tam Bac River – at the dam construction site
         o Cam River – at Ngo Quyen Bridge
         o Ruot Lon River – at the Ruot Lon Bridge crossing
•   Monitoring parameters: pH, COD, BOD₅, TSS, Total Nitrogen, Total Phosphorus, Total Oil
    and Grease.
•   Monitoring frequency: Once every 06 months.
•   Applicable standards for comparison:QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT – National Technical
    Regulation on Surface Water Quality (Table 1 for Total Oil and Grease; Table 2, Column B
    for pH, COD, BOD₅, TSS, Total Nitrogen, Total Phosphorus); QCVN 10:2023/BTNMT –
    National Technical Regulation on Marine Water Quality.

(3) Soil Quality Monitoring
•   Monitoring locations: 04 locations, including aquaculture ponds along the Re River, areas
    along Ring Road No. 3, Ruot Lon Bridge, and Ngo Quyen Bridge.
•   Monitoring parameters: pH, salinity, As, Cd, Cu, Zn, Pb, Cr.
•   Monitoring frequency: One-time monitoring (prior to transportation to disposal areas).
•   Applicable standard for comparison: QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT.

(4) Monitoring of Domestic Solid Waste, Construction Solid Waste, and Hazardous Waste
•   Conduct classification and segregation of domestic solid waste, non-hazardous solid waste,
    and hazardous waste in accordance with the Law on Environmental Protection, Decree No.
    08/2022/NĐ-CP dated 10 January 2022 (as amended by Decree No. 05/2025/NĐ-CP dated 6
    January 2025), and other relevant legal regulations.
•   Periodically transfer solid waste and hazardous waste to licensed entities for collection,
    transportation, and treatment in compliance with applicable regulations.
•   Applicable regulations: Law on Environmental Protection and other relevant legal regulations.

(5) Riverbed and Riverbank Erosion Monitoring
•   Monitoring locations: Re River embankment areas; construction areas of bridges crossing the
    Cam River and Ruot Lon River.
•   Monitoring frequency: Daily monitoring.

5.4.2.       Operation-phase monitoring
(1) Automatic Wastewater Monitoring
•   Monitoring location: 01 location at the effluent monitoring channel of the centralized
    wastewater treatment plant.
•   Monitoring parameters: Wastewater flow rate (influent and effluent), pH, temperature, TSS,
    COD, Ammonium.
•   Monitoring frequency: Continuous, automatic monitoring.
•   Applicable standard: QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on Domestic
    Wastewater Discharged into Water Sources Used for Domestic Water Supply Purposes
    (Column A; 2,000 < F ≤ 20,000).
•   Automatic monitoring data shall be connected and transmitted to the Environmental
    Monitoring Center of the Hai Phong Department of Agriculture and Environment.


                                                                                             366
                                                                                             Official Use Only
(2) Automatic Air Monitoring
•   Monitoring location: 01 location at the sludge treatment area.
•   Monitoring parameter: H₂S.
•   Monitoring frequency: Continuous, automatic monitoring.
•   Applicable standard: QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on Ambient
    Air Quality (Table 2).

(3) Periodic Wastewater Monitoring
•   Monitoring location: 01 location at the effluent monitoring channel of the centralized
    wastewater treatment plant.
•   Monitoring parameters: BOD₅ (20°C), total dissolved solids, sulfide (as H₂S), nitrate (as N),
    phosphate (as P), animal and vegetable oil and grease, total surfactants, total coliforms.
•   Monitoring frequency: Once every 03 months.
•   Applicable standard for evaluation: QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT – National Technical
    Regulation on Domestic Wastewater Discharged into Water Sources Used for Domestic Water
    Supply Purposes (Column A; 2,000 < F ≤ 20,000).

(4) Monitoring of Solid Waste and Hazardous Waste
•   Conduct classification and management of solid waste and hazardous waste, particularly
    sludge generated from the wastewater treatment plant, in accordance with Decree No.
    08/2022/NĐ-CP (as amended and supplemented by Decree No. 05/2025/NĐ-CP), Circular No.
    02/2022/TT-BTNMT (as amended and supplemented by Circular No. 07/2025/TT-BTNMT).

(5) Trial operation, monitoring and environmental permit (in accordance with Circular No.
02/2022/TT-BTNMT and Circular No. 07/2025/TT-BTNMT)
•   The centralized wastewater treatment plant shall be subject to trial operation prior to official
    operation, as required for facilities discharging wastewater subject to environmental
    permitting.
•   During the trial operation period, the project owner shall conduct environmental monitoring of
    wastewater discharge, consistent with the monitoring parameters, locations and frequency
    specified for the environmental permit, including both automatic monitoring and periodic
    monitoring as applicable.
•   Monitoring during trial operation shall be carried out by a qualified environmental monitoring
    organization in accordance with technical requirements stipulated in Circular No. 02/2022/TT-
    BTNMT and Circular No. 07/2025/TT-BTNMT.
•   Monitoring costs during the trial operation period shall be borne by the project owner and
    included in the project budget, as part of the responsibilities for environmental protection
    works prior to handover.
•   Results of monitoring during trial operation shall be compiled and submitted as part of the
    environmental permit application dossier, serving as the basis for demonstrating compliance
    with applicable wastewater discharge standards.
•   The environmental permit must be issued by the competent authority before official operation
    and prior to handover of the wastewater treatment facilities from the contractor to the investor,
    in accordance with the above Circulars.



                                                                                                 367
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
•     Only after completion of the trial operation, submission of satisfactory monitoring results, and
      issuance of the environmental permit may the wastewater treatment system be put into official
      operation.

          implementation. Training for PMU staff is expected to continue during the initial years of

          held twice a year for at least 2 years.

5.5.     Cost Estimation for ESMP Implementation
5.5.1.       Environmental and social monitoring costs
The costs for environmental quality monitoring activities include: (1) the costs of periodic
monitoring of surface water quality, air quality, and noise levels during the construction phase;
and (2) the costs of wastewater quality monitoring during the operation phase, calculated for one
year of operation.
The automatic and continuous monitoring of wastewater and air quality is included in the Project’s
equipment procurement costs and has been incorporated into the total investment cost of the
Project.


                                                                                                      370
                                                                                                      Official Use Only
Monitoring activities during the trial operation phase (as mentioned in Section 5.4.2 (5)) are
included in the wastewater treatment plant construction package and will be carried out by the
construction contractor.
In accordance with Vietnamese regulations, prior to official operation, the Project Owner will carry
out the procedures to obtain an Environmental Permit in compliance with Decree No. 08/2022/ND-
CP dated 10 January 2022 of the Government, as amended and supplemented by Decree No.
05/2025/ND-CP dated 06 January 2025 of the Government. The budget for this activity is included
in the total investment cost of the Project and will be further detailed in the subsequent stage.

                                                                                                 374
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
5.5.5. Project-level complaint settlement mechanism
Within the framework of Vietnamese law, citizens who make complaints have the right to
protection. As part of the overall implementation of the project, the complaint resolution
mechanism (GRM) will be developed by the EO, SO of the PMU, according to the procedure, the
responsible person and contact information will be prepared. It will be easy to ensure that
complaints will be handled and resolved at the lowest level as quickly as possible. This mechanism
will provide a framework in which complaints about environmental and safety issues can be
handled, complaints can be resolved, and disputes can be resolved in a timely manner. The GRM
will be launched before the start of construction.
During the development process, the Complaint Resolution Mechanism (GRM) will also be
managed by contractors under the supervision of CSC. Contractors will inform affected
communities and communes of the availability of a complaint settlement mechanism to resolve
complaints and problems related to the sub-project. This will be done through a public consultation
and information disclosure process under which contractors will regularly communicate with the
affected community and relevant agencies. Meetings will be held at least quarterly, monthly,
project fact sheets will be published, announcements will be posted in local media, and
announcements of upcoming activities will be posted, Contractors should provide contact
information for any complaints when contractors communicate construction progress to the local
community.
All complaints and corresponding actions taken by contractors will be recorded in the sub-project
monitoring report. Claims and claims for damages can be made in the following ways:
   -   Verbally: directly to CSC and/or the contractor's security guard or representative at the
       regional office.
   -   In writing: by handing over or sending a complaint to specific addresses.
   -   By phone, fax, e-mail: to the CSC, security guard or the contractor's representative.
Upon receipt of the complaint, CSC and the Contractor's safety officer or representative will
register the complaint in the complaint file and will record the facts related to the complaint
thereafter, until the complaint is resolved. Immediately after receiving the complaint, it is
necessary to copy the complaint into 4 copies. The original will be kept in the file, a copy will be
kept by the contractor safety officer, a copy will be sent to CSC, and the final copy will be sent to
the PMU within 24 hours of receiving the complaint.
The information documented in the complaint will include:
   -   Date and time of complaint.
   -   The complainant's name, address, and contact details.
   -   A short description of the complaint.
   -   Actions are taken to resolve the complaint, including who to contact and detect at each
       stage of the complaint settlement process.
   -   The date and time when the complainant were contacted during the settlement process.
   -   Final decision of the complaint.
   -   The date, time and manner in which the complainant was notified.


                                                                                                 375
                                                                                                 Official Use Only
    -   Signature of the complainant when the decision is issued.
Small complaints will be resolved within a week. Within two weeks (and weeks thereafter), a
written response will be sent to the complainant (by hand, fax, e-mail) outlining the procedures to
be carried out and the process by date.
The main goal is to solve the problem as quickly as possible by the simplest means, involving as
few people as possible and at the lowest possible level. Only if the problem cannot be solved at
the simplest level and / or within 15 days, then other authorities will step in. Such a situation may
arise, for example, when a claim for damages is made, the amount payable cannot be settled, or
the causes of the damage are identified.
Complaint and complaint settlement procedures shall be carried out as follows:
Level 1 – People's Committee of commune/ward
The affected household shall submit its complaint to one of the officials of the commune/ward
People's Committee, through the village/hamlet head or directly to the commune People's
Committee, in writing or orally. The officer(s) of the commune/ward People's Committee or the
village head shall notify the ward/commune People's Committee of the complaint. The People's
Committee of the commune/ward will work directly with the affected households and will come
up with a plan to settle the complaint within 5 working days after receiving the complaint. The
Secretariat of the People's Committee of the commune/ward is responsible for making records and
recording all complaints that are being resolved. After the People's Committee of the
commune/ward issues a settlement decision, the household can continue to complain within 30
working days. In case the second decision has been issued and the above-mentioned household is
still not satisfied with that decision; such household may lodge a complaint with the provincial-
level People's Committee.
Second level – Provincial People's Committee
Upon receiving complaints from households, the Provincial People's Committee will have 30
working days to resolve the case. The Provincial People's Committee is responsible for compiling
and archiving records of all complaints sent. After the Provincial People's Committee issues a
decision, households can complain within 45 working days. In case a second decision has been
issued but the household is still not satisfied with that decision, it has the right to lodge a complaint
with the People's Court within 45 working days. After that, the Provincial People's Committee will
have to deposit the compensation money into an account.
Third Level - Courts
In case the complainant initiates a lawsuit to the commune or provincial People's Court, and the
Court shall settle in favor of the complainant. The complaint settlement decision must be sent to
the complainant and related parties and publicly posted at the head office of the relevant People’s
Committee. The complainant will receive such decision after 3 days from the date of complaint
settlement results at the ward and commune levels and 7 working days for communes and
provinces.
The GRM Officer will need to coordinate with the above levels to maintain a database of
complaints related to the Project from relevant parties, including information such as the content
of the complaint, the source of the complaint and the date of receipt of the complaint, etc. the full
name and address of the complainant, the handling plan and existing status, including complaints


                                                                                                    376
                                                                                                     Official Use Only
received orally and in writing. In case of necessity, the GRM Officer may contact the complainant
directly and instruct them to carry out the project's formal complaint settlement process.

Grievance mechanism for workers: Project workers (all three types: direct workers, contract
workers, and main supply workers) can directly submit their grievances to the GRM for workers.
Members of this GRM will be trained to handle worker complaints, including workplace
grievances, effectively and reasonably, in compliance with national labor regulations and the
World Bank’s Environmental and Social Standard 2 (ESS2).

     World Bank Grievance Mechanism: Individuals and communities who believe that a
component supported by the World Bank adversely affects their lives can submit their grievances
to the existing component-level grievance mechanism or directly to the World Bank’s Grievance
Redress Service (GRS). This mechanism ensures that submitted grievances are reviewed promptly
to address issues related to the component. Individuals or organizations affected by a component
may also submit complaints to the World Bank Inspection Panel to determine whether any harm
has occurred or could occur due to non-compliance with World Bank policies and procedures.
Complaints can be submitted at any time when the issue is of concern to the World Bank, and the
Bank’s management is responsible for responding. For more information on how to submit a
complaint to the World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS), please visit
www.worldbank.org/grs. For more information on submitting a complaint to the World Bank
Inspection Panel, please visit www.inspectionpanel.org.

                                                                                             Official Use Only
                               PUBLIC CONSULTATION & DISCLOSURE
6.1.     SUMMARY OF PUBLIC CONSULTATION PROCESS
    The World Bank's policy on Environmental and Social Standards No.5 (ESS5) and No.10
(ESS10) require consultation and notification to affected people and local authority on social and
environmental issues during project preparation.
    Public consultation for the ESIA report must also comply with the requirements of Decree
No. 08/2022/ND-CP and Decree No. 05/2025/ND-CP dated 6 January 2025 of the Government.
      Public consultation is conducted with the purposes of: (i) sharing information about the
project's items and expected activities with the project area communities and stakeholders; and
(ii) collecting comments and concerns about local characteristics and environmental sensitive
issues in the project area from the government and local communities. On that basis, public
concerns will be included in the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report and project
mitigation measures.
      Project stakeholders are defined as individuals, groups and other entities who: (i) are impacted
directly or indirectly, positively or adversely by the project (also known as ‘affected parties’); and
(ii) have an interest in the project (‘interested parties’).
     The project mostly employed interviews, focus group discussions, open meetings, and
workshops as a means of carrying out consultations. There will be conducted for three phases of
project implementation i) preparation phase (before project appraisal); ii) Pre-construction and
construction phase and iii) Post construction/maintenance phase. Public consultation strategy
including targeted groups/attendees, subjects, formats, time frame, and responsibilities is shown
in the following table
       Table 6.1. Public consultation strategy for the three project implementation phases

    In accordance with the provisions of Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP and Decree No.

              Consultation with affected communities and directly impacted
           individuals
     a. Consultation Through Public Meetings

     The Project Owner coordinated with the local authorities to organize community consultation
meetings with households directly affected by the Project in the wards within the project area
(including Dong Hai, Nam Trieu, Bach Dang, Hoa Binh, Luu Kiem, Hong Bang, An Duong, An
Hai, Gia Vien, and Le Chan Wards). For Thuy Nguyen and Ngo Quyen Wards, the project area is
located within zones already cleared, with no adjacent households directly affected; therefore, no
community consultation meetings were organized in these locations.
    Expected participants of the consultation meetings include:
     - Representatives of the Ward/Commune People’s Committee;
     - Representatives of the Project Owner; Representatives of the consultant preparing the
ESIA report;
      - Attending delegates: representatives of the Fatherland Front Committee, political–social
organizations (Party Committee, Youth Union, Veterans’ Association), social organizations
(Women’s Union, Farmers’ Association, Land Administration, Environment Unit), representatives
of residential communities, and entities directly affected by the project.
    Main consultation contents include:
     - Presentation of key project information (proposed project components, scale of
construction items, construction schedule, and potential negative impacts on the environment and
community health during project implementation);
      - Proposed mitigation measures and the Project’s Environmental Management and
Monitoring Program;
      - Consultation with affected households on the contents of the ESIA report;
      - Recording and addressing comments, feedback, and recommendations from stakeholders.

               Written consultation
     The Project Owner conducted written consultation by sending official letters together with the
ESIA report to the People’s Committees and the Vietnam Fatherland Front Committees of the
wards/communes within the project area, as well as to affected agencies and organizations, in order
to request their comments on the Project’s ESIA report. The details are as follows:

6.2.    RESULTS OF PUBLIC CONSULTATION
    The PMU and ESIA consultants had cooperated with the local authorities to invite the
project’s affected households and representatives of all project-related targets to the organized
consultation meetings. The meetings with the participants of the 13 wards/communes in the project
areas were conducted during from Oct 9 to 18 of 2025. Contents of the meetings include:
       - Introducing the project including the proposed locations, investment contents and ROW of
all the project’s items/ components.
       - Presenting the WB environmental and social standards/policies, and relevant regulations
of Vietnam, anticipated environmental and social impacts and respective mitigation measures, the
grievance redress mechanism (GRM) for environmental and resettlement problems; and
       - Seeking for the feedbacks from all attendees and providing clarifications or responds if
needed.

                                                                                                                          407
                                                                                                                           Official Use Only
6.3.   INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
     The draft ESIA in Vietnamese was disclosed at the offices of 13 wards/communes from 1
October 2025, pursuant to Official Letter No. 1594/BQL-DA1 dated 1 October 2025 regarding
public consultation during the EIA process of the Hai Phong Climate Resilient Urban Development
Project. The final ESIA both in the local language and English will be disclosed locally at the
project sites. The final ESIA, together with RPF/RP, SEP, LMP, and ESCP will also be disclosed
locally in an accessible place to project-affected parties and other interested parties as set out in
ESS10, and the World Bank external website. Disclosure is expected to take place immediately
after the reports are approved and prior to the commencement of major construction activities, in
order to ensure that affected communities and other stakeholders have sufficient time to access the
information and provide feedback, as appropriate.

                                                                                                 Official Use Only
